==============================

# CHAPTER ONE
Energetics of Traditional Medicine

Herbs, plants, and other medicine manifestly operate, either by heat, coldness, dryness, or moisture, for the world being composed of so many qualities, they and only they can be found in the world, and the mixtures of them one with another.
—NICHOLAS CULPEPER (1653, 376)
In The Earthwise Herbal I have used a variety of different systems and ideas to describe the properties (and energetics) of medicinal plants. Because this is a Western herbal, I have largely used Western systems—the four qualities of Greek medicine (hot, cold, damp, dry) and the six tissue states of physiomedicalism (nineteenth-century American botanical medicine). In addition, I have used other Western and Eastern approaches. In this chapter we will examine the most commonly used energetic systems of the Old World: Greek, Ayurvedic, and traditional Chinese medicine (TCM). In addition we will discuss the alchemical perspective, which follows a somewhat different approach to energetics. In my subsequent volume on plants of the New World used in Western herbal medicine I will discuss the energetics of American Indian medicine and nineteenth-century North American botanical medicine in greater detail.

## Greek Medicine

It is generally held that Greek medicine arose in the fifth century BCE, when the original doctrines were laid down by Empedocles, Plato, Aristotle, Hippocrates, and the “Hippocratic school.” However, it was not until the third century CE, during the Roman period, that a Greek, Galen of Pergamum, pulled the entire mass together into a comprehensive system. Greek medicine, so called, actually reached its peak during the Middle Ages. Ibn Sina brought it to its peak of internal logic, following the Aristotelian model.
By the late medieval period Greek science and medicine were becoming increasingly rigid and theoretical. In 1527 Paracelsus threw the Canon of Avicenna into a bonfire in Basle to mark his disdain for the classical system. He introduced a new method that gave birth, on the one hand, to modern chemistry, pharmacology, and physiology, and on the other to “alchemical medicine,” described later in this chapter.

### THE FOUR ELEMENTS

The Greek philosopher-scientists opined about various numbers and kinds of elements until Empedocles arrived at four, consisting of earth, water, air, and fire. A generation before Socrates, Empedocles attempted to explain the operations of the body in terms of the four elements. He was dismissed as a “theorist,” by one of the Hippocratic writers, who thought it was speculative to attempt to understand anything about the inner workings of the body. Although it is true that many of the Greek philosophers were theorists, Empedocles was more of an archaic throwback to shamanism. His use of the four elements suggests magical roots more than philosophical ones.
Empedocles considered the duty of a healer to include both medicine and weather-making. This may explain why he gravitated to the concept of four elements. For Empedocles there had to be four directions or winds and these would naturally have suggested four elements in the Earth and the human body.
Modern scientists are apt to laugh at the four elements as naive constructs, but they simply represent the four phases of matter known to the chemist—solid (earth), liquid (water), gas (air), and combustion (fire). Such “elements” very adequately explain the natural constituents of the body. Probably due to their shamanic roots, the four elements of Greek cosmology are strikingly compatible with the four directions of American Indian wisdom teachings.
Plato adopted the doctrine of the four elements from Empedocles. However, his student Aristotle argued that the four elements were not ultimate substances of matter, but the derivatives of four more primal substances: hot, cold, damp, and dry. Thus, fire was warm and dry, air was warm and damp, water was damp and cold, and earth was cold and dry. Against this the Stoics and other Greek philosophers argued that fire was hot, air dry, water damp, and earth cold. Proclus, who held the seat of Archon of the Academy, eight hundred years after Plato and Aristotle, shortly before the closure of that great school by the Christian emperor Justinian, showed through textual analysis that Aristotle had actually misunderstood Plato.
The physicians adopted the Aristotelian model, although Proclus had an influence on the alchemists and other proto-scientists who adopted the Platonic paradigm. Thus, the four qualities of hot and cold, damp and dry, became the basis of the “energetics” in Greek medicine. Polybus, the son-in-law of Hippocrates, fashioned a system of four humors and temperaments corresponding to the four qualities, and Galen officially adopted the qualities—this was five hundred years after Aristotle first introduced them—as the basis of medicine.
The four elements, or the four qualities, were considered to be the basis of the “formal” causes of events and bodies in the world. The word “form” (eidos, eidaea) means something like “archetype”—the elements or qualities were conceived to be the archetypal and formative substratum of the world. At the same time they were conceived in physical terms—solid, liquid, gas, combustion. For Aristotle, the form was inseparable from matter, upon which it was stamped like an identity pattern.

### THE FOUR QUALITIES

We should not imagine that the four qualities—hot, cold, damp, dry—are identical to changes measured on a thermometer or barometer, or even to the sensations we experience by those names. The Greeks were unable to measure temperature and humidity so they described their actions. Here is Aristotle’s description of the properties of the four qualities (Mure, 1964, 73):
Hot is that which associates things of the same kind … while cold … associates homogeneous and heterogeneous things alike. Fluid is that which, being readily adaptable in shape, is not determinable by any limit of its own, while dry is that which is readily determinable by its own limit, but readily adaptable in shape.
If we define substances in this way what we call water would have been “cold and dry” when it was ice, “cold and wet” when it was liquid, and “wet and warm” when it was vapor. So we see that hot and cold, damp and dry, don’t signify what we take them to mean today.
The four qualities were further divided into four degrees of intensity. Once again, these degrees have nothing to do with temperature, barometric pressure, or any kind of scale or continuum of measurement. Rather, each degree corresponds to a property of the quality, and each degree is more intense as the number increases from one to four. Let’s try to define these differences.
HOT
The primary effect of heat is to purify a substance by removing impurities, like ore removed from metal in a forge. Heat in the first degree opens the pores of the skin and tissues to let fluids flow out; heat in the second degree thins fluids so that they can flow out through the pores; heat in the third degree increases the innate heat of the body that drives the fluids out through the pores; and heat in the fourth degree burns the skin to remove foreign growths. Thus, heat has four actions, increasing in intensity: it is opening, thinning, warming, and burning.
COLD
In contrast, cold is conceived as that which binds together completely different constituents, no matter what their relationship. (This, of course, makes them “compound,” or impure, in comparison to fire, which makes them “simple” or pure.) The different parts of the organism—organs, tissues, and psychic components—are bound together by the cold quality; it is necessary for integrated life. The organism, for the Greeks, is a “compound” body. Hence, cold remedies not only cool and relieve heat, but they hold together the organism. Thus, cold in the first degree cools the body on a hot summer day, refreshes the spirits, and returns the temperature to normal; cold in the second degree abates active inflammation; cold in the third degree thickens and contains substances within the organism, such as sweat and diarrhea, also condensing “vapors” that rise and cause nervousness, restlessness, and madness. Cold in the fourth degree causes unconsciousness and pain relief. Consequently, cold has four actions: it is refreshing, cooling, thickening, and anodyne.
DRY
Damp and dry are measurements of humidity rather than temperature. Like hot and cold, the ancients understood them as categories rather than as sensations or observable chemical states. Thus, dryness is that which hardens to such an extent that it causes a boundary that keeps things within, especially fluids. Dry remedies are therefore “strengthening to nature” (acclimatizing, resistance-building), binding (astringent), hardening, and drawing.
DAMP
Moisture spreads unstoppably in all directions until it meets a boundary. Damp remedies are loosening or relaxing, moistening, softening, and nourishing.
The four qualities were the basis of practice for Galen. They were the foundation of his “dogma” or “rationale,” from which he called his school the “dogmatic” or “rational.” According to Galen, treatment was always by “hot to cold,” or “cold to hot,” “damp to dry,” and so forth. This was formulated as the so-called “law of contraries.” However, Galen also recognized another school called the “methodists” because they believed in a single rationale or dogma (purging and astringing). They developed the following energetic system.

### THE TWO TISSUE STATES

The methodists took their cue from the atomic theory of Greek science—all substances are composed of invisible primal particles or “atoms.” If the atoms were compacted and condensed then the tissue was dense, hard, and constricted. If they were spread out or loosely compacted they produced loose, soft, relaxed tissue conditions. For the methodists, medicine adjusted the organism or its parts to the right tension. These two states were called status strictus and status laxus. An admixture of the two states in one, status mixta, was also recognized.
It is unfortunate that the advocates of the four qualities and the two tissue states could not agree long enough to conjoin their systems, because they supplement each other nicely. The four qualities do not address tension or relaxation. Used together, the qualities and states produce six basic pathological conditions that correspond fairly closely with the six “tissue states” recognized in physiomedicalism (nineteenth-century botanical medicine), described later in this chapter.
There was a third “school” of medicine recognized by Galen, in contrast to the dogmatic and the methodist, which he called the “empirical.” This school consisted mostly of village herbalists, simplers, and untrained practitioners who had no doctrine but practiced from experience (empiricism). Galen noted that when these practitioners did follow a doctrine, however, it was usually “like treats like” based on the “doctrine of signatures” (the plant looks like the disease, person, or organ of the body).
The empirical school was long persecuted, but it was organized into a system by Paracelsus in the sixteenth century. This became the alchemical school, which will be discussed below. For Paracelsus, the four elements were largely unimportant; instead, he divided the natural world into three substances: essence, energy, and body (mercurius, sulphur, and salt).

### THE FIFTH ELEMENT, OR QUINTESSENCE

The doctrine of a “fifth essence” or ether from which the other elements arose is as old as the doctrine of the four elements. The first element or quinta essentia was associated with the empyreum, or “fixed stars,” and thus with unchangeable eternal reality, while the four essentia or elements were associated with the changeable conditions on Earth. The doctrine of the quintessence never became important in Greek medicine, but the idea of the underlying essence reappears in alchemical medicine, where it is very important. The fifth element is also very important in Ayurvedic and Chinese thinking.

## Ayurvedic Medicine

The most ancient texts of Ayurvedic medicine show affinities to the shamanism that was brought down from Asia by Aryan invaders one or two millennia before Christ, as well as to indigenous concepts of the Indus Valley cultures they conquered. At a later date we find the tendency toward rational explanation typical of Greek medicine. Indeed, there is evidence of borrowing back and forth between Greece and India. Still later, these rational treatises are framed with references to the gods and the divine seers who gave out the texts so that the texts became divine and unquestionable.
India is a big place and the timeline for the writing of these texts is long, so there is evidence of different systems being brought together from different sources. During the period of rational analysis attempts at integration and subdivision were made. Thus, Ayurvedic medicine has many different categories that are subdivided logically into smaller categories or pathological descriptions. The approach has more of the analytical, logical feel of Aristotelianism than the intuitive approach of traditional Chinese medicine.

### THE FIVE ELEMENTS

Ayurvedic medicine possesses the same four elements of Greek philosophy (fire, air, water, earth), with the addition of a fifth (ether, or wind). This is so similar to the Greek concept that we might suppose there is some cultural continuity here between the two systems. Wind or ether, as in Greek philosophy, has the attribute of acting on the other elements and causing them to change. However, in India the fifth element has assumed greater importance and interactivity with the other four, so that in truth there are five elements.

### THE THREE DOSHAS

The use of three basic categories to classify constitutions has been developed both in India and, more recently, in the West. The Indian conception refers to three doshas or “tinges” that influence the health of the constitution. The Western system boils down to three body types: thin, medium, and thick. The two systems are quite similar and compatible, but also different in subtle ways. Many practitioners use them interchangeably and the Indian names, which are easier to pronounce than the Western terms (“asthenic” or “ectomorph,” for instance, would be “vata”), have been adopted to the Western conceptions.
Nineteenth-century Western physicians used a system of three types—nervous, motive, and visceral—which correspond closely to the system of three somatotypes established through statistical studies by Dr. William Sheldon, a psychologist: ectomorph (thin), mesomorph (medium), and endomorph (thick). German medicine recognizes thin (asthenic) and thick (sthenic). These terms are sometimes used in biomedicine in America and Germany as descriptive terms on intake forms.
VATA
When vata is exaggerated the skin tends to be dry, appetite reduced, with tendencies to gas and constipation, excessive nervousness, wasting, weakness, anxiety, insomnia, tremor, and fatigue (even from thinking about stress). The tongue tends to be dry, thin, long, narrow, and in severe cases, withered. As a constitutional type, vata is thin, underdeveloped physically, but mentally overactive, slightly dark, with cool, dry, rough, or even cracked skin. The ectomorph tends to be thin, with a high forehead and a long face. This indicates enhanced intellectual and sensory activities with medium muscular and digestive powers. The mind is open but changeable, the physical body of medium to poor strength, with little endurance.
PITTA
The mesomorph tends to be medium built, strapping, strong, with a strong, fixed eye, prominent cheekbones, strong will power and determination. There are two types of pitta, the dark and the red. When the latter is exaggerated what we call the circulatory system and the immune system are overactive, with full vessels, reddish surface, tendencies to heat, irritation of the capillaries, allergies, high blood pressure, and heart disease. In the darker people, the liver and gallbladder are strong, leading to strong willfulness, but a tendency to fevers, sallow complexion, and indigestion. The tongue is elongated, carmine red, and pointed.
KAPHA
The endomorph tends to be more thickly built than the others, with large, round surfaces. The lower part of the face, especially the jawbone, is prominent, indicating a strong digestive tract. The eyes tend to have a relaxed or even sleepy look. The countenance is relaxed, more peaceful than the other types, with subtle feelings. When kapha is aggravated there is a tendency for water to build up in the tissues, for mucus to form, for respiratory congestion. The system becomes heavy, congested, cold, inert, fatigued, and sleepy. The digestion and elimination are faulty, with tendencies to constipation. The tongue is round and moist, sometimes coated.
Although the three doshas, or tridosha, are commonly and widely used by American and Canadian herbalists, they represent only one aspect of Ayurvedic teaching and they have been modified (as I have done above) by native traditions within the Western inheritance, so that their use is not exactly representative of standard Ayurveda. Furthermore, it represents use of only a tiny portion of the entire tradition.

## Traditional Chinese Medicine

Like the Greek philosophers and the early Ayurvedic physicians, early Chinese science, philosophy, and medicine were founded upon the idea of providing rational explanations for natural events, but whereas the Greek approach became in time attached to a system that was primarily mathematical and logical, the Chinese were more interested in an intuitive or energetic approach, thus using the doctrine of correspondences. About the fourth century BCE, two schools emerged, one centered on the doctrine of dual opposites or complements (yin/yang) and the other on the doctrine of five elements (wind, fire, earth, water, metal). Whereas, in the West, the tendency has always been for one doctrine to triumph over and eradicate the other, in China syncretism has been more common. Thus, the early proponents of two different systems of yin/yang theory and one system of five element theory fought a sometimes heated battle for supremacy, but instead of one school winning, all were adopted alongside one another. When the earliest great classic of Chinese medicine appeared, The Yellow Emperor’s Classic, the yin/yang and five element schools had both been adopted by scientific physicians.

### YIN AND YANG

Originally yin meant the shady side of the mountain and yang the sunny side. This poetic dichotomy was developed as cosmological principles, so that yin stood for the feminine, dark, passive principle and yang for the masculine, light, active side. In medicine yin and yang came to have quite practical rather than metaphysical meanings. Yin substance is sedating, moistening, and nourishing, while yang energy is stimulating, commanding, and protective. Yin diseases are deep, internal, chronic, cold, and deficient or empty, whereas yang diseases are superficial, external, acute, warm or hot, and full.

### THE EIGHT CATEGORIES

The study of opposite qualities eventually led to the construction of eight guiding criteria or categories used to describe the basic parameters of disease in practical terms. The yin categories are: deficiency, cold, internal, and yin, while the yang categories are excess or full, hot, external, and yang. These categories are very practical and are well worth study by students of all systems of medicine.
COLD
Person is quiet, withdrawn, assumes a fetal position in bed, pale or greenish complexion, clear or tearing eyes, desire to close them, excess saliva, no thirst, desire for warm food, pale or bluish violet lips, nail flesh of the same color, tongue moist, coating of the tongue lacking or white and slippery, body of the tongue pale or pink, expectoration (if any) thin, clear to whitish, copious, clear, copious urine, liquid feces, pulse slow or deep.
HOT
The person is restless, moves readily, lies on back with legs extended, complexion red, sclera of the eye red, thirsty, desire for cold food, reduced saliva, lips red, swollen, dry, or cracked, nail flesh red to violet, coating of the tongue increased, yellow, sometimes showing red, swollen papillae, tongue body hard, leathery, shrunken, copious expectoration yellow and thick, scanty, dark urine, constipation, extremities warm, pulse rapid.
DEFICIENCY
Mental exhaustion, nervous exhaustion but easily overstimulated, insomnia, dizziness, tinnitus, loss of appetite, pale complexion, lips pale, weak voice, sore throat, shortness of breath, irregular breathing, asthma, spontaneous perspiration, reduced warmth of extremities, intermittent bloating of abdomen, abdominal pains better by pressure (massage), diarrhea, limpness and numbness of hands and feet, palpitations, twitching.
EXCESS
Expressions loud, excessive, violent dizziness, headache, great quantities of sputum, impeded breathing (patient cannot lie down), mouth breathing, pulls up shoulders, noises and rumbling in stomach, foul or sour eructations, nausea, vomiting, bloated, distended abdomen, stool dry and hard, or diarrhea, painful, tense muscles, worse from pressure (massage); if the blood is involved (congealed blood) bruises easily, stabbing pains, purple or darkened tongue body, choppy pulse.
INTERNAL
Disturbance arises in or has moved down into the deep interior of the body, hence diseases arising from or affecting the organs and deep emotions, deep-seated, established, prolonged, and chronic illness, continuous fever rising and falling periodically, pains in the abdomen and breast, dry mouth, coating of the tongue yellow, gray, or black, pulse deep.
EXTERNAL
Disturbance is in the outside, surface, or periphery of the body, resulting in a superficial, ephemeral, acute, recent, or receding disease affecting the skin and external defenses of the body. Fever accompanied by chills, aches of the head and extremities, nose stopped up, coating of the tongue missing or reduced in thickness, superficial pulse.
YIN
Cold, deficiency, and internality are all yin symptoms. Face sallow, pale, greenish white, demeanor atonic, listless, tired, looks wasted, enfeebled, reclining position, reluctant to move, tongue body pale, swollen, soft, coating moist, sleek, slippery, voice low and weak, faltering, reluctant, feeble or heavy breathing, shortness of breath, reduced thirst and appetite, refuses food, sometimes wants warm beverages, urine clear and copious, abdominal pains better by pressure, cold body and extremities, pulse deep, slow, always weak.
YANG
Hot, excess, and externality are all yang symptoms. Hence, the person is red, restless, lips dry or cracked, tongue body red or scarlet, coating yellow, loud, excessive voice, talkative, noisy, breath loud, panting, feeling of heat, asks for cold air, refuses food, finds even smell of food repulsive, dry mouth, thirst intense, urine scanty and dark, stool hard, difficult to expel, sometimes malodorous, abdominal pains, worse from pressure, warm or hot body and extremities, pulse superficial, rapid, full, always strong.
“Describing symptoms observed with reference to the guiding criteria not only brings clarity into an otherwise purely subjective description,” writes Manfred Porkert (1983, 32), but “it also is the first step toward a differentiation and generalization of symptoms.”
One person can be a combination of the different parameters. For instance, a person can suffer from excess external heat (yang blazing out of control), deficient external heat (the periphery is weak and lets in the yang but only on the surface), deficient internal heat (internal yin deficiency causes heat), excess internal heat (too much internal heat production), deficient internal cold (no internal heat), deficient external cold (weak periphery lets in cold), external cold (cold from the environment), and so on. “Such combinations give almost unlimited flexibility of precise qualification” (Porkert, 1983, 32).
Yin and yang are also subdivided in a different fashion that is useful. Yin consists of a general yin (fluids) and a more specific yin (blood). The former is more moistening, the later more nutritive. Yang consists of a more general yang (heat) and a more specific component (qi, energy). Pathology in TCM is often explained by deficiency: yin deficiency (lack of fluids), blood deficiency (lack of nutrition), yang deficiency (lack of heat), and qi deficiency (lack of energy).

### THE FIVE ELEMENTS

The doctrine of five elements or “phases” appears very early in Chinese history, well before the fifth century BCE. It would take some time before they became a part of medical diagnosis and therapy. Whereas the four elements in Greek science are really chemical (solid, liquid, and so forth), the five Chinese elements are more like “phases” that succeed one another in a continually changing world. They are also the basis of an extensive system of correspondences. They act upon each other creatively and destructively.
One can see a remote resemblance between fire, metal, water, and earth in China, and fire, air, water, and earth in the West, with wood (or sometimes wind) taking the position of the fifth element.
It should be pointed out that the five phase system, as used in Chinese acupuncture and herbalism, is more metaphysical than the pragmatic zangfu or organ system method, which emphasizes the deficiencies (or occasional excesses) of yin, blood, qi, and yang in the major systems of the body. Some practitioners think the five phase system is too theoretical. For instance, Li Shi Zhen’s twelfth-century classic on Pulse Diagnosis (Hoc Ku Huynh, 1985) doesn’t even mention the five phases and instead attributes pathology to yin and yang, qi and blood, excess and deficiency, and a few other pathological conditions that are observed in the organism and have no basis in correspondence. The modern classic, Ted Kaptchuk’s The Web That Has No Weaver (1983), almost ignores the five phases.
Because the five elements are not used here, I have not included information on them or their correspondences, which can be found in most textbooks on TCM.

## Alchemical Medicine

All three traditions described above included an alchemical component in their worldview. Alchemy was basically concerned with the breakdown of naturally occurring substances into their primal constituents. From an early era, all three traditions recognized three primary substances, which could be called essence (primal configuration), energy (primal energy), and body (primal material). The first was that which gave an entity its form, pattern, or constitution, the second was the intrinsic energy or life force, while the third was the matter. By itself, matter was formless, requiring form to stamp some sort of characteristics upon it.
Paracelsus (1493–1541) was the primary instigator of the alchemical revolution in medicine. This gave rise to the physio-chemical impulse in conventional medicine and foreshadowed the chief doctrines and practices of homeopathy. It was Paracelsus who gave the classic definition for the three primal substances: “that which rises in the smoke is mercurius [essence], that which burns is sulphur [energy], that which remains in the ash is salt [matter].” As can be seen, this provides a simple conception of chemistry embracing distillation, oxidation, and precipitation, while at the same time associating chemical substances with spiritual identity. This is in fact the major difference between alchemy and chemistry. Both work with substances, but one with spiritual/physical matter, the other with physical matter alone.
In the West each of the three primal substances was further divided into a part that was spirit-like or essential and a part that was material or “gross.” Thus, mercurius was divided into the “volatile spirit” that preserved the essence and a sort of watery waste product or phlegm (mucilage). Sulphur was divided into a “volatile oil” that was responsible for the expression of energy in a constitutional fashion, and a “fixed oil” that merely burned as the life force, from birth to death. The “fixed oils” were nutritious. Salt was divided into salts or minerals that carried more essence or intelligence into the material realm, and the mortuus caput or mere material (sand, alumino-silicates). These subdivisions became the basis of pharmacy and chemistry, and some of these terms are still with us today.
Because it was concerned with uncovering the essence (essentia), alchemical medicine in any of these cultures tended to move medicine in a different direction. Instead of emphasizing hot and cold, and treatment by opposites (hot to cold, and so forth), alchemy tended to emphasize the primal essence of the remedy and treatment of the primal essence or disturbed essence in the sick person. The remedy and the sickness were seen as having a “similarity” and thus followed the “law of similars,” or like treats like. This very often involved treatment by the law of correspondence or the doctrine of signatures (the plant looks like the organ or disease).
According to the law of correspondence all creatures, plants, and minerals are in correspondence through like essences, so that bloodroot (Sanguinaria) contains an essence related to blood, while eyebright (Euphrasia) contains an essence related to the eyes. These correspondences are revealed through “signatures”—bloodroot has an unusual red sap and eyebright has a marking on it like an eye.
Not only are remedies and diseases related through “similarity” but by “specificity” because bloodroot is a specific for the blood. They are also bound by “affinity” because eyebright has an affinity to the eyes. The term “organ affinity” was introduced by some of Paracelsus’s students, though he himself still used the Galenic term, appropriation—the remedy is appropriated by a specific part of the body.
Although alchemical themes are found in the earliest layers of traditional medicine, in all parts of the world, Paracelsus was the first to bring them together into a comprehensive system. This in turn became the origin of pharmacy, drug-making, and the modern chemical perspective in medicine, in which the molecules are seen as the primal seat of medicinal power or disease expression. At the same time, through a different interpretation, Paracelsian medicine became the origin of a practice more akin to homeopathy, where the essential nature of the remedy is related by similarity to the essential nature of the disease. For Paracelsus the relationship was primarily through the doctrine of signatures. On the other hand, for Samuel Hahnemann, the founder of homeopathy, the relationship was through drug-action: the remedy causing the symptoms for which it was remedial. These are really two very different approaches, the former magical, the latter pharmacological.
The fact that alchemical medicine and homeopathy work by the “law of similars” while Galenic, Chinese, and Ayurvedic herbalism work by the “law of contraries” is interpreted by many as evidence that herbalism and homeopathy are opposing systems of medicine. This is not correct, however, as most remedies cure the same condition by both similarity and contrariety, depending on whether one arrives at the prescription by the doctrine of signatures or by opposing energetic categories. Thus, for example, angelica acts on the blood by virtue of the red/blue/purple color of the large, hollow stalks and on the circulation by virtue of the warming and stimulating presence of volatile oils.
Differences in application can sometimes be achieved by reducing or increasing the dosage—another difference between herbalism (large dose) and homeopathy (small dose). However, in almost all cases herbs used in homeopathy are used for the same purpose as they are used in herbalism—chamomile for calming babies, elder for fevers and coughing, boneset for influenza, yarrow for bleeding, and so forth. It is only when a plant is poisonous that it truly shows opposite properties in its herbal and homeopathic application, but The Earthwise Herbal is not concerned with poisons. The difference between herbs and poisons will be discussed in the volume on the New World materia medica of traditional Western herbalism.
In my experience, the herbal, homeopathic, and flower essence application of a medicinal plant are all related, though not always identical, since there are some variations when different parts and amounts of a plant are used. The indications from each school should be viewed as lying on a continuum rather than an opposition—except where an agent is so strong that it is poisonous—that is, it is forceful in its action on the organism.
In short, the alchemical heritage particularly emphasizes the idea of an essence or essential plant personality, which has come to suffuse homeopathy and flower essence therapy, and to have some influence on herbalism through writers like Dorothy Hall and myself. Another element that descends from this tradition is that of the “specific indication” found in eclectic medicine or the “characteristic” or “keynote” symptom found in homeopathy. I would like to better define these concepts before continuing.
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS
The doctrine of “specific medicine,” or the use of a specific remedy based on characteristic, specific indications, was developed by Dr. John Scudder and was one of the guiding lights of eclectic medicine. The eclectics, with the physiomedicalists, were nineteenth-century American botanical doctors. Yet, the doctrine has roots in the ancient practices of the simplers, or empirics, who used a small number of remedies for specific indications or conditions. The alchemists, with their emphasis on the “essence” of the plant also used specific remedies. William Salmon (1710) cited a “specifickation” for almost every herb in The English Herbal.
Scudder pointed out that old practitioners often knew a certain number of remedies that they used very reliably as “specifics.” These were usually derived from experience rather than pharmacological theory or knowledge. Scudder noted that these remedies were not specific to the name of a disease, which was man-made, but to a natural pattern or characteristic expression that occurred as the disease unfolded. For example, quinine was considered a specific for malaria, but it was not suited to all cases. “Probably there is no remedy in the materia medica which gives so many failures as this,” Scudder (1870, 219) commented. “I think we may be safe in assuming that it is given ten times where its specific action is obtained once.” His specific indication is: “A soft and open pulse, a moist skin, and moist and cleaning tongue.”
A specific indication not only points to a precise remedy but to a precise pathological state, not a disease name. In the above example the pulse and tongue indicate that a free secretion of perspiration and other fluids in the body has been established prior to the use of the quinine. Clearly, there can be levels of specificity, from “malaria” at the low end to “malaria with free secretion of fluids” to Scudder’s precise pulse and tongue indications.
In this volume I have attempted to bring out the specificity, but I have included even symptoms that are “poorly developed.”
The terms “characteristic symptoms” and “keynotes” are used in homeopathy to describe symptoms that lead to a specific remedy and I have sometimes used these terms instead of “specific indications.” Homeopathic characteristic symptoms, however, do not lead to as clear a pathological picture as Scudder’s “specifics,” which are not just characteristic of the remedy, but of the entire pathology.

## Physiomedicalism

After the demise of Greek medicine, European medical doctors still had to use humoral or energetic evaluation to understand and treat pathology because they knew so little about the inner workings of the organism. It is often thought by modern critics that in fact they knew nothing, but medicine can be inventive as well as misguided, and I have often been impressed that the age of “heroic” medicine, a period of great confusion between the fall of the Greek system and the rise of modern biomedicine, produced many practical medical solutions. It was this age (not Greek or traditional medicine) that discovered willow bark and aspirin, the mechanism for which was uncovered only in the last several decades.
The fall of the Greek theory meant that doctors essentially used the same ideas but gave them “modern” names. Thus, instead of heat, they spoke of irritation or excitation or overstimulation. The “theory of irritation,” in vogue in the eighteenth century, explained that nerve tissue, and by extension, all tissue, was irritated by stimulation, became constricted, then returned to relaxation. Failure to respond to stimulation was called depression. Lack of reaction due to malnutrition and wasting was known as atrophy. Thus, five “tissue states” (excitation, tension, atrophy, relaxation, and depression) came to be informally recognized in medicine—one will still find these terms used descriptively in a physiology textbook.
Sometimes a sixth condition was added, torpor, which was used to describe the prolongation of fever and other diseases by the presence of toxins, humors, or fluids that slowed down the natural response of the organism to healing and made the healing process long, drawn-out, and clouded or difficult-to-follow, so to speak. Thus, for instance, when a fever would have been expected to bring a rash to the surface quickly, instead the rash receded or delayed or became complicated with toxic materials that made for high heat or irregular interludes. Torpor was often associated with “humors in the blood” (English), “impure blood” (German), or “bad blood” (American Indian). I have called it “stagnation.”
Dr. Joseph M. Thurston (1900), a physiomedicalist, or follower of the early American herbalist Samuel Thomson, organized the “six tissue states” in 1900. The names Thurston gave to them are so convoluted that I have ignored them. However, the “six tissue states” of Thurston correspond closely with the four qualities and the two tissue states of Greek medicine and to various “energetic” values in traditional medicine. I give them the following links:
These are the energetics used in The Earthwise Herbal. In one way or another, either in their Greek or post-Greek form, these energetic categories underlie the arrangement of the Western pharmacopoeia into medical categories. Thus, for example, an astringent is drying, or counters damp discharges and relaxation of tissue. Again, a stimulant is activating and warming, so it opposes cold and depression. Once we understand the energetics of Western herbal medicine, we can see the underlying logic of the whole enterprise.
The six tissue states correspond with the four qualities and two tissue states of the Greek physicians. The correlations with their most simple indications are:
HOT/EXCITATION
Overstimulation, overactivity of the organism, resulting in heat, redness, tenderness, swelling, a rapid or raised pulse, an elongated, carmine red, pointed tongue, mental lack of focus, lack of concentration, or restlessness. When the heat goes deep in the organism the tongue color is dark red.
COLD/DEPRESSION
Inability to respond to stimulation, hence tissue depression, lack of sensation, insensibility, cold extremities, cold, inactive skin, poor digestion, fatigue, sluggishness, tissues pale or dark, blue, purple, gray, or black, pulse slow or low, tongue pale or dark.
DRY/ATROPHY
Lack of fluids (water and oil), hence inability to receive nutrients or carry away waste products, resulting in a thin, emaciated condition with nervousness and restlessness (because the nervous system does not waste), weakness, lack of trophism (function) in organs, dry skin, dry colon, constipation, poor digestion with gas and bloating, pulse tense and weak, tongue body thin, long, and in bad cases withered.
DAMP/STAGNATION
Excess of fluids caught in the tissues, not able to excrete or eliminate, thickening and collecting, with collection of waste products as well, resulting in cold (water is cold), indigestion, lymphatic congestion, tendency to easily get sick (low immunity), or tendency of fevers and sickness to run exaggerated, longer courses than usual (to burn off the accumulating toxins), lack of expression of the tissues, lack of facial expression, thickened mucus on the mucosa, pulse “obscure” (filminess obscures the sharpness of the beat), tongue coated, body enlarged, pale.
DAMP/RELAXATION
Continuous loss of fluids from the body due to open pores and collapsed or prolapsed tissues, resulting in excessive perspiration, salivation, mucous expectoration, diarrhea, clear, copious urine, or menstrual bleeding, with exhaustion, fatigue, pale skin with blue veins showing through, sometimes with red on the cheeks, anemia from kidneys not signaling the bone marrow to produce red blood cells, pulse nonresistant, tongue moist, with streamers down the sides between the midline and the edges.
WIND/TENSION
I have introduced the word “wind” from Asian medicine as it describes this tissue state. Psychological or physical tension or both, nerves and muscles taunt, conditions that come on suddenly or leave suddenly or alternate back and forth, especially diarrhea and constipation, or fever and chills, resulting in a tense, hard, or wiry pulse.
When reading Western herbal literature written prior to 1950 there is usually an underlying supposition that the herb fits one of these categories, even if it is not stated or defined in the text.

## Integration: Pharmacology, Taste, and Energetics

Another element of empiricism or experiential medicine involves the knowledge of taste. Here I must express my indebtedness to David Winston, herbalist (AHG), for making a correlation between tastes, pharmacology, and energetics. I have given my own interpretation to his teachings, so they do not represent his approach entirely, but his research is the basis of my own.
Another practitioner who relies heavily on taste is Keith Robertson, co-director with his wife, Maureen, of the Scottish School of Herbal Medicine, in Glasgow. He teaches students medicinal plants by introducing them to the taste, soliciting their impressions based on the taste, and only then revealing their names, traditional uses, and pharmacology. Thus, the student from the beginning has a pragmatic experience of the plant.
The old empirical herbalists, by the sense of taste, often gained their understanding of the medicinal properties of plants. For instance, Samuel Thomson (1769–1843), the great popularizer of herbalism in nineteenth-century America, looked for three basic properties in the taste: heat, roughness (astringence), and bitterness. Galen analyzed taste and its relationship to the four qualities. For instance, the sharp flavor (pungent or spicy) was warming, astringence drying, sweetness moistening. Turning to modern worldwide herbalism we find that taste is the basic category used for classification in Chinese and Ayurvedic medicine.
In considering taste, we have to acknowledge a limitation in our vocabulary. Very often what the old herbalists were looking for was not just what we call the flavor, but the impression a medicine makes through the nerves or sensations of the mouth. Thus, for example, astringence is not a taste proper, but a sensation. The four actual flavors are sweet, salty, bitter, and sour. To this is usually added pungence or spiciness, though it is technically a smell, not a flavor. (Thus, in herbalism, pungence is associated with the “aromatics” or herbs containing large quantities of volatile oils.)
In this book the word “taste” will be subdivided to include the flavor, temperature/humidity, and impression. Flavor refers to sour, bitter, salty, sweet, and pungent. Temperature and humidity refers to the four qualities of Greek medicine: hot, cold, damp, dry. Impression refers to the sensation the medicine produces on the nerves and tissues. For instance, the tingle felt in the taste of echinacea is not technically a flavor but is evidence of nerve stimulation.
Some “tastes” have been included that are rather uncommon in the Vegetable Kingdom: meaty (meat, mushrooms, eggplant, nettles) and metallic (taste of blood in the mouth). Aromaticity (a word I learned from Avicenna) is listed under impression.
In The Earthwise Herbal each herb will be described as follows:
Taste: flavor • temperature/humidity • impression
Although care has been taken to define these terms, I will be forthright in stating that the entries under taste are still somewhat contingent. It is difficult to appraise tastes. I have used my own sense of taste or “reputable sources”—that is, well-known literature. For best results, the taste should be determined by using a wide pool of participants. Keith Robertson, who has done such experimentation with a wide group, pointed out places where I had the taste incorrect in my earlier writings.
Although correlations between tissue states, pharmacological substances, and the major tastes are not exact, a general breakdown runs something like this:
Bitters that act on damp/stagnation cause secretion in the gastrointestinal tract and gallbladder, thus stimulating feeding and waste removal. Acrid bitters produce the sensation of bile in the back of the throat. They can cause a shiver to go through the body. Thus, they are for tension, which, through the sympathetic branch of the autonomic nervous system, causes bilious upset and shivering. Cyanogenic bitters (the taste of bitter almond) are cooling since they shut off the Krebs cycle, stopping energy production.
Most herbs follow the simple set of relationships given on the above table. However, there are always exceptions. When plant hormones, alkaloids, and various poisons are present there is a different kind of reaction in the body that is more complicated and often moves us out of the successful use of the above classification scheme.

==============================
# CHAPTER TWO
Herbal Actions

The medicines in herbs are derived from the cosmic forces of sunlight, moonlight, and starlight; from rain and dew and the minerals of the earth’s soil layers, as well as the hereditary properties. Any herb can have its medicinal properties analyzed to a certain extent; only the cosmic and the hereditary cannot as yet be measured, which is most unfortunate, for it is in this “streaming spirit” of the herb that most of the healing powers are contained.
—JULIETTE DE BAIRACLI LEVY (1973, 188)
As far back as the Egyptian period—several thousand years ago—physicians expressed the properties of medicines as “actions.” Thus, some plants cause vomiting (emetics), purging (laxatives), urination (diuretics), sweating (diaphoretics), warmth (stimulants), pain relief (anodynes), more appetite and better digestion (carminatives), and so on. Changes in medicine caused the retirement or addition of different actions. However, the tables of herbal actions are still found today in nearly every herbal.
I always had a great deal of difficulty with these actions because they seem to break apart and scatter the properties of an herb, not revealing any sense or concept of the primal nature and wholeness of the plant. It was for this reason that I turned to the study of homeopathy—in order to study herbs. Hahnemann, the founder of homeopathy, experienced the same problem. In a famous passage in his translation of William Cullen’s Materia Medica, he rebuked the author for trying to explain that quinine palliated or cured malaria because it was a bitter (increased and regulated secretions), antiperiodic (controlled alternating chills and fever), carminative (settled the stomach), rubefacient (increased circulation to the skin and periphery), and so forth. Hahnemann felt that Cullen was treating the actions as if they were “real things,” rather than mere names.
Because the lists of herbal actions seem to show no theory or concept of underlying “energy patterns” and “essences,” they have frustrated many in the modern revival of natural medicine. It is often said that Western herbalism “lacks a system of energetics.” However, as I showed in The Practice of Traditional Western Herbalism, in fact there are energetics embedded within the lists of herbal actions. The problem is that the key to these energetics has been lost.
When we run through the tables of actions, both in the Greek period and today, we find that they fall into three broad categories:
Many of the properties in the third category are associated with the four qualities. For instance, emollient remedies are usually moistening, hardening remedies astringent or drying, and so forth.
As we will see in the next chapter, defining a disease or herb by energetic, organ affinity, and property provides an exact foundation for a good, holistic treatment plan with herbs.

## Modern Actions

The following is a list of terminology developed down through the centuries, from ancient Egypt to the present, to represent the properties or actions of medicinal agents. The list has been modified over the years, depending on how people visualize medical action. This list represents a general average from many sources, ancient to modern.
Abortifacient. Capable of inducing a miscarriage. The stronger of the emmenagogues.
Adaptogen. This is a new term for plants that help the body adapt to stress, usually by acting on the hypothalamus to improve endocrine regulation.
Alterative. “Blood purifier.” Removes toxins and humors (congealed mucus or mucopolysaccharides) from the lymph and blood by improving “alteration,” the Greek word for “metabolism,” and elimination. These plants generally act on the liver, thyroid, endocrine system, skin, kidneys, colon, and lungs. The major symptoms of “impure blood” (German), “humors in the blood” (English), or “bad blood” (American Indian) are skin lesions. Broda Barnes (1976) considered “bad blood” equivalent to low thyroid, but this is not always the case.
Anodyne. Pain-reliever.
Antiscorbutic. Relieves scurvy; contains vitamin C.
Antiseptic. Curtails septic, putrid conditions where pus is being generated by bacteria. Equivalent to warm in the third degree in Greek medicine.
Antispasmodic. Prevents or lessens spasms and cramps.
Aperient. Causes a gentle bowel movement. Aperients often work simply by increasing secretion in the digestive tract, or relaxing the autonomic nervous system, rather than by increasing peristalsis (which would be laxative or cathartic).
Aphrodisiac. Stimulates the sex function in both sexes.
Aromatic. Plants high in volatile oils are aromatic. They are usually pungent or spicy to the taste and act as stimulants, with a few exceptions, on depressed function. Known as “warming” agents in Greek medicine.
Astringent. Plants that contain tannins cause a contraction of tissue and close pores so that fluids cannot leak out of the body. Known as “drying” agents in Greek medicine.
Bitters, bitter tonics. Plants that have a bitter taste contain a wide variety of bitter substances, most of which stimulate the flow of saliva, bile, and gastric juices. Act on the GI tract, gallbladder, and back into the liver. They can be either warming or cooling.
Carminative. These plants are usually bitter and pungent, so that they improve secretion from the stomach, expel gas, and promote the digestive function.
Cathartics. Agents containing large amounts of anthraquinones that stimulate drastic purgation. Stronger than laxatives.
Cordial. This old term refers to a plant that acts on melancholy or depression.
Demulcent. Plants containing mucopolysaccharides (mucilage), which have a coating, soothing effect similar to natural mucus. They prevent the mucosa and skin surface membranes from becoming too dry and abraded.
Diaphoretic. An agent that produces perspiration, either of water or of oil (sebum). Most diaphoretics are cooling because they relieve fever, but some are stimulating because they promote the internal fire to drive substances to the surface.
Diuretic. Agents that increase the flow of urine, either the water or the solids or both. When irritation is present diuretics are frequently combined with demulcents to soothe the tissue.
Emmenagogue. Agents that bring on the monthly menstrual period, “term,” or “courses” in the old literature. Contraindicated during pregnancy, though many are not strong abortifacients.
Emollient. Agents that break up hard swellings. They usually contain sodium, which increases the ability of water to cross boundaries and get into dehydrated tissue. Culpeper (1653, 386) makes an interesting comment: “What is hard, and what is soft, most men know, but few are able to express. Physicians define that to be hard which yields not to touching, and soft to be the contrary. An emollient or softening medicine is one which reduceth a hard substance to its proper temperature.”
Escharotic. Burns away tissue to remove growths and cancers.
Essential oils. These are the volatile oils that easily vaporate to produce an aroma. They are usually associated with stimulating and pungent remedies—warming agents in Greek medicine.
Expectorant. Loosens and helps to expel mucus from the respiratory tract (sinuses, throat, lungs).
Febrifuge. A remedy that reduces fever.
Fixed oils. Plants contain volatile oils (see ) that evaporate in heat, and fixed oils that are more stable. The latter are used to soothe and coat (like a mucilage) and to excite the gallbladder and as a nutrient substance.
Galactagogue. Increases lactation.
Homeopathic. A remedy that causes what it cures.
Laxative. Plants containing small amounts of anthraquinones that stimulate peristalsis in the gut to promote bowel movements.
Mucilage. Mucopolysaccharides that coat and soothe mucosa and skin. Demulcents contain mucilage and are sometimes called mucilages.
Mucolytic. Removes mucus.
Nervine. Plants that act on the nervous system, usually to sedate, calm, or relax when excitement, irritation, or tension is present.
Normalizer. An agent that acts on two opposite states (such as hot and cold) to induce a normal balance between them. Most herbs are normalizers. By comparison, drugs suppress imbalances.
Relaxant. Reduces tension, acts on cramps and spasms. Originally called “loosening.” “I do not mean purging,” writes Culpeper (1653, 386). “The chief use of loosening medicines is in convulsions and cramps.”
Sedative. Calms the nerves, reduces irritation and excitement.
Spasmolytic. Removes cramps and spasms.
Specific. A remedy with a powerful, specific action in a local area of the body; the opposite of a tonic.
Stimulant. Increases functional activity of the organism. Equivalent to warming remedies in Greek medicine.
Stomachic. Substances that improve appetite and tone the stomach.
Sweet tonic. A new category adopted from Chinese herbalism, sweet tonics are building and nutritive. They usually possess carbohydrates or proteins that are nourishing to the body. An example of a Western herb that fits this category is slippery elm bark. Eastern examples include rehmannia root, codonopsis, American ginseng, red ginseng, and reishi mushroom.
Tannins. The active ingredient in astringence.
Tonic. A remedy with a slow, generalized influence on the body, rather than a precise, localized, powerful influence. Bitter tonics increase secretion, sweet tonics increase flesh, astringent tonics increase tissue strength or tone, and so forth.

==============================
# CHAPTER THREE
Putting It All Together:
The Art of Herbal Practice

I shall desire thee, whoever thou art, that intendest the noble (though too much abused) study of physic, to mind heedfully these following rules; which being well understood, shew thee the Key of Galen and Hippocrates their method of physic.
—NICHOLAS CULPEPER (1653, 376)
It is generally admitted that medicine is a mixture of science and art. That is why it is called a “practice.” This holds true for herbal medicine. In fact, in the present state of knowledge, herbalism must remain more of an art than biomedicine because it has not been as thoroughly researched, nor as widely practiced.
One of the greatest difficulties encountered by the beginning herbalist is learning “how to practice.” In my estimation it is easier to learn to read a horoscope than plan out a good herbal program for a client. There are a great many different variables that are very little defined, if at all, in contemporary herbal education. One of the best descriptions of herbal education I ever heard was given by a holistic medical doctor in St. Paul, Chris Foley. He said, “Learning herbalism is like learning to play baseball by learning to run from first base to second, then how to catch a ball in left field, then how to throw a strike.” That is, indeed, how most of us in my generation learned herbalism.
This chapter is dedicated to changing all that. We are going to learn the logic of how to use herbal medicines and we’re going to find out that this method goes back to the most ancient sources of our tradition. It was known to Galen, who built the edifice of Greek medicine. It was also known to the founder of English language herbalism, Nicholas Culpeper (1616–54).
Herbalism has long been associated with eccentrics and self-taught individuals fighting against the grain of society. That would be Nicholas Culpeper in a nutshell. Even his surname meant “rubbing someone the wrong way,” or literally, “rubbing pepper” in their face. The son of a conservative Anglican clergyman, he studied astrology, ran off with a wealthy heiress (who was struck by lightning and killed), dropped out of college, apprenticed himself to an apothecary, practiced illegally with an apothecary, and put his education to work making medical knowledge available in plain English. He championed herbal medicine as a relatively safe, effective method of medicine suitable for self-treatment. Taking advantage of a breakdown in censorship and law enforcement during the English Civil War and Interregnum, Culpeper published many popular books on medicine that rubbed pepper in the face of the medical establishment. He gave the general public a simple, readable plan for self-treatment called The Complete Herbal. It is the only book other than the Bible that has never been out of print since 1653. (Let’s keep it that way!)
Culpeper is best known for his astrological approach to herbal medicine. He uses the planets and signs as his energetic system. He was a practicing astrologer and he saw the logic and worth of putting these two systems together. Many had done this before him, but few would do it afterward. Unfortunately, most people (both in his time and today) do not practice astrology and this has made his system difficult to follow. I personally wish he had organized The Complete Herbal around the old Greek system. It is easier to understand—especially the way he explains it. This explanation is, I believe, one of the most simple, practical lessons on how to practice herbalism ever written. It is certainly easier to understand than his astrological system.

## “A Key to Galen’s Method of Physick”

Galen had written dozens of compendious tomes on medicine. Out of these he distilled his essential directions down to a large encyclopedia called De methodo medendi. Then he wrote a shortened version, Ad Glauconem de medendi methodo libri II. This was still too long so he wrote a final abridgement called the Ars medica (Art of Medicine).
The first of these three treatises was known in medieval times as the Megatechne or Ars magna (The Greater Work). It runs to a thousand pages in a modern German translation. The short abridgement, Ars medica, was known as the Microtechne, Tegne, or Ars parva (Lesser Work). It is just over a hundred pages in modern translation. It was popular, not only because it was easier to read, but because there wasn’t a lot of paper to spare in the Middle Ages. (Ever wonder why all those ancient books disappeared?) The Ars medica was Galen’s most popular book; it has come down to us in numerous manuscript copies made in Greek, Latin, Arabic, and Hebrew. It was “one of the most widely used medical books of the whole past” (Sarton, 1956, 67). The Ars medica was translated by Culpeper and issued in 1653 under the title Art of Physick. It is still the only English translation of this important work.
Culpeper felt that Galen’s system could be abbreviated, so he wrote “A Key to Galen’s Method of Physick.” This was included in some editions of his pharmacopoeia and herbal. It takes up a mere twenty pages, and yet it proves to be one of the most practical, complete, and succinct set of directions for the practice of herbal medicine possible to imagine. The “Key” is reproduced in the Meyerbooks publication of Culpeper’s Complete Herbal, & English Physician, Enlarged (1990), and elsewhere.
The “Key” says that there are three basic aspects of disease we need to know in order to differentiate it sufficiently from other diseases so that the appropriate remedy or formula can be found. These are:
Quality (Energetic)
Appropriation (Location in the Body)
Property (Action)
We have already covered all three of these subjects in the preceding chapters, so now we can put them together. Let’s take an example. Imagine a person has a stomach problem. With some questioning and perhaps medical diagnosis we reach the conclusion that it is definitely situated in the stomach—that it is not a heart attack or an intestinal problem. Thus, we have the appropriation or location for the disease. Next we want to find out what is the type or kind of disease afflicting the stomach in energetic terms. In other words, is it a hot, cold, dry, damp, tense, or relaxed stomach? For the sake of completeness, I will go over each of these conditions. A hot stomach will show in an overstimulated upper GI tract, with excess salivation and appetite, excessive acid, gastric reflux, and an elongated, red, pointed tongue. The remedies are those that cool the stomach—peach and yellow dock would be my favorites. A cold stomach would be depressed, so it would need stimulation. There would be a lack of appetite, saliva, and digestive juices, so the person neither enjoys nor handles food very well. It sits in the stomach, rots, and generates acids and esophageal reflux of a different kind. This is the condition for which European medicine prescribes the bitter and pungent carminatives (orange peel, angelica, calamus, caraway, dill, fennel, anise, centaury, and others). The damp, relaxed stomach is what we see in the baby—easy vomiting of the latest meal, complete with a batch of mucus. This needs astringents, to dry out the stomach—raspberry leaf, blackberry leaf, wild geranium. The torpid stomach lacks secretion of digestive juices and mucus. Nerve reflexes are torpid—there may be nausea, but there is no vomiting. The mucus adheres to the walls of the lining so there is less secretion and a need for cleansing bitters to wash off the walls and stimulate the bowels—goldenseal, yellow dock root, barberry root, Oregon grape root. The dry stomach is like the cold or torpid stomach in that there is a lack of secretion, gas builds up as a result, but here warming agents will not serve to turn the condition, since it is due to lack of moisture (secretions) and a weakness of the stomach muscles and nerves. Consider a moistener like marshmallow root, a moistening sweet tonic like anise seed, or bitters to stimulate gastric juices like goldenseal and Oregon grape root. The tense stomach traps gas, like the dry one, but it needs relaxants and antispasmodics—chamomile, elder, fennel, dill, catnip, and the demon herb, lobelia.
Let us consider a more complex organ system, the cardiovascular tract. In aging the heart is affected through changes in the vessels. If the tongue is elongated, red, and shaped like a flame it is likely that the capillaries and vasculature are hot and prone to irritation, so that lots of LDL cholesterol will be laid down to repair the surface, followed by HDLs. If there is an imbalance in the heat of the capillaries or in the cholesterols there will be high blood pressure, arterial disease, and tendencies to heart attack and stroke. This is especially the area where hawthorn, or sometimes wild cherry bark, is required. (Sometimes the vasculature can be cooled through another system, say for instance through the GI tract by using yellow dock root or rhubarb root, which are not cardiac remedies at all. Galen called this a “consensual” relationship between organ systems, when one is being attacked by conditions in another, or where one is trying to protect the other. This type of holistic thinking is ignored in modern biomedicine.) If, on the other hand, the extremities are cold, the pulse low and slow, the tongue pale or dark, cold and depression may be affecting the vascular system. If there is blue coloration the blood is congealing. Heart attacks and strokes will occur through a different etiology. This condition needs stimulants such as cayenne, collinsonia, aesculus, angelica, madder, yarrow, and others. The heart can also be affected by relaxation in the vasculature that especially affected the veins. This used to be called “passive venous congestion,” and was indicated by blood stagnation in the veins, hemorrhoids, varicosities, and other symptoms. Most of the remedies in the last category are beneficial here as well; also sumach, which is excellent for high blood pressure from this condition. Passive congestion can cause vascular tension through back pressure against the heart. Constriction or tension, psychological or physical, will also cause vascular problems and high blood pressure. Here think of the relaxants, but especially viscum, melissa, and the little-known liriodendron. High blood pressure is often caused by sympathetic excess, so remedies that relax the sympathetic and reinforce the parasympathetic are beneficial, even if they are not “heart remedies.” High blood pressure that is caused by salt imbalance in the kidneys, or fluids of the body, should be treated by use of a naturally occurring, broad spectrum table salt—the red or gray salts, or flowers of salt. Also, marshmallow root and celery seed, the emollient or salty diuretics. Diuretics may be necessary—rosemary and dandelion leaf are famous for congestive heart failure, but the preceding two should also be considered.
And finally, let us examine a “system,” the condition of which affects the entire body, and the treatment for which can have repercussions throughout the entire system—the skin and mucosa—that is, the epithelial cells. The condition of the surface reflects everything that is going on in the interior. If the skin or mucosa is hot, red, swollen, and tender, there is heat and excitation with need for the cooling remedies—rose hips, peach leaf, hawthorn, wild cherry, elder, lemon, yarrow, and so on. If the skin is hot and dry consider elder. If it is hot and sticky in some places, from incomplete sweat, consider yarrow. If the mucosa is hot and dry consider peach leaf or marshmallow root. If the skin is dry the sweat pores are not open: marshmallow root, pleurisy root, elder, sage, and red clover. But the problem could also be from lack of the oily component of the sweat: burdock, angelica, sunflower seed, hawthorn. If the mucosa is dry: peach leaf and marshmallow. The former is for heat causing drying, the latter for drying causing heat—at least, that is what I think. If the skin is moist and cool with a watery sweat, the pores are stuck open: sumach (especially if there is a lot of urine) and monarda (especially if there is internal heat). The loss of cooling fluids is draining the system. If the skin is oily and sticky: burdock root. If there are night sweats, shivering, or periodic sweating there is an element of wind or tension: blue vervain, boneset, dogwood—the acrid bitter diaphoretics. Sage is beneficial for aging of the skin—dry or periodic sweating. If there is evidence of congealed blood or passive venous congestion, move the blood with arnica, yarrow, safflower, cayenne, angelica, collinsonia, aesculus, madder. If the skin is pale with evident blue veins there is need for treatment of passive venous congestion: lady’s mantle, feverfew. Pale and blue swelling: elder. If a person is sensitive to both heat and chill: an adaptogen—eleuthero, American spikenard. Turbidity or toxins in the blood show up on the skin as eczema, psoriasis, acne, and similar conditions. They show up on the mucosa and tongue as a heavy yellow or white coating. They indicate the use of blood cleansers and bitters. If there is abscess and pus on the surface we need the antiseptics (baptisia, echinacea, calendula, thuja) and lymphatic cleansers (calendula, thuja, ceanothus).
If we can correct the condition of the skin or mucosa, many other internal changes for the better will occur. The “condition of the surface” represents the “dragon tracks” left by earlier diseases as they plunged into the depths of the body.
We have not referred to the third category used by Galen and Culpeper to get to the right prescription. Culpeper calls this the “property,” though the term “action” would more likely be used today. This category includes many different concepts, energetic, organ-specific, ancient, and modern. Use the “action” to further refine the picture given by the energetic and location of the disturbance (see -).

==============================
# CHAPTER FOUR
Preparation and Dosage

Coming down to the first part of the nineteenth century, we find that purveyors of medicinal and savory herbs then wandered over the whole of England in quest of such useful Simples as were in constant demand at most houses for the medicine-chest, the store chest, or the toilet-table. These rustic practitioners of the healing art were known as “green men,” who carried with them their portable apparatus for distilling essences, and for preparing their herbal extracts. In token of such functionaries having formerly officiated in this capacity, there may yet be seen in London, and elsewhere about the country, taverns bearing the curious sign of “The Green Man and (his) Still.”
—DR. WILLIAM T. FERNIE (1914, 8)
Most herbals and pharmacopoeias have directions for preparation. We find them in the opening pages of Dioscorides’ Materia Medica. I have not attempted a detailed discussion of preparation, but have included opinions and options I think are interesting. Generally, the following directions should be noted.

## Preparation

Major methods of preparation are given by many different authors. Here are some of the general directions.

### TEA

When making teas prepare only enough for one day. The material begins to break down as soon as it is wet. Teas are usually made from dried herbs but can be made from the fresh.
INFUSION
This is when the plant material is steeped in hot water, instead of being boiled. This is used for dried plant parts that are easily oxidized: flowers, leaves, powders. A usual method is to boil 32 parts of water and pour it over 1 part of dried herb and let stand for 10–60 minutes. Keep covered to prevent loss of volatile oils. Usual dose: 1–3 tablespoons, 3x/day. Do not keep for more than three days. Add honey, if desired.
COLD INFUSION
This is used when the plant material is broken down by heat. Let stand in room temperature water, for at least 6 hours, preferably overnight, 1 part plant material in 32 parts water. “It is a very efficient method and, as it uses no heat, the least altering to plant constituents of any tea process” (Michael Moore, 2003, 24).
DECOCTION
This is used when the plant material is thicker and harder so cooking is needed to break the material apart. Boil 1 part herb in 32 parts of water, with the lid on, for 1/2 to 1 hour, cool, and strain. Add honey, if desired.

### PRESERVED PREPARATIONS

Fresh or dried plant material can be extracted in water and alcohol, and preserved in alcohol or vegetable glycerine. Different alcohols have different properties. A fellow student of mine at the Scottish School of Herbal Medicine, Rosari Kingston, studied the media used to extract and tincture herbs and found that there were no scientific tests establishing the differences between modern and traditional menstrua (ethanol and ether versus wine, brandy, whiskey, vodka, and so on), or the concentrations to be used in most cases. There are, however, extensive studies by alcohol producers of the physiological effects and side effects of different kinds of alcohol, and they do differ from one to another. (This information may not be available to the public.)
Ethanol in the United States is made from corn; in Europe it is made from whey.
FRESH PLANT TINCTURE
This is an ideal way to preserve fresh plant material, although it is not the usual commercial method, which uses dried material, if not otherwise specified. A standard tincture is made from the fresh plant (1 part by weight) in ethanol, grain alcohol, or 95% alcohol (2 parts), macerated 7–9 days (Michael Moore).
High alcohol content is believed to extract more active ingredients, in most cases, but it also breaks down the constituents. A lower alcohol content (40–50%) does not extract as much, but preserves the molecules, hence the far more recognizable flavor and smell of such products. This is how I usually make tinctures, although there are exceptions. It is almost impossible to compare the beautiful taste of chamomile, cleavers, rosemary, or hawthorn prepared in this fashion to any other method. On the other hand, some fresh plants are too watery for the low alcohol method—here I think particularly of calendula, which should be dried.
William LeSassier called the fresh, low alcohol approach “energetic tinctures,” a good name. (By the way, he did not like to use them, though he did not criticize me for using them.) I like this method because it preserves many subtle organic molecules that would be broken down by the scissors-like action of the alcohol. A friend, who made tinctures from the fresh plant in 40% alcohol (brandy, for best taste in most cases), had her products tested by the ATF. The markers were actually better than tinctures made from dry plants in higher alcohol extractions, much to the surprise of the ATF.
DRY PLANT TINCTURE
This is the usual commercial method, since it is hard for most herb companies, unless they have their own gardens, to have fresh herbs on hand. The plant material is weighed out exactly and compared to the alcohol and water used to extract and preserve it (like 5 parts to 1 part; 1:5). The best product is improved by percolation (see Michael Moore, 2003).

### POULTICES, SALVES, AND OILS

Now let us look at several methods of external application. A poultice from the fresh leaves is made by chopping them up and putting them directly on the part. Leaves can be beat up between stones for this purpose. A compress is made by warming up an infusion or decoction, moistening a clean cloth in it, and placing on the body. A hot poultice is made the same way except that the plant parts are wrapped in the cloth and put on externally.
Herbs for external use are extracted in oil overnight, say in a double cooker at a low temperature or on a warm radiator. Tend the mixture at first to make sure the temperature doesn’t boil over the product or make a mess. Olive oil and sesame oil are good, because they are medicinal in their own right and preserve well, but different oils have different properties. For instance, the most penetrating of all is skunk oil (that is, the lard not the scent), which can be applied to one side of the hand and come out the other. The most penetrating of the vegetable oils is grapeseed. Other oils keep the material in them on the surface—olive oil is more in this category. Sesame oil is nutritious, sunflower seed oil is detoxifying, safflower oil is blood-moving and thinning.
Some plant constituents extract better in oil and some worse, so that sometimes the extraction or tincture in alcohol is placed in the oil and boiled off—this is the case with St. John’s wort. Once the oil is impregnated with the properties of the plant add wheat germ oil as a preservative (1 part to 7 parts) and wax to harden it up. Essential oils may be added to improve scent or add medicinal properties.
Essential oils are different from oil extractions because they represent the pure essential or volatile oils taken from the plants and not adulterated. This is the material used in aromatherapy.

## Dosage

No scientific standards for posology or dosage exist. All indications are based on theory, tradition, opinion, or individual experience. These include the oft-quoted dosages given in the British Herbal Pharmacopoeia. One of my fellow students at the Scottish School of Herbal Medicine, Mary Tassel, made inquiries into the origin of these guidelines and found that there were no records to show how the BHP dosages originated.
To repeat: there is no scientific basis for posology or dose. A more accurate approach would stress what herbalists do, rather than why they think their approach is more scientific or more effective. Thus, I have often recorded under preparation and dosage the practices of actual green men and green women. However, a few generalizations can be made.
Generally, the herbal field divides into two segments, the high dosers and the low dosers. The former tend to use preparations made from dried plants in a range of 20–60-drop doses, less if the plant is considered toxic. The low dosers tend to use preparations made from fresh plants in a range of 1–10 drops. These are sometimes called “Wood doses” after yours truly, though they were also used by the eclectics in the nineteenth century and others. Some people use both.
The idea behind high dosage practice in herbalism is that the physical constituents of the plant act on the body in a mechanical fashion and a large amount is necessary to make an impact. Low dosage practice tends to be based on the concept that the preparation has a more “energetic” and less physical reaction on the body. The plant is seen as having an intelligence or virtue that acts on the vital force or self-regulating system of the body to effect change.
Interestingly, the same division is found among homeopaths, except that here the high dosers are those who use the extreme dilutions (say, 1 part diluted a thousand times on a 1/100 scale) whereas the low dosers use drop doses or slight dilutions (say, 1 part diluted 6–30x on a 1/10 scale).
From the standpoint of material science, the use of the small doses does not seem reasonable. From the standpoint of energetic medicine, forcing the body to react, by using large doses, is not holistic or curative.
A further question arises as to whether the body reacts oppositely to large and small doses. In homeopathy the diluted medicine sometimes cures symptoms the opposite of the diluted drug. For instance, atropine (from Atropa belladonna) is used to relax sphincters and muscles in conventional medicine while Belladonna (from Atropa belladonna) is used in homeopathic dilution for conditions where the sphincters and muscles are overly relaxed.
The problem with extending homeopathic thinking to herbalism is that most of the medicines that display opposite properties are poisons.
