Most of the herbal remedies, on the other hand, are made from common, nontoxic herbs—Eupatorium perfoliatum, Hypericum perforatum, Sambucus nigra, Chamomilla matricaria, and so on. These actually have the same effects in both herbal and homeopathic preparation, as a comparison of an herbal with a homeopathic materia medica would show.
There are instances where herbs can be better directed by the use of large or small doses. For instance, there is no reason to give large doses of yellow dock to someone with diarrhea. The plant contains both tannins and anthraquinone laxatives, so it acts both on diarrhea and constipation, but large doses would aggravate the diarrhea unnecessarily.
Where it has been possible, the posology used by the eclectic medical movement has been given in this herbal, since the eclectics used both high and low dosages. A good source here is John William Fyfe’s Specific Diagnosis and Specific Medication (1909), which I have quoted whenever available. Fyfe, for example, gives the dosage of 1–60 drops for Vitex. I have sometimes given my own posology where I have extensive experience with an herb.
According to Graeme Tobyn (1997), Nicholas Culpeper, the progenitor of English language herbalism, taught an interesting posological principle that is very much worth reprinting. Culpeper was of the opinion that less of the plant was needed when it was being used to treat internal, central functions, but more was needed when treating those that lie in the periphery, farther from the governing heat and intelligence in the core of the body. (This may have a basis in Greek medicine.)
This doctrine agrees generally to my approach. It takes very little amount of herb or “plant intelligence” to influence the core intelligence of the organism, but much to influence functions and parts that are more material. I have long used medium to large doses when there were deposits of material in the body that had to be moved, because these are not “energetic” but material. Even more so, when foreign processes like cancer at are work there is an alien intelligence actively opposing the innate intelligence of the body. In these cases, large doses (20–35 drops) are required.
However, in my opinion, large doses should always be fractionated, or taken over a period of time (60 drops over 1–10 minutes) because organic tissues probably become “cloyed”—too full of a material to absorb more. This is my one and only concern with large doses. I do not care whether people use them or not, as long as the dose is somewhat fractionated to prevent cloying.
In conclusion, I use small doses (1–10 drops) in “energetic” disturbances involving the core intelligence of the body, infinitesimal (homeopathic) doses when poisonous plants are involved, and “material” doses (10–35 drops) when there is material to be moved or cancer is involved. Sometimes I simply follow the directions of the person or source that taught me, because they are my “elder” or mentor, and I treat them with respect.
PART II
Materia Medica
I open all the drawers and cases of my apotheca, and let every one look into them, even to the last little tea-box and the smallest oil bottle.
—SEBASTIAN KNEIPP (1898, 148)

==============================
# Using the Materia Medica

One of the most perplexing features of traditional—and regular—therapy is why so many seemingly diverse uses have been recorded for medicinal plants.
—JOHN CRELLIN AND JANE PHILPOTT (1990, 11)
The Earthwise Herbal has been written to bring out the underlying unity behind the actions of a plant so that its application is not, as the above authors comment, “perplexing.” It aspires to be “complete,” as the subtitle brags, in terms of the depth in which each plant is examined. It also aspires to relative completion in terms of the scope of traditional literature searched in its production. The selection of plants covers nearly all nontoxic medicinal herbs used in traditional Western herbalism. Space limits this volume to Old World plants only—there will be another volume on materia medica of the New World. The Earthwise Herbal is less comprehensive in its coverage of plants introduced within the last twenty years. The emphasis has been placed upon traditional materials of medicine. As a formulary, or workbook for the clinic, it is also less complete. No coverage of compounding has been undertaken, and the dosages given are suggestive.
The herbal is laid out in the following manner.

### LATIN BINOMIAL (GENUS AND SPECIES).
COMMON NAMES.

I have sometimes used older Latin binomials when I felt that the original names are important. Botanists rename plants for their own convenience, not for herbalists, horticulturists, or pharmacists. I do not feel we need to follow botanists slavishly, but I also have tried to indicate the modern name, if it differs from the Latin binomial I have used.

### NATURAL HISTORY, PLANT FAMILY, HISTORICAL
AND GENERAL BACKGROUND, PHARMACOLOGY

Rather than crowding up the description of plant properties and uses with excess information, I have included a separate section, in italics, to provide the milieu of the plant.

### THE ESSENCE, NATURE, LOGIC, PROPERTIES, AND USES OF THE PLANT

The main body of the text consists of a description of the plant uniting its diverse properties and uses into a whole profile that makes sense, empirically, intuitively, and pharmacologically. The greatest effort has been made to bring out the essential nature, personality, or logic of the plant. When this is understood the disparate traditional uses, modern uses, and physical properties usually form a unit that makes sense. Some case histories and anecdotes have been included to illustrate application and give the text some interest.
The reader has a right to know how many plants the author has actually used in practice, and how many he dares to write about without the basis of personal experience. I have not indicated this anywhere in the text, but most of the long accounts, commonly used herbs, and certainly all those in which I cite myself as a source have been used in my practice. There is another group of plants that I feel I know well because friends have filled me in on their experiences, and I trust and understand their methods. In a work of this magnitude there must be some herbs I have used but little or not at all. However, after more than twenty-five years of clinical experience and continuous study, always searching for the core essence of plants, my understanding of them has developed to such a degree that I write confidently even about plants I have yet to meet. I also have developed an eye for the literature, often quoting sources who clearly did understand their subject, while avoiding others who wrote without experience.
Young herbalists should know that the day will come when they will be able to look through the materia medica and it will simply make sense, because signatures, tastes, and constituents do correctly indicate uses, and the pieces integrate into whole pictures with definite properties.

### TASTE

The following template has been used: flavor • temperature and humidity • impression. As used here, the word taste includes the flavor, temperature (hot, cold), humidity (damp, dry), and impression made on the nervous system (diffusive, etc.). These properties often give us the best indications for the “energetics” or tissue state favored by the medicine.
I have done the best I could with plant tastes, working from experience. Out of habit and love of herbs, I taste virtually every formula or simple I give out and every plant I stop to seriously look at in the field or garden. On occasion I have quoted from some other author—this is always indicated. Yet, the sense of taste is subjective and I do not hold myself out to be especially keen in this area. I have been corrected in the past, and if the reader knows the taste more perfectly than the author, please correct me.

### TISSUE STATES

This follows the template: excitation (irritation, overstimulation), tension (constriction), atrophy, relaxation, stagnation, depression.
Each herb acts on a particular tissue state or combination of states.

### ORGAN AFFINITIES

Originally, this was a separate category, but I have worked organ affinities into the arrangement of specific indications. Thus, there are now headings lumping symptoms under location, organ, system, and sometimes constitution. Strong organ affinities are italicized.

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

These are the collection of symptoms and indications that come down to us in the literature or from the personal experience of practicing herbalists. Symptoms that are “poorly developed” (like “diarrhea”) are listed, but an effort has been made to collect and develop more detailed symptoms or “specific indications.” For instance, sometimes we just have “gastritis.” This is not a specific indication, but it is the best we have. With more experience we have more definition: “gastritis; gurgling, nausea, mucus in vomitus,” or “gastritis; with liver involvement,” and so forth. A fully developed specific indication is so detailed that it leads to the selection of a small number of remedies or even just a single medicine. At this level the specific indication not only contains detail, but it tends to indicate the specific affinities in tissue state and organ that characterize the plant. For instance, “gastritis; gurgling, nausea, mucus in vomitus” indicates an astringent.
Of least value are the names of illnesses: “diabetes.” What kind of diabetes? Yet, we must include even these indications, nonspecific as they are, because the account would be incomplete without them.
When an indication is italicized it may be taken to be particularly characteristic and reliable, or often proven by experience.
David Winston, registered herbalist (AHG), makes it a point not to include new indications unless they have been tried out in practice more than twenty times. I set more liberal standards. If a newly discovered usage makes sense, in keeping with the general logic of the plant, and has been verified a half dozen times, it will be included. A few suggestive indications that have not been proved out in practice are included from time to time because they ought to be investigated more fully. The only symptoms taken over from traditional literature that I have thrown out are those where a mistake in the transmission of information is apparent and the symptoms clearly do not belong to the remedy.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE

It was not my intention to write a formulary, or workbook giving specific preparation methods, formulations, and standard dosages. I have instead merely handed on a collection of notes on preparation from my own or others’ experience that should prove useful and may, in the future, provide material for a formulary. For a good contemporary formulary refer to Richo Cech, Making Plant Medicine (2000). I do not feel that it is a good idea to copy the United States Pharmacopeia or National Formulary slavishly. Herbs are like condiments; they can be prepared in subtle, beautiful, and almost countless ways.
The standard of dosages given in this book is highly variable. My favorite source of posology (dosage) is John William Fyfe (1909). This is because he gives advice on dosage like: “1 to 60 drops.” I personally use small doses, but I do not advocate small or large dosages for other practitioners. I simply use what works for me. Some herbalists dismiss my work as homeopathic rather than herbal, because they mistakenly think the effect changes with small or large doses. This is only true for poisons, which are harmful in large doses and toxic in small ones. The Earthwise Herbal is limited to nontoxic herbs. People who are doctrinaire about posology miss the main point: the healing virtue is in the plant, not the preparation or amount. What works best is the dosage level a practitioner feels comfortable with, rather than a doctrinal position.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS, CAUTIONS, TOXICITY

This section has been included when necessary.

### LITERATURE

Throughout The Earthwise Herbal I have attempted to give credit for original work, quotations, and case histories. In the “monograph,” or main body of the text under each herb, credit is indicated by the usual methods: quotation marks, sources, dates, and pages. In the section on “Specific Indications” the sources for symptoms are given at the end of the account of the herb, under “Literature.” The numbers following the authors represent the symptoms they contributed to, counting down from the top under “Specific Indications.” Dates and page numbers here have been ignored—one will have to turn to the reference section to find the original book or lecture, and then to the index of that book to find the plant and the symptom. Some undated references refer to conversations or class notes that I saved on bits of paper or by memory and could not include in the References section.
Confirmation of an important or unusual symptom is often noted in the text with the symptom number followed by “-confirmed.”

## Acacia senegal. Acacia, Gum Arabic.

This small tree in the legume family is native to North Africa and the Middle East. A gum is exuded from the tree that has been used since antiquity both as a curative remedy and a carrier for other agents. It is collected by Bedouin tribes who use it as a food, medicine, and trade item. In addition to the gum, there are small amounts of sugar and mineral ash including calcium, magnesium, and potassium. It is so nutritious that during the harvest the Bedouin can live off the gum alone.
Acacia is a good example of a fairly nonspecific herb that is used in a broad fashion as a mucilage and emollient for a wide variety of conditions in which other such agents could be substituted fairly indiscriminately. It is, therefore, not the kind of medicinal plant well suited to the present book, where the emphasis is on specific uses. However, it still serves as a good teaching example because a careful reading of traditional herbal literature does bring out some specific uses, demonstrating that even a plant with general properties can be used in a specific fashion. Notice the allopath John Eberle’s comment about it being “peculiarly beneficial” in the bowel complaints of children. That is a good example of a “specific indication.”
The mucilaginous and emollient constituents of acacia are released by boiling, straining, and made into a soothing application for irritations to the skin and mucous membranes. Gum acacia is commonly used as a syrup to coat and soothe the windpipe and digestive tract. Because it is adhesive and viscid, it helps to keep poultices on the surface, while retaining moisture and warmth. It is also applied as a binder and coating for pills and tablets and to suspend water insoluble substances in a liquid form. It has even been used in paints and glues as a polishing and adhesive substance.
Gum acacia is used externally on burns, painful nipples from nursing, and inflammation of the skin. It is used internally on inflamed mucosa of the throat, windpipe, lungs, and gastrointestinal tract. The gum is beneficial in coughs, catarrh that needs to be loosened and raised, inflammation of the bowels, and diarrhea. “In the chronic bowel complaints of children it is peculiarly beneficial,” wrote Dr. John Eberle, of Philadelphia (1834, 2:456). “I have prescribed it with complete success in such cases, after a variety of other remedies had been tried ineffectually.” In one case, “a little patient [was] restored to health by this treatment alone, after the case appeared to be hopeless.” In the past, many children died from chronic diarrhea.
Before the advent of intravenous feeding, gum arabic served an important purpose. “In acute disease, where it becomes necessary to use the lightest and most readily digested food, there is no article, probably equal to gum arabic” (King, 1898, I:10).

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MUCOSA AND SKIN
Oral plaque, periosteal disease (oral preparation).
Skin irritations, inflammation, burns (external).
RESPIRATION
Laryngitis, hoarseness.
Inflammation of the respiratory tract, irritable cough, catarrh (cough drops).
“Coughs depending on irritation in the fauces or glottis.”
“Coughs from a thin sharp rheum.”
Adhesive phlegm in the throat and bronchia.
DIGESTION
Nutrient in malarial cachexia.
Gastritis.
Diarrhea, dysentery, typhoid.
Tenesmus and painful passage of stool.
Specific for chronic dysentery in children.
CIRCULATION
High cholesterol.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Kidney failure; reduces urea in the blood (cf. Urtica).
Strangury, difficulty passing urine.
Gonorrheal discharge.
FEMALE
Sore nipples from nursing (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Macerate 4 ounces of gum arabic in 4 fluid ounces glycerine and 8 fluid ounces water. Let stand, stir from time to time, until it is dissolved. This method, which yields a pint, retards the formation of acetic acid that occurs when only water is used (Shook, 1978, 37).

### CAUTIONS:

History of food usage. Long considered safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 8, 9, 14, 15, 16), Edward Shook, John King (2, 3, 11, 12), Christopher Menzies-Trull, John Eberle (5, 11, 15, 16), Ben Charles Harris (7), John Hill (6, 15), Hakim Chishti (3, 9).

## Achillea millefolium. Yarrow.

Yarrow is native to the Old World (A. millefolium var. millefolium) and the New (A. millefolium var. lanulosa). Although it is somewhat more widely documented from European sources, I have included it in The Earthwise Herbal under American plants, mostly for space considerations.

## Aegopodium podagraria. Goutweed,
Bishop’s Wort, Herb Gerard.

Goutweed is a member of the parsley family native to Europe, widely grown as an ornamental and naturalized throughout the temperate regions, but seldom used in modern herbalism. It was once important enough to be planted in the monastery herb gardens and was called “herb Gerard” after the patron saint of gout-healing. Although seldom described by modern herbalists, a good account is rendered by an “herbal journalist,” Audrey Wynne Hatfield (1973, 90). The following is quoted from her account.
“Goutwort acts on the kidneys and bladder, stimulating the discharge of urine to flush away poisoning secretions which can be the hidden cause of many aches and pains, gravel and skin disorders. It is sedative and it eases pain. Taken internally, the tea infusion can successfully cope with aching joints, rheumatic pains, gout, and sciatica.” These are the most common traditional uses. Hatfield rewards us with some personal experience. “I have known cases of eczema that for years have defied other treatments, to be completely cured in a few weeks by the daily drinking of a half a pint of the tea. This has been infused with 2 or 3 good leaves of goutwort and 4 dandelion leaves, with a pint of boiling water.” By itself the dose is 3–4 fresh leaves per infusion, taken in wineglassful doses. “Externally, the roots and leaves boiled together and applied to the hip as a fomentation, renewed occasionally, will give wonderful relief in some cases of sciatica.”
A few additional indications can be gleaned from the literature. Goutweed is often found around old monastic ruins, testimony to its onetime use in medicine. Gout was originally considered a “rich man’s disease”—that is, a disease of the aristocrats of church and state. In the Physica Hildegard von Bingen (1998, 74) says that goutweed is effective for stomach pain. Culpeper (1653) notes that it abates unnaturally high color, making a person more pale or less red. “It is not to be supposed that goutweed hath its name for nothing,” he comments.

## Aesculus hippocastanum. Horse Chestnut.

Horse chestnut is a medium sized tree native to Western Asia but naturalized in Europe and now in the western hemisphere, where it has several cousins such as Ohio buckeye (Aesculus glabra). The nuts are mildly toxic but have long been used in folk medicine for the treatment of hemorrhoids and varicose veins. They also make an excellent liniment for muscle spasms in horses, hence the name “horse chestnut.” It is not related to the true chestnut (Castanea) but is in the Sapindaceae family. Much work has been done with the horse chestnut in nineteenth-century botanical medicine, twentieth-century pharmacology, and homeopathy. Dr. Bach, of flower essence fame, used it under the name white chestnut. It contains flavonoids, tannins, fatty acids, sterols, triterpene saponin glycosides (3–6%) in a complex called “aescin,” and coumarins.
Aesculus is most famous as a specific for hemorrhoids. “Two or three drops, with a spoonful of water, taken before meals and at bedtime, will cure almost any simple case of piles in a week,” writes Dr. W. T. Fernie (1914, 97), a reputable author, of the tincture. If we understand the processes behind the formation of hemorrhoids, we can grasp many additional functions to which horse chestnut is remedial.
Horse chestnut was one of the classic remedies for “passive venous congestion” (William Boericke, 1927). This describes a state where the veins are distended and full, with blood collecting in them, so that there is poor return circulation from the feet, varicosities and even phlebitis in the legs, pelvic and uterine congestion, hemorrhoids, backing up of blood through the portal vein to the liver, which may be congested and full, and up the veins to the right or venous side of the heart. The stasis of blood in the venous side of the vascular system causes the heart to have to pump against this pressure, resulting in high blood pressure and dilatation and weakening of the right side of the heart. There may be asthma from stasis in the pulmonary veins. Horse chestnut is indicated for hemorrhoids, varicosities of the legs, phlebitis, thrombosis, swollen liver, portal stagnation, distention of the right side of the heart, high blood pressure, and asthma, when the characteristic mental and pulse symptoms are present. It contains tannins, coumarins, and triterpene glycosides that tone the veins, thin the blood, stimulate the circulation, and relieve the heart.
Aesculus hippocastanum is a top notch remedy for obsessive and compulsive thinking, as if one were chased by one’s own thoughts, round and round, and had no control over them. This indication comes from Edward Bach but I have seen it confirmed in homeopathic and herbal dosages. It is as if there were not enough room between the thoughts. The problem shows up in the pulse, which feels as if there is not enough room or rest between the beats, which bound up to the surface, like a stag jumping over a fence. The bound-up pulse of Aesculus indicates mental tension, blood being held back under pressure, and often indeed, high blood pressure, for which this remedy has long been used. However, it can only be used successfully for hypertension when indicated by specific symptoms.
One time a woman came to see me who was suffering from glaucoma. She had a bounding pulse with a lack of space between the beats so I asked a few questions. Yes, she had obsessive/compulsive thinking, insomnia (“I haven’t slept in six months”), and hemorrhoids. Aesculus hippocastanum 12x homeopathy brought a flood of tears to her dry eyes and rapid elimination of the glaucoma. This condition, in her case, was evidently due to venous congestion in the eyes. Remember, glaucoma should be treated like a “high blood pressure of the eyes.”
Rudolf Steiner (1975, 221) recommended a mild aqueous dilution of the husks of the horse chestnut to help mineralize the teeth. “The juice of the horse-chestnut contains something of the same principle as that which builds up our teeth. There is always some substances out in the macrocosm with an internal organizing effect.” Aesculus is therefore analogous to white oak bark—both are for varicose veins and mineralization of the teeth. They are also analogous to calcium fluoride, used in homeopathy to tighten varicose veins and build the teeth. Astringents have a mineralizing effect because they stop the outflow of fluids that carry away electrolytes.
Aesculus is a pungent/stimulating astringent like Collinsonia—another great hemorrhoid, vein, and heart remedy. They are often used in combination. A common formula would add Hamamelis.

### TASTE:

pungent • warm, dry • astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

relaxation, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION
Passive venous congestion.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Obsessive/compulsive thinking; thoughts go round and round; feels persecuted by his own thoughts; no down time from the mind; insomnia.
HEAD
Headache.
Glaucoma (one case only).
RESPIRATION
Asthma, associated with hemorrhoids and rectal fullness.
DIGESTION
Dyspepsia, associated with hemorrhoids and rectal stasis.
“Congestion of the colon, rectum, and the entire pelvic viscera.”
LIVER
Swollen liver; uneasiness, fullness, aching, throbbing; associated with portal and rectal engorgement.
HEART, CIRCULATION, AND BLOOD
Pulse tense, not enough space or time between beats.
High blood pressure.
Hemorrhoids; large and purple; rarely bleed; sense of fullness.
Phlebitis.
Varicose veins, ulcerations, edema, eczema.
Hemangioma (cf. Salvia, Sambucus).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Backache associated with hemorrhoids, rectal fullness.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Internal use: homeopathic or flower essence. Tincture: 5–15 drops (Fyfe), 20 drops adult dose, twice a day (de Vries). Long-term use advisable (John Scudder, Jan de Vries). External use: salve, ointment, cream.

### CAUTIONS:

Cautions for those on blood thinning drugs. Not to be used internally in large doses.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Edward Bach (2), J. Compton Burnett, Matthew Wood (2-confirmed, 4, 9, 12), William Boericke (1), John Scudder (1, 3, 5, 6, 8, 11, 14), John William Fyfe (7), David Winston (13), modern research (10).

## Agathosma betulina. Buchu.

This is the newer binomial for buchu; see under .

## Agrimonia eupatoria, A. gryposepala. Agrimony.

Agrimony is a member of the rose family native to temperate regions of the globe. It has a history of moderate use in Europe, China, and North America. It has properties similar to its close cousin cinquefoil (Potentilla spp.). The latter was generally more popular in folk medicine due to the resemblance the five leaves have to the fingers. Both Agrimonia and Potentilla are astringents that improve tissue tone, but they have also been used to relieve tension—both were used for the tense intermittent chills of malaria back to the time of Dioscorides. They can be used fairly interchangeably. It is in tension that their properties manifest most completely. Agrimony contains tannins, flavonoids, coumarins, polysaccharides, small amounts of glycosidal bitters, and minerals (including silicon). The American species (A. gryposepala) can be substituted for the European—I think it is a little more sweet and aromatic than the European.
“Agrimony is a simple well known to all country folk, and abundant through England in the fields and woods, as a popular domestic medicinal herb,” writes Dr. W. T. Fernie (1914, 18). “Its use in the cottage for gargles, and as an astringent application to indolent wounds, is well justified.” It is best known as a specific for diarrhea and bedwetting, applications that carry over into modern British herbalism. However, this ancient simple is for so much more than this. It is, as Plinius wrote, an herb of “princely authoritie.”
Agrimony is a superlative remedy for tension. This indication, originated by Dr. Edward Bach, holds true on both the mental and physical levels, for both the flower essence and the herbal preparation. It is for the person who tries to hide his or her tension behind a false facade of suave sophistication or cheerfulness. He denies the pain with a smile. The phrase “torturedly cheerful” is expressive of the appearance. In other instances, agrimony people like speed and danger to escape from their pain (Bach). Such activities push the agrimony person into an excess sympathetic condition that masks the deeper pain and gets them stuck there. Bach also says there can be abuse of drugs, alcohol, and stimulants to escape the pain. The problem often revolves around business and hierarchical relations (employee/employer). The person feels “caught in a bind.”
Agrimony is a specific in any condition where the person holds the breath to stop the pain. This causes the release of endorphins—the body’s own opiates—which suppress pain. Agrimony people need to learn to “breathe through” the pain. As a consequence, the respiratory apparatus is affected. Agrimony is suited to conditions where the person is “tortured to capture the breath,” as Dr. Bach noted. “Agrimonia exerts a specific influence upon mucous membranes, checking profuse secretion and favoring normal activity. This action has suggested its usefulness in chronic bronchitis and in phthisis” (John William Fyfe, 1909, 355). Samuel Henry (1814, 12) treats us to a case history. “I knew a man at Flat brook, near Sussex [New Jersey], who informed me that he had spent near three hundred pounds with Dr. Kanady, without receiving any benefit; but by drinking the decoction of agrimony root … was perfectly cured of his asthmatic complaint.” Dr. J. V. Cerney (1976) also used agrimony root for bronchitis. He mentions a child with unstoppable coughing, night and day, until he turned blue, who was cured with agrimony root. I have several times used agrimony leaf with success for bronchitis and asthma, or for conditions elsewhere in the body that cause the person to hold the breath to stop the pain.
One would think that as an astringent agrimony is purely for relaxation, and hence for diarrhea and excessive urination, as it has been traditionally used. However, David Hoffmann provides the context. For example, he says that agrimony is suited to problems when they arise in children who are having trouble with toilet training. So there is tension combined with symptoms of relaxation.
As an astringent, agrimony has been used for all sorts of digestive problems, but it is suited to those where tension and relaxation combine to cause incorrect timing of functions. This element of the picture is developed by Dorothy Hall (1988, 78). It is suited to diarrhea, as are all astringents, but especially to cases where there are loose, floating stools with fats in them due to irregular or deficient discharges of bile and poor digestion of fats and oils. Acting further back up into the hepatic apparatus, it is good for hepatitis and cirrhosis from chronic alcoholism or food poisoning, with faulty fat metabolism, and irregular expulsion of bile. There may be ulceration of the bowels, blood in the stool, and bloody hemorrhoids.
Circulation to the liver, gallbladder, and gastrointestinal are off so that the hepatic cells do not receive the correct amount of “life-giving” arterial blood when they receive the dose of toxin laden blood from the portal vein and the intestines—at least, this is my picture of the situation. Consequently, there are liver problems, allergies, skin rashes and wasting (due to poor liver anabolism and catabolism of proteins and fats). Scudder notes that there is cirrhosis beginning in the lower parts of the liver, where the input of blood from the hepatic artery is less direct. Tension and suppressed anger are emotions associated with the liver and gallbladder, East and West. “I have found it helpful against liver disorders, especially jaundice” (Donald Law, 1973, 26).
Agrimony acts strongly on the kidneys, a situation Dorothy Hall attributes to the presence of silica in the plant. The leaves “are found by experience to be good in the diabetes and incontinence of urine” (John Hill, 1740, 55). Peter Smith, an “Indian doctor” in Ohio, considered it indicated for incontinence following the passage of stones. Dr. John Scudder writes, “Given a pain in the region of the kidneys, and I always think of agrimonia as the remedy. In my practice I have seen wonderful results from it, in cases of months’ and years’ duration, and when everything had failed. I have found other uses for it, but this has been so prominent that I always associate the medicine and the position of the pain.” He considered it specific in “pointing pains” in the kidneys, from any cause.
“Among neuralgias, nephralgia is one of the severest. It is torture that might be borne for an hour or a day; but continued night and day for a fortnight or a month, the sufferer may well pray for relief or death. It varies in cause, in some a well-defined lithemia [stones], in others there is absolutely nothing to be determined wrong with the urine.”
“It is one of those singular remedies that follow the specific indications sharply; and the indication is, pain in the region of the kidneys, especially of the right side” (Scudder, quoted by Fyfe, 1909, 355–56).
Agrimony helps relieve the pain accompanying passage of gravel. Peter Smith (1812) used it for leaky bladder following the passage of kidney stones—here we see perfectly the idea of an astringent used for problems caused by tension. It is also for weakness from tension (Matthew Wood). “Strengthening in case of weakness” (Donald Law, 1973, 103). John Wesley (1791) recommended agrimony tea at breakfast for three months to prevent the return of kidney stones.
One of my students found it very unpleasant to pick agrimony because it produced symptoms she called soroche (Spanish for altitude sickness). “You feel really tired, like you want to be lower—like from the bed to the floor. Your digestion just stops. You get a queasy/nauseous feeling. Gas builds up, but you might get diarrhea.” These were the symptoms of soroche and, for her, exposure to agrimony, so this may be a remedy for altitude sickness.

### TASTE:

(before flowering) mildly sweet, sour • astringent (after flowering) increasingly astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

constriction, relaxation, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Mental tension; feels caught in a bind, “torturedly cheerful,” hides anger and frustration behind an artificial facade; suave but inwardly tortured.
Work-related tension; employer-employee tension.
Holds the breath to suppress the pain.
Resorts to drugs, alcohol, speed, and danger to suppress emotions; “sex and drugs and rock ’n’ roll” (Julia Graves).
Tense, wiry pulse.
HEAD
Tension headaches.
Slight yellow/gray complexion.
Allergic welts associated with tension.
Hardened ear wax (warm infusion in the ear).
Ulcerations of the tongue, sores in the mouth, aphthae.
RESPIRATION
Pharyngitis.
Tonsillitis, relaxed throat (with Salvia).
Respiratory problems; whooping cough, bronchitis, asthma; “tortured to capture the breath”; holds the breath.
Catarrhal flow.
DIGESTION
Low appetite.
Ulceration of the gastrointestinal tract: tongue, mouth, stomach, intestines.
Difficulty with fat digestion and metabolism.
Nausea, vomiting, diarrhea with griping and spasms; bellyache from spasms anywhere, especially guts.
Constipation suddenly followed by diarrhea.
Loose, fatty, floating stools; undigested food, large lumps, nuts, proteins in the stool.
Food poisoning, amebic dysentery, gastroenteritis; doesn’t kill the microorganisms but tones the bowels (cf. Rubus).
Mucus colitis.
“Appendicitis”; traditionally used in British herbalism for low-grade irritation; may actually be cases of ileo-cecal irritation.
LIVER
Hepatitis, cirrhosis, hepatic atrophy, jaundice.
Tension and pain during the passage of a gall- or kidney-stone; blue, purple tongue, wiry pulse, and holding of the breath to stop the pain.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Atonic conditions of the urinary apparatus, and where the urine is thick and gelatinous.
Pointing pains in the kidneys; with or without the passage of stones, in various kinds of urinary problems.
Urinary tract infections with severe pain.
Bedwetting and diarrhea; in children suffering anxiety about toilet training.
Incontinence in the elderly; after passing stones.
Edema with welts, coming and going suddenly.
Acid urine, mild bladder and urethral inflammation; especially with dull pain in the lower back and flank (tea).
FEMALE
Menstrual pain, cramps, and colic; hides the pain, pretends to be fine, composed.
Vaginal ulceration.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Articular, spinal pain, acute and chronic; torturous; sprains and strains.
SKIN
Ulceration on the labia, buttocks, legs.
Skin rash, allergies, ulcers, psoriasis; with mental tension.
Wounds, bruises, splinters; passive bleeding.
FEVER
Influenza; intermittent chills and fever; with pain in the joints.
OTHER
High LDLs and platelet aggregation (tea).
Altitude sickness (?).
Weakness from tension.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

It is best to pick the leaves off the sides of the stem, leaving the leader to go into seed, in early summer, before flowering. Harvested after flowering they are less sweet and more astringent (Matthew Wood). An old Southern manuscript notes that a “strong odor” is desirable (Moss, 1999, 169). This is true. Harvest from many different sites to ensure full range of properties (Dorothy Hall). Dose of the tincture: 1–15 drops, 1–3x/day (Matthew Wood), or 1–2 drams to 4 ounces of water (Rolla Thomas).

### CAUTIONS:

None known (German E Commission; Bisset and Wichtl, 1994).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (22, 27, 37), Edward Bach (1, 4, 11, 31), Matthew Wood (1–8, 10, 13, 21, 25, 29–31, 33–37, 39, 42), Julia Graves (1, 3, 4, 6, 16, 18, 30), Samuel Henry (13, 27, 37), John Scudder (21, 27), Finley Ellingwood (10, 16, 24, 28, 33), Michael Moore (32, 40), David Hoffmann (29), Jack Ritchason (24), Dorothy Hall (4, 17–19, 21), Rolla Thomas (26), Christopher Menzies-Trull (9, 11, 12, 14, 16), John Hill (29–30), Dioscorides (39), W. T. Fernie (10, 12, 38), Thomas Easley (31-confirmed), Sarah Duncklee (41), Donald Law (42). An extensive account is given in Matthew Wood, The Book of Herbal Wisdom.

## Agropyron repens, Triticum repens,
Elymus repens. Couchgrass.

This plant is commonly called couchgrass in herbalism, but most gardeners would know it by the name quackgrass. These names come from the Anglo-Saxon qwice, meaning “full of life” (as in the word “quickened” or Yorkshire dialect “wick”). This testifies to a very early usage of this grass as a food or medicine. The root was sometimes made into bread. Couchgrass was classified as Triticum by the older botanists, and appears under this name in the older herbal literature. Today it is called Elymus, but it was still Agropyron when I started writing The Earthwise Herbal, so I kept that name. It is almost impossible to keep up with botanists and their changing nomenclature; they have no regard for herbal or medical tradition. The roots are high in sugars, polysaccharides, mucilage, gums, acids, flavonoids, potassium, and silicon.
Couchgrass has been used as a remedy for heat and irritation of the kidneys and bladder from ancient times down to the present. The rhizomes are cool, moist, sweet, highly nutritious, and demulcent, so this is a remedy for dryness and atrophy. The silicon—all grasses rely on silica in their sheaths—makes it strengthening to the nervous system (Eli Jones) and urinary sphincters (Dorothy Hall). By increasing water passing through the renal tract it decreases heat and irritation. It is said to dilate the renal capillaries, thus allowing more water through and decongesting the kidneys. This effectively reduces heat and inflammation. Like most plants high in natural sugars couchgrass has the ability to control high blood sugar.
Ellingwood (1918) classified triticum as a “renal sedative.” He says that it increases “the flow of the watery portion of the urine without to the same extent influencing the actual renal secretion.” It does not cause the kidneys to work harder (filter more blood), but opens up the tubules to allow more water through. “It is bland, mild, unirritating, and is used whenever urine, having a high specific gravity, causes irritation of the kidneys or bladder, more especially of their mucous surfaces.” It is also used to help pass gravel and stones and soothe the injured membranes as they pass. By dilating the renal tubules and opening up the flow of water, triticum also cleans out deposits of gravel in the tubules. It is thus an important remedy in passing gravel and stones—a general condition the old doctors called “lithemia.” To continue with Ellingwood: “It will relieve the constant ache in the back, which is due to precipitation of the crystalline secreted products within the tubules of the kidneys by furnishing abundant water for their solution. It flushes the kidneys, as it were, to an admirable extent, when renal sand has accumulated within the pelvis. Under these circumstances it is one of our most useful remedies.”
“Internal dosing with an infusion of the root of couch grass” is especially good for bladder troubles, stone, gravel, irritability, and inflammation, writes Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1973, 105). However, “this infusion is prepared differently from the standard method, the herb having to be simmered a full quarter-hour in the water.” She adds, “Couch-grass root possesses remarkable stone-dissolving properties.”
Couchgrass is one of the main grasses used by dogs to induce vomiting to relieve their head colds and stomach problems. Vomiting stimulates the vagus, which stimulates the respiratory tract—this is the same principle used in modern medicine (“nauseant expectorant”) to clear the lungs. The vagus also stimulates the vomiting reflex. For this reason couchgrass is sometimes called “dog grass.” I observed my little eight pound poodle/terrier use the couchgrass to expel a cold picked up from a dog visiting the day before. Pliny complains that dogs use a grass to cure themselves every day, right in front of people, but are so ungenerous as not to show us which grass it is! This is only one of numerous clues showing that Pliny was not an observer, but a bookworm. His grand tome, The Natural History, might better be called The Unnatural History.

### TASTE:

sweet • moist • mucilaginous, fibrous

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, irritation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
RESPIRATION
Always blowing the nose; chronic catarrh, bronchitis.
Sore throat, tonsillitis (combined with sage and pennyroyal).
DIGESTION
Vomiting.
Worms in children, kills and expels (combined with wormwood).
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Jaundice.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Irritation of the urinary apparatus.
Aching in the back that accompanies stone formation.
Pain and straining with urination.
Prostatitis, acute and chronic; benign enlargement, strangury, hematuria.
Passage of scanty, bloody urine.
Lack of free secretion from the kidneys.
Deposits of any kind: phosphates, uric acid, or calcium salts.
Excessive irritability of the bladder from any cause.
Incontinence, dribbling.
Kidney disease with drain on the nervous system; enfeeblement, eyes bright, surrounded by contracted tissues.
Albuminuria, glomerulonephritis; frequent and scanty urination, burning and scalding in the urinary tract, heavy dull aching pains in the region of the kidneys.
LYMPH/IMMUNE
Lymphatics, swollen, chronic.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis, gout, rheumatism.
SKIN
Eczema, chronic, dry or wet.
Ulcers, cuts, and wounds.
FEVER
Use during fevers when the urine is dark and scanty and there is thirst.
OTHER
High blood sugar.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The rhizomes can be collected while weeding the garden, dried and used as a tea, or prepared in alcohol. “This agent gives better results when given as an infusion” (Rolla Thomas). This is often the case with diuretics and nephritics. Decoction: 2 teaspoons per cup, simmer 10 minutes; 1 cup 3x/day. The tincture is better made from the fresh rhizomes. Dose: 5–60 drops (Fyfe). Juice: “crush the fresh rhizomes in a mortar and express through cheesecloth; or use a wheat grass juicer. Preserve with 25% vodka, or freeze in ice cube trays” (Rogers).

### CAUTIONS:

History of food usage. Generally regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (6, 8, 10, 11, 13, 14, 17), homeopathic (1), Finley Ellingwood (7, 10, 11, 12, 21), Eli Jones (15–16), John William Fyfe, Christopher Menzies-Trull (5, 16), Dorothy Hall, Robert Dale Rogers (1, 17, 19, 22), Chalid Ottway (2, 3, 4, 19).

## Ajuga reptans. Bugle.

Bugle, a low, creeping member of the mint family, was once one of the most popular wound remedies in European herbalism. It qualifies as one of the “most forgotten” vulneraries of the Middle Ages (Peter Holmes). Culpeper may be referred to for a summary of the older uses. Maude Grieve gives an account based upon the experience of herbalists still using it in the early twentieth century.
“In herbal treatment, an infusion of this plant is still considered very useful in arresting hemorrhages and is employed in coughs and spitting of blood in incipient consumption and also in some biliary disorders,” writes Grieve (1931, 140). One of her sources, evidently a physiomedicalist, refers to its action in “lowering the pulse and lessening its frequency,” and likens it to digitalis. This harkens back to the empirical use of digitalis to control fever, as well as heart conditions, before the action of digitalis was completely understood. “It allays irritation and cough, and equalizes the circulation.” It is compared to a mild narcotic in its relaxant power. “It has also been considered good for the bad effects of excessive drinking.”
John Hill (1740, 95) notes that the juice is “a very good diuretic.”
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Relaxes the mind.
RESPIRATION
Irritable cough.
Pulmonary hemorrhage of tuberculosis.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Gallbladder congestion.
HEART AND CIRCULATION
Lowers a superficial, elevated pulse, decreases its frequency, increases strength, and equalizes the circulation.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Hernia (external).
SKIN AND WOUNDS
Bruises, cuts, lacerations, stab wounds, hematomas, ulcers.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Native to Europe but naturalized in North America. “The whole herb is gathered in May and early June, when the leaves are at their best, and dried.… A wine glassful of the infusion—made from 1 oz. of the dried herb to 1 pint of boiling water—being given frequently” (Grieve). Bugle was commonly made into a salve for use on external wounds.

### CAUTIONS:

Members of the mint family are seldom toxic.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Nicholas Culpeper, Maude Grieve.

## Alchemilla vulgaris, A. mollis, A. xanthochlora. Lady’s Mantle.

This Old World remedy is a member of the rose family. It has an astringent and bitter taste, resembling aspirin, reflecting the presence of salicylates. Lady’s mantle was one of the most popular woundworts of the Middle Ages, like the preceding remedy, but in the Renaissance its use as a female medicine replaced the earlier reputation as a woundwort. It is a unique plant that, though it has never been highly popular, has never been completely forgotten. The leaves preserve droplets of water well into the afternoon of a hot day, reminding one of a raincoat, hence the name “Lady’s mantle.” This also reminds us of the use of dew by the alchemists, giving rise to the name Alchemilla, the “little alchemist,” or as Elisabeth Brooke translates it, the “little magical one.” The leaves also look like a lion’s paw, hence another common name, “lionsfoot.” The furred brown rhizomes rising out of the soil give rise to yet another interesting name, “bearsfoot.” One can either read in the scientific literature that Alchemilla contains tannins and salicylates, or one can determine this for oneself on the tongue.
The leaves of lady’s mantle generate and collect droplets of water, indicating that this plant has a particular relationship to the management and economy of water. This is confirmed by the presence of tannins and salicylates. The former control the outflow of excess water, while the latter thin the blood and increase flowability of fluids. Thus, alchemilla has long been used to absterge or drive water from tissues that are damp and weak. It strengthens fibers and brings them back together into a healthy, toned condition. Hence it is useful in poor tone of the pelvic floor, hernia, rectal tear, prolapse of the uterus, and uterine bleeding. It controls pain associated with damp wounds, menstruation, and rheumatism. It possesses a remarkable ability to bring together torn tissues, thus has proved efficacious in repairing torn eardrums, hernia, muscles, tooth extractions, and so forth. The author has confirmed these indications and uses in practice.
Lady’s mantle was originally an important woundwort. We may guess, from its other applications and constituents, that it is indicated in fistulous, purulent discharges and infected passageways (ears, vagina) with pain. Later it came into folk use as an important female remedy. It lifts tissue and structures that have become bogged down: sagging breasts after lactation and abdominal tissue following childbirth. “The good women in the North of England apply the leaves to their breasts to make them recover their form after they have been swelled with milk” (Hill, 1740, 226).
Alchemilla is associated with relaxed tissues that need astringence. Because the veins are more prone to relaxation than the arteries—the latter have strong neuromuscular coats—there may be prominent blue veins. The blood is usually more watery when the tissues are relaxed due to the outflow of fluids, so the skin may be pale and damp with prominent blue veins. Salicylates tend to thin coagulated blood, also reducing venous stagnation and prominence. This gives the characteristic constitutional type: “pale, sensitive women with prominent blue veins” (William LeSassier, confirmed Matthew Wood). This is the constitutional type that, according to the old empirical doctors, is prone to leucorrhea—for which lady’s mantle has long been used.
Bisset and Wichtl (1994, 53) warn us that the female uses of Alchemilla are based on folk medicine—probably the doctrine of signatures—and are therefore not derived from sound scientific concepts. Thus, the German E Commission indications are limited to “mild, non-specific diarrhea.”

### TASTE:

bitter • cool, dry • astringent

### TISSUE STATE:

relaxation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION
Pale, anemic, sensitive women with prominent blue veins and moist skin.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Female energy; reinforces, protects, and connects.
Nervousness, agitation, insomnia, and mood swings.
HEAD
Headache from congested blood, worse before the period.
Conjunctivitis (external).
Infections in deep tubes; ears, vagina; after tooth extractions.
Perforated eardrum.
Mouth sores and ulcers (mouthwash).
Laryngitis (gargle).
DIGESTION
Nausea and indigestion, especially after consumption of fats.
“Specific in acute and epidemic diarrhea”; diarrhea of relaxed intestines.
HEART
Strengthens heart muscles, walls, atria.
Arteriosclerosis.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Muscles weak, relaxed; heart, abdominal walls, uterus, pelvic floor.
Hernia.
Anal fistula.
FEMALE
Vaginitis, leucorrhea, yeast infection; especially in pale, anemic women.
Leucorrhea; discharge yellow.
Pruritus vulvae.
Menstruation: dysmenorrhea, amenorrhea, menorrhagia, metrorrhagia.
Menstrual bleeding profuse (symptomatic relief, not necessarily cure of the cause).
Endometriosis, fibroids.
Menopause, hot flashes.
SKIN AND WOUNDS
Cuts; flaps of skin hanging loose; cuts and ulcers that have become fistulous, or discharge fluids and pus; encourages clotting in wounds.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

At present the leaves are the part used. They are picked in late spring and early summer, before flowering, when they are tender and green. Infusion of the dried leaves: 2 teaspoons to 1 cup, steep for 10–15 minutes, 3x/day. The tincture is made from dried herb at 1:5 in 25% alcohol, dosage: 2–4 ml, 3x/day. I use a tincture made from the fresh leaves at about 1:1, dosage: 3–25 drops, 1–3x/day.
“The root is the part most valuable,” writes John Hill (1740, 226). “A decoction of it fresh taken up is an excellent remedy for the overflowings of the menses, for bloody-fluxes, and all other bleedings.”

### CAUTIONS:

None known (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (6–9, 11–12, 14–15, 20, 21), William LeSassier (1, 14), William Salmon (11, 12, 24), Maria Treben (12, 16), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (3, 5, 10, 13, 18, 20, 23), Matthew Wood (8, 22, 24; confirmed: 1, 2, 4, 6, 13–15), Julia Graves (2, 4, 19), Dorothy Hall (21), Christopher Menzies-Trull (6, 15–17, 24), David Hoffmann (9, 10).

## Allium cepa. Onion.

This common member of the lily family is widely used as a food and was once almost as widely used in medicine. The Old World culinary onion is used in modern folk medicine; related alliums were used in North America by the Indian people, frequently in the same manner—as a topical stimulant and drawing agent. Onion contains volatile oils, sulphur, phosphorus, alkaline salts, phosphoric and acetic acids, phosphoric and calcium citrate, starch, sugar, mucilage, and fiber.
Onion is one of the many pungent, warming remedies found in the food/herb group of plants. It also has drawing powers. Hence, onion is used topically to draw out poisons, mucus, and pus. Chopped and roasted onions are spread on the chest as a stimulant in cases of bronchitis and pneumonia. They warm the chest, liquefy mucus, and stimulate the cough reflexes. A roasted slice is placed on the ear to draw out infection and ache. The onion should not be used fresh, topically, or it will burn the skin. “A syrup made from the juice with honey is an excellent medicine for old phlegmatic persons in cold weather, when their lungs are stuffed, and the breathing is hindered.” Raw onions increase the flow of urine, and are used in edema, increase perspiration (given in a hot soup), quicken the circulation, assist digestion, prolong and deepen sleep (eaten at night), and are “almost the best nervine” known, “no medicine being equally useful in cases of nervous prostration, or so quick to restore and tone up a jaded physical system” (Fernie, 1914, 193).
There is an old tradition, mentioned by Fernie, of hanging up onions in the house to prevent epidemic disease. He mentions a house in a district where cholera was present that escaped the disease, supposedly because of the onions hanging in the cottage. An elderly Dakota woman of my acquaintance, now deceased, remembered that her family escaped the dreadful flu epidemic of 1918 because they hung onions in the house. Another American Indian, who used onion to draw out malevolent influences, either physical or psychic, noted that it should be buried afterward. Fernie (1914) notes that the rotten part of an onion should never be eaten because it may have picked up something undesirable.
More subtle uses are brought out in the homeopathic provings. Allium cepa (red onion) has produced “melancholy and depression, with severe catarrh” (Fernie, 1914, 196). Consequently, it is specific in small doses for head cold with running, clear phlegm that irritates the nostrils, with nonirritating tears in the eyes. It is a good remedy for social alienation, loneliness, and shock—“nobody will play with me.” Sore throat, torn, broken up feeling in the throat, as if one wanted to cry. For these homeopathic indications just cut a piece of raw onion into a glass, stir, and drink.

### TASTE:

pungent, sweet, salty • warm, moist • stimulating

### TISSUE STATE:

depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Depression, sadness.
Chronic or acute social alienation; “nobody wants to play with me”; shock from accidents, financial setback, social or familial loss.
RESPIRATION
Tearful eyes, watery, sneezy, irritating discharge from the nose, with social alienation.
Head colds with sneezing, irritation around the nostrils.
Earache (put a slice of roasted onion to the ear).
Sore throat, feeling as if torn, broken, as if one wanted to cry.
Impacted mucus in the lungs, threatening or developed pneumonia (poultice of chopped roasted onions on the chest).
SKIN AND WOUNDS
Drawing agent (externally, upper half of a roasted onion on boil, bruise, bite, wound, and so on).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Red onion is more stimulating, yellow or white onions more sweet, so the former is preferred in the above symptoms where a stimulant is needed. Chop a piece into a glass of water and sip for upper respiratory problems. Use fresh to prevent loss of the volatile oil.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (5, 7), homeopathic provings (1, 3, 4, 6), Matthew Wood (2, 6), W. T. Fernie (1, 3, 4, 5, 7, 8).

## Allium sativum. Garlic.

Garlic is one of the most important, widely known, and universally applicable of all medicinal plants in the Old World pharmacopoeia. John Heinerman showed that it was widely used in ancient Sumeria, and is thus one of the most ancient medicines in the world. He adds that it is still a widely used folk medicine in Iraq. Galen called garlic theriaka rusticana (village panacea) because it was so widely available and useful. In English folk medicine it was named “poor man’s treacle.” It is considered to possess five out of six of the primary tastes of Ayurvedic medicine. Modern research has isolated a medicinal compound, alliin, that justifies many of the traditional applications. Medical research carried out in the first half of the twentieth century established unimpeachable scientific proof of its efficacy in many serious and chronic disorders. Garlic contains organic sulphur compounds (including alliin, which converts to the medically active allicin), enzymes, B vitamins, minerals, and flavonoids.
Although garlic has been (and still is) rated as a general panacea, it possesses specific properties that are due to its strongly warming and mildly moistening capacities. These make it beneficial in conditions that are cold and dry. Like many warming medicines garlic is rarefying or dissolving, so that it thins cloying substances and then expels them. These were the properties most highly valued by Galen (Megatechne XIII). More than a thousand years later garlic was still appreciated for the same properties. William Salmon (1692, 50) recommended “Essentia Allii, or essence of Garlick” when the bowels were “afflicted with a gross, tough, and cold matter.” It “is an admirable thing; for it exceedingly heats & warms; discusses wind profoundly, and withal prevents the breeding and increase of the cold flegmatick humor.”
The strong smell of garlic is due to the sulphurous alliin compounds that are stimulating, detoxifying, and antimicrobial. Garlic increases secretion from the mucosa to improve digestion and lubricate the passage of viscid mucus, stimulates internal metabolism, and kills bacteria through direct attack (like a sulfa drug) and by changing the environment in which they thrive. A peculiarity about garlic is that it does not indiscriminately kill bacteria, but is selective in its action so that (unlike antibiotics) it tends to destroy foreign pathogens while supporting the positive flora of the intestine. This has led to its successful use killing intestinal parasites and bacteria while reestablishing the healthy flora of the colon. One early researcher/clinician, Dr. E. E. Marcovici, suggested that the principal modus operandi of garlic was based on the fact that it corrects imbalance in the intestinal flora that causes toxification through the bloodstream. (This theory is not accepted in modern biomedicine.) He first became acquainted with the curative properties of garlic in dysentery and cholera during World War I. Another researcher/clinician, Dr. Minchin, used it successfully in tuberculosis, diagnosed by the presence of the bacteria (Fernie, 1914).
In addition to acting as an antibiotic, the sulphur in garlic is also stimulating to the metabolism. The oil is nutritive, so garlic is both catabolic and anabolic. Thus it helps the body eliminate waste material that supports bacteria and builds replacement tissue. This would help explain the powerful and well-documented influence on cardiovascular health. We now know that bacterial infection in the walls of the arteries combines with deposition to cause heart disease. However, an even more exact mechanism is proven: garlic stimulates production of a substance that relaxes the arteries. This is reliable, like an allopathic drug, but also only palliative—it does not cure high blood pressure—in other words, symptoms return when it is discontinued.
We should not overlook the fact that garlic is nutritive and anabolic because it contains fixed oils that provide good food for the body. Thus it is indicated in cases of sympathetic excess, where there is nervousness, anxiety, insomnia, and weight loss. It helps move the organism into the parasympathetic, improving appetite, digestion, and absorption, removing gas, bloating, colic, GI inflammation, lack of appetite, and anemia. It can be effective in either weight loss or gain.
There are certain persons in whom garlic causes uncomfortable symptoms. Dr. Kristin Nolfi, a Danish physician (My Experiences with Living Food, n.d.), notes that it will cause digestive upset in those with a weak liver. The reason for this is that garlic stimulates metabolism. I have seen dietary use of garlic cause symptoms of sympathetic excess or hyperadrenalism (anxiety, nervousness, high blood pressure, sweating, rapid heartbeat). This may be considered an inadvertent homeopathic proving, or it may be considered a warning about susceptibility—people with adrenaline excess tendencies might be intolerant to garlic. So it is generally contraindicated in heat and nervous irritation.

### TASTE:

pungent, sweet, slightly salty • warm, moist • stimulating, oily

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Dull, obtuse, loss of memory, premature aging.
Nervousness, irritability, anxiety, tiredness, agitation, and mild depression.
HEAD
Headache, throbbing; dizziness; ringing in the ear; blurred vision.
Ear infections (drop of oil in the ear or a sliver wrapped in gauze).
Incipient head cold; burning and itching in the throat; sneezing, stuffiness, allergies.
Chronic inflammation of the tonsils, salivary glands, neighboring lymph glands; prevents swollen glands after acute upper respiratory disease.
Empyema of the maxillary sinus.
Loose teeth, tartar.
Sore throat, pharyngitis, laryngitis; “clears and strengthens the throat.”
RESPIRATION
Respiratory conditions; coughs, flu, chronic bronchitis, bronchial asthma, shortness of breath; impacted mucus (rub oil on chest or eat fresh).
Chronic cough remaining after whooping cough, with debility.
Congestion with mucus dried out and adhesive, at the bifurcation of the tubes; impacted, dried out mucus in the lungs; dry cough.
DIGESTION
“Wind in the stomach caused by biliousness.”
Weak digestion, lack of appetite, diarrhea, weight loss, pallor, anemia, numbness, tingling, fatigue, shortness of breath after slight exertion and fever (case history, cured).
Digestive stagnation; parasites, worms, candida; phlegm, gas; diverticulitis.
Diarrhea, cholera.
Constipation.
“Soreness in the bowels.”
HEART AND CIRCULATION
Hypertension, cholesterol, arteriosclerosis (palliative, sometimes curative). Breaks up cholesterol, makes it water soluble, and kills bacteria associated with vascular changes. Releases gas in the blood vessels, reducing hypertension.
Increases circulation.
Angina pains.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema; swelling of the extremities.
“Dries all the moisture in the body” and decreases attendant “sluggishness.”
Kidney stones.
Lumbago.
FEMALE
Swelling and tenderness of the breasts.
Leucorrhea.
“Promotes menstruation and dissolves swellings.”
Menstruation; cramping during the first two days (case history, cured).
Menstruation; extreme weakness, so that she could hardly do anything except lie still and not talk; very shaky on her feet; with nervousness and trembling; cold sweats, extreme thirst, faintness, sometimes violent nausea and diarrhea (case history, cured).
Menstruation, cramping; worse on the right side, dragging and pressing downward, starting in the back and spreading to the front, with bearing down pains that felt as if everything would fall out of her pelvis (case history, cured).
Morning sickness; almost constant nausea and vomiting.
Menopause: hot flashes, irritability, insomnia, palpitations.
Menopause: excess androgenism in women, facial hair, baldness, weight gain, premature aging, memory loss.
MALE
Impotence.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis; numbness, tingling.
Paralysis.
SKIN
Athlete’s foot (external).
Infectious impetigo (cf. Juglans).
“A wonderful remedy for psoriasis.”
Boils, suppurating sores, blisters, bedsores.
FEVER
Blood poisoning; bites of scorpions and snakes.
Fever, chills, epidemic fever.
Hot flashes.
OTHER
Dandruff.
Antitoxic, anticancer reputation.
Cold feet (rub on oil).
It is said to cause satiation when used in food (Greek-Arabic medicine).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Garlic is best used fresh. Fresh cloves or cloves preserved in oil or honey, or capsules of the oil are some of the best methods of preparation. It is taken with parsley to cut down the smell. For a child of six or seven, Mrs. Child (1837, 56, 121) recommends, “a third of a common sized clove, morning, noon and evening; gradually increasing the dose as the system becomes accustomed to it.” A ten-year-old or older can start on half a clove. A medicinal tincture can be made in brandy, dose 10–20 drops several times a day (Fernie, 1914).

### CAUTIONS AND CONTRAINDICATIONS:

“Large doses, especially in excited states of the system, are apt to occasion irritation, flatulence, and fever” (Mrs. Child). Also, they often are vomited up; combine with butter to reduce the emesis (Julia Graves). Eating too much causes diarrhea. Garlic aggravates hot conditions. It is contraindicated in hot stomach and liver, hepatitis, pancreatitis, and hiatal hernia. To counteract the smell of garlic on the breath parsley leaf is taken (Fernie, 1914; and subsequent authors).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Rex Adams (collected case histories, medical research, 1–10, 14–16, 19, 21, 22, 25–27, 29–36, 38, 39, 41), Chalid Ottway (1, 9, 10, 13, 17, 18, 20, 23, 24, 28, 37, 40, 42, 44, 45), Julia Graves (10, 12, 47), Matthew Wood (12), Lydia Marie Child (11).

## Alnus glutinosa. European Alder.

European alder seems to have virtually the same properties as its cousin, the American tag alder (Alnus serrulata). The details are not as well developed in the clinical picture, but we see the same emphasis on tissue cleansing, glandular swelling, poor digestion, and dry, chronic skin disease. Today both species have been largely forgotten.
European or black alder, not to be mistaken for the American black alder (Ilex verticillata), is traditionally classified as a bitter and astringent. The bitters purge the gallbladder and intestines, but the tannins improve the tone, which is much desired in a purgative. “No part of the black alder is used in medicine except the inner rind; this is yellow, and is a good purge” (John Hill, 1740, 54). Bitter yellow substances frequently act on the gallbladder and intestines. “It is excellent in the jaundice. In Yorkshire they bruise the bark with vinegar, and use it outwardly for the itch.”
“A decoction of the bark is useful to bathe swellings and inflammations, especially of the throat, and has been known to cure ague,” writes Maude Grieve (1931, 18). Malaria, or intermittent fever (ague), is frequently associated with the gallbladder and with bitter and astringent tonics. “Peasants on the Alps are reported to be frequently cured of rheumatism by being covered with bags full of the heated leaves.”
“It is a tree which has a gloomy history in many sagas and stories, not least in Goethe’s Erlekonig describing the Alder-king with his terrible daughters, waiting to snatch up innocent children” (Law, 1973, 26).
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
RESPIRATION
Rhinitis.
Sore throat, pharyngitis.
Enlarged glands.
DIGESTION
Aphthous ulcers (canker sores).
Stomach debility; lack of gastric secretion; with indigestion.
Inactive colon, with constipation, or diarrhea.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Gallbladder congestion, jaundice.
KIDNEYS
Edema (leaf tea).
FEMALE
Leucorrhea (douche).
Mastitis (leaf poultice).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Rheumatism (external).
SKIN AND WOUNDS
Skin conditions; pustular, scaly, chronic; eruptions, acne, eczema, furunculosis, impetigo, scruffy scalp.
Passive hemorrhage (cones).
Bruises, burns (leaf poultice).
Gangrene (leaf poultice).
Inflammation (buds).

### PREPARATION, CAUTIONS, AND DOSAGE:

The inner bark of the alder is so bitter that it is hard to avoid vomiting if moderate to large doses are used, according to Donald Law (1973, 26). He recommends not using it internally and records an odd factoid: “Once used by witches to induce trances.” Decoction for purgation: “boil an ounce of it in a quart of water, and throw in at least two drams of ginger and some caraway seeds; let the patient proportion the quantity to his strength” (John Hill).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Maude Grieve (11), Donald Law (8, 14), Christopher Menzies-Trull, John Hill (6, 7, 12).

## Alpinia officinarum. Galangal.

Galangal is a product of the Spice Islands, long used in Western medicine and herbalism. It is a warming stimulant, sort of a cross between ginger and cayenne. “Galangal is a stimulating aromatic and has been successfully employed to aid the digestive process, preventing fermentation and removing flatus. It will be found especially useful in some forms of dyspepsia, preventing vomiting or sickness of the stomach, and facilitating digestion. It may be used in all cases in which a stimulating aromatic is indicated. It has some reputation as a remedy for perineal relaxation with hemorrhoids, and for a lax and pendulous abdomen” (King, 1898, II:906).

### TASTE:

warm, aromatic

### TISSUE STATES:

relaxation, depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Indigestion when there is fermentation and flatus.
Dyspepsia when there is vomiting or sickness at the stomach.
Hemorrhoids, with relaxation of the perineum.
Lax and pendulous abdomen.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Fluid extract, 5–10 drops (Fyfe, 1909).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, John King (1–4).

## Althaea officinalis. Marshmallow.

Marshmallow is native to salt marshes along the ocean in Europe, but is widely cultivated and naturalized in the New World. It can also grow along inland waterways and ditches. The root and leaves contain large amounts of mucilage and pectins, sugars, flavonoids, tannins, salt, and phenolic acids. Hollyhock (Althaea rosea), low mallow (Malva neglecta), and other mallows are used as substitutes or cognates.
The flowers, leaves, and roots of marshmallow and many of its cousins have been used around the world as a source of a mucilage that is moistening, soothing, anti-inflammatory, and softening. It is the most anti-inflammatory of the common mucilages. It is salty and is therefore also diuretic and emollient. As an emollient it will soften and break up hard tissue (water follows salt). These properties give it a broad, general, nonspecific applicability as a palliative or curative in almost any hot, dry, hard condition. It would appear from the length of the account given by Nicholas Culpeper (1653) that it was one of his primary remedies and that he used it in a general way for a wide variety of conditions. He mentions saving his son from dysentery with marshmallow. It is very important as a palliative in such serious bowel diseases as Crohn’s disease and colitis, though it is not usually curative (Matthew Wood).
Although althaea has a broad, general application on the mucosa throughout the body, it has a specific relationship with the kidneys. This is not surprising for a salty mucilage. It has been called the “most diuretic of the mucilages” and “the most mucilaginous of the diuretics.” It has a soothing operation on the kidneys and bladder while at the same time increasing diuresis. The famous early-nineteenth-century London herbalist, Samuel Westcott Tilke, wrote, “I have given it successfully for gravel; and to persons of a costive habit of body, I believe nothing can surpass it. If it be taken every day for a month, in a decoction or infusion, it will, from its oily nature, entirely do away with the necessity of ‘forcing’ medicines [cathartics], which do more harm than good” (Webb, 1916, 232).
The job of the kidneys is to balance the water and solids. Some interesting symptoms related to this were described by two different students at a lecture I gave. One reported that marshmallow was beneficial for dried out people who lacked thirst, while the other said it was good for people with an excessive thirst and copious urination. We often see exact opposites treated by a single herbal medicine because these natural remedies are normalizers that reestablish balance rather than drugs, which force the body.
A good specific indication for althaea is a tongue that is red and dry, with a glazed, shining surface, sometimes with horizontal breaks or cuts in the glaze. This indicates dryness, hardening, and heat (Matthew Wood).
Althaea has long been used externally. Galen considered it particularly effective for preventing the formation of pus. He recommended making a cataplasm by cooking althaea roots in water, adding barley flour, and cooking for a long time (Ad Glauconem II). One student recommended from experience the use of a wash of marshmallow on dry eczema and psoriasis. It also has a lubricating effect on the knees and joints, and on stiff muscles.
Berkeley herbalist Pam Fisher, who has worked with many cancer patients and survivors, says that “marshmallow is going to be your best friend” during chemotherapy. People will fare through chemo “magically.” Drink 1/2 gallon of a cold extraction of the root per day. It is, in my experience, nearly a specific for smokers, or recent smokers, who have dried out, overheated membranes.
David Dalton (2006, 60) gives an extensive account of Althaea officinalis as a flower essence. He considers it to be a remedy for hardening of the personality, inflexibility, hardheartedness, intolerance, and inability to feel one’s emotions. He looks upon it as a heart remedy that helps keep the heart in contact with and regulating the lower chakras and strong emotions. He mentions it also for hardening of the arteries. I have noticed that marshmallow will sometimes bring down high blood pressure.

### TASTE:

(root) salty, sweet • cool • mucilaginous

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy and excitation dependent on lack of moisture
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Inflexible, narrow-minded, emotionally rigid or unfeeling.
Hardhearted, insensitive, unsympathetic.
HEAD
Eyes, sore, inflamed (external).
MUCOSA
Lips red, dry, sometimes with a slight white powder around them.
Tongue red, dry, glazed, burnished, horizontal cracks; with dry coating.
Irritated, inflamed, hot, dry mucosa.
Sore throat.
LYMPHATICS
Swollen, hard, indurated glands (external).
RESPIRATION
Dry cough in the throat or upper bronchus; in children; worse at night (syrup).
Congestion, catarrh, hardened mucus.
Bronchitis and asthma.
Whooping cough.
Emphysema.
DIGESTION
Soothing and anti-inflammatory to the digestive tract; acid stomach, burning (capsules).
Overacidity, ulcers, gastric, duodenal, mucus colitis (roots).
Low digestive enzyme production (leaves) with gastric acidity (leaves and roots).
Diarrhea, dysentery, Crohn’s disease (palliative), colitis (capsules, tablets).
Constipation.
Hemorrhoids (external).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Painful, scanty urination; soothes the passage of stones.
Cystitis, strangury, hematuria.
Gravel.
FEMALE
Lactation: nipples, sore (external).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis in knees; with dry mucosa, tongue red, dry, latitudinal cracks (cured).
SKIN
Sores, ulcers, burns, boils, and inflamed skin (external).
Skin red, dry, with white powder exfoliating.
OTHER
High blood pressure with water retention.
Diabetes.
Chemotherapy.
Thirst with copious urination or no thirst with systemic dryness.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The flowers, leaves, and roots are used somewhat interchangeably. However, “marshmallow root has been used primarily in the digestive system, and leaf in the urinary system and lungs” (Hoffmann, 2003, 526). The leaves are harvested just before flowering to enhance the mucilaginous extraction (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994). Mucilage extracts poorly in alcohol, so it is often used in capsules or tablets. The root is best cold extracted in water (Michael Moore). The root and leaf can be used in a tincture, as a specific remedy, rather than as a general mucilage.

### CAUTIONS:

None known (German E Commission; Bisset and Wichtl, 1994).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, David Dalton (1, 2), Nicholas Culpeper (18), Margi Flint (4), Dorothy Hall (15, 16), William LeSassier (16), Samuel Westcott Tilke (18, 22), John William Fyfe (14, 15, 17, 20, 21), Matthew Wood (5, 6, 24, 26, 27), A. W. and L. R. Priest (19), Louise Tenney (3, 7–13, 17, 18, 21–23, 25, 28), Pam Fisher (29).

## Amygdalus communis var. amara. Sweet Almond.

Almond is a member of the rose family, closely related to plum, peach, and apricot. It is native to the warmer regions of Asia Minor and North Africa, but cultivated throughout the Mediterranean and in similar climates around the world. The sight of almond trees blossoming in the orchards, fields, and hillsides of a Mediterranean countryside in February is a sight to behold.
There are two very different almonds, the sweet and the bitter. They are considered to be varieties of the same species, but they differ greatly in culinary and medicinal value. The kernel of the sweet almond is edible but bitter almond contains high levels of a substance (amygdalin) that breaks down into cyanide, making it toxic. For this reason, bitter almond has not received treatment here. Both contain about fifty percent of a fixed oil that is pressed and used in cooking, skin care, and medicine—almond oil. The cyanide is easily removed from the oil of the bitter kernel. The long history and storied names of those who have used almond testify that it is as powerful a remedy as it is commonplace. Sweet almond is principally used in the form of almond oil, almond paste, or simply as a food. It is lubricating, nourishing, and cooling, thus reducing dryness and heat. This sets it in a category similar to its cousin Prunus persica (peach), except that almond is more moistening and less cooling.
Galen recommended almond. Abu Merwan Ibn Zuhr observed that it protects the moisture of the organs, including the brain. He recommended that the spine be frequently massaged with lukewarm almond oil to prevent problems in old age. Almonds also specifically strengthen the stomach and liver and “are among the most salutary of fruits.” These statements are quoted with approval by Moses Maimonides, who in addition recommended almonds prepared with rose leaves (Maimonides; Rosner, 1989, III:306, 347, 349). This increases the cooling properties; the combination of cooling, astringing, and nourishing powers that result would make a perfect instrument for the palliation of tuberculosis, which is how it was sometimes used. Almond continued to be esteemed in Arabic medicine; a modern account is given by Chalid Ottway (1933).
