Hildegard von Bingen (1994, 113) confirms the use for the brain, giving some peculiar details. “One whose brain is empty, and whose face has a bad color from a pain in the head, should frequently eat the inmost kernels of this fruit. They fill his brain and give him the correct color. Also, one who ails in his lungs.”
Almond was especially associated with the treatment of pleurisy, a dry condition of the lining of the lungs. Gerard and Culpeper repeat this application. Dr. Thomas Sydenham (1847, 63) gives great detail about the cases of cough in which almond is remedial. “It often happens that the patient is harassed by a troublesome cough during the whole course” of a lengthy fever. The cough is dry at first, but “afterwards brings up thickened sputa, the expectoration whereof is difficult, inasmuch as the fever has taken its time to bake it hard, and to parch it dry. And now the patient fears that he will be suffocated, for his strength is gone, and the tough and viscid phlegm is more than he can hawk up. In cases like these I rarely use anything beyond the oil of sweet almonds, newly drawn; except when it happens, as it sometimes does, that the patient has an aversion to it.” The dosage should be less than a spoonful, as “the stomach is already weak enough, and sufficiently inclined to vomit.” It is interesting that peach is used to settle nausea and vomiting. In addition to cooling and lubricating the lungs almond “will act as a bland nutriment, and restore, in some degree, the impaired powers of the patient.”
John Hill (1740, 54) emphasizes the use of small quantities of almond emulsion to treat “people in consumptions and hectics.” That would be tuberculosis or conditions where there is weakness and emaciation associated with a fever that comes on in the afternoons and leaves the person weak and debilitated.
“The sweet almond oil deserves one of the first places among the oils in the apotheca,” writes Father Sebastian Kneipp (1898, 135). “It operates … in a softening cooling, and dissolving manner. It dissolves phlegm in the windpipe, or in the stomach, and in the latter case it restores appetite and digestion. In inflammations, especially in the dreaded inflammation of the lungs, it cools.”
Maurice Mességué (1979, 22) emphasizes the effect of sweet almond oil on the digestive mucosa, though he cautions, as does Sydenham, that it is difficult for those with a weak stomach. It was the worm remedy of choice during his childhood and is somewhat purgative. It soothes the urinary tract (compare with its cousin, peach for worms and cystitis) and is effective when used externally on burns, irritations, itching, erysipelas, and superficial inflammation of the skin. Mességué describes almond somewhat more as a cooling agent than a nourishing or lubricating one. He combines it with violet leaves for the lungs—as Sydenham did on occasion.
Dr. Edward Shook (1978, 136) recommends an emulsion of the kernels (see below) for delicate children, wasting, aging, and those disposed to tuberculosis and diabetes. “We did not exaggerate when we said that sweet almond was one of the greatest builders of brain, bone, and flesh that is available to us.” Compare this with the statement of Ibn Zuhr, above. The testimony of the ages weaves a clear pattern upon which to base the use of this classical remedy.
The references to almond’s use as a tonic to the brain and stomach perhaps explain the following comment by Dr. Fernie (1914, 20): “Persons who can readily digest these products [almonds] are believed to derive from them a quickening of the intellect in its magnetism, and in keenness, or argumentative force.”

### TASTE:

sweet, nutty • moist, cool • oily

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, excitation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION
Thin, delicate constitutions and children; wasting and aging.
Brain, spine, and marrow tonic.
RESPIRATION
Humming in the ears, pain in the ears, cramps in the ears, obdurate ear wax (6–8 drops of the oil in the ear, stopped with cotton).
Deafness due to obstruction of the middle ear (almond oil, 2–8 drops in ear, 4x/week).
Pleurisy.
Dried out mucus in the lungs, with weakness, making expectoration difficult.
Stubborn cough.
Asthma.
DIGESTION
Inability to bear much food.
Constipation from dry mucosa.
Worms (oil or ash of the roots in olive oil).
Diabetes mellitus type II, as a food source without sugar.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Bladder; burning sensation.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Creakiness of the joints (external).
SKIN
Burns, skin irritations, itching, erysipelas, dry, scaly skin.
Ringworm, eczema, itch, skin irritation.
Burns (ash of the roots).
Swellings, with great heat, inflammation, piercing pain.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Fresh almonds are blanched (skin removed) to limit irritation of the stomach in some people. They are then pulverized with a moderate quantity of water to form an emulsion “of elegant flavor and superior demulcent properties” (Cook, 1869, 241). This ancient recipe was modified by Edward Shook: 1 ounce almond meal, 1 ounce sugar, 1 dram gum acacia, combine and stir slowly into a mixture with 1 ounce glycerin and 2 ounces water, then beat into a paste. Almond oil is drawn off commercially. It can be made into an emulsion with egg yolk, sugar, and water for a nourishing pulmonary remedy. It is also used as a base for massage lotion.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Ibn Zuhr (1, 2), Sebastian Kneipp (3, 4, 5, 14, 18), Maude Grieve, Thomas Sydenham (5, 6), Edward Shook (1, 9, 12), Maurice Mességué (9, 11, 13, 15, 16), Chalid Ottway (2, 4, 7, 8, 11, 13, 16, 17).

## Amygdalus persica. Peach.

See Prunus persica.

## Anagallis arvensis. Scarlet Pimpernel.

This attractive little plant is a European member of the primula family. It is not commonly used in herbalism at the present time, but a few people still use it, and it possesses many intriguing qualities and constituents, making a longer account excusable.
Pimpernel thrives in my garden, but in the cold north it does not hop the dangerous boundary into the wild regions beyond. The dainty flower is most attractive and mysterious, a tiny fleck of salmon growing in a sea of green stems and leaves. The flower closes at once if rain threatens or falls. This earned it the name “poor man’s weather glass” or “Venus’ weather glass.”
Poor men and poor Venuses have to work in the sun, so they are prone to freckles and roughness of the skin—for which this plant was used. “The face or skin being washed therewith, it is good to cleanse it from its roughness, ill color, and other deformities thereof, and to make it soft, smooth, and clear” (Salmon, 1710, 865). That sounds Venusian indeed. I once asked a Southern girl why freckles were so undesirable and she explained, “Nobody wanted to have freckles or a sun tan in the old days because it meant you worked in the fields.” Not a fit activity for Venus.
At one time scarlet pimpernel was held in high esteem. It was considered especially active for melancholy, mental diseases, and epilepsy. Among its traditional uses are gout, rheumatism, edema, blockage of the liver, spleen, and kidneys. It is a diaphoretic used to bring on sweat and eliminate fever.
Grieve (1931) relates an old rhyme, “No heart can think, no tongue can tell / The virtues of the Pimpernel.” Today the virtue of pimpernel has been almost completely forgotten—no tongue told, I guess. This is despite the fact that the plant possesses very active constituents. It contains saponins and has a soapy, acrid taste. Looking over accounts from the old literature one might guess that modern writers were embarrassed by the enthusiasm of their predecessors. In order to render an account of the medicinal properties of pimpernel we need to return to rather old sources.
Galen classified pimpernel as hot and dry in the first degree, meaning that it opens the pores to drive out sweat but tones them and strengthens resistance to disease. This corresponds closely to the actions for which it was most valued in the old days: sweating to drive out fever and normalizing the tone of the skin. It was used for drying up running ulcers and wounds and also for dry conditions where the fluids had been driven out and the tissues were hot and irritable. The relationship with water is reinforced by the way the flower closes in wet weather, as if to protect itself against a surfeit of water. Galen noted that pimpernel had an “attractive” faculty, drawing out thorns and splinters.
Pimpernel continued to be used during the Renaissance. “The whole plant is used; and the best method of giving it, is in an infusion, made by pouring boiling water upon it fresh gathered: This is an excellent drink in fevers; it promotes sweat, and throws out the small-pox, measles, or any other eruptions” (John Hill, 1740, 298).
William Salmon (1710) recounts that, in addition to expelling the toxin of fever to the surface, pimpernel “cures the bitings of serpents, as vipers, rattle snakes, mad-dogs, and other venomous creatures: is good against the strangury, stone, and gravel, and heals all wounds and ulcers, whether inward or outward; stops the flux of humors into any part, eases pains in the ears, and the piles, and prevails against an ophthalmy, and other diseases of the eyes.” Unlike most British herbalists, Salmon had lived in South Carolina where rattlesnakes, cottonmouths, copperheads, and all manner of poisonous snakes abound, so I am inclined to consider his testimony.
The tincture made in vinegar is Salmon’s preparation for the plague, pestilential fevers, and contagious diseases, “extinguishing almost in a moment the preternatural heat. It quenches thirst, restores a lost appetite, causes good digestion, opens all manner of obstructions of the viscera, but chiefly of the lungs, spleen, and reins, and is good against the bitings of mad dogs, and other venomous creatures. Dose: so many drops as will make the vehicle pleasantly sharp, and to be often repeated in the day time.” The ointment or balsam applied “to a pestilential bubo [plague sore], it ripens and breaks it.”
Anagallis was given a homeopathic proving that reproduced many of the symptoms for which it was traditionally used in herbalism. Despite this, it did not retain a footing in either herbalism or homeopathy. The proving did reproduce the “hilarity” with which the plant has been associated since ancient times. The name Anagallis is from the Greek meaning “to laugh out loud.” This led to its use in depression. In parts of England it is still known as “Laughter Bringer.” It is necessary when laughter is absent. “Will calm rages in adults and children, if they can be induced to drink it” (Juliette de Bairacli Levy, 1974, 111).
One of the few contemporary practitioners to use scarlet pimpernel is Eliot Cowan. It is one of his favorite plants for imbalances of the “fire element.” He journeyed to meet the plant spirit, who revealed that pimpernel is for opening the heart after it had been closed due to experiences of emotional cold and heartlessness. It is for gruff people who hide their heart, and for learning to laugh with abandon and cheer. He associates it with the heart protector organ system (Cowan, 1995, 123).

### TASTE:

acrid, soapy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Depression and despondency.
Rage.
HEAD
Convulsions, fainting, swooning, fits, “vapors.”
Migraine, headache; inveterate.
Vertigo, lethargy, apoplexy, epilepsy, nightmares.
Eyes; cataracts; weeping.
Toothache; “dropt into the ear on the contrary side.”
Roughness of the skin of the face, discoloration, and freckles.
RESPIRATION
Nose; cold, moist, and pituitous discharges.
Ulcers of the lungs.
DIGESTION
Hemorrhoids; painful (compress).
Jaundice.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Urinary tract; strangury, pain, heat, stoppage, edema, stone, gravel.
Ulcers of the kidneys and bladder.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Gout pain.
SKIN AND WOUNDS
Contusion, swelling (external).
Bites; serpents, scorpions, hornets, venomous creatures (apply fresh plant).
Uncomplicated fresh wounds; draws together the lips and heals them rapidly.
Ulcers; old, fretting or running, and filthy; from poisonous bites; cleanses and incarnates new flesh.
Poison ivy.
FEVER
Plague or pestilential fevers; brings on a heavy sweat (the decoction, as hot as the patient can bear).
Plague sores or buboes; “ripens and breaks.”

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

William Salmon (1710) states that the plant is best given fresh. John Hill (1740, 298) makes the same point. “The dried leaves may be given in powder or a tea made of the whole dried plant, but nothing is so well as the infusion of it fresh; those who have not seen it tried this way do not know how valuable a medicine it is.” Thomas Green (1824) reiterates this point. Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1974, 112) has a special way to make the tea that maintains its freshness. “Macerate the whole plant and steep in cold water in sunlight for several hours a handful of the herb to a small cup of water. Take a tablespoon three times daily.” The tincture is made from 10 ounces of fresh leaves and flowers in a pint of medium proof alcohol. Dose is 1–5 drops (Grieve, 1931, 634).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Maude Grieve, William Salmon (1, 3–11, 13–20, 22), John Hill (20), Eliot Cowan, John Henry Clarke (homeopathic provings confirm most of the traditional indications), Juliette de Bairacli Levy (2, 12, 13, 16, 17, 21).

## Anethum graveolens. Dill.

Dill is a member of the apiaceae family native to Europe. It is similar to other mild carminatives such as fennel, caraway, and anise. It contains flavonoids, coumarins, fixed oils, proteins, phenolic acids, triterpenes, and volatile oils. It is traditionally used in Europe for colic and gas. Because it is so mild it is especially favored for children. It also is used to stimulate lactation. These uses are also found under its cousin, fennel. A more unusual use is mentioned by W. T. Fernie (1914, 145). “The weird gipsy, Meg Merrlies, crooned a cradle song” at the birth of an infant to protect it against witches: “Trefoil, vervain, John’s wort, Dill / Hinder witches of their will.”

## Angelica archangelica, A. atropurpurea. Angelica.

Angelica archangelica is one of the grand traditional remedies of northern Europe. It grows along rivers and on the shore of the Baltic regions and the Scandinavian states, south into Alpine areas. It is an important shamanic plant among the Lapps or Saami of the Far North. It can be grown in gardens in North America and is naturalized in some places. It contains volatile oils (warming, cooling, antiseptic), resin, wax, bitters, furanocoumarins, flavonoids, sugars, organic acids, and phytosterols.
A related species, Angelica atropurpurea, of eastern North America, can be used instead of the officinal archangelica. “Its uses are very similar to the European” (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994, 71), but it is not accepted in herbal commerce and there is testimony that the fresh root is toxic. I use the American species in my own practice and find it commensurate with the European.
These angelicas are also similar in properties to the angelicas of China. They open the periphery (induce perspiration) and regulate menstruation like their Eastern relatives.
The root of the angelica is brown, furry, oily, and pungent—the picture of a “bear medicine” in American Indian medicine. Bear eats such roots in the spring to wake up and start rebuilding his mass. Other members of this group include osha, lomatium, American liquorice, balsam root, and spikenard. They stimulate the cortisol side of the adrenal cortex, to increase appetite, digestion, and nutrition. Hence they are suited to thin, pale, undernourished people. However, they are sometimes suited to large, stout, strong, bear people. In European phytotherapy it is said that angelica calms both the sympathetic and the parasympathetic sides of the autonomic nervous system. When the sympathetic is overactive it creates a condition of nervousness and shuts down the digestion, creating a condition of “asthenia”—the pale, thin, dry, vata type. When the parasympathetic is dominant we have a condition of gastrointestinal excess, creating a hot digestion with a heavy, damp, cool interior. Because angelica affects the circulation, the thin types may have purple fingers from poor peripheral circulation, while the thick ones may have blue-green-yellow-gray coloration around their veins (look at the forearms) due to blood stagnation.
Angelica root is pungent, bitter, sweet, oily, warm, and stimulating. Hence, it has long been used in European herbalism and medicine as a carminative, to warm and stimulate the digestion. The bitters and sugars get the appetite and secretions going, while the spicy compounds stimulate circulation to the stomach and periphery. The oil stimulates the “fat of the kidneys,” the adrenal cortex, releasing the cortisol, which supports digestion and raises blood sugar levels. The circulation of the blood is opened to the periphery to bring nutrition to all parts of the body, to stimulate fluid movement in the possibly dried out joints, to rebuild the fatty deposits insulating the nerves, and to improve circulation to the hands, feet, and uterus. Thus, angelica is an excellent remedy for an undernourished “asthenic” (Rudolf Weiss).
Looking at the root from a different angle, it is light and airy. In fact, it contains pockets of air. It grows in damp conditions. Thus, it aerates or brings air into the watery realm, as it is expressed by anthroposophical writers (Husemann and Wolff). It makes fluids more active and breaks up excessive concentrations of water, phlegm, and blood. It promotes peripheral circulation, and opens the lungs and skin. Hence it is beneficial for heavy, stout, blood-stagnant people—bear people—who need their relaxed, watery side activated.
The bear medicines, including angelica, are used “on the rocks” in the American Indian sweat lodge. This serves both a spiritual and physical purpose, as the vapors open the imagination and mind, as mentioned below, while at the same time helping to open the skin and bring the circulation to the surface. The sweat lodge teaches the value of perspiration. When the skin is functioning in a healthy manner, the inner pores are also open and water is able to move, with oxygen, food, and wastes, through the body like a fine vapor. Angelica fulfills this function, opening the sweat pores to release perspiration and feeding the oil of the body to support the sebaceous glands. Hence it is remedial in chill from dampness and cold. It was a traditional remedy for contagious diseases.
The lungs are closely allied with the skin, and here angelica also shows its stuff. It is indicated in old bronchitis cases where there is exhaustion and the mucus is thin and difficult to expectorate. It drys and warms the lungs. It improves circulation into the lungs, thus aeration through the blood. As the phlegm improves in texture and the blood comes in, the mucosa secrete new immune cells and enzymes, which renew the defenses of the respiratory tract.
Angelica acts on the lymphatics, as it does on the vasculature. “A most impressive angelica therapy is the treatment of swellings, inflammations, and glandular indurations, particularly of the head and neck region, and above all in children with an adenoidal constitution” (Husemann and Wolff, 1982, 340). This is the “scrofulous” constitution of old-time medicine.
Through its action on the blood angelica also assists the liver. It improves circulation through the portal vein and the hepatic artery, bringing in more food/toxins for metabolism and protecting the hepatic cells against injury. Thus, it is useful in wasting or “asthenia of hepatic origin, hepatic insufficiency (hemorrhoids, constipation), insufficient immunity of hepatic origin, viral hepatitis” (Kenner and Requena, 2001, 107).
Again, through the circulation and liver angelica influences the uterus. Although European angelica is not traditionally used as a female remedy, and is not considered to have estrogenic compounds, like the Chinese angelica, or tang kwei (Angelica sinensis), in fact it does act on the female organism. I have used both Angelica archangelica and the native American Angelica atropurpurea on the indications for tang kwei with success. By warming and stirring up the blood the Western angelicas relieve blood congestion and cramping in the uterus. Stephen Buhner has even developed a profile for women in whom the Western angelicas are most efficient. He notes that it is particularly beneficial in women in their late twenties who are trying to contend with role-model issues that have blocked their self-development.
Through stimulation of the circulation angelica acts on the brain, head, joints, and extremities. Thus it has been used for syncope, vertigo, nervousness, and fatigue (Kenner and Requena). The increased blood flow to the joints probably helps move the waters into the cartilage, which is not directly sanguified. Angelica probably also helps through the oils, which contribute to the building of cartilage and nerve sheaths. And it helps the skin, as we have seen.
My friend Julia Graves argues that I should include it as an alterative, partly on traditional grounds, partly because it contains bitters, and partly because it cleanses so many areas of the body. So, Julia, it is done.
Angelica is used to stop smoking and alcohol use. “The consumption of the tea temporarily changes the taste,” explains Australian herbalist Harald Tietze (1996, 38). “Alcoholism and smoking is often reduced due to the lack of taste. Angelica calms the nerves and helps in moments of craving.” Smokers can also chew on the seeds. “Said to cause a disgust for spirituous liquors” (W. T. Fernie, 1914).
Just as the bear goes into hibernation through the winter, bear medicines usually relax the mind, open the imagination, and bring people into dreamtime. Angelica certainly has this capacity. The most active way to bring on this effect is to burn the root and smell the fumes. They are gently relaxing to the mind and body, while activating the imagination. Angelica is a shamanic medicine among the Saami or Laplanders. Julia Graves says, “Angelica aligns you to walk with your guardian angel.” Harald Tietze (1996, 39) says, “If you have space for only one plant in the garden, plant the Angelica.”

### TASTE:

(seed, root) pungent, sweet, bitter, salty • warm • oily, stimulating, diffusive

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Thin, dry, pallid persons with poor digestion, gas, bloating, but tendencies to accumulation of fluids; poor peripheral circulation, cold, purple hands and feet.
Thick women with blood stagnation.
Complexion around veins yellow/gray/blue/green.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Emotionally empty, hollow; stimulates the imagination and relaxes the mind.
Cut off from spiritual world; “I can’t believe, I can’t pray”; life no longer makes sense, depression.
Mental dullness, exhaustion, and obtuseness, sometimes with phlegm congestion.
Intense mental acuity and focus; relaxes the mind.
Anguish, spasms, nervous excitation, and fatigue; calms excess in both the sympathetic and parasympathetic branches of the autonomic.
HEAD
Headache (from stress), migraine, vertigo, fainting.
RESPIRATION
Catarrhal deafness (ear-drops).
Influenza, chills.
Lung congestion, dullness of the mind, pallid complexion.
Catarrh of the elderly and feeble.
Seasonal allergic asthma.
DIGESTION
Lack of appetite; anorexia nervosa (decoction to produce an aromatic bitter).
Gastritis, dyspepsia, stomach pain, heartburn, sour stomach, hyperacidity, nausea, vomiting (infusion for excess activity, pain).
Intestinal gas, colic, diarrhea (tincture for cramping).
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Liver insufficiency; increases digestion and metabolism of oil and production of bile, secretion of bile, hence digestion and nutrition; elevates blood sugar.
Viral hepatitis.
Asthenia, anemia, wasting; deficient immunity associated with hepatic insufficiency.
CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM AND BLOOD
Strengthens the heart; poor peripheral circulation; cold, pale, sometimes purple hands and feet.
High and low blood pressure.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Increases urination.
FEMALE
Menses; excessive bleeding, cramping, or amenorrhea with weakness.
Middle-aged women, full-figured, with blood stagnation, veins blue/green/yellow and sometimes gray; with cysts, excess bleeding.
MALE
Prostate problems.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis, gout; joints stiff, extremities cold; fingers purple.
Muscular cramps, spasms, nerve problems, epilepsy.
SKIN AND WOUNDS
Bruises, blue/yellow/green/gray.
FEVER
Intermittent fever; chills and fever.
OTHER
Angelica root worn around the neck for protection against psychic and epidemic contagion in many traditional societies.
Helps stop craving for tobacco and alcohol.
Lipoma (angelica oil externally; cf. Salvia, Stellaria).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The roots are hard to dry and easily turn rancid. They should be cut lengthwise, rapidly dried in a heated stove, crushed, and stored airtight. Under these circumstances they last 3–4 years. Tea of the root is made from 1 teaspoonful macerated overnight in 1 cup of water. In the morning it is warmed up. One-third is taken as a dose before every meal. The seeds easily lose their properties; freezing preserves them and promotes germination. The leaves and the stalks are of less potency (Harald Tietze, 1996, 38). The constituents have varying solubility, so they are brought out by different preparation methods. Boiling the root produces an aromatic bitter, steeping produces an anesthetic and astringent for the stomach lining, alcohol improves the relaxing, antispasmodic properties (Michael Moore). Extract the recently dried root in high percentage alcohol. Dose: 1–3 drops, 1–3x/day, or more in acute conditions (Matthew Wood).

### CAUTIONS:

Not to be taken during pregnancy. It can irritate the stomach and kidneys. The furanocoumarins can cause photosensitivity and can irritate the skin in sensitive individuals. They are not easily extracted in water. Sunbathing should be limited when angelica is in use (German E Commission; Bisset and Wichtl, 1994). Small doses are relaxing. Excessive doses can cause depression of the central nervous system.

### TOXICITY:

Talalaj and Czechowicz (1989, 46) report of Angelica atropurpurea that the “fresh root is exceedingly toxic, and it is used as a homicidal poison among Canadian Indians.” I was loath to credit this as possible, yet Horton Howard (1836, II:216) writes of the Angelica atropurpurea specifically, “The fresh root is said to be poisonous, which is dissipated in drying.” There is, of course, a terrific poison in Canada and the northern United States, Cicuta virosa, that looks somewhat like its cousin Angelica.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (31), Nicholas Culpeper, Rudolf Weiss (1), Stephen Buhner (4), Julia Graves (1, 5), Ellen Evert Hopman (1), David Winston (1), J. V. Cerney (9), Matthew Wood (1-confirmed, 2, 3, 6, 7, 20, 26, 29), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (8, 9, 12, 14, 15, 16, 19, 20, 28, 30), W. T. Fernie (13, 16, 17, 32), Harald Tietze (32), Michael Moore (15, 16, 17).

## Anthemis nobilis. Chamomile.

Considered to have the same properties as Chamomilla recutita.

## Aphanes arvensis. Parsley Piert.

This small plant is little known or used in North American herbalism but commands respect in Great Britain, where it is native. It is a member of the rose family.
Maude Grieve (1931, 615) writes, “This species is still in high repute with herbalists, and has been used for many centuries for its action on stone in the bladder, on account of which it was given the name of ‘Parsley Breakstone’ and ‘Parsley Piercestone,’ which has been corrupted into Parsley Piert, the ‘parsley’ referring to the form of its cut-into leaves, not to any relationship to the true Parsley.” It is akin to lady’s mantle, hence the names Alchemilla arvensis and field lady’s mantle. “Its chief employment is in gravel, stone, dropsy, and generally for complaints of the bladder and kidneys. Acting directly on the parts affected, it is found very valuable, even in apparently incurable cases. It operates violently, but safely, by urine and also removes obstructions of the liver, being therefore useful in jaundice.” Culpeper (1653) writes, “It will bring away gravel from the kidneys insensibly and without pain. It cures strangury.” Derby herbalist George Slack (1919, 115) writes that he “has cured many bad cases of gravel and stone with this and pellitory of the wall.” It is classified as a demulcent, astringent, and diuretic. The whole herb is used fresh or dried. It is often combined with urinary stimulants or mucilages for better effect.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

“The whole plant is used in infusion of an ounce of the dried herb or two ounces of the green.” 1–2 wineglassesful, 3x/day (Neil, 1998, 75).

## Apium graveolens. Celery Seed.

The seed of the wild celery, native to wet, salty ground near the sea in southern Europe, is a powerful medicinal agent. It is a member of the apiaceae or parsley family. In addition to high levels of sodium, it contains calcium, essential oils (many and complex, but especially the cooling limonene and the diuretic apiol), essential fatty acids, phenols, flavonoids, and furanocoumarins.
Celery seed is generally looked upon as a cooling and sedative diuretic. The apiol and sodium stimulate the kidneys. The improvement in kidney function, though due to stimulation (warming), is overall cooling to the body. It removes irritable deposits of calcium and uric acid in the body. Christopher Menzies-Trull (2003, 446) classifies it as a “uric acid solvent diuretic.”
Celery is indicated for arthritis, inflammatory heat, dryness, aches, pains, and irritability of the tissues. It also probably helps the cartilage in the joints. Like many salty emollients it penetrates into calcified or hard deposits, breaks them up, brings material back into the bloodstream and out through the kidneys, while increasing the flow of water through cartilage and joint, which is essential for their cleansing and feeding. Celery also contains furanocoumarins that thin the blood and improve the circulation. That would help the hard-to-reach connective tissue, which lies just outside the periphery of the circulatory system—cartilage is not directly sanguinated. Celery has been found to lower blood pressure like medical diuretics and perhaps adjusts sodium imbalances that influence angiotensin levels. It is overall cooling, thinning, cleansing, diuretic, and antiseptic.
The primary use of celery seed (and even the stalks) is to remove arthritis, gout, and rheumatic complaints. One author went so far as to write that a diet of celery made “rheumatism impossible” (Gibson Ward, quoted by W. T. Fernie, 1914, 86). Dr. Jim Duke has confirmed, in modern herbalism, the use of celery seed on himself and others, in this regard.
Traditional herbal literature paints a picture of sympathetic excess and tension. Maude Grieve (1931, 182) writes that it is “useful in hysteria, promoting restfulness and sleep, and diffusing through the system a mild sustaining influence. Good combined with scutellaria for nervous cases with loss of tone.” It has been used in panic attacks. Dorothy Hall (1988) relates that celery seed is indicated in people with strong but conflicted will power. They are anxious for others, busy, conscientious, high paced, “hot and bothered.” William Boericke (1927, 64) gives a short mental profile: “feeling of fidgets.” Norman Grainger Bisset and Max Wichtl (1994, 82) report folk use for “nervous restlessness” and scientific classification as a sedative and antispasmodic. David Hoffmann (2003, 528) recommends it particularly for “rheumatoid arthritis associated with mental depression.”
Celery has been found to be antineoplastic in some circumstances. Like a number of remedies that have been used to remove tumors, celery seed is also abortifacient. This property is even found in the edible celery stalks, if large amounts are eaten.

### TASTE:

salty, slightly pungent, slightly bitter • cool, moist

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Restlessness, sleeplessness, nervousness; brain debilitated from overwork or excitement.
Anxiety, concern, guilt, irritability, conflicted will.
Hot and bothered.
Idleness is unpleasant; depression, melancholy.
Busy, conscientious, overpaced.
Emotional blackmailers.
HEAD
Headache, throbbing; neuralgia.
RESPIRATION
Eyes hot, burning, dry; hay feverish look; strengthens the eyesight.
Colds, influenza, bronchitis, asthma; ticklish dry cough.
Chronic respiratory insufficiency; asthma, emphysema.
DIGESTION
Lack of appetite, halitosis, flatulence, heartburn, hiccoughs, vomiting.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Hepatic disorders, jaundice, ascites.
Chronic weakness, assists detoxification.
CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
Hypertension.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Diabetics with water retention.
Urinary tract; inflammation, infection, bladder retention.
Kidney weakness; albuminuria, stones, edema.
Better from drinking and immersing in water.
Tissues atonic, water-logged, weight gain increasing with stress load.
Swelling and water retention worse from stress and concern.
FEMALE
Amenorrhea.
Pregnancy: heartburn and weight gain (small doses only).
MALE
Irritated prostate.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Aches and pains better from warm water, worse from cold water.
Arthritis, pain; chiefly around joints and large bone ends.
Rheumatoid arthritis, gout, sciatica, muscle spasm.
SKIN
Bruised leaves have been applied to the feet in panic attacks.
Eczema; itching, burning, blotches.
Cancers, tumors (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Tincture or extract of the seed. The root is also sometimes used. The boiled root produces a jelly. The vegetable can be eaten in large amounts for the medicinal effect but mark that it is abortifacient. Celery is potentiated by dandelion (Menzies-Trull).

### CAUTIONS:

Although everyone usually thinks of celery as safe, since both seed and stalk are eaten, there are a number of cautions. Some people are allergic to celery, experiencing shortness of breath and swelling of the face (Fernie, 1914). Also, the plant, like many members of the apiaceae family, is abortifacient. Even the vegetable, eaten in large quantities, can sometimes have this effect—I have known this to occur. The volatile oils work as an irritating diuretic on the kidneys (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994). Therefore, like juniper, celery is contraindicated in acute inflammation or any kind of heat involving the kidneys.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (15, 21), Maude Grieve (1, 23), Dorothy Hall (2–6, 8, 11, 20, 22, 23, 24, 25), James Duke (25), Rex Moore (27), Julia Graves (16), Christopher Menzies-Trull (1, 4, 7, 8, 9, 11, 12, 14, 17, 19, 21, 26, 28, 29), William Boericke (1, 4, 7, 9, 11, 28), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (10, 13, 17), W. T. Fernie (7, 25, 26).

## Arctium lappa. Burdock.

One would expect burdock to have a long history of use in medicine, but a review of traditional literature shows that it was little used until the eighteenth century, when it started its career as a diuretic and “blood purifier.” Some specific indications are found in the eclectic literature in the nineteenth century, but it was not widely used until the twentieth, when it finally appeared in many formulations—still as an alterative or blood purifier. As far as I know, the use of burdock as a specific, and the accumulation of a list of specific indications, have awaited my own hand. Burdock is a member of the Asteraceae family. It contains lignans, bitter priniciples, inulin (starch), mucilage, sugars, pectin, minerals including sulphur, and organic acids.
Burdock root is bitter, sweet, and oily. It is therefore well suited to dry/atrophic conditions where there is a need to increase secretions, tissue feeding, and tissue cleansing. The bitters stimulate secretion in the digestive tract. The oils increase secretion of bile, promoting better absorption of fats and oils through the small intestine, better activity of the gallbladder, and increased processing of oils by the liver. Burdock root is called for when there is poor secretion of bile, indicated by dry stool and constipation and poor emulsification of fats and oils. With low absorption of lipids there is a shortage of these substances around the body. Burdock is thus associated with dry, scaly skin conditions and dry skin in general. Sometimes the sebaceous glands get blocked, due to a lack of oil moving through them, resulting in inflammation—hence the association of burdock with acne and boils. The sinuses and lungs are also dry and the secretion through the kidneys is limited.
Burdock seed is more diuretic and diffusive than the root. It is particularly used when the skin and kidneys are involved. It is suited to “affection of the kidneys, such as atrophy or wasting away,” writes Richard Hool (1922, 17). This occurs with dry tissues, though there are local areas of water retention—edema. “When the water cannot move, some of it gets stuck,” comments Phyllis Light. “The virtue is diuretic,” proclaims John Hill (1740, 97), “and is very powerfully so. It has cured dropsies alone.” Samuel Henry (1814, 63) calls burdock root “diuretica mundificans.”
Henry also recommended burdock root highly in syphilis, a condition for which it was long used. “For the benefit of our poor gallant, but unfortunate seamen, &c. I shall reveal my celebrated antivenereal remedy, by the use of which I have cured numbers both in its recent and fourth stage, without the use of mercury.” Burdock root, one pound; yellow dock root half a pound; yarrow half a pound; parsley roots and leaves half a pound; comfrey four ounces. Boil in eight quarts of water, down to one gallon. “When it is to be carried to sea in warm weather” add gin. A gill to be taken 3x/day.
The liver is also affected by burdock. With a lack of fats and oils, there is reduced anabolism of lipids for the body to use, with wasting and dryness. In order to get nutrition there may be dependence on “quick fixes” of sugar. Thus, it is a remedy for sugar imbalances. Because steroid and sex hormones are made from oil and require oil for transmission through the body, and all hormones of any kind require adequate fluids for movement, burdock comes up as something of an endocrine and female remedy.
Burdock has a powerful action on the skin. This is attributed to its influence on the liver and blood. Here is a case history from Derby herbalist George Slack (1919, 107). “The author once cured a man who was suffering from boils and abscesses nearly all over his body with burdock and yarrow made into a strong decoction, and drunk freely. He had been ill five months, and was given up to die; but in one month he was restored.” Here is another case history from Lise Wolff, registered herbalist (AHG), of Minneapolis. A three-and-a-half-year-old girl was diagnosed with ichthyosis. The skin was flaking off all over the body in large patches and was dry, hot, and red. If she got hot the skin became red and peeled off. Fever turned the skin bright red. She would get cranky and irritable when it itched and couldn’t sleep at night. Healthy birth, no medications, no antibiotics. Chamomilla (for the attitude), 2 drops, Arctium radix, 2 drops, Salvia officinalis, 2 drops; 3x/day. Two months later the skin is slightly flaky, in fine, small flakes, the hands are still inflamed, sleeps fine, less irritable.
Dorothy Hall (1988) recommends burdock for conditions where the skin is broken open and weeping an exudate, but I would still maintain that this is in cases that are intrinsically dry—the weeping is due to water that can’t get out through the skin and kidneys in the healthy fashion.
Burdock, with its brown, fur-like burs, could be classified in American Indian medicine as a “bear medicine.” Indeed, such an application in ancient Eurasia is indicated by the name arctium, from the Greek arctos (bear). The oily bear medicines usually stimulate the gallbladder, liver, thyroid, and adrenocortical functions, encouraging the digestion and metabolism of fats and proteins. The metabolism of the bear, which swings greatly from winter hibernation to summer feeding, is dependent on these organs for storage and utilization of fats and proteins.
David Hoffmann (2003, 528) uses an arresting expression in his discussion of burdock. It will “move the body to a state of integration and health.” Burdock helps to restore the primal blueprint of health, so to speak, when it has been lost in persons suffering from long, chronic illness. It was thus once an important remedy for syphilis, a disease that attacks the basic integrative forces of life, the primal essence, or genetic material, and the hard and supportive structures of the body.

### TASTE:

(root) sweet, bitter, pungent • warm • oily (seed) bitter, pungent, sweet • warm • diffusive

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Heat, dryness, lack of oil.
Thin, withered persons with dry skin and scalp.
Emotionally stoic; phlegmatic about pain.
Tired, debilitated, worn out.
Worried.
HEAD
Dry scalp with red, scaly patches and hair loss.
Headache, frontal, with dry sinus.
RESPIRATION
Respiratory conditions with dry mucosa; allergies, sinusitis, bronchitis.
DIGESTION, LIVER, AND GALLBLADDER
Lack of secretion in the upper gastrointestinal tract (seed).
Lack of lubrication in the lower gastrointestinal tract; constipation (root).
Poor glandular function; lymphatics, pancreas, endocrine, prostate, liver, spleen.
Liver and gallbladder congestion; gallstones.
Blood sugar lability.
FEMALE
Uterine prolapse; with tired feet.
Pregnancy: balancing and nutritive during; helps prevent water retention and jaundice in babies.
MALE
Specific for swollen prostate in men who lift weights incorrectly.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Leaches out concretions, kidney stones, arthritis.
Edema.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis, bursitis; better from movement; worse when immobilized.
Lower part of spine stiff.
Sciatica; unbearable at night.
Gouty pain.
Better from movement, exercise, bending, open air; worse from inactivity, closed, warm room.
Carpal tunnel syndrome.
FEVER
Fever with excessive oily sweating and worry.
SKIN
Profuse underarm sweat.
Dry or oily skin, acne, eczema, dandruff; boils, abscesses, poison ivy, rashes, itching, measles, psoriasis (tincture internally, leaf externally).
Retention of water in persons who are generally dry; swelling in the upper thighs and across the belly and the upper eyelids, with bags under the eyes, absence of perspiration, the skin is trying to conserve water.
Pulse thin.
Boils, abscesses, carbuncles.
Single large pimples that are not coming to a head at the surface.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The root is used for chronic conditions, the seed (or fructs, actually) in acute. A biennial, the roots are dug at the end of the first year. The seeds are harvested just after the end of the second year, before the plant is completely dead. The leaves are sometimes used externally as a poultice. The roots, high in oils, easily go rancid and need to be dried with artificial heat and left in the open air. They extract well in alcohol. Brandy brings out the nice bittersweet flavor. Dose: 3–60 drops, 1–3x/day. The dried root and seeds may be used by decoction as a tea. One teaspoon of ground root to 1 cup of boiling water.

### CAUTIONS:

Widely used as a food. May cause allergic reactions in a few people allergic to asteraceae plants. Has caused skin outbreaks in some people; perhaps a cleansing effect.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (9, 10, 14, 18, 21, 27, 30), W. T. Fernie (4), Nicholas Culpeper (14), Jack Ritchason (15), John King, John Hill (17), William Boericke (14, 21), Dorothy Hall (3, 14, 19–23), Cyril Boger (1, 25), Matthew Wood (3, 4, 5, 6, 29, 30), Julia Graves (31), Lise Wolff (7, 8, 14), Phyllis Light (11, 13, 17, 28), Margi Flint (15), Harvey Felter (2).

## Armoracia rusticana. Horseradish, Daikon.

Horseradish is a very hot member of an already hot family, the cruciferae or cabbage clan. It has been in cultivation from such an early time that its exact origins are unknown. It is perhaps native to eastern Europe, where it is widely appreciated as a condiment, but it is now widely grown, eaten, and used as a medicine throughout the world. Under the name daikon it is an important condiment in Japanese cooking and was reintroduced into modern American herbal usage by Michio Kushi.
Fresh horseradish roots are so stimulating that they will cause redness, irritation, and blistering of the part to which they have been applied. They contain a volatile oil also found in mustard. Thus, horseradish roots are used like mustard as a poultice to the chest to stimulate expectoration and to thin stuck phlegm in pneumonia (see Cautions). They have also been used as an irritant to raise a blister on arthritic joints and neuralgic pains. This is a very ancient method of treatment. Dr. Bernard Aschner (1957) gives more than a hundred case histories of arthritis and neuralgia cured by the application of an irritant on the joint or part—he used a cantharis compound. The blister is then lanced to remove the “toxins.” The effect may also have to do with increasing the cortisol in the area.
Taken internally horseradish in modest doses is a powerful stimulant, aperient, and antiseptic for the stomach. It is traditionally taken with oily, rich meat or food, by itself or steeped in vinegar. It is a powerful diuretic that has been employed to remove calculus and edema. The “chief powers” of horseradish roots “are expended upon the kidneys and skin, and they gradually raise the general capillary and then the arterial circulation; but a considerable portion of their influence is expended upon the stomach, and some upon the gall-ducts and the secretions generally,” writes William Cook (1869, 370). “The roots arouse a gentle warmth and fair gastric secretion in the stomach; and are good in decidedly atonic, viscid, and semi-paralyzed conditions of that organ. It steadily increases the flow of urine, and the amount of insensible perspiration; procures warmth of the surface, and greater fullness and firmness of the pulse, and favors an increase of alvine action.”
Horseradish is specifically indicated in cold and depressed conditions. Dr. Cook is very specific about its general indications and contraindications. “It is useful only in very sluggish conditions; and should not be given in any case of local or arterial excitement, nor to sensitive patients. The manner in which it arouses the general casting out of excretions, makes it peculiarly useful in all half-paralyzed and viscid conditions, where accumulations of tenacious mucus clog” the channels of elimination. “Such conditions are common in dropsy; and are also met in some cases of jaundice and rheumatism. The article is most suitable in chronic cases; though a warm infusion used very freely, the patient being well covered, will usually incite a remarkably copious diaphoresis, and may be given in dropsies.” It is used as a stimulant in recent colds (sometimes with cayenne or bayberry bark).

### TASTE:

pungent • hot

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, torpor

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Sluggish, torpid conditions, viscid, tenacious mucus.
Sinusitis (internally, often in combination with capsicum and myrica; externally, a poultice of freshly grated horseradish on a piece of cloth applied to the forehead or checks; causes skin irritation, which increases local circulation and healing; avoid contact with the eyes; put Vaseline on the eyes).
Relaxed sore throat; loss of voice.
Indigestion after a fatty meal (grated fresh root; root preserved in vinegar).
Jaundice.
Edema, kidney stones (decoction of the fresh root).
Depression and torpor of the female tract; vaginitis, odors, itching (poultice of the dried leaves soaked in water).
Rheumatism, arthritis.
Fever (hot infusion of the freshly grated root as a diaphoretic).
Pulse low, weak, obscure.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The roots picked in the fall are hotter. They can be saved in sand, in a cool environment during the winter. One-half to 1 dram of the grated root can be given 3x/day. Prepared fresh, or preserved in vinegar, wine, or brandy.

### CAUTIONS:

Burns skin and eyes on contact. Do not place fresh horseradish directly on skin. Use a cloth between.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, William Cook (1, 4, 5, 6, 8, 10), W. T. Fernie (3, 8, 9), Otto Wolff (2), Michio Kushi (4, 6, 7, 9), Maude Grieve.

## Artemisia absinthium. Wormwood.

Wormwood is a member of the artemisia genus of the Asteraceae family. It is native to disturbed ground in Europe and Asia, though widely naturalized in North America. It flourishes around old walls, broken foundations, and overused pastures. It is one of Nature’s promises that out of devastation life will spring up anew. It was anciently known as absinthium. Wormwood contains flavonoids, phenolic acids, lignans, bitter sesquiterpene lactones, acetylenes, and volatile oils (including the toxic thujone).
Wormwood was one of the most important herbs in the medieval European materia medica. Hildegard von Bingen cites it as the most important herb, Leonard Fuchs placed it on the first page of his herbal, and Nicholas Culpeper uses it as his example herb for illustrating various astrological and archetypal concepts.
Wormwood has long been used as an aromatic bitter tonic for stimulating the digestion. This usage is justified by the taste, pharmacology, and tradition. According to Galen it liquefies and washes the “bad humors retained in the stomach,” causing them to descend, after which the stomach “contracts and strengthens.” To effect the descent of humors retained on the walls of the stomach mix “honey water with absynthium” (Maimonides; Rosner, 1989, III:321, 316). This usage is attested by authors down to the nineteenth century. “As a tonic and appetizer it is far-famed,” wrote New Zealand herbalist James Neil (1998, 25) in 1891. He testifies: “While in Chicago we visited the office of a very wise old eclectic doctor” who used wormwood for “impaired appetite; it makes them eat well.” Neil treats us to a case history. “Some time ago a person from Oamaru was troubled with indigestion, so badly that he lost flesh and became very weak. He was recommended an infusion of this plant with [white] horehound; the effect was a return of health.” An Australian author, Harald Tietze (1996, 46), writes, “No other herb works so quickly to relieve heartburn, indigestion, gastritis, or liver congestion.” These usages are still credited to wormwood in European phytotherapy. The German E Commission cites wormwood as a remedy for loss of appetite and stomach disorders resulting from insufficient production of stomach acid (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994, 47).
Wormwood also acts farther in from the GI tract, upon the liver and gallbladder. Culpeper (1653): “The sun never shone upon a better herb for the yellow jaundice than this.”
Maurice Mességué mentions a case in which wormwood restored the urge to life in a young woman suffering from hopelessness. This is very much how I see the use of the plant. In my first book, Seven Herbs (1987), I described the use of sagebrush (Artemisia tridentata) for people suffering from devastation, hopelessness, destitution, poverty, abuse, and unwinnable situations. I now use wormwood for this mental state because it is easier for me to obtain.
Wormwood is also to be thought of as a remedy where the hopelessness is due to severe physical trauma. I helped a woman whose life had been severely damaged by a truck-on-car collision thirty years previous. The other passengers in the car died; she survived with constant, excruciating pain. Marital problems made her life even more difficult. Wormwood in small internal doses, and in large external doses on the scar tissue on her legs and the painful joints, returned her to functioning health and she was very grateful. A similar case history, with a relatively complete recovery, is reported by Medicine Grizzlybear Lake (1991, 149).
Wormwood has survived in modern American herbalism largely as a medicine for the muscular and skeletal system—reminding us of its cousin arnica. A strong decoction of wormwood is a “very strengthening application for bruises and sprains” (Child, 1837, 103). “It makes a good fomentation in sprains, rheumatism, and other sub-acute difficulties about the joints; and in bruises and local congestions” (Cook, 1869, 270). Dr. John Christopher included wormwood in his bone, flesh, and cartilage formula.
A wide number of uses are recorded by Lake (1991, 149), who has used it on “numerous occasions.” This includes a bath for children with diaper rash, or for adults with jock itch and athlete’s foot, children and animals with pin worms, scabies, and skin rashes. Also externally in a poultice for bursitis, torn ligaments, sprains, broken bones, cranial fractures, and arthritis.
Wormwood is suited to people who have been brutalized by the reverses of life, including poverty and abuse. It is effective where there is physical malnutrition. It is indicated for coldness and depression in the autonomic nervous system, hence a deep depression of the vital forces of digestion and of life in general. The lack of digestive energy and the general coldness and lifelessness may cause atrophy and it is indicated in thin, scrawny, weak, malnourished individuals. I have also used it for large, water-logged, stiff people with success.
Wormwood had an ancient reputation against pests of various kinds. It was classified as a vermifuge. James Neil (1998, 25) qualifies this: “Experience has proved that it is weak in this respect. It makes a very good injection in seat or pin worms, which infest the lower bowels,” but does not live up to its name for more potent parasites. Culpeper (1653) gives wormwood a very odd property: mixed with ink it repels mice and rats, so they will not eat the paper written on. In the closet it protects against moths, he says.
Culpeper (1653) relates the following curious story about Mars and wormwood. “When Mars was free from war, (for he loves to be fighting, and is the best friend a soldier hath) I say, when Mars was free from war, he called a council of war in his own brain, to know how he should do poor sinful man good, desiring to forget his abuses in being called an unfortunate [planet]. He musters up his own forces, and places them in battalia. ‘Oh,’ quoth he, ‘why do I hurt a poor silly man or woman?’ His angel answers him, ‘It is because they have offended their God.’ (Look back to Adam.) ‘Well,’ says Mars, ‘though they speak evil of me, I will do good to them: Death’s cold, my herb shall heat them; they are full of ill humours (else they would never have spoken ill of me), my herb will cleanse them, and dry them: they are poor weak creatures, my herb shall strengthen them; they are dull witted, my herb shall fortify their apprehensions.’ And among astrologers all this does not deserve a good word: Oh the patience of Mars!” Culpeper ranked wormwood warm and dry in the first degree with his predecessors. Yet this story shows that he used wormwood in both dampness and dry/atrophy.

### TASTE:

bitter • warm • fragrant

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Chronic depression, lack of affect, deadness, hopelessness; helps a person dream solutions.
Sleep disturbed, restless, wakes in a convulsion or trance; “night terrors.”
HEAD
Clenches the jaw; earaches, headaches.
DIGESTION
Loss of appetite.
Bad breath, putrid taste, putrid eructations, flatulence.
Inactive stomach and intestines.
Nausea, morning sickness, heartburn.
Malnutrition, wasting, atrophy, debility.
Constipation.
Pin worms.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Hepatitis (external).
Weakness of the gallbladder; lack of bile, lack of digestion, constipation.
Obstruction of the liver and gallbladder; jaundice (juice of the large leaves growing from the root, John Hill).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema (“juice of the large leaves … which grow from the root, before the stalk appears,” John Hill).
FEMALE
Amenorrhea, infertility.
Menstruation: cramps in slender, cold, stiff, atrophic women (cf. Artemisia vulgaris).
Parturition: painful.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Stiff and cold; lower back pain.
Joint pain; arthritis; sore, bruised feeling.
Sprains and strains; muscles weak, stiff, cold, sore.
Arthritis, rheumatism, gout.
FEVER
Chills and fever with lack of perspiration.
Ague, intermittent chills and fever (the tops, with the flowers on them).
WOUNDS
Boils, eczema, scabies, skin rashes, diaper rash, jock itch, athlete’s foot.
OTHER
Externally to reduce hair loss.
Hangover.
Very specific when there is hardness in the middle pulse on the left hand (liver/gallbladder position).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Large doses are toxic because of the thujone. In my experience it is sometimes wise to give this remedy in extremely limited doses (1 drop per day or week) in order not to cause depression. Large doses disorder the stomach. Sir John Hill (1740, 390) instructed the use of small doses to avoid the wretched bitterness. “The tops of the plant are to be used fresh gathered, a very slight infusion of them is excellent for all disorders of the stomach, and will prevent sickness after meals, and create an appetite; but if it be made strong, it will not only be disagreeable to the taste, but will disgust the stomach.” James Neil (1998, 25) wrote, “Very large doses or long continued will weaken the stomach, cause headaches, etc.”
Contemporary Australian herbalist Harald Tietze (1996, 46), who is very precise in his indications for herbal teas, recommends 1–2 cups of the tea a week, made by pouring a quarter liter of boiling water over a half a teaspoonful of the dried herb, steep for 5 minutes. Dose: 1/3 cup before a meal. The German E Commission gives the average dose as 1–3 grams a day in aqueous infusion (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994).
John Hill considered the tops (flowers and leaves) and the bottom leaves to have slightly different properties. The former are vermifuge and antimalarial whereas the latter are opening, diaphoretic, and diuretic.

### TOXICITY:

Wormwood was formerly used in the manufacture of vermouth, but persistent doses were believed to cause mental illness. This is thought to be the origin of some of the mental problems of the early impressionist painters, such as Vincent Van Gogh. The thujone was supposed to be the culprit; it penetrates the blood/brain barrier. It is a toxin to the brain and causes convulsion resulting in nausea and stomach disorder. (This, however, testifies to its organ affinity to the stomach.) For these reasons wormwood was long banned for internal consumption in the USA and in some other countries. However, it is recommended by the German E Commission, and was recently reinstated as an alcoholic drink in the U.S.
Fortunately, aqueous extracts (tea) do not take up much of the thujone. A cup of the tea can be used, but only for a few days or a week. The tincture can be therapeutic in small doses. I use extremely small amounts—1 drop a week is sufficient for the depressed mental state, in my experience. More than this can cause depression from bringing up issues too quickly. The very small dose, on the other hand, brings issues up for processing and relief at a reasonable rate. Wormwood can also be applied externally but the bitterness is needed internally for the digestive symptoms and probably even the mental state. Occasional large doses can be very beneficial, but are not legal.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Father Sebastian Kneipp (5, 6), Nicholas Culpeper (1, 2, 4, 5–13, 16, 18, 20, 21, 22, 26), John Christopher, Harald Tietze (7), William Cook, Matthew Wood (1, 2, 3, 8, 27), Medicine Grizzlybear Lake (24), John Hill (13, 14), Maurice Mességué (1), Louise Tenney (3, 4, 7–13, 15, 19), James Neil (4, 10), W. T. Fernie (5).

## Artemisia annua. Sweet Annie.

Sweet Annie is an annual artemisia native to China, but widely grown and naturalized. It is a traditional medicine that has caught the attention of modern science because of its effectiveness and safety in the treatment of malaria and other diseases dependent on microorganisms. It contains essential oils, hence the name “sweet,” flavonoids, and a sesquiterpene lactone, artemisinin, that bonds with parasite proteins, and is antimalarial and antineoplastic. The dried herb is used.

## Artemisia dracunculus. Tarragon.

Tarragon (“little dragon”) may be called the “mild artemisia,” since it does not have the extremely bitter taste of its cousins. It contains coumarin and volatile oils and has a “diffusive” taste—that is, it produces a mild tingle on the tongue. It is better known for its culinary properties than as a medicinal. It is used to make a vinegar. As a medicinal herb it is used to promote appetite, secretion of bile and digestion juices (Dan Kenner and Yves Requena, 2001, 113).

## Artemisia vulgaris. Mugwort.

Mugwort is another Eurasian plant of very ancient medicinal usage. It is called the oldest of plants (yldost wyrta) in the Charm of the Nine Worts, an ancient Anglo-Saxon leech book. It finds a place in the pharmacopoeia of Europe, India, and China. The name mugwort was originally motherwort, according to Aemilius Macer (tenth century). The word mother here means the uterus. The Latin binomial associates it with Artemis, goddess of the moon and mother of nature. Mugwort shines in the moonlight and affects dreaming. It contains flavonoids, sesquiterpene lactones, triterpenes, coumarin derivatives, and volatile oils. It is not quite as bitter as its cousin wormwood.
Mugwort is an important medicine in traditional European and Asian medicine. The taste and pharmacology emphasize its action as an aromatic bitter, which increases digestion by stimulating digestive and bile secretion, thus enhancing nutrition. It is less active as a vermifuge than many of its artemisia cousins. It is mildly stimulating to the nervous system, reducing tension, hence improving autonomic regulation of the digestion, and increasing circulation and nutrition in the nerves and muscles. It has long been used in traditional medicine to induce the menstrual flow.
In traditional medicine the association of mugwort with women and the menses is emphasized. “What is Venus but the Artemisia that grows in your garden?” asks Paracelsus. Contemporary herbalist Susun Weed, emphasizing a different epoch of female life, calls it “cronewort.” It is suited to young, middle-aged, and older women, to the “feminine instincts” and allied faculties—intuition, art, creativity, dream—and to the feminine side of men. Artemis (who was a sort of tomboy goddess) is the protector of virgins and women suffering from illness. “It is in greatest Esteem amongst Midwives and Nurses,” wrote John Quincy (1736).
Herbalist William LeSassier considered mugwort to be especially suited to weak, sensitive women, or those who have been through abuse, poverty, obstetric injury, difficult pregnancies, and abortions with scar tissue in the womb. There can be a “withdrawal of spiritual aspirations from the womb, from sex.” This may occur in conjunction with anger, frustration, bitchiness, depression, deadness, lack of affect. It is restorative when there has been obstetric injury, abortion, abuse, poverty, deprivation. It is as if the male part has rushed ahead to protect the injured female part. Mugwort is restorative to the injured female nature.
In European phytotherapy mugwort is considered to stimulate secretion of the pituitary sex hormones (FSH and LH). It is indicated in insufficiency of the corpus luteum from anemia (Dan Kenner and Yves Requena, 2001, 112). Independently, both herbalist Isla Burgess, of New Zealand, and myself came to the conclusion that mugwort is a remedy for excessive androgenism. That would indeed be a condition where the masculine has raced ahead and the female is behind. In hyperandrogenism we often see frustration, anger, depression, lack of affect (deadness) or excessive affect, susceptibility to chills or a general chilliness, with sore, cold joints and pains, especially in the lower back, pelvis, and uterus, infertility, menstrual disorder, blood sugar imbalances, abdominal weight gain (in lean, thin women and men), polycystic ovarian disease, and infertility. In many of these conditions, mugwort will prove remedial.
Although one would think that excess androgen would overstimulate heat processes in the body, in my experience it tends to bring on conditions of chill and fever. It is as if cold were stuck in the system and the heat is trying to drive it out, resulting in chill/fever, or what the old doctors called “intermittent fever.” Such conditions are especially associated with autonomic stress in the liver, gallbladder, and stomach. Thus, there may be digestive, assimilative, and nutritive problems. The muscles are weak and malnourished, subject to bouts of cold, soreness, pain, and spasm. There may be scar tissue interfering with muscular movement and nutrition. These symptoms are similar to those one would associate with mugwort’s cousin, wormwood. However, there is more of a female emphasis with mugwort and more of a gallbladder/digestive influence with wormwood.
In Chinese herbalism fibers from the leaves of mugwort are rolled into moxa sticks and burned as “moxibustion.” The glowing sticks are placed over cold, stiff joints and cold areas to increase warmth and circulation. They can be used skillfully in two different ways. Applied close, moxibustion causes burning and blistering, which brings the blood to the surface. This stimulates local cortisone reaction in joints. It also draws toxins out of the connective tissue in the joints, which are hard to flush, since they do not receive direct circulation of blood, but are nourished and flushed by squeezing and use of the joints. (Blistering is an ancient and widespread practice for the relief of arthritis; see Bernard Aschner, 1957.) When the moxibustion is applied further away from the skin, causing the area to warm up but not to blister, blood is drawn into the connective tissue and the deeper parts of the joints to relieve lack of nutrition and strength in the joints. Blistering is better in surplus conditions, where the toxins need to be brought out, whereas warming is better in deficiency conditions, where the deep connective tissue needs to be nourished.
A specific pulse indication for mugwort has been described by Eliot Cowan. In a weak pulse the beat is usually weaker on the left hand and stronger on the right, but in a mugwort person it is the other way around—and this is unusual. Cowan calls it “wife before husband.” It indicates that the yin has been damaged, so the yang within the yin rises up too strong.
Randolph Stone (1986, 2:210) describes this same pulse: “a weak right pulse and a stronger left pulse.” He takes it as evidence of an imbalance between male and female polarities. “It is like the male energy going to war, without the feminine checks, causing devastation and famine.” The brain, spinal cord, and autonomic nervous centers along the cord are in a state of hunger for oxygen, nutrition, and circulation. Stone cautions the student not to draw the blood to the surface because that will make the situation get worse by depleting the deeper blood supply. There is a condition of “excessive capillary dilation.” Blood may stagnate in pools in the abdomen. This can cause stagnant blood or adhesions, with pain, stiffness, and cramp. This imbalance is the source of atonic conditions with exhaustion, mental instability, oversensitivity, and talkativeness. (Or, conversely, mugwort is withdrawn and asocial.) Note that moxibustion is contraindicated in this constitutional imbalance. This is an example of how a remedy used locally is damaging when it should be used constitutionally.
Mugwort acts strongly on the female system, increasing circulation to warm up the uterus and pelvic region, move the blood, remove stagnant blood (the source of cramps, stiffness, and pain), and regulate hormone levels. It has been traditionally used for violent menstrual cramps and epilepsy associated with birthing. Some have used it as a general menstrual cramp regulator. William Salmon (1710, 739) writes, “It provokes the terms powerfully, and facilitates the birth of women in labor, bringing away the afterbirth, and causing a due cleansing. It is good to expel a Mola, when the woman has gone beyond her time; as also the child when it is dead. It suppresses vapors [fits, hysteria connected with menstruation], being duly taken; and I have cured hysterick fits with it to admiration; but then in these two last cases, it ought to be given in its own distilled water, or in fair spring water.”
In Japan mugwort is pounded into glutinous rice to make a product called gomogi mochi. This food is used to provide stamina and is traditionally given to new mothers to stop postpartum bleeding and anemia, and to promote lactation (Dan Kenner and Yves Requena, 2001, 112).
Australian herbalist Dorothy Hall has developed an interesting profile of the mugwort constitution. It is suited to highly intelligent people with complex thoughts that are difficult to describe, speech disorders and dyslexia, highly elevated senses, sensitivity to light and sound, with great difficulty getting deep sleep. It is suited to people in whom the intuitive, psychic, psychological, creative, and artistic side of the mind is highly developed, but who have trouble with expression, or with the world around them. Independently I developed a similar profile for sagebrush (Artemisia tridentatax) and wormwood (A. absinthium) (see Seven Herbs: Plants as Teachers). Ironically, after some twenty years of practice, I have come to prefer the more mundane indications for the artemesias. These plants are exceptionally useful for physical complaints that, many times, appear independently of such fascinating constitutional perspectives. However, what has been reported by Dorothy Hall and myself about these remedies still remains true—they are beneficial for connecting the left and right sides of the brain and for complex psychological problems.

### TASTE:

bitter • fragrant

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, constriction, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Highly intelligent, gifted, artistic people; heightened acuity of any or all of the five senses; but lacking common comprehension and skills.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Dyslexic, difficulty recognizing words but has complex thoughts.
Can’t remember the most simple words, names, but can describe complex, abstract, difficult concepts.
Becomes angry or arrogant when others cannot follow his thought; enjoys silence and solitude from others; feels special or different.
Speaking difficult; garbled, fast, mumbled, syllables or words reversed.
Disorders of sleep and imagination: absorbed in the imagination, creative process, daydreaming, internal conversations; seems absent in another world.
Body seems to be asleep, but mind is awake.
When they do sleep deeply they have vivid dreams.
Lies awake in bed, for long periods of time, thinking, imaging; sensitive to noise and light; sleepwalking.
Springs up immediately from shallow sleep; or extremely tired, exhausted from lack of sleep.
Chronic insomnia.
HEAD, EYES
Eyes sensitive to the light; better from wearing sunglasses; worse from bright light.
Worse from flickering fluorescent lights, camera flashes, strobe lights, low winter sun shining through trees, which can cause anger, absence, vagueness, petit-mal seizures, for a second or two.
Epileptic seizures, with sensitivity to light, menstrual and parturitive problems, sleep disorders. Most effective in cases where there are seizures several times a day; in young women with sensitive, weak constitutions.
Irritation of the nervous system.
Exhausted, restless, thrashing about.
DIGESTION
Poor appetite, lack of bile and digestive secretion.
Gastric fevers, typhoid, with nervous disturbances, spasmodic or benumbed.
Constipation; spastic bowel; torsions, griping, and crampings.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Straining to make water; kidney stones and matter in the urine.
Stiff lower back, better from heat.
Stiff, tired, does not wish to mix with people.
FEMALE
Violent menstrual pains at the beginning of menstruation.
Weak, sensitive women.
Women who have been through harshness, poverty, abuse, abortions, obstetric injuries; with stiff, cold lower back, joints; stiffness entering middle age.
Adhesions in the uterus, from abortions or other problems, with menstrual difficulties.
Uterus prolapsed, ruptured, injured (internal, or moxa).
Profuse menstrual bleeding.
PMS, not as much emotional as nervous; brings on the period.
Labor; contractions violent and painful; miscarriage; increase of the lochial discharge.
Expels the dead child, or defective embryo.
CIRCULATION
Cold hands and feet (externally, the oil of the herb).
Hardness in the pulse.
Pulse: weak on the person’s right arm and stronger on the left arm.
Syncope.
Hypotension.
OTHER
Seizures in children who are teething.
Sweat profuse and offensive.
Injuries to the nerves; benumbed, painful, cramped; to limbs weary from long walking and travel; contusions.
Marijuana use and abuse.
Opium use and abuse; restores the paralyzed nervous tissues.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The herbaceous part of the plant is picked (preferably at full moon, before going to seed). Infusion of the dried herb: pour 1 cup of boiling water over 1–2 teaspoons and infuse in a closed vessel for 10–15 minutes; 3x/day. Tincture (1:1): 1/2–2 ml, 3x/day (BHP); 1–3 drops, 1–3x/day (Matthew Wood).

### CAUTION:

Potential of causing allergies in some people.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (10, 14, 15, 18–21, 23, 24, 27, 28, 30, 31, 32, 37, 39, 41), Dorothy Hall (1–13, 40), William LeSassier (16, 24, 29), W. T. Fernie (14, 24), Matthew Wood (10, 21, 22, 25, 26, 33), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (17, 35, 36), Flower Essence Society (32), Eliot Cowan (34). Traditional indications are mostly from William Salmon.

## Asparagus officinalis. Asparagus.

Asparagus is a member of the lily family native to Europe, but widely naturalized in North America. It grows near the seacoasts, showing its affinity for salt, and the taste confirms the presence of sodium, which enhances its diuretic effect. It contains flavonoids, saponins (source of the slight bitterness), and volatile oils.
Asparagus is what the late William LeSassier used to call a “nutritive diuretic”; it works by strengthening the kidneys rather than forcing them to work. This is always the preferred method, if we are attempting to heal the kidneys.
“The asparagus is a wild plant in many parts of England about the seacoasts, and its root, in this wild state, is better than that of the cultivated plants, but its shoots have not that fine fleshy fullness” used in cooking. “The root is a powerful diuretic, and is good in all obstructions of the viscera. It has been known singly to perform cures in jaundices and dropsies. It is best given in decoction” (John Hill, 1740, 67).
Asparagus migrated to the New World, where it is grown and naturalized. “Asparagus officinalis is a gentle but certain diuretic,” writes J. W. Fyfe (1909, 398). “This remedy possesses diuretic properties of a considerable value, and has been employed with advantage in various forms of dropsy. It has also given marked relief in some cases of enlargement of the heart.”

### TASTE:

salty

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Respiratory congestion and wheeziness accompanied by light colored, copious urine.
Enlargement of the heart, congestive heart failure, edema.
Jaundice.
Dropsical conditions.
Uric acid diathesis; gout, arthritis, kidney stones.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Decoction of the root (Hill, Moore). Tincture: usual dose: 20–30 drops (Fyfe).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, John Hill (3, 4), Michael Moore (1, 5), John William Fyfe (2, 4).

## Avena sativa. Milky Oatseed, Oatstraw.

Oats are a food crop native to the Old World but widely grown. The seed in the milky stage (just before maturity) produces a beautiful, moistening, sweet nutritional fluid that superficially resembles almond milk. It contains starches, fixed oils, proteins, vitamin E (like wheat germ), flavones, a great deal of silicon with some manganese, iron, and zinc. Oats, like all grasses, are high in silicon. This strengthens the connective tissue, skin, mucosa, and nerves. Oat straw or grass, the stem, is sometimes used. It contains less of the nutrients, but has terpenoid saponins.
Milky oatseed is a very important tonic for the nervous system depleted by stress, prolonged illness, sexual excesses, drug use, or protracted illness. It was first introduced for these purposes in the early twentieth century. “Exhaustion is the keynote,” writes Christopher Menzies-Trull (2003).
Oatstraw has a somewhat different history and usage. Father Sebastian Kneipp introduced “hayseed tea,” for which oatstraw is now used, as a bath and tea. It is usually used for arthritis and rheumatism (fibromyalgia).

### TASTE:

(milky seed) sweet • moist

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Nervous prostration, debility, weakness, paralysis (neurasthenia).
Nervous system exhausted, all played out, drained; from stress, excessive duties, drug use, prolonged illness, sexual excess.
Inability to keep the mind fixed on any one subject; lack of focus, concentration, and memory; vagary of thought and manner.
Nervous prostration following mental strain.
Insomnia associated with nervous exhaustion, despondency, mental irritability.
Occipital headache extending down the spine and into the lower extremities, associated with neurasthenia; with burning sensation on top of the head.
Paralysis of the throat in diphtheria.
Nervous palpitations, tachycardia.
Nervous, stressed digestion with emaciation, weakness, diarrhea.
Bedwetting, nocturnal emissions; with weakness.
Emissions of phosphates and other white substances in the urine, with exhaustion.
Impotence in men under middle age; from nerve exhaustion, sexual excesses, nocturnal emissions.
Swollen prostate with neurasthenia.
Amenorrhea with nervous debility; mental depression, pain in the pelvis.
Nervous headache at menstruation.
PMS with headache, exhaustion, panic, or nausea.
Holds shoulders tight and raised; or shrugged.
Extremities weak; numbness, lack of strength; nervous tremors, spasm, epilepsy.
Paralysis and wasting diseases of the elderly.
Weakness and convalescence from protracted disease, fever, typhoid, drug addiction, tobacco.
Weak, broken hair, nails, skin (cf. Equisetum, homeopathic Silicea).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The seed is collected in the milky stage, before maturity, and dried or tinctured fresh. Tincture: 10–15 drops in hot water. “In hot water its action is quicker. It is always best to give it well diluted” (Frederick Petersen, 1905, 53).

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Oats are too damp and sweet for some constitutions. Eating too much can cause diarrhea and a weak GI tract. They may be fattening to the kapha (heavy) constitution.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, H. E. Russell, quoted by John William Fyfe (3), Rolla Thomas (2, 5), Yadubir Sinha (8), Julia Graves (9, 17), Christopher Menzies-Trull (8, 16).

## Ballota nigra. Black Hoarhound.
