Black hoarhound is a mint native to Europe, but naturalized or easily grown elsewhere, including in my garden. It is similar to white hoarhound in appearance, but has a dark green leaf instead of a light one; both are furred or “hoary.” Ballota is intensely bitter and rankly aromatic, containing volatile oils, flavonoids, and bitter diterpenes.
Black hoarhound “has more virtue than most imagine,” wrote Sir John Hill (1740, 208). “It is to be given in form of tea, it promotes the menses, and is superior to most things as a remedy in hysteric cases, faintings, convulsions, and low-spiritedness, and all the train of those disorders.” This description and the following indicate that ballota has a powerful influence through the nervous system, as well as through the secretory glands, which any bitter would typically stimulate.
Richard Hool (1922, 34), a Lancashire herbalist and member of the National Association of Medical Herbalists (precursor to NIMH), is the only author I could find who penned a long account of this plant.
“In black horehound we have one of the most efficacious remedies that we can use for the cure of biliousness, bilious colic, and sour belchings. In the above complaints it is as near a specific as any remedy well can be. The relief it affords is both prompt and certain, for if only a leaf or a piece of the stem be chewed, and the juice swallowed, it will be found to act as if a current of electricity had passed into the stomach, allaying all the symptoms momentarily. In coughs, bronchitis, and asthma it is exceedingly useful. In chronic coughs, accompanied by spitting of blood, it will be found most excellent, either alone or combined with other reliable remedies, such as lobelia, marshmallow, hyssop, etc. Its action is most reliable, as it resolves the viscidity of the mucus secretions, and acts as an alterative-tonic upon the mucous follicles, deterges and heals the diseased membranes, and corrects the acrimony of the discharges. In all cases of consumption it will be found a most useful remedy, as it not only corrects the acrimony of the mucus discharges, but soothes the irritation of the nerves caused by the fit of coughing, thereby making it easier for the patient in nervous affections. In the pains of travail during the time of carrying, combined with motherwort, it will be found an excellent remedy; in amenorrhea, and also in menorrhagia, it will act in a most wonderful manner.”
Hool gives a remarkable case where some postal workers requested his help for one of their co-workers who was down with the cholera—loss of control of the bowels, shivering, trembling, sweating, and weakness. They said they had noticed Hool selling herbs in the marketplace and heard he had a good reputation. He sent them off with ballota, telling them not to worry. By afternoon one of the men returned to say their co-worker was back at work, joking and laughing as usual.
David Hoffmann (2003) is one of the few contemporary authors who mentions Ballota. He rightly states that it “is an excellent remedy for calming nausea and vomiting when the cause lies within the nervous system, rather than in the stomach.” He notes its value in motion sickness, nausea and vomiting caused by pregnancy, and as a “normalizer” in menstruation.

### TASTE:

bitter, pungent • aromatic

### TISSUE STATES:

stagnation, depression (including sudden collapse)
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Asthenic and nervous debility.
Panic attacks.
Low-spiritedness.
Convulsion, fainting.
HEAD
Migraine.
RESPIRATION
Irritable coughs, viscid, acrid mucus, bloody expectoration.
Bronchitis, pneumonia, asthma, tuberculosis.
DIGESTION
Loss of appetite; nervous debility, general weakness, loss of energy.
Sour belchings, biliousness, bilious colic.
Intestinal parasites.
Nausea and vomiting caused by irritation of the nervous system rather than the stomach.
CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
Tachycardia, hypertension.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Gravel, dropsy.
FEMALE
Menstruation: amenorrhea, dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, metrorrhagia.
Parturition: eases pain of labor (combine with Leonurus).
SKIN
Gout (anodyne, poultice).
Angry boils (poultice).
OTHER
Motion sickness.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The leaves are used, fresh or dried (Hill). “It may be used in infusion, decoction, fluid extract, solid extract; in powder, pills, or tincture; or it may be chewed in the mouth either in the fresh or dried state, and the juice swallowed” (Hool).

### CAUTIONS:

Members of the mint family are generally considered to be safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, John Hill (3, 4, 14), Richard Hool (1, 6–10, 12, 14, 15), Christopher Menzies-Trull (1, 2, 5, 10, 13, 18), David Hoffmann (11), Matthew Wood (11-confirmed), W. T. Fernie (16, 17).

## Barosma betulina, Agathosma betulina. Buchu.

Buchu (pronounced boo-koo) is a member of the citrus family native to a tiny area of the South African cape. It was used by the indigenous Bushmen and is now cultivated for its medicinal properties. The wild population is protected. Barosma contains volatile oils, flavonoids (including rutin), mucilage, and resin.
Buchu is warm and stimulating. It has been used extensively in bladder infections since the nineteenth century. It is held to be tonic, as well as stimulating. It is considered to be more specific where there is excess uric acid in the urine. It acts on the kidneys to resolve solids in the urine and on the mucosa of the bladder to reduce mucus discharge. Often these discharges are associated with sexually communicable bacteria, and Australian herbalist Dorothy Hall (1988, 104) associates buchu with both sexually and genetically transmitted venereal taints. The latter manifests as urogenital sensitivity and joint pains and aches, worse during puberty and times of emotional stress.
John William Fyfe (1909, 408) summarizes the tradition: “Buchu is a valuable remedy in subacute and chronic diseases of the genitourinary tract, but it is not adapted to cases in which there is active inflammation. It has a specific soothing and stimulating influence upon the mucous membranes of the urinary organs, and is especially indicated in any case of lowered systemic tone in which there is an acid urine heavily loaded with mucus or muco-pus.” This would concur with a depressed tissue state. “In cases in which there is a constant desire to micturate, and the act gives little or no relief, its action is markedly corrective, and in retention or incontinence of urine, due to an impaired condition of the parts involved, buchu is a very efficient remedy. It also constitutes an excellent medicament in gastric debility, especially when complicated by wrongs of the urinary organs. In chronic catarrh of the bladder, it is a desirable remedial agent, and in subacute or chronic cystitis it exerts a corrective influence. In pyelitis, hematuria and gravel it may well constitute a part of the treatment. In simple and gonorrheal urethritis it is employed with advantage, and in gleet and some prostatic affections it is regarded as a remedy of considerable value. It is also of some value in chronic rheumatism which is complicated by wrongs of the urinary apparatus.”
“In dropsy it is mainly useful in the asthenic forms, particularly when the kidneys, from want of tone, are tardy in the elimination of the absorbed fluid, or are loaded with uric acid deposits,” observes Grover Coe (1858).

### TASTE:

pungent • warm

### TISSUE STATE:

depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION
Uric acid diathesis.
DIGESTION
Atonic dyspepsia (infused brandy).
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Gallstones.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Chronic conditions of the urinary tract.
Highly acid urine, with constant desire to urinate, without relief.
Kidneys, urethra, bladder, prostate.
Constant desire to urinate, with but little relief from the act.
Catarrhal conditions of mucous membranes of the genitourinary organs; secretions excessive, tissues sensitive, mucus, and mucopurulent discharges; gonorrhea, cystitis.
Gravelly conditions characterized by the deposit of a pinkish-colored sediment in the urine.
Edema from lack of tone, depression of the kidney function, nephritis.
Scalding urine.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritic conditions dependent on a uric acid diathesis; after the acute symptoms have subsided.
Lumbago associated with renal conditions.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The dried leaves of commerce are used by infusion, 1 ounce to 1 pint. When well-indicated, buchu will react promptly and obviously (Hall).

### CAUTIONS:

Generally regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1), Maude Grieve (2), Frederick Locke (2), Rolla Thomas (6), Louise Tenney (3, 4, 5, 8, 10–12), John William Fyfe (7–9), Grover Coe (10, 12, 13), Dorothy Hall.

## Berberis vulgaris. Barberry Root.

Barberry is native to Europe and has been used in herbal medicine since ancient times. It was lauded by the Egyptians and is a remedy of great repute in Ayurveda. The Western herbal, Ayurvedic, and homeopathic uses and indications coincide perfectly. Barberry root is extremely bitter, containing isoquinoline alkaloids, of which the most significant is berberine (cf. Hydrastis, Berberis aquifolium). It is used as an intense bitter to influence the liver, gallbladder, and digestion. It also works on the kidneys and bladder.
Barberry root has properties similar to its cousin Oregon grape. They are bitters that increase secretion from the mucosa and internal organs such as the liver and kidneys. Both thus counteract heat and dryness, but there is more evidence of stagnation, torpor, and dampness in the barberry patient whereas the Oregon grape person is hot, dry, and even sclerotic.
Herbalist Richard Hool (1922, 15) observed, “A piece of the bark placed in the mouth and chewed on the hottest day in summer, however dry or thirsty you may be, will slake your thirst by making the saliva run or flow from the salivary glands of the mouth. Barberry exercises an especial influence over the mucous surfaces, its action in this being so manifest that the indications for its employment [heat and dryness] cannot be mistaken. Upon the liver it acts with equal certainty and efficacy. As a cholagogue and a deobstruent it has few equals; in affections of the spleen, mesentery, and abdominal viscera generally, it is an efficient and reliable remedy. In the treatment of chronic derangements of the liver and portal circulation, the bark, when boiled and then made into a syrup, and used freely as a drink, will be found excellent in cases of diarrhea and dysentery, fluxes, and malignant fevers. For abating the heat, quenching the thirst, raising the strength, and preventing putrefaction, scarcely its equal is to be found in any other remedy, as it promotes secretions, expels and removes morbid matter from the stomach and bowels.”
Some of the more specific symptoms are given by Dr. John William Fyfe (1909). “Barberry exercises a direct influence upon the kidneys, bladder and liver, and is used with marked success in abnormal conditions of these organs, especially where there are tearing pains in the renal region, or pains extending down the back and down the ureters into the bladder. In wrongs of the liver [and gallbladder], especially when there are sharp pains extending from the liver to the abdomen, it is deemed a remedy of merit.
“Barberry also constitutes a very efficient tonic in debilitating and anemic conditions, especially when the tongue is deep red or brownish [coated] in color. It promotes natural secretion, favors the flow of gastric juice, and aids the digestive organs in their work of assimilation. It has also been employed with much success in malarial diseases.”
The specific indication that I find most useful is the rapid, hard pulse. This comes from the homeopathic provings: “Pulse slow and weak, or full, hard, and rapid” (Adolph Lippe, 1865, 77). This indicates a forceful heat, driving the body—what traditional Chinese medicine would call “full heat.” The weak, slow pulse comes afterward, when the system is exhausted and resistance has been worn down.
The berries are also sometimes used in herbalism. They are sour rather than bitter. Gerard (1633) describes their use in acute hepatitis with “biliary flux” or thin, yellow diarrhea. “The sparingly produced juice of the berries is cooling and astringent,” writes Fernie (1914, 43). He gives many recipes for its preparation. “Barberry is one of the most beneficial medicinal plants,” comments herbalist Michael Moore (2003, 47).

### TASTE:

(berries) sour, astringent • cooling, drying (root) bitter, astringent, pungent • warming, drying, nourishing

### TISSUE STATES:

(root) atrophy, stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Full heat conditions with inflammation; tongue deep red (Fyfe), coated (Ellingwood, Fernie), yellow, slimy, drying out, or damp and sticky; pulse rapid and hard (Lippe).
After full heat the fluids are burned out and exhaustion sets in; “debilitated and anemic conditions” (Fyfe); “pale face, with a dingy-grayish tinge, sunken cheeks, and deep-seated eyes, surrounded with bluish and blackish-gray border”(Lippe).
Chronic inflammation and congestion, with water and phlegm blocking the fire (cf. Taraxacum).
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Deeply shattered in mind or body, even multiple-personality, from abuse, long-lasting illness.
Mind dull, like a chronic hangover.
Brain disorders.
HEAD
Migraine; worse from movement.
Eyes sore, dry, biting, burning, or itching.
Mouth dry and sticky.
Throat; “inflammation of tonsils and pharynx, with swelling and fiery redness, and a sensation as if a lump were lodged in the side of the throat” (Lippe).
Chronic swollen glands in the throat.
DIGESTION
Lack of appetite, bad breath, mouth ulcers, gum disease.
Relaxed, atonic, catarrhal conditions of the gastrointestinal mucosa.
Stomach, full, congested, lack of secretion; heartburn.
Gastric and duodenal ulcers.
Congestion of abdomen and pelvis.
Constipation, hard stools.
Chronic diarrhea and dysentery; cholera; bilious diarrhea.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
“Jaundice from congestive liver, with furred tongue, lowness of spirits, and yellow complexion” (Fernie).
Torpid liver; gallstones; bilious colic; jaundice; sharp pains under the right ribs; pains extending from the liver to the abdomen (from liver to back, Chelidonium).
SPLEEN AND PANCREAS
Thirsty diabetes.
Swollen spleen from malaria.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Pain in the kidneys, shooting down the thighs, around the torso; kidney stones.
Acute kidney stone colic (5 drops on a sugar cube every 5 minutes).
FEMALE
Pregnancy; vomiting.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Muscular wasting and debility.
SKIN
Skin rashes, eczema, psoriasis (cf. Berberis aquifolium); a superlative remedy when the skin shows small, dry patches, red, brown, or white; ringworm, itching.
Acne, pimples, boils.
Pimples and roughness of the skin, promotes a clear complexion, soft, smooth, and naturally moist, “in sensitive young ladies, when the cause is not a reflex one from ovaries or uterine irritation, or menstrual irregularity.”
FEVER
Chronic, low-grade bacterial infections; intestinal, hepatic, vaginal; giardia, ringworm, etc., hot and dry, or hot and damp.
Chronic malarial conditions, intermittent fevers, with disorder of the stomach, liver, intestines, and lymphatics.
Rapid, hard pulse when fever is present.
Scarlet fever.
Inflammation in acute disease (berries and leaves).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The extremely bitter root tincture can be used in very small doses, 1–5 drops. However, it is commonly used in doses up to 20 or 30 drops. Small doses are tonic, large ones are purgative (Tirtha).

### CAUTIONS:

Too bitter to easily overdose.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, homeopathic provings, Andrew Lange (4), Adolph Lippe (1, 2, 16, 17, 32), John William Fyfe (1, 2, 13, 14, 18, 20), Finley Ellingwood (1, 11, 12, 17, 25–28, 30), William Boericke, Swami Sada Shiva Tirtha (6, 15, 16, 24, 33), Samuel Henry (34), W. T. Fernie (1, 18, 19, 21, 23), Matthew Wood (30, 32), Michael Moore (34).

## Beta vulgaris. Beet.

Beet is a member of the Chenopodiaceae family growing wild on the seacoasts of southern Europe, north Africa, the Middle East, and India, but now widely grown as a food crop. It is the source of the sugar beet. Its use as a common article of food should not dull our appreciation for its powerful effects.
Beet contains up to one-tenth part glucose, which enters the bloodstream almost immediately. Hence, it is likely to cause blood sugar reactions. This is probably why some people, including myself, find it overstimulating, whereas it puts others to sleep. The “food doctors,” who pioneered the use of beet juice as a remedy, recommended its introduction in small doses (up to an ounce of the juice per day at first). In addition to glucose it contains mucilage and gums. It is sweet and slightly bitter, nutritive, cleansing, and alterative.
Beet root is used to build the blood and cleanse the liver. It acts on the liver, heart, and circulation. The leaves are more bitter and are used as a liver cleanser.

## Betonica officinalis, Stachys officinalis. Wood Betony.

This mint is today called Stachys officinalis, which seems to me rather high-handed, since it has been known as Betonica for a few thousand years. It contains betaine, choline, tannins, and alkaloids.
Wood betony is a very ancient and important nervine, at one time considered a cure-all and, with Hypericum, the most important remedy in the Middle Ages for psychiatric problems (demon possession and results of witchcraft, as it was then expressed). These applications are not unmerited, for it has a strong influence on the stomach and enteric brain, and on the brain, head, and nervous system generally, improving gut-level instincts, thinking, and groundedness. It is a most important remedy for the elderly, and for persons who are thin, intellectual, lacking in common sense. It improves the autonomic innervations of the digestive tract, imparting strength and regularity. It is indicated in weak digestion, pallor, poor assimilation, and weak gallbladder reflexes. Thins the blood, strengthens nerve force, opens the circulation, reduces nervousness and hypertension. Severe pain causing hysteria, frenzy, or lack of groundedness. “The habitual use of it will cure the most inveterate head-achs” (John Hill, 1740, 83).
Compare wood betony with its minty cousins Melissa, Leonurus, Lycopus, Scutellaria, and Monarda, all of which are relaxing and blood pressure reducing.

### TASTE:

sweet (vanilla-like) • neutral • mildly diffusive, aromatic

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION AND COMPLEXION
Tall, thin persons, disassociated from their body, instincts, and common sense; frail, elderly people, disconnected from their surroundings; thin, spaced out, with stomach problems.
Complexion pale, wan, leaden.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Traditional for demon possession; extremely helpful for persons who have had unwanted alien abduction experiences.
Frenzy, hysteria, anxiety; from the pain or situation; delirium; insanity; insomnia.
HEAD
Headache with pain causing hysteria; neuralgia.
Head injury, concussion, stroke; increases circulation and nervous activity; restorative after concussion.
RESPIRATION
Colds, bronchitis, coughs.
DIGESTION
Stomach and abdominal problems; bloating, heartburn, gas, worms, colic, mild pains, diarrhea or constipation from weakness.
Fear of vomiting.
Improves digestion of meat.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Pains in abdomen, hepatic region, transverse colon, gallbladder, right inguinal region, and spermatic cords.
Weak gallbladder reflexes and solar plexus; worse from eating; lack of bile, anemia, asthenia, constipation, jaundice.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Weak bladder sphincters, bedwetting.
Weak expulsion of urine, retention.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Thin, weak extremities; poor circulation to extremities.
Arthritis.
Parkinson’s disease.
FEVER
Fever; night sweats; convulsions from loss of fluids and strength during fever.
SKIN
Sores, ulcers, varicose veins.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The leaves are collected “when just going to flower” (Hill, 1740, 83); infusion, tincture, etc., in small to moderate doses.

### CAUTIONS:

Generally regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

The original work was done by Antonius Musa, physician to the emperor Augustus. Traditional, Matthew Wood (1, 2-confirmed, 3, 4, 6, 12), Plinius (2, 7, 9), Julia Graves (6, 9), Yadubir Sinha (11).

## Betula alba, B. lenta. Birch.

The leaves, bark, and sap of various kinds of birch are an important remedy, usually used as a diuretic, in European folk and professional medicine. White birch (B. alba) is the officinal species. In the United States sweet birch (B. lenta) is usually considered officinal. The tree contains oil of wintergreen and salicylates, making it topically anodyne and soothing in joint disease and skin lesions. The resemblance to the skin may have led to the use of birch bark in healing skin.
The medicinal uses of birch are “better known in Scandinavia than in more southerly countries,” notes Donald Law (1973, 32). “In most of the northern lands it is held efficacious against evil spirits and witchcraft.” And Alma Hutchins (1992, 26) says birch “is inseparable from the Russian people, as it is their most poetic tree.” It is also honored in central Europe. Despite the commonness of birch, it is very little used in North America.
In Germany it is traditional to use the tea of the leaves or the spring sap of the birch (Betula alba) to remove proteinaceous and mineral waste from the blood, muscles, and joints. The tea of the leaves is a nonirritating diuretic that removes these waste products without inflaming the kidneys. It can even be safely used when there is nephrosis and chronic nephritis—birch contains saponins that are diuretic and have a cortisone-like effect. It is used to dissolve earthen deposits like kidney stones and gouty and arthritic deposits. It opens the sweat glands, which assists the kidneys to remove waste, and the bark is used as an emollient externally and internally to remove hard, indurated, mineralized skin. As a diaphoretic birch leaf can be useful in intermittent fever.
Birch has a long history of use in Europe as a hair tonic. I am compelled to testify on this matter due to a single case history. While in Australia a woman brought her fourteen-year-old daughter to see me about something or other. I commented that she had some of the healthiest, thickest hair I had ever seen. The mother explained that her daughter’s hair was originally spindly and thin. For several years they rinsed it in nettles and birch. The girl also had an excellent skin and tan—which many Anglo-Celtic Australians do not have, including her mother.
Birch bark is used in anthroposophical medicine for hard, indurated, mineralized skin. Rudolf Steiner pointed to the mineralization of birch bark, which is dry and scaly, as an indication for its use in hardening or sclerosis. The bark is high in potassium, which helps the body retain water. In order to stimulate the periphery, the skin, anthroposophical doctors give birch by subcutaneous injection (Bott, 1978, 76, 195). However, a warm or hot tea is a highly effective method for stimulating the surface and opening the pores. Another anthroposophical author adds that birch is suited to the treatment of problems of aging associated with drying, hardening, and sclerosis (Wolff, 1991, 19).
It could more simply be said that birch bark looks like the skin. This is particularly true in North America, the inner bark resembling very closely the dark skin of Native Americans. In American Indian medicine the oily inner bark of the birch is decocted for external use on the skin.
Unfortunately, birch is not used much in North American herbalism. The sap of the sweet or black birch (Betula lenta) is tapped, like the maple tree, to produce a sweet beverage or syrup. The leaves and twigs are gathered in midsummer to make a tea. It is considered diaphoretic, diuretic, and (due to the oil of wintergreen) beneficial in rheumatic complaints (fibromyalgia). The sugars make it nutrient, probably more than any other birch. Ben Charles Harris (1972, 136) reports that “in 1861 after the Battle of Carricks Ford, the edible bark of Black Birch probably saved the lives of hundreds of Garnett’s Confederate soldiers during their retreat to Monterey, Virginia. For a number of years after that, the route the soldiers took could be traced by the peeled Birch Trees.”

### TASTE:

sweet, bitter, astringent

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Insomnia.
HEAD
Hair; dry, thin; poor scalp health, dry dandruff; also in oily hair (external).
RESPIRATION
Dry, irritable coughs in winter, from indoor heating, wood smoke (tea of the fresh leaves).
Colds (buds).
DIGESTION
Lack of appetite.
Bleeding gums, canker sores, stomach ulcers.
Poisoning, gas, bloating, indigestion (birch charcoal).
Diarrhea, dysentery, cholera.
Worms.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Gallbladder stagnation (boiled buds used for the bitter resin; or fresh buds).
CARDIOVASCULAR
Cardiac edema.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Kidney stones.
Bladder infections; chronic, purulent, and catarrhal.
Edema.
FEMALE
Menses; prolonged, in weak, “winter tired” women (native use; Michael Moore).
Postpartum; slow recovery (native use; Michael Moore).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Tight muscles, joints, strains (oil massage).
Stiff knees from meditation posture.
Arthritis, rheumatism, gout.
Stiff muscles, aching joints and tendons, from overuse.
SKIN
Eczema, psoriasis, bruises, cuts, burns, wounds (bark, external, internal).
Acne, boils, abscesses (bark, external).
Hard, indurated, mineralized skin; dry dermatitis; corns, calluses (bark, external).
FEVER
Intermittent fever.
OTHER
Arteriosclerosis.
Hardening, drying, and sclerosis of old age (internal).
Anemia.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The tender leaves are picked in the spring, before the bitterness is highly developed, juiced and used as a blood cleanser in Germany or infused in a tea. In Russia the fresh buds are preserved in vodka for subsequent use. The diuretic effect is enhanced by adding a fraction of sodium bicarbonate or drinking cold. The diaphoretic effect is enhanced by drinking hot. The leaves produce diuresis best in small, frequent doses (Michael Moore). The oil of the leaves is used as a liniment. The oily inner bark is used in North American Indian medicine for the skin (Tis Mal Crow).

### CAUTIONS:

Edible; regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Ludwig Kroeber (8, 9, 10, 12–14, 17–22, 24), Alma Hutchins (4, 5, 6, 7, 10, 11, 12, 19, 27), Mannfried Pahlow (2, 21), Julia Graves (19), Jack Ritchason (1, 6, 7, 8, 10, 11, 14, 21, 25), Victor Bott (19, 23, 24), Otto Wolff (26), Ben Charles Harris, Michael Moore (3, 15, 16), Juliette de Bairacli Levy (14).

## Bidens tripartita, B. cernua. Bur Marigold.

Bur marigold is a member of the composite or Asteraceae family. It is seldom mentioned in herbal literature. However, it was the favorite herb of Richard Hool (1847–1920), a Lancashire herbalist and early member of the National Association (now Institute) of Medical Herbalists, in Great Britain. He learned about it from his mother, a wise “herb woman.”
“Bur marigold, in the treatment of dropsy and gravel affections, depends more upon its diaphoretic and astringent properties, than on its direct diuretic influences. It resolves mucous deposits, and deterges and heals abraded mucous surfaces in catarrh of the bladder, engorgement of the uterus, and in all atonic conditions of the urinary apparatus. It is peculiarly useful in dropsy, strangury, hematuria, gout, etc., being a most valuable and auxiliary agent. Its utility in the last-mentioned diseases is owing to the power of resolving viscidity of the secretions and of promoting renal depuration. It seems to exercise a peculiar soothing and toning influence upon inflamed and abraded mucous surfaces. It also promotes assimilation and restrains diarrheal tendency, but it is in all cases of haemorrhage and where bleeding is concerned that its greatest qualities are found. In bleeding from the lungs, stomach, or bowels its equal cannot be found, for as a styptic it puts all other remedies in the shade, no matter whether they be vegetable or mineral.
“In all diseases of the respiratory organs, where bleeding is concerned, bur marigold is of the highest value and utility, as it first resolves the viscidity of the pulmonary secretions, the plasticity of the venous blood, and promotes cutaneous depuration, healing the part affected without causing any irritation of the patient” (Hool, 1922).
It is interesting to note that burr marigold or “beggar’s ticks,” as it is more commonly called in America, only flourishes in wet years, near ponds and swamps that are flooded. Sometimes it can be grown near a rain spout. This indicates that it is a remedy for tissue that has been flooded. As a pungent aromatic it thins and warms the fluids, as a bitter it moves stagnant fluids and phlegm, and as an astringent it tones relaxed and swollen tissue.

### TASTE:

pungent, bitter • astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, torpor, relaxation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Nervousness, insomnia, in children.
“Tension of fear.”
General debility.
HEAD
Headache.
RESPIRATION
Allergic rhinitis congestion; not for viral rhinitis.
Laryngitis.
Bronchial affection with viscid mucus and difficult coughing; croup, severe coughs, asthma.
Tuberculosis; bleeding from the lungs.
DIGESTION
Diarrhea, dysentery.
Bleeding hemorrhoids.
CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM AND BLOOD
Palpitations of the heart.
Ruptured blood vessels, bleedings of all kinds, life threatening, internal and external.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Kidney disease, edema, gravel; opens the skin to relieve the kidneys.
Cystitis, catarrh in the bladder.
FEMALE
Severe uterine hemorrhage.
Lack of or painful periods.
MALE
Benign prostatic hypertrophy.
SKIN
Eczema.
FEVER
Fever; thins mucus, opens the skin.
OTHER
Liver and blood purifier.
Gout.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The whole plant (herb) is used (Brown, Hool). “Take 2 oz. of bur marigold, cut into small pieces, and place with 1 oz. of crushed lump ginger into 3 pints of cold water, and simmer it down to one quart. Strain, and it is ready for use. The dose is half to one tea cupful, taken either warm or cold as often as required; the severer the case the more frequently it should be given” (Hool). Herb and root (Hutchins). Herb before or during flowering, tinctured fresh (7Song).

### CAUTIONS:

Potential allergies in sensitive people.

### LITERATURE:

O. Phelps Brown, Richard Hool (3, 7–12, 14, 15, 19, 21), Alma Hutchins (1, 2, 4, 7, 14, 18, 20), Maude Grieve, Robert Dale Rogers (6, 7, 13, 15, 17, 19), 7Song (5).

## Borago officinalis. Borage.

Borage is a member of the comfrey family native to the eastern hemisphere but easily grown in gardens elsewhere. It has been used since the most ancient times and has always been classified as a cooling herb. In the days before iced drinks it was known as “cooltankard.” Borage is still liberally used in British herbalism, but not much in North America. From British herbalists I have obtained my understanding of the remedy and I use it, as they do, as an important remedy for severe nervous exhaustion.
Borage is a deep-acting nervine suited to cases where there is thorough exhaustion and low spirits. Often the person is just run to death by responsibilities or, alternately, runs themselves down with self-criticism and impossible standards. Rosari Kingston, herbalist (MNIMH), of County Cork, Ireland, says borage is indicated in “menopausal women who are overworked and totally exhausted. Teachers are a good example. Start on vervain, then go to borage to tone the entire system from the hypothalamus on down. Or follow with lycopus. It is assisted by avena.” Another experienced herbalist, Anita Ralph, MNIMH, of Kent, confirms the use of borage in the nervous exhaustion sometimes found in menopause. Borage contains silicon, which has a powerful rebuilding effect on the nervous system (cf. Avena, Equisetum, homeopathic Silicea).
“Borage for courage” is an expression oft quoted in herbal lore. Lise Wolff explains: “It is for people who are so run-down that they lack the fortitude or courage to face a responsibility or stress.” To which I add: “confirmed.”
In addition to acting on the nerves borage has an action on the endocrine system. Mrs. Leyel cited it as a remedy for adrenal exhaustion. It probably works higher up the endocrine cascade. Bernard Jensen recommended it for thyroid issues. It has been traditionally used to increase lactation, so it probably acts on the top of the endocrine chain, on the hypothalamus and pituitary.
Maude Grieve (1931, 120) notes: “By virtue of its saline constituents, it promotes the activity of the kidneys.” Borage was traditionally used to treat “melancholic” conditions, especially those affecting the heart. The properties of borage are still somewhat unknown to modern herbalism, so I will quote from a traditional source.
William Salmon (1710, 112) writes that borage “effectually purifies the blood, and is of excellent use in all putrid, malign, spotted and pestilential fevers, to defend the heart from their poison and malignity, and to expell the same, as also the poison of other creatures.” That would make it cooling in the third degree—that is, capable of sedating heat from systemic infection, microbes, and poisons. It has been used for contagious fevers, scarlet fever, and chicken pox. “Mixt with juice of fumitory, it cools and cleanses the blood, and is profitable against the yellow jaundice.”
By strengthening the heart borage is good “against fainting and swooning fits, and other passions of the heart” and “revives the spirits.” The infusion of the herb in wine “admirably recreates the spirits, and gratifies or pleases the stomach, is good against the cardiack passion, and melancholly, and is prevalent against the falling-sickness.” He also mentions heart palpitations.
Borage contains some mucilage. The syrup is used for coughing, wheezing, shortness of breath, colds, and asthma. The distilled essence “strengthens nature,” a phrase meaning that it improves resistance to chills and fevers by toning the sweat pores or the basic immunity.
Borage is used externally on wounds, running sores, and ulcers “in bodies of an ill habit”; also on inflammation of the eyes. Rycharde Banckes (1525) used it to cure abscesses due to collection of melancholic humors (conditions tending toward uncleanness and putrefaction).

### TASTE:

slightly sweet and salty • moist, cooling

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Downcast, heavy-hearted, weighed down, depressed (borage oil, borage herb).
Nervousness, insomnia, fainting, dizziness, melancholy.
Overwork, nervousness, exhaustion; especially in menopausal women.
HEAD
Sore and inflamed eyes (eyewash).
RESPIRATION
Colds, bronchitis; chronic catarrh, congestion, pleurisy, fever.
DIGESTION
Indigestion, jaundice.
CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM
Heart palpitations (probably from hyperthyroidism, not heart disease).
Varicose veins (lotion or compress, 20% Borago).
ENDOCRINE SYSTEM
Hyperthyroidism.
FEMALE
Pregnancy: softens the cervix (borage oil, black currant oil, primrose oil).
Postpartum: nervous exhaustion (borage with passionflower tea).
Diminished lactation; increases flow of milk.
Menopause in exhausted women.
Menopausal hot flashes.
SKIN
Corns.
Skin rashes, ringworm.
FEVER
Fever with mucus.
Scarlet fever, chicken pox.
Peritonitis (cf. Eupatorium maculatum).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The annual herb is picked at the end of the season during flowering. Culpeper preferred to use the fresh herb preserved in syrup, which increases the cooling and moistening properties (Graeme Tobyn). John Hill (1740, 90) required that the herb be infused or tinctured cold rather than warm. “Throwing it into cold wine is better than all the medicinal preparations.” Due to the proven toxicity, dosage should be small. I find 1–3 drops, 1–3x/day a fine dose, or the flower essence.

### CAUTIONS:

The hairs on the fresh leaves can irritate the skin. Borage contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids that have demonstrated hepatotoxicity. Dosage should be small.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (18), Flower Essence Society (1), Julia Graves (1), Anita Ralph (1, 14), Rosari Kingston (1, 2, 3, 13), Otto Wolff (8), Bernard Jensen (9), Jack Ritchason (15), Louise Tenney (4, 5, 6, 12, 15), Maude Grieve (16), Hakim Chishti (5, 12, 19).

## Brassica nigra, B. alba. Black or White Mustard Seed.

Mustard seed is fiery and stimulating—it is classified as hot and dry in the fourth degree in Greek/Arabic medicine. That is the category for medicines that raise a blister. After the demise of classical medicine these agents were classified as stimulants. In folk medicine around the world mustard seed is used as a topical stimulant on arthritic joints and rheumatic, stiff muscles. A mustard plaster or “sinapism” is placed on the chest to break up heavy mucus and pneumonia (cf. Allium cepa, Zingiber).
“The seeds are more used in sauce, than in medicine,” commented Dr. John Quincy in the London Pharmacopoeia (1736, 75). However, “they are mighty efficacious in stimulating the fibers, and loosening and discussing viscidity’s; and therefore excellent in all paralytic cases, and the decays and defluxions attending old age. They are also very diuretic, and serviceable in abscesses and dropsies.”
The most comprehensive account of mustard as an internal remedy comes from Dr. A. C. Gunn (1847, 705), who moved from London to frontier Tennessee. “The white mustard seed is one of the most valuable medicines and certain remedies that can be presented in the disordered functions of the stomach, liver and bowels. In twenty years’ practice I may with great certainty say, that I have performed some of the most astonishing cures with this apparently simple remedy; particularly in cases of intemperate persons, and apparently worn-out constitutions, or general debility of the system.”

### TASTE:

pungent

### TISSUE STATE:

depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Old, worn-out constitutions, where the digestive tract has been much abused by the use of alcohol and stimulants, or is simply in old-age decline.
Delicate, sensitive, elderly people who easily catch cold and come down with respiratory infections that tend to viscosity and chronicity.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
All pervasive depression, despondency, and despair; comes out of nowhere, for no reason; overcast, dark and gloomy (flower essence).
RESPIRATION
Severe colds, numbness of the limbs, loss of appetite, failure of sleep, weakness of nerves, depression of spirits, with general debility of the whole system.
Sinusitis (if the abdomen and lower extremities are cold, a mustard foot bath for 10 minutes).
Asthma and shortness of breath, wheezing cough.
Pneumonia (sinapism; mustard pack on chest).
DIGESTION
Indigestion, or sense of weight after eating; heartburn, sickness or pain at the pit of the stomach.
Used to prevent indigestion from eating fats and difficult to digest meat.
Cramp, pain in the sides and lower part of the body.
Relaxed bowels, flatulence, cramp, and habitual costiveness.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Torpor and other morbid affections of the liver.
SKIN
Deficient perspiration, gravel, unhealthy state of the urine, disorders of the skin and kidneys.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis (sinapism; mustard pack on joint).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Internal: a moderately hot mustard should be used. The adult dose is a half to whole teaspoonful of the seed, 3x/day (before lunch, after dinner, and before bed). Persist for two to six months in chronic cases to receive the full benefit; improvement is not generally felt the first week. The seeds should be swallowed whole. They act gently through the digestive tract, the seeds passing whole with the loss of a little mucilage along the way. External: mustard packs can be purchased already made. If making them oneself, do not touch the mustard directly to the skin or it will burn.

### CAUTIONS:

Can burn the skin.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (7), A. C. Gunn (1, 2, 4, 6, 8, 10–14), John Quincy (1, 2), Edward Bach (3), Otto Wolff (5).

## Brassica oleracea. Cabbage.

Cabbage is an ancient horticultural development from the wild kale of European seacoasts, sometimes naturalized elsewhere. Due to its maritime environment, it is rich in sodium, chlorine, and iodine. It also contains sulphur and phosphorus, which contribute to the thiols that make it fiery. “When cabbage is combined with the meal, we have a considerably greater amount of minerals, but cabbage is extremely hard to digest and, due to the large amount of phosphorus and sulphur, is acid in its reactions” (Clymer, 1933, I:37). It is said that cabbage was the chief medicine of the Romans, before the appearance of Greek medicine.
Cabbage contains a high amount of sulphur and is warming and stimulating. It has long been used as a stimulant for depressed circulation, skin, and mucosa. It has an ancient reputation in gangrene and ulceration. Cabbage juice is still widely used by nutritional practitioners but is less commonly mentioned in the literature of professional herbal medicine. It is a popular remedy for ulceration of the stomach, specific to conditions where the problem is due to tissue depression. In people who are suffering from overactive heat, burning, and acidity, it will cause irritation, diarrhea, and even bloody stool. It is for the inflammation that arises from depression, where the tissues are understimulated. The remedy is prepared from a ripe cabbage head, juiced or chopped and pressed through a sieve.
Cabbage leaves are sometimes used as an external compress. It was highly recommended as a specific on the lungs to prevent or cure pleurisy. A leaf in the bra is a modern folk remedy (usually very successful) to help the milk come in and prevent or cure mastitis. For use in a poultice, “the stem should be cut out, and the rough inequalities bruised with a rolling pin” writes Mrs. Child (1837, 140).
Cabbage in large doses depresses the thyroid; hence it has been used in hyperthyroidism, but is contraindicated in low thyroid. It is paradoxical that it should be a stimulant and yet reduce hyperthyroidism.

### TASTE:

pungent • warm • stimulating

### TISSUE STATE:

depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Nervous agitation, anxiety, examination fear, depression, insomnia, nightmares.
Hyperthyroidism; antagonist to the thyroid.
HEAD
Migraine (external).
RESPIRATION
Tonsillitis, laryngitis (gargle).
Dry cough, asthma, pain in the side and pleurisy (external).
DIGESTION
Congested liver (external and internal).
Stomach ulcers, ulcerative colitis, irritable bowel syndrome, intestinal worms, and constipation.
Gastrointestinal debility, especially colon.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Cystitis, burning, discharges, difficult urination, and inflamed prostate.
FEMALE
Painful periods (external).
Mastitis (external).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis, rheumatism, muscle pain, sciatica, gout (external).
SKIN
Gangrenous wounds, burns, carbuncles (external).
OTHER
Chronic fatigue.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Almost any member of the kale family can be used, but the red cabbage is preferred. It can be prepared as a juice, syrup, decoction, compress, or poultice. The properties change with the preparation. The raw plant is most pungent and heating. The cooked leaves are mildly heating. The broth is used as an intestinal cleanse in traditional German folk medicine. Arnold Ehret, of “mucusless” fame, called it “the intestinal broom.” Lacto-fermentation produces sauerkraut, which is sour and salty; used to lose weight and restore intestinal flora. Even the leaf, laid on the part, as a stimulant, can be used.

### CAUTIONS:

Cabbage can burn the skin and mucosa. Can depress thyroid function.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Dioscorides (13), William Buchan (5), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli, Maurice Mességué (1, 3, 4, 10, 12, 14).

## Calendula officinalis.

Calendula is a member of the composite family native to Europe but widely grown for its beautiful golden orange flowers and occasionally naturalized elsewhere. In a mild climate calendula blooms throughout “the calends of the year” (Gerard, 1633), also entitling it to the title “herbal sunshine.” It is suited for “places where the sun doesn’t shine” (acupuncturist Chris Hafner), such as the glands under the neck, axilla, breasts, groin, and so forth. Calendula contains flavonoids (isoquercitrin, rutin), polysaccharides (immune stimulants), bitters (triterpene glycosides), resins, volatile oils, and minerals (including iodine).
Calendula was classified as warm in the first degree (diaphoretic) and neutral with regard to damp and dry (it is both drying and moistening in different situations). It was well known as a folk remedy when Samuel Hahnemann adopted it into the homeopathic materia medica as a specific topical dressing for wounds, sores, burns, and infections. It improves internal drainage from purulent wounds, indicating an affinity to the lymphatic system and the veins. The indications for internal use have not been well developed in homeopathy; they come to us from herbal sources. David Hoffmann recently commented that St. John’s wort is the topical dressing of choice in Europe, whereas calendula is more popular in America. Both of these uses reflect homeopathic influence.
Calendula possesses at least seven main properties: (1) it is applied externally to wounds as an antiseptic, bacteriostatic, and hemostatic, (2) used internally it is therapeutic for swollen glands and lingering, unresolved infections, cleansing the lymphatic glands and ducts, (3) it lowers high enzyme counts from damaged liver, (4) it soothes the digestive mucosa, and other mucous membranes, (5) it warms the stomach, drives heat to the periphery, thins fluids, and causes sweating in fever, (6) it promotes the period, and (7) it is a traditional European peasant tonic taken to prevent sickness in winter—what we would call today an “immune tonic.” Neurological changes have been reported from its use but its action in this sphere it not well documented, or at least, not well known to this author.
Calendula is most famous as a wound remedy. It can be used to treat a minor scratch or serious laceration. It prevents the appearance of pus and inflammation, encouraging the body to heal the tissues at its own pace. However, if pus has formed, it keeps the inflammation from spreading, lessens the pain, slowly resolves the pus, and moves the debris away through the adjacent lymphatics. It is often used as a gentle application on sunburn, burns, sores, ulcers, insect bites, bruises, and other topical injuries, but it has a specific affinity for swollen, hot, painful, pus-filled tissue, especially where there is no vent. It is particularly effective on cat scratches. Because it is salty, calendula can be considered an emollient. It keeps wounds from hardening and forming scar tissue, yet it can also be used for hard-to-cure wounds—here combine with horsetail (Tietze, 1996, 40).
By observing its actions on the drainage from wounds, we realize that calendula is an effective lymphatic remedy and can be used topically or internally for swollen glands. It helps resolve stagnation in the lymphatics from wounds, gland removal, or sickness. In this capacity it is assisted by its immune stimulating contents. It may help the liver through the extensive immunological tissue found in that organ, or through the mild bitterness of its leaves, stimulating the liver and gallbladder. It has not been known, however, to increase bile production. Its action on the digestion is mostly through soothing the mucosa, reducing inflammation and perhaps immune overactivity.
In traditional Greek medicine it was classified as “warm in the first degree,” meaning that it promotes sweating. Herbalist Matthew Becker commented to the author that calendula is “one of the best diaphoretics for deep fever,” and is especially indicated when the bones hurt. In this he compares it to boneset.
Although I am loath to pick a fight with the ancient authors, who knew Greek medicine much better than I do, it is my experience that calendula is warm in the second and third degrees as well as the first. That is to say, it thins fluids to remove them from the body (especially pus), and it warms the stomach to drive heat to the periphery. I personally have a hot stomach and find calendula to be too warming, and very irritating, to my stomach.
Generally, plants hot in the third degree are antiseptics because they so “warm” or stimulate the body that impurities are sent to the surface, thus cleansing the interior of food and lodging for bacteria. This is a different definition from that of modern science, which views antiseptics as substances with the capacity to kill bacteria. In traditional and holistic medicine great value is placed on changing the environment, so that bacteria and parasites have to move. Dr. R. Swinburne Clymer (1933, I:163) explained antiseptic qualities of calendula by the fact that the leaves contain high levels of organic iodine. “In septic sore throat, in fact, in all throat inflammations, the combination of calendula, echinacea, and myrrh should be the first combination to be applied.” He also used calendula “in the treatment of ulcers and cancers of the stomach,” sometimes in conjunction with Hydrastis.
Clymer preferred a nonalcoholic tincture or infusion of the dehydrated green herb, which is not the usual preparation. This would contain more of the vegetable bitters. He continues: “Internally it is a mild stimulant, and, as in cancer and tumors of the stomach, a normalizing and healing agent. In hot infusion it influences the circulation toward the surface and is diaphoretic. Its nature being to heal, it is valuable in formulas in pneumonia and other internal ailments where inflammations exist. It is also a soothing antispasmodic nervine, and gently influences the menstrual flow. It is valuable as an alterative to cleanse the blood stream in strumous diseases”—that is, lymphatic swellings.
Julia Graves writes me from Normandy, “You do not mention Calendula as an emmenagogue, however this is one of the most basic all-over-Europe traditional indications. Also for dysmenorrhea.” She explains: “It stimulates upana vayu, the downward bearing wind, or in other words, really increases the life force in the pelvic region. When I was young, an elderly farmer lady told me that she was using calendula tea for all her female farm ‘interns’ (students, etc., who wanted to spend some time on her organic farm) to relieve dysmenorrhea. She said it always helped.”
As an “immune tonic” European peasants gathered the flowers throughout the year and threw them in soups in the fall and winter to warm and protect against wind and chill. In this regard it is an analog to astragalus. It prepares for the stress of winter by removing old lymphatic congestion and lingering infections.
Calendula flowers have a mild, sweet, slightly warm taste (similar to astragalus), and I used to classify the herb based on this, but Keith Robertson, NIMH, co-director of the Scottish School of Herbal Medicine, pointed out to me that the flowers are bitter, pungent, and even salty as well. It turns out I was only tasting the petals, which are basically sweet. He was (rightly) using the flower base as well.

### TASTE:

sweet, bitter, pungent, salty • warm • resinous, astringent

### TISSUE STATE:

depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Yellow look about the eyes; tired eyes, feels and looks “bone weary”; sometimes the facial skin is red and dry; generalized yellow complexion.
Tongue; slightly puffy, pink or red papillae down the sides of the tongue [spleen area], indicating unresolved lymphatic inflammation.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Heavy depression; candida history.
LYMPHATICS
Swollen glands; residual after infections; unresolved infections.
Inflammation of the lymphatic ductwork, resulting in pain in the upper left side of the left chest (rarely, right) due to congestion of the thoracic duct.
RESPIRATION
Bronchitis, asthma; associated with old, lingering infections.
Deep cough; mucus yellow, green, sticky (combine with anise to thin mucus).
DIGESTION
Tongue slightly puffy and atonic, with indentations; heat in the digestive mucosa.
Gum disease, stomach irritation, ulcers, hemorrhoids.
Mouth ulcers, stomach upset, nausea, vomiting, ulcers, diarrhea; soothes torn-up mucosa.
Sluggish liver; high enzyme counts; viral hepatitis.
FEMALE
Vaginal wall, irritated or dry, bleeding, tearing at childbirth.
Vaginal warts.
Fibroids at menopause or after partus.
Leucorrhea.
OTHER
Lacerations, abrasions, burns, stings, bruises, blisters, chicken pox, measles, frostbite, acne; with redness, tenderness, swelling (external).
Diaper rash; rash between the legs in damp, hot weather.
Septic conditions, where tissue life is threatened.
Prevents and lessens scar formation.
Neuralgia, cramp.
Positive psychological effect for people with fear of cancer.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The flowers are collected throughout the calends of the year and dried. Fresh flowers produce too watery an extract so they are traditionally dried for use in infusion or tincture. Fresh calendula flowers and leaves can be dried for direct external or internal use. Tincture, 10–15 drops up to 4x/day (Daniel Gagnon), 1–60 drops (John William Fyfe).

### CAUTIONS:

Long used as a food and generally regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Chris Hafner, William Boericke, Matthew Wood (1), Kim Dudley (3), Matthew Becker (8), Samuel Hahnemann (15), Aviva Romm (7), Dorothy Hall (12–14), Julia Graves (10, 16), Yadubir Sinha (17), William LeSassier (2, 4, 21).

## Capsella bursa-pastoris. Shepherd’s Purse.

Shepherd’s purse is a member of the mustard or Brassicaceae family native to Europe but extensively naturalized on disturbed earth in the temperate regions. It is rather unique in the family, partly for its taste and partly for the shape of its seedpod. In addition to the pungence we expect from the mustard family, shepherd’s purse has a salty taste. It has a urinous or seaweedy smell, depending on the nose. The ripe seedpod looks like a traditional European shepherd’s purse (bursa pastoris), hence the common and Latin names. (These purses were made from the scrotum of a goat.) Capsella contains flavonoids, carotenoids, vitamins A, K, and C, potassium, amino acids, and volatile oils (including camphor).
The old authors were at odds about whether it was hot or cold, but all agreed that it was drying or astringent. “It stops all sorts of bleeding whether inward, as spitting, vomiting, pissing of blood, or overflowing of the loches (lochia, after birth), or courses in women; or outward, as that of wounds; and cures all sorts of fluxes of the belly of what kind soever. It cools or represses inflammations and Anthonie’s Fire; cleanses mattering or running ears, and heals green wounds, … closing their lips; and is an especial thing for wounds of the head” (Salmon, 1710, 1035). I agree with those who consider it warming, because even when it “cools inflammations,” as Salmon says, it acts on those conditions that need a stimulant to remove toxic matter (erysipelas, St. Anthony’s Fire). Furthermore, it acts best on bleeding conditions where the blood is dark and oozing—where a stimulant is needed. Thus it contrasts with that other great hemostatic, yarrow. The latter is a “cooling antihemorrhagic” indicated in excited, red hemorrhaging.
Dr. Johann Gottfried Rademacher, the early-nineteenth-century Saxon Paracelsian, extended our knowledge of shepherd’s purse from limited traditional indications. He noted that it was originally used for three basic purposes. The first was to staunch bleeding. However, this property was questioned by the science of his day because Capsella does not contain tannins and at that time tannins were considered the basis upon which plants stopped bleeding. In folk medicine, however, stimulants were used to stop bleeding. They work by increasing circulation to the extremities, which can soak up to twenty times their normal content of blood, thus taking blood away from the wound. The second traditional use of shepherd’s purse in Rademacher’s time was to stop diarrhea. This is another form of flux for which stimulants are suited as well as astringents (cf. Erigeron). The third use was to stem intermittent fever.
Rademacher used Capsella several times for chronic diarrhea due to relaxed or depressed intestines (not from the liver or gallbladder) and found it smartly effective, but he did not try it in intermittent fever, since this is usually complicated with the liver and gallbladder (the chills run through the autonomic and damage the timing and release of bile).
One day Rademacher accidentally discovered the fourth major usage for which shepherd’s purse is now most famous. He was called to a woman suffering from kidney stones. The abdominal cavity was full of water and the lower extremities were edematous. The urine was red in color from bloody sediments. He gave shepherd’s purse 30 drops 5x/day to relieve the bleeding and was surprised to find that it removed vast quantities of the sediment. He let her continue, all of the sand passed, she started to urinate more, the edema disappeared, and she returned to good health. Since that time he tried it in many similar cases and eventually recommended it as a remedy for bloody, sedimentous urine. He also found that it removed sand so small that it could not be seen with the naked eye and deduced that Capsella might cleanse out hardening and plugging up in any organ, but especially the fine tubes of the kidneys. Since this time shepherd’s purse has been used as a diuretic as well as for bloody diarrhea, bloody urine, and bleeding from the uterus.
A further key to understand this plant is provided by Maria Treben. She is the only author I am aware of who speaks of the action of shepherd’s purse on atrophied muscles. Capsella is suited to conditions where muscle tone is poor. When the uterus does not expel the blood quickly, it sits and clots and is dark, the muscular wall atrophies, and fibroids may form. This is a good remedy for pre-menopausal women with uterine weakness, fibroids, profuse bleeding, and dark, clotted blood. When given in a well-indicated case, the woman may feel the uterus lift and move into the appropriate position within an hour of administration (Isla Burgess, Matthew Wood).
I sent some of the dried herb to a woman in Mississippi who was suffering from a continual uterine hemorrhage. She felt the uterus lift up shortly after taking an infusion and was permanently cured. I still get Christmas cards from her occasionally, twenty years later. My friend Lise Wolff, herbalist (AHG), of Minneapolis, passed on a similar case. A thirty-nine-year-old woman had a heavy, thick, dark menstrual discharge with cramping. After shepherd’s purse the period was fine and she conceived a healthy baby boy. In another case a woman reported that the remedy made her feel “sparkling and warm inside” the womb.
Here’s an old case history. “Dr. von Ehrenwall relates a recent case of puerperal hemorrhage, which had defied all the ordinary remedies and for which, at the suggestion of a neighbor, he tried an infusion of the Shepherd’s purse weed, with the result that the bleeding stopped after the first teacupful of the infusion had been taken a few minutes” (Fernie, 1914, 470).
Here is a case involving fibroids. A fifty-three-year-old woman, somewhat thin and dry, was suffering from uterine fibroids with continual bleeding. Shepherd’s purse tincture stopped the bleeding within the first few weeks. A year later an x-ray showed that the fibroid had migrated from the muscle to the inner wall of the uterus and could now easily be removed. The radiologist mentioned that the shape of the uterus had returned to normal from an elongation. I felt confident of shepherd’s purse in this case because the woman had some inguinal hernias repaired in the past and had extreme bloating before the period—both characteristic symptoms.
Shepherd’s purse is indicated when the walls of the bladder are atrophic, leading to weak expulsion of urine. Deposits and sediments may build up and the urine is heavy, dark, and in some cases, bloody. It is also a remedy for abdominal hernia, a condition that is often seen when the other pelvic muscles are weak and atrophic.
An understanding of the mental symptoms associated with shepherd’s purse was developed by Dr. G. F. Parks, a nineteenth-century American eclectic. “It works best in those women that are of a high-strung, ambitious nature, who generally do the work of two or three ordinary women. I say women, as it is a woman’s remedy, although I have had good results with it in some of my male patients. For these I give it in acute gonorrhea where they just slobber. But in these cases they must be of that high-strung nature, exceedingly anxious to get rid of that drain.” He also used it for teenage girls passing large amounts of clear, limpid urine, free of albumin. They are generally the sort who “are anxious to get out into the world to do something for themselves.” He describes many of the traditional uses in addition (Ellingwood’s Therapeutist, 1909, 3:12).

### TASTE:

pungent, salty • slightly diffusive

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, excitation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Women; thin to medium build; tendencies of muscles to atrophy; uterine fibroids; subinvolution of the uterus.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Ambitious, high-strung, anxious; anxious to recover.
Strong above, weak below.
CARDIOVASCULAR
Cardiovascular stimulating tonic; promotes circulation to the extremities, increases coronal perfusion, reduces blood pressure, may increase myocardial tone.
DIGESTION
Peptic ulceration, bloody vomitus.
Acute or chronic diarrhea; irritable bowel syndrome, hemorrhoids, passive intestinal hemorrhage, i.e., dark, oozing.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Sluggish kidney and bladder function; heavy, dark, concentrated urine (without fever), frequent desire to pass urine; deposit of phosphates, sediment, blood; edema; irritation of the urinary tract, cystitis.
FEMALE
Chronic menorrhagia; menses occurs too frequently or continue too long or are almost constant; barely recovers from one period when another starts; oozing, dark, clotted blood; uterine hemorrhage with violent cramping.
Leucorrhea, oozing, bloody.
Pain in the back or general bruised sensation; sore pain in womb on rising.
Uterine fibroids (cf. Achillea); oozing discharge of blood, dark and clotted.
Menstrual hemorrhage; sluggish, slow, oozing, dark; miscarriage; discharge between periods.
Postpartum; subinvolution of the uterus; oozing, dark, clotted bleeding; Capsella will often lift the uterus even after one dose.
EXTREMITIES
Rheumatism (apply green herb).
Gout, acute attacks; or other conditions where uric acid is elevated.
Atrophy of muscles and limbs.
Hernia; weakness of the pelvic ring.
Varicose veins.
FEVER
Intermittent fever; malaria, protozoal fever.
WOUNDS
Wounds that ooze and won’t stop bleeding; nosebleed.
Bruises, strains.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The aboveground plant or the entire plant including roots is picked at flowering in the spring and extracted fresh in alcohol. The hemostatic properties are said to be lost on drying. Tincture, 30 drops (J. G. Rademacher); 15–30 drops, 3x/day (Daniel Gagnon); 5–60 drops (John William Fyfe); 1–5 drops (Matthew Wood).

### CAUTIONS:

Large or persistent doses can cause heart palpitations. Not to be used during pregnancy. Depresses thyroid function.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (4–5, 7–11, 13, 19–21), J. G. Rademacher (6–8), William Boericke (7–10), Daniel Gagnon (15), George Park (2), Maria Treben (10, 15, 17), Matthew Wood (1, 3, 11, 13), Isla Burgess (13), Christopher Menzies-Trull (4, 5, 6, 14, 18–19).

## Carduus marianus. Milk Thistle.

See Silybum marianum.

## Carica papaya. Papaya.

This fruit is used, fresh or dried, as a digestive aid. It emulsifies fats, begins the breakdown of proteins, and converts starches to maltose. “We think of it where patient complains an hour or so after meals of pain in the bowels, general distress during digestion in the stomach and bowels. Useful in failing digestion in fevers, digestive disorders in pregnancy [but not early pregnancy, see below], catarrh of the stomach [watery mucus in stomach]. In neuralgic pains, pains occurring before meals or continued pains it is of no value. The powder as a solvent of the false membrane of diphtheria is of great value” (Petersen, 1905, 110).
“Women in Sri Lanka eat papaya fruit as a natural contraceptive. Papain, a powerful protein breaking enzyme, attacks progesterone, a hormone essential for conception. Papain becomes 24 times more active in the blood stream by interacting with a protein from the blood, and confirms papaya fruits’ ability as a natural contraceptive” (Christopher Menzies-Trull, 2003, 511).

## Carthamus tinctorius. Safflower.

Safflower is a member of the Asteraceae, or sunflower, family native to central Asia, from which place it has been brought both East and West, as a crop, and so introduced into medicine from Europe to China. It is used in Arabic medicine—“Carthamine cleanses the three natures, bitter, sweet, and salty” (Chalid Ottway, 1933, 167). In traditional Chinese medicine and martial arts safflower is used to remove blood stagnation, adhesions, and bruises. In Western herbalism it is used to cleanse the blood of septic infection and bring rashes to the surface. These symptom-pictures may be united: we see an overall tendency to stagnant and septic blood.
Safflower is indicated in bruises where there is a septic quality to the blood, from reinjury or from limited circulation. Like its cousins achillea and arnica it is suited to cases where the bruise has a red and blue aspect, indicating recent injury, with active oxidation or fever, but it has a darker cast, indicating stagnation. In addition it acts markedly on vascular spasm, so that it controls the blood flow via the nervous system, and probably also has some action on clotting and internal reabsorption of blood from a wound, like yarrow and arnica.
I had a case where a woman was brought in by her partner, pretty much against her will, in a sulky mood. Several years previous she had received a bad injury to a hand in a machine accident, almost costing her the hand and leaving it somewhat paralyzed. It was still noticeably blue and gray, and the fingers were subject to Raynaud’s disease (turning white or purple and freezing in the winter). Safflower did her so much good that she called me back to sincerely thank me and to apologize for her bad attitude. I felt rewarded both by her kind change of heart and by the great lesson I learned in this case: safflower is not only for bruises, but for Raynaud’s disease and for neurovascular spasm in the blood vessels. I have since lost track of that woman, but I would like to tell her: “You have helped many people since I learned from you this valuable indication.”
As a stimulant to the circulation, safflower moves the blood to the surface to detoxify in fevers and was traditionally used to bring the rash out to the skin in smallpox, thus preserving the internal organs and nervous system from damage by toxins. I remember a case where a young mother caught chicken pox from her baby. Already weak and feeble, she was about the last of my regular clients I would have wished this on. She had a fever of 105°F for five days before she called me (she was also impoverished). I made a house call on a Sunday afternoon when the stores were closed. I had no safflower tincture with me, but fortunately she had the oil in her refrigerator. A teaspoonful brought the fever down quickly and after a few days on this she was well recovered.
Any remedy for congested blood is likely to be a uterine medicine. The late Matthew K. Cameron (1989, 129), an herbalist in Toronto, wrote that safflower “will restore the menstrual discharge and stop chronic blood discharge from the uterus.”
Safflower cooking oil is said to have a positive effect on the liver and pancreas. By stirring and cleaning the blood safflower herb detoxifies the portal circulation and decongests the intestine, removing constipation, bloating, and gas and improving blood supply to these organs. However, it also has an influence on the metabolism, especially involving sugar. Cholesterol-reducing properties are attributed to the oil and there may be an influence on the positive use of lipids, cholesterols, and sugar. When the flowers are aged the volatile oils interact with oxygen to form a sweet compound that is nourishing to the endocrine system.
The first I ever heard about safflower was from a conventional, middle-aged man who came into our alternative style herb store looking for safflower. He brought out a copy of the herbal by Penny C. Royal, which I had never gotten much use out of—it just lists names of diseases and symptoms without a pattern. He said this book nailed his condition exactly: safflower was for hypoglycemic persons who easily got sore muscles on exertion from lactic acid trapped in them. That was much too exact a symptom for that book, but I looked at the page he was holding out to me and there it was. That is about the only specific symptom in Penny C. Royal’s herbal. And I remembered it after that, and also the fact that his eyelids were very red, in case that had anything to do with safflower—jury still out on that one, twenty years later.
Like its cousin arnica, safflower is good for bruises or trauma with shock. A woman reacted badly to the cement used by her dentist to fix a crown. Even while in the dental chair her mouth started to burn and she became agitated. Three weeks later she came to see me. She was agitated all day, slept little, and always awoke with the burning in her mouth. Clearly she was still reacting to the cement. And just as clearly, she was in shock. Her tongue was somewhat blue, her pulse raised and choppy, her mind constantly afflicted, her skin sweaty. I didn’t have arnica (for shock and congealed blood) so I gave safflower tincture. This calmed her down right away. She needed the dose several times an hour at first but this brought her the first security since the original trauma three weeks previous. Meanwhile, I had her start a detoxification program that I won’t go into here.

### TASTE:

sweet, slightly pungent • warm

### TISSUE STATE:

depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Stagnant and congealed blood; septic conditions of the blood; incomplete circulation; uric acid buildup.
Bluish-reddish complexion (cf. Achillea, Arnica).
HEAD
Hysteria, melancholy, illusions.
Red, irritated eyelids (?).
RESPIRATION
Cough, bronchitis, asthma, tuberculosis.
Hardened phlegm.
DIGESTION
Poor appetite; heartburn; pain, distention, and fullness in the epigastrium; constriction in the chest due to poor digestion; wind and gas.
Stomach ulcer.
Diverticulitis (cf. Achillea).
Diarrhea and constipation.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Liver sluggish; lack of bile, constipation, obstruction, jaundice.
HEART AND CIRCULATION
Strengthens the heart.
Cholesterol, supposed to lower (fixed oil in cooking).
Pelvic congestion in women, lower abdominal pains, clotted blood, obstructed flow, menstrual pain, abdominal masses.
KIDNEYS
Increases the flow of urine; kidney stones.
FEMALE
Amenorrhea and menorrhagia.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL, EXTREMITIES
Arthritis, gout; poor circulation to the periphery; cool, moist skin of hands and arms.
Raynaud’s disease (cf. Achillea, Xanthoxylum).
Hypoglycemic persons who get sore muscles easily; sore muscles from retention of lactic acid.
Prevents soreness after exercise.
FEVER
Fever with rash, septic tendencies; brings the rash to the surface, cleanses the blood, regulates the periphery, normalizes perspiration.
Children’s fevers; chicken pox, scarlet fever, measles, mumps. In the old days it was used for smallpox.
Boils, hives.
WOUNDS
Bruises with septic tendency; blue/red color to the injury.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Infusion of the flowers, 1 tablespoonful per cup, 1–3x/day. Dose of the tincture, 3 drops, 1–3x/day (Matthew Wood).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1, 12, 17, 21–24), Chinese herbalism (1, 24), Louise Tenney (1, 3, 5, 6, 10, 11, 17, 22), Penny C. Royal (19), Matthew Wood (2, 4, 16, 18, 20, 24), Phyllis Light (18), Maude Grieve (22), Chalid Ottway (3, 5, 7, 23), Matthew Cameron (16).

## Carum carvi. Caraway.

Caraway is native to the Middle East and Europe, but naturalized widely in Asia and North America. Plinius says it is named for the province of Caria, in Asia Minor. The names Carum carvi and caraway come down to us through the Arabic karawya. However, no nation seems to love the seeds (or fruits, really) more than the Germans and Dutch. They cultivate and use the seeds in cooking, pastry, and baking.
I was surprised to find caraway growing in northeastern Minnesota, close to Lake Superior, where the climate is brutal, windy, and cold. Later I was informed that it is grown as a crop and widely naturalized on the Canadian prairies. That grown in northern latitudes is supposed to have more activity. Caraway seed (fruit) contains volatile oils (including carvone and limonene), flavonoids, polysaccharides, protein, fixed oil, and calcium oxalate.
Caraway is a bitter, pungent carminative used to remove gas and improve digestion in European cooking and medicine. The German phytotherapist Dr. Rudolf Weiss (1988, 69) established a context from which to view caraway. He places the three carminative umbelliferae—caraway, fennel, and anise—on a continuum from the most bitter to the most sweet, respectively. The former is most provocative of secretions whereas the latter is most nutritive. Along with cumin, these three constitute the “four greater hot seeds” of Greek medicine—used to stimulate digestion.
Caraway fruit contains volatile oils and fixed oils, so it especially has an action on the digestion of fatty foods and the lipid metabolism. Overall, the seed or fruit is pungent, oily, bitter, and slightly astringent, with a warm temperature. Yet it has some cooling constituents as well—limonene and flavonoids. The bitter tinge stimulates the flow of digestive juices and reduces inflammation. The astringence gives it an action on atonic digestive mucosa and autonomic debility. It has a normalizing effect—warming up tissues that are tense and tightening tissues that are loose. These properties together give caraway an affinity to sub-acid states of the digestive tract with tension, flatus, and the beginnings of inflammation; or with lack of tissue tone, pallor from poor nutrition, and diarrhea. It also can be used as a gargle for mild inflammation of the throat, or respiratory conditions with slight irritation, before serious inflammation has set in and the cough-reflex is overstimulated. Consequently it is used for bronchial spasm, asthma, and as an expectorant.
“In olden times,” writes Grieve (1931, 157), caraway “was deemed to confer the gift of retention, preventing the theft of any object which contained it, and holding the thief in custody within the invaded house. In like manner it was thought to keep lovers from proving fickle (forming the ingredient of love potions), and also to prevent fowls and pigeons from straying. It is an undoubted fact that tame pigeons, who are particularly fond of the seeds, will never stray if they are given a piece of baked Caraway dough in their cootie.”

### TASTE:

pungent, bitter • oily, slightly astringent and diffusive

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, atrophy, relaxation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Pallor in young girls.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Hysteria.
RESPIRATION
Deafness (caraway seed tincture in oil of sweet almonds; a drop in the ear).
Earache.
Sore throat, laryngitis (gargle), colds, bronchitis.
Incipient respiratory infections, especially in children.
Asthmatic spasm.
Pleurisies, pains, and stitches in the sides.
DIGESTION
Bad breath.
Lack of appetite.
“A weak and enfeebled stomach” (Salmon); “a cold, languid stomach” (Fernie).
Flatulent dyspepsia, fermentation in stomach.
Intestinal colic, especially in infants and children.
Hiatal hernia, stomach ulcers.
Diarrhea.
Settles the stomach after taking nauseant medicines.
Heal the bowels after the use of purgatives.
FEMALE
Menstrual cramps.
Deficient menstrual flow.
Deficient milk production.
OTHER
Bruises (external).
Old, stinking ulcers, boils, fractures, contracted and benumbed members (externally, anointed in palm oil).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The fruit is eaten plain or can be made into an infusion or cordial. Crush them to release the volatile oils, and pour 1 cup of boiling water over 1 teaspoonful, steep 10–15 minutes. Soak the crushed fruits in wine to produce a cordial.

### CAUTIONS:

Overuse can cause drying. The distilled oil can be neurotoxic.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1–15, 18, 20), Dioscorides (1), William Salmon (3, 8, 9, 11, 17, 22), Maude Grieve (1, 2), Rudolf Weiss, Robert Dale Rogers (13), Louise Tenney (17, 19), Meyrick (quoted by Grieve) (21), W. T. Fernie (1, 4, 11).

## Centaurium erythraea. Centaury.

Centaury is a member of the gentian family long used in European medicine as a digestive bitter. The name comes from Chiron the Centaur, master of arts and crafts, including medicine. He used this plant to cure himself of a serious wound. Centaury contains a bitter iridoid (amarogentin), bitter triterpenoids, phenols, alkaloids, and flavonoids.
This popular European folk remedy is still so widely picked by home herbalists that wild populations have been placed under environmental protection in parts of Europe. It has long been used in a manner similar to gentian as a bitter tonic to invoke secretions from the stomach, gallbladder, and liver and to reduce chills and fever in malarial and intermittent fever. It is considered strengthening in convalescence—in stomach weakness following acute disease—and old age. A specific indication is given by Fernie (1914, 86): “For languid digestion, with heartburn after food, and a want of appetite, the infusion prepared with cold water, an ounce of the herb to a pint, is best.”
“The flower essence has indications for servility and lack of courage to say no and stand up for oneself. These are analogous to its action on the gallbladder, the organ associated with the will and courage in traditional Chinese medicine” (Julia Graves).

### TASTE:

bitter, acrid, pungent

### TISSUE STATES:

constriction, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Convalescence; run-down people suffering from lack of appetite, dyspepsia, languid digestion, and heartburn, worse after eating.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Overly servile; can’t say no; lacks courage (flower essence).
Depression associated with digestive disturbance.
HEAD
Eyes (external).
DIGESTION
Sore mouth, aphthous ulcers, inflamed gums (mouthwash).
