Poor appetite; tardy, languid digestion, dyspepsia; heartburn after food, gas, ulcers.
Worms.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Weakness of the liver, gallbladder; jaundice; oversecretion of bile.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Incontinence, weakness of bladder control; in the sick, weak, and elderly.
FEMALE
Amenorrhea.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Muscular rheumatism (fibromyalgia).
FEVER
Intermittent fever, chills alternating with fever; resulting in biliousness.
SKIN
Eczema.
EXTERNAL
Wounds and sores.
OTHER
Kills lice and fleas.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The flowering plant is used, often in combination with other herbs in European herbalism.

### CAUTION:

Now considered to be environmentally endangered in some parts of Europe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Edward Bach (2), Christopher Menzies-Trull (3), Louise Tenney (1, 6–14), W. T. Fernie (6).

## Centella asiatica. Gotu Kola, Hydrocotyle.

This is an old Ayurvedic remedy that has been used in the West since the nineteenth century and is currently quite popular as a cerebral and longevity tonic. It contains a wide range of medicinally active constituents, including triterpene saponins (stimulates the skin), flavonoids, alkaloids, volatile oils (terpenoids), bitters, fatty acids, sterols, resins, tannins, proteins, and phenols. It stimulates blood flow in the capillaries, reduces venous stagnation and insufficiency, improves cholesterol balance, helps to break down aged blood cells, reduces urea in the blood (acidity), and increases circulation to the extremities and brain. Gotu kola is therefore used for mental fatigue, lower vitality, senility, and high blood pressure. It combines three of the most inactive and deteriorative tissue states: depression, relaxation, and atrophy.

### TASTE:

pungent, sweet, astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, relaxation, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Mental and physical fatigue, nervous breakdown.
Anxiety, depression.
Senility.
Loss of memory.
Drug withdrawal (cf. Avena).
HEAD
Retinal detachment.
Acne, dermal ulcers.
RESPIRATION
Sinusitis.
Tonsillitis.
Influenza.
Pleurisy.
HEART AND CIRCULATION
Hypertension, high cholesterol.
DIGESTION
Peptic ulcers.
Diarrhea, cholera.
Anal fissure, fistula, hemorrhoids.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema.
Bladder ulcerations, syphilis.
SKIN
Inflammation of the skin, involving microorganisms; lupus, scleroderma, dermal tuberculosis, psoriasis, leprosy (reduces decay), septic wounds (external).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis, rheumatism, damaged ligaments and tendons, tendinitis.
Parkinson’s disease.
Varicose veins and ulcers; static leg ulcers.
FEVER
Fever.
ENDOCRINE SYSTEM
Thyroid gland, low function contributing to “emotional depression, dry skin, cold extremities, poor digestion, weight gain, and/or little endurance” (Daniel Gagnon).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Care should be taken to obtain high quality gotu kola leaf, since this plant is native to polluted drainage ditches in India (perhaps a signature showing its excellence in clearing out the blood). Infusion, 1–3 ounces. Tincture, 12–30 drops, morning and mid-afternoon (Daniel Gagnon). Capsules, 5–10 #0 (30 to 60 grains).

### CAUTIONS:

Can produce sleeplessness if taken in the evening.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Christopher Menzies-Trull, Louise Tenney, Daniel Gagnon (23).

## Cetraria islandica. Iceland Moss.

Iceland moss is a lichen native to bare, rocky slopes in the Far North. Like most other lichens it contains mucilage. It also contains bitters. This makes it an appropriate medicine in atonic conditions of the mucosa, where there is dryness and undersecretion. Such a state is often found after a chronic bronchial catarrh with recurrent, continuous cough resulting in weakness and exhaustion. Thus, it is especially applicable in the elderly, and in asthenic (vata) persons, and those suffering from chronic and protracted coughing. It is a traditional remedy in tuberculosis. The bitters also stimulate gastric secretion, autonomic activity, and the vasomotor sphere (Weiss, 1988, 199).

### TASTE:

bitter • mucilaginous

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Weakly children, invalids, aged persons.
Asthenic (thin), pale, anemic.
RESPIRATION
Hoarseness, dryness.
Catarrh and congestion.
Coughs, dry and irritative.
Bronchitis.
Tuberculosis; repetitive coughing.
DIGESTION
Diarrhea, dysentery.
Gastritis.
FEMALE
Lactation.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The decoction is preferred to deliver the mucilage; 1 teaspoonful to a cup, morning and evening for several months (Rudolf Weiss).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1–9), Rudolf Weiss (2), Louise Tenney (1).

## Chamomilla recutita, C. matricaria, Matricaria chamomilla. German Chamomile.

Several plants go by the name chamomile, all belonging to the Asteraceae or compositae family. Two of them are used in herbal medicine, Chamomilla matricaria and Anthemis nobilis, called “German chamomile” and “Roman camomile” respectively. They have been used interchangeably since antiquity and their uses are not well differentiated in the literature: not even in homeopathy. Both contain a blue volatile oil (azulene), long considered the “active ingredient” responsible for the majority of medicinal properties. It is relaxant and antiallergenic. In addition Chamomilla contains flavonoids (cooling and relaxing), bitter sesquiterpene lactones (stimulate digestive juices), volatile oils (antipyretic and antispasmodic), mucilage (soothing, nutritious, immunostimulating), amino acids, fatty acids, and coumarins. The fresh preparations, preserving the volatile oils, are relaxant, whereas the dried preparations are bitter and promote secretions to the stomach, GI, and liver.
Chamomile is a folk medicine of great antiquity in Europe. It is primarily used for tension, pain, and fever, especially where the digestive tract is involved: especially the mouth and teeth, stomach, and intestines. It is suited to conditions where there is mental and physical tension and spasm. Unlike agrimony, which does not express anger, the chamomile person (often a child) complains, whines, and demands. They are angry and they show it. Where agrimony holds the breath, chamomile breaths out loudly to let you know they need your attention. As soon as you give it, chamomile snaps at you. It is suited to babies, “babies of any age.” Repetitive pain that becomes difficult to bear, hence menstrual cramps. The pains of chamomile are not really that terrible, but there is a low threshold of pain, and the person lets you know it. “I can’t bear it” is a common expression.
Chamomile has a long history of use in folk medicine as a sedative for cross, whining, irritated babies, and the problems incident to babyhood, such as teething, earaches, and intolerance to pain. The child is peevish, whining, complaining, angry, demands attention and petting. Demands a toy, then throws it on the floor. Whatever the age, the behavior is unmistakable. “Babies of any age,” as I say. Petulant, self-centered, intolerant of pain or not having their way, inclined to pick quarrels, yet averse to being touched, soothed, or spoken to. It is not suited to infants (Sambucus) or children (Cichorium), but to babies. In health, the chamomile person is as “sweet as a baby.”
Certain structures of the body are more susceptible to treatment by chamomile. The sensory organs (ears, eyes, teeth, and sexual organs) are especially irritable (here use the fresh preparations with their relaxing volatile oils). The sympathetic innervations involving the gastrointestinal tract and gallbladder are also strongly influenced (here use the bitter preparations). Many cases of earache and toothache have been cured with chamomile.
Chamomilla is suited to conditions where wind (tension) combines with heat (overstimulation, excitation, irritation). Thus, the fever is irregular and comes and goes; one cheek red, the other pale (homeopath Carrol Dunham). It is an old remedy for intermittent fever. William Salmon (1692, 13) writes in Practical Physick, “I have oftentimes cured tedious quartans and tertians, by giving half a pint of the crude juice of camomille, an hour before the coming of the fit, and repeating the same dose for four or five fits.”
Chamomilla “stimulates the body to put calcium in serum. Doesn’t sedate HCl production. Balances emotional and astral energies. If a lactating woman drinks chamomile tea, it will affect her nursing baby,” notes William LeSassier. “I practice in New York City, where everybody whines all the time. If they whine I send them home with chamomile and start more active treatment at the next session.”
W. T. Fernie (1914, 91) recounts that chamomile is a physician to the plant world. He relates: “It is remarkable that each chamomile is a plant physician, since nothing contributes so much to the health of a garden as a number of chamomile herbs dispersed about it. Singularly enough, if another plant is drooping and apparently dying, in nine cases out of ten it will recover if you place a herb of chamomile near it.”

### TASTE:

sweet, pungent, bitter • cooling, drying • oily

### TISSUE STATES:

irritation, constriction, stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Children, during teething, “terrible twos”; fretful, must be carried all the time; wants different things, throws them away when getting them; nightmares. Adults who act in a similar manner; i.e., “babies of any age.” Improves self-esteem. “Sweetens the soul.”
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Petulant, quarrelsome, vitiated humor; inclines to be angry and out of sorts with everything and everybody; very impatient, can hardly answer with civility; contrary to normal condition, out of humor, particularly at menses or when sick.
Whining, peevish, complaining, wants attention but rejects it; low resistance to pain.
Children whose emotional upsets cause digestive trouble.
HEAD
Dandruff.
Facial neuralgia.
Eye irritation, conjunctivitis (wash), earache.
Aphthous ulcers (canker sores) and gum disease; associated with stomach heat.
Tongue tends to be irritated along the sides; coated white-yellow in the middle.
Stiff neck.
RESPIRATION
Acute colds, fevers, flu, coughs, earache.
Bronchitis, spasmodic cough, catarrh, asthma.
DIGESTION
Acute digestive upset, coming on after vexation and anger; with heat of the face, thirst, taste of bile, nausea, vomiting of bile, anxiety, restlessness.
Colic in babies, with pain and whining.
Lack of appetite.
Cramps, colic, diverticulitis, colitis, diarrhea, constipation, irritable bowel syndrome, stomach ulcers; from stress and tension.
Hemorrhoids, anal fissure.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Liver and gallbladder upset; jaundice, gallstones, gallstone colic.
“Subacute inflammation or congestion of the liver with green stool, soreness of the anus with colic” (Menzies-Trull); green, watery diarrhea (Rolla Thomas).
FEMALE
Menstrual pain and cramping associated with great tenderness; cannot be borne with any patience; pain in the abdomen and breasts, cysts.
Pregnancy: false labor pains.
Breast disorders: mastitis, abscess (external).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema.
Enuresis.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Tightness and tension in the tendons, ligaments, muscles, and extremities; paralytic pains, with numbness and tearing, in the extremities; convulsion, spasm, tightness, rheumatic pain and neuralgia.
Bruising, cramp, rheumatic muscular pain, gout, articular pains.
Carpal tunnel syndrome.
FEVER
Incomplete fever; excitement, increased sensibility, prostration; heat partial, confined to one cheek; profuse sweat of the head. The fever does not last long, but often recurs.
Intermittent fever (alternating fever and chills with incomplete expression) (cf. Eupatorium perfoliatum).
SKIN
Abscess, pus formation, especially in the face, jaws, with tenderness and pain.
Irritation and swelling from any cause.
Eczema, psoriasis, urticaria, burning, burns, blisters, radiation burns, acne.
Impetigo.
Skin; light-, delicate-skinned people with excessive sensitivity.
OTHER
Pulse small, tense, rapid.
Car sickness, motion sickness.
Nightmares, night terrors (infusion at night).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The same conditions are met by the material herb, the tincture, and the homeopathic potencies. However, the fresh preparations preserve the relaxing volatile oils, whereas the dried herb brings out the bitters. The homeopathic preparations are made from the fresh juice of the herb. The essential oil can be used in external preparations. Some of the ingredients are only extracted in alcohol.

### CAUTIONS:

A few people are allergic to chamomile. Herbalist Daniel Gagnon, of Santa Fe, wondered just how toxic chamomile actually is. He searched twentieth-century references and found six confirmed cases of serious reaction. More than sixty million bags of chamomile tea are dispersed per day.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Rudolf Weiss, Julia Graves (4, 14), Aviva Romm (10), Christopher Menzies-Trull (6, 8, 17, 19, 21, 22, 24, 26, 27, 31–33, 36, 37), Rolla Thomas (19), William LeSassier (34), Daniel Gagnon (15, 16). The most specific information comes from homeopathic provings and clinical experience, including the following indications and authors collected by Temple Hoyne: William Bayes (9), Samuel Hahnemann (13), Carrol Dunham (28), Adolph Lippe (35), Dr. Schall, quoted by W. T. Fernie (37).

## Chelidonium majus. Celandine.

Chelidonium is a member of the poppy family native to Europe but widely naturalized in temperate regions. It possesses a bright orange sap. The doctrine of signatures led to the use of chelidonium as a decongestant for the yellow bile. However, it possesses many active properties such as we would expect in the poppy family, including isoquinoline alkaloids (antibacterial, antifungal), chelidonine, proteolytic enzymes (dissolve proteins, warts), sanguinarine (antitumor), chelidoxanthine (similar to berberine), fruit acids, flavonoids, and phenolic acids.
Chelidonium is primarily known as a liver and gallbladder remedy. According to the old authors, it is suited to conditions where the bile becomes thickened and is retained in the bile ducts, generating congestion and heat (Edwin Hale). The bile is thickened into stones and the walls of the gallbladder and ducts are inflamed. Bile backing up into the liver causes swelling and pain. The most characteristic symptoms are given in Ellingwood’s Therapeutist (1908, 2, 6, 22): “Specifically there is a yellowish or greenish discoloration of the skin with usually more or less emaciation, there is pain in the right hypochondrium, sometimes full and distinct in character, at other times a throbbing pain or a short pain which extends through to the lower border of the right scapula.”
Chelidonium is also suited to cases of migraine where the tension from the gallbladder and liver congestion moves back up through the scapula to the back of the head, from the occiput across the side of the head and into the eyes. This type of migraine is called “gallbladder headache” in traditional Chinese medicine because it follows the gallbladder meridian. Chelidonium is also one of the best remedies for migraine headache with pain coming up one side of the head or the other, with great sensitivity to light in the eyes, setting off the headache, nausea; sometimes all of this is even preceded by a sense of uneasiness in the gallbladder or the emotions. (These migraine symptoms were brought out in homeopathic provings by O. Buchmann in the mid-nineteenth century, but they have been lost to subsequent homeopathic literature and are not influential in herbalism.)
In addition to dissolving stones in the gallbladder, chelidonium has an anti-fibrous capacity that has been used to break down warts, malignant tumors (especially in the abdominal region), fibroids in the uterus, and cataract. It has also been used for edema and swollen spleen associated with gallbladder problems.
Culpeper (1653) speaks highly of the properties of celandine in eye complaints. “This is an herb of the Sun, and under the celestial Lion, and is one of the best cures for the eyes; for, all that know any thing in astrology, know that the eyes are subject to the luminaries.” He is extremely certain of its virtues here. “I can prove it doth [by] both my own experience, and the experience of those to whom I have taught it, that most desperate sore eyes have been cured by this and only this medicine.” At this time “sore eyes” often meant glaucoma. And he is confident of its uses also for cataract. “For if this does not absolutely take away the film, it will so facilitate the work, that it might be done without danger” from surgical operations. Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1973, 111) makes note of Culpeper’s enthusiasm on this point. “Bathing the eye with an infusion of the leaves and flowers of the greater celandine, favorite eye remedy of the famed herbalist of ancient times, Culpeper,” twice daily for cataract.
The psychological profile of chelidonium emphasizes gallbladder issues as much as the physical. It is associated with self-will and “taking authority,” either appropriately or inappropriately. It is a specific when people have abused “occult powers” by attempting to control them with their mind (Matthew Wood, Julia Graves).

### TASTE:

bitter • hot • penetrating, sharp, stimulating

### TISSUE STATES:

torpor, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Authority issues; too dominating, too dominated; taking authority for oneself.
Indicated in people who have abused their will power, especially in relationship to the occult and spiritual practices, or in business.
HEAD
Yellow, sallow, pallid complexion.
Migraine starting in the occiput, extending over one of the temples and into the eye; with light sensitivity and nausea; better by darkness and rest.
Cataract (external; traditionally a wash is made with goat’s milk).
Gunk in the eyes.
RESPIRATION
Pneumonia with congestion in the lower lobe of the right lung, above the liver.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Dissolves fibrous tissue: gallstones, abdominal masses, uterine fibroids, cataracts.
Congestion of the liver; pain under the right scapula.
Absence of bile production; clay-colored stools with no jaundice (also, jaundice without clay-colored stools); constipation; scybalous stools (dry, hard balls).
Congestion and heat in the gallbladder and ducts; gallstones, pain, heat, swelling.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Achy, dull, beat-up feeling in the muscles and bones; sprains, strains, influenza.
“Dull, stretchy, tired feeling, cold extremities” (Petersen, 1905, 67).
ABDOMEN
Fibrous tissue, cancer, in the abdomen or female system.
SKIN
Warts (fresh juice externally).
OTHER
“Full, pale, sallow tongue and mucous membrane, skin pale, doughy, sometimes even greenish” (Petersen, 1905, 67).
Pulse slow.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The fresh green leaves are picked in the spring and fall. Best if extracted fresh in alcohol immediately or freeze-dried. Somewhat toxic and strong; the dose need not be great: 1 drop, 1–3x/day, or the homeopathic potencies (3x–30x).

### CAUTIONS:

Toxic; dose limited to 10 drops.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (3, 5, 6, 8, 9, 10, 15), Matthew Wood (1, 2, 3), Julia Graves (1, 3), O. Buchmann (quoted by Temple Hoyne) (homeopathic provings: 4, 7, 9, 10, 11, 12), J. G. Rademacher, Chanchal Cabrera (14), Nicholas Culpeper (15), Frederick Petersen (13, 16, 17), Rolla Thomas (10).

## Chondrus crispus. Irish Moss.

Irish moss is a seaweed native to areas where fresh water and salt water meet (William LeSassier). It contains mucilage, polysaccharides, and minerals. The mineral content of chondrus is quite important. Dr. Edward Shook writes (1978, 29), “You will doubtless remember that we told you that Irish moss (Chondrus crispus) contained the organic salts of potassium phosphate (brain and nerve food), calcium phosphate (bone food), magnesium phosphate (found in every cell in the body), together with chlorides, sulfates, iodides, and bromides, all beautifully balanced in ideal proportions which could never be equaled by any trituration process man could invent.”
Chondrus is mucilaginous, sweet, and salty, thus highly fitted for use in moistening, nourishing, mineralizing, and soothing tissue. It is particularly used for congenital weakness, tuberculosis, convalescence, and old age, in cases where there is dry, irritated mucosa and especially if there is glandular enlargement (thyroid, lymphatics, prostate). It also has a special affinity to the parathyroid gland, so that it helps the body pick up calcium, strengthens the glands and veins and probably the bones. We see these types of problems in children who are “born weak,” with developmental problems and chronic glandular and respiratory trouble. The lack of calcium causes a predisposition to tuberculosis.
Because chondrus absorbs water and swells after consumption, it has a mechanical ability to fill the stomach and reduce appetite.

### TASTE:

salty, sweet • slightly warm • mucilaginous, absorbent, astringent

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Congenital weakness, endocrine weakness, convalescence, tuberculosis, old age.
Dried out, inflamed surfaces, dry, wrinkled skin, dried out mucus.
Swollen glands (thyroid, lymphatics, prostate); goiter.
Children with chronic respiratory problems, constitutional weakness.
RESPIRATION
Chronic bronchitis; chronic catarrh and swollen glands; pneumonia, tuberculosis.
DIGESTION
Bad breath.
Peptic ulcers, gastritis.
Diarrhea, dysentery.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Bladder weakness.
OTHER
Varicose veins.
Convulsions.
Parathyroid cancer.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Decoction in water or milk, infusion, powder, capsules.

### CAUTIONS:

Edible; generally regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Edward Shook, William LeSassier (4), Louise Tenney (1–3, 5, 6, 7, 9, 10), Jack Ritchason (12).

## Chrysanthemum parthenium, Tanacetum parthenium,
Pyrethrum parthenium. Feverfew.

The genus name has been changed numerous times by taxonomists, but the species name parthenium and the common name have remained in place. Parthenium refers to a young woman (gynecological uses) while feverfew comes from the Latin febrifuga—it was at one time considered an important fever remedy. It is native to stony ground in the Balkans and Greece, but widely grown for its horticultural and medicinal virtues. It contains volatile oils (beta thujone and camphor), steroidal compounds, mucilage, terpenes, tannins, resin, and melatonin.
Feverfew can be used as a general, nonspecific remedy for migraines and arthritis, or it can be prescribed on precise indications based on an understanding of the constitutional makeup of the individual and the organ affinities and energetic action of the plant. As a nonspecific it is given continuously and is palliative, rather than curative. This usage was developed through clinical research by Dr. Stewart Johnson (1984).
As a specific, feverfew is not indicated in all migraines, but especially in conditions where blood congestion and stagnation cause fullness before the period, headache, heavy bleeding, and pain. The person has a pale, blue complexion that becomes full, red, and hot, with fever or headache; sluggish and depressed digestion from poor circulation to the stomach, causing fermentation, flatulence (William LeSassier).

### TASTE:

bitter, pungent • warm

### TISSUE STATES:

stagnation, depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Stagnant blood; tendencies to varicose veins.
Nervousness and low spirits.
Headache and migraine from congestion of blood to the head, before the period; forehead full, pounding, throbbing, often involving the left eye and left temple; temple hot to the hand.
Complexion normally blue and pale, turning red and full during the headache, during fever, before the period.
Face ache, earache, tinnitus, dizziness.
Inflammation of the eyes, hordeolum (lotion).
Tonsillitis; diphtheria, diphtheritic membrane on the upper pharynx causing difficult breathing.
Coughs, wheezing, difficult breathing, viscid mucus.
Poor appetite, nausea.
Diarrhea, thread worms, flatulence; pruritus ani (unguent).
Hepatitis, jaundice.
Abdomen full, congested, with poor liver function, constipation, clay-colored stool, worms.
Renal disease, strangury, edema, gout.
Sciatica, muscle spasm.
Congestion before the period; full, red, swollen; headache; heavy bleeding, thick endometrium, clotted bleeding.
Menopausal symptoms, hot flashes.
Intermittent fever, chills, flu.
Arthritic pain; rheumatoid arthritis (external, oil).
Bites of insects, vermin, fleas, lice, scabies (wash with infusion).
Varicose veins (lotion).
Hypertension.
Panic attacks.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Leaves and flowers, fresh, dried, and taken by infusion or capsule. Somewhat abortifacient. Small, persistent doses.

### CAUTIONS:

Not to be taken during pregnancy.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (2, 3, 5, 7–20), Maude Grieve (2, 5, 12), William LeSassier (1, 3, 4, 8, 15), Christopher Menzies-Trull (6, 7, 9, 10, 11, 13, 14, 18, 20, 21, 22).

## Cichorium intybus. Chicory.

Chicory is a member of the Asteraceae family, distinguished by its rare blue flower. It was anciently used by the Greeks and Romans. Thessalus of Tralles (c. 50 CE) states that it is an herb of the sun; he reports learning his astrological medicine directly from the demigod Asclepius. The name chicory comes from the Greek kikorion. The German name wegwarten refers to its favored environmental niche and also to the old Teutonic township position known in English as “highway master” or “overseer of the roads.” To this day chicory is found along roadsides.
A plant can act as an illustration of biological principles in the human or animal organism, if we can trace out the functions that they themselves depend upon, seeing their correspondences in the animal sphere. Rudolf Steiner (1975, 131) described such a plant as “an instructor in the realm of nature.” He considered chicory to be a good example. Chicory is “an interesting subject for inquiry. One finds its effects extending fanlike in so many directions.”
Steiner, like other traditional herbalists and physicians, considered the curative faculty of chicory to be due to its bitter constituents. These have an affinity to substances in the human organism that have not yet been broken down by digestive and metabolic processes and that still contain some of their original, external identity. Bitters are used in herbalism and medicine to enhance digestion and metabolism. Chicory helps to complete these processes, and then helps in the absorption of this material, first into the walls of the intestines, then into the blood. This absorptive property Steiner attributes to the potassium in chicory. After it has strengthened digestion, absorption, and metabolism, chicory preserves the blood as it carries nutriment to the body, keeping it from becoming “slack in essential processes.” Chicory then, with the help of silica, brings the nutritive materials to the periphery of the body, where they help build the skin, bones, nerves, and muscles. Thus, chicory strengthens those tissues that digest and assimilate food, the blood that carries the nutriment, and the periphery in which the nutriment is deposited—skin, senses, nerves. It has been used for developmental problems and atrophic limbs (see below).
Chicory was classified by Galen as one of the “four lesser cold seeds,” along with lettuce, purslane, and endive. That means it is cooling and moistening. Avicenna saw chicory as the sovereign remedy for a hot liver. Moses Maimonides recommended it for both cold and hot liver. It is widely regarded by the old herbalists for eye problems, including inflammation. Dr. J. V. Cerney (1976, 120) considered endive juice (domesticated chicory) to be a specific for cataract. The liver is associated with the eye in traditional Chinese medicine. Chicory is often compared to its cousin dandelion, which is also used to facilitate liver and blood cleansing, or neatening up the metabolic economy of the body.
Father Sebastian Kneipp noted that chicory removes “mucus from the stomach, flushes out superfluous gall, purifies the liver, spleen and kidneys, and leads morbid materials out through the urine.” It also will restore the stomach that has been upset “by any kind of food,” assist digestion, and settle cramp and inflammation in the stomach.
The psychological profile of chicory was developed by Dr. Edward Bach. It is suited to people who remain in the selfish, self-centered stage of development characteristic of young children (age 5–6). Analogously, chicory root is used for developmental problems that remain stuck (hence, atrophic limbs).
The energetic profile was developed long ago. “French writers say it is contra-stimulante, and serving to correct the excitation caused by the active principles of coffee [to which it is frequently added], and therefore it suits sanguineo-bilious subjects who suffer from habitual tonic constipation. But it is ill adapted for persons whose vital energy soon flags, and for lymphatic or bloodless people its use should be altogether forbidden” (W. T. Fernie, 1914, 497). The sanguine and bilious temperaments are both equivalent to pitta in Ayurveda. The former is more prone to heat conditions, the latter to dryness and atrophy, so they also correspond with the cooling and moistening properties associated with the plant.

### TASTE:

moderately bitter and sweet • cool and moist

### TISSUE STATES:

excitation, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Childishness, selfishness, emotional immaturity, needy, clingy, dependent (flower essence).
HEAD
Headache, feverish head.
Hot, inflamed eyes; especially in children.
Amaurosis; loss of visual power in the eye.
Cataract (endive or chicory juice).
RESPIRATION
Respiratory congestion and bronchitis; especially in children.
Hay fever and asthma (endive, carrot, and celery juice).
DIGESTION
“Venous passive congestion in the digestive organs within the abdomen, and a fullness of blood in the head.”
Phlegm in the stomach; upset by food; cramp, indigestion.
Constipation or diarrhea connected with bile problems.
Malnutrition, cachexia, anemia, atrophy.
“Sick and feeble persons that have weak, hot, and feeble stomachs.”
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Heat and congestion in the liver and gall ducts; gallstones, jaundice.
“Bilious torpor”; low bile production and ejection, gallstones, constipation, anemia.
Heat and congestion in the spleen, lymphatics, liver, and kidneys; swollen glands, edema, urinary tract infections.
FEMALE
Anemia, atrophy, infertility.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis, gout; calcium and uric acid deposition.
Externally as a rub on atrophic limbs.
FEVER
Epidemic, continual, or intermittent fever.
SKIN
Pimples, welts, rashes, sores, inflamed and red eyes, mastitis.
Externally on hot sores and inflammations.
Skin affections associated with gout.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

All parts of the plant have been used, but the roasted root is most commonly available in commerce. The roots are best collected in rainy weather, when they can be more easily pulled out of the ground. Kneipp recommended a cup of the tea made from the root, morning and evening, for a period of time, until symptoms abate. Externally, “Wegwartspiritus” or poultice on inflamed, atrophied, or decayed parts. The root is often roasted to provide a coffee-substitute, but this changes the properties of the plant. It is more bitter in this aspect, so it stimulates the digestion, liver, and bowel movements. J. V. Cerney (1976, 120) recommends the juice of endive leaves, the cultivated form of the plant.

### CAUTIONS:

Edible; generally regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Edward Bach (1), Avicenna (13), Sebastian Kneipp (9, 18), Matthew Wood (6-confirmed), J. V. Cerney (5, 7), Louise Tenney (9, 11, 13, 14, 16), W. T. Fernie (3, 4, 8, 10, 12, 14, 22).

## Cinnamomum spp. Cinnamon.

Cinnamon has long been used in the medicine of the Old World. It is a small tree native to the Spice Islands. It was shipped to Rome, the Middle East, Europe, Africa, India, and China. The dried inner bark was taken in rolls (quills).
Cinnamon is warm and stimulating, so it tends to warm up the digestion and the interior, but it is also sweet and astringent, so it nourishes and tones. It is therefore indicated as a stimulating diaphoretic in weakness, tiredness, chilliness, and lack of resistance. As a stimulant it opens up the peripheral blood supply to lessen local bleeding. A tincture of the oil of cinnamon “is the most certain remedy I know in postpartum hemorrhage,” commented Rolla Thomas (1903). Finley Ellingwood (1918) combined it with oil of erigeron to make an oil famed for stopping bleeding of delivery. As a warming astringent it is excellent for some kinds of diarrhea, where it restrains fluid loss. It has been shown to have an influence over the microorganisms associated with botulism, staph infections, aflatoxin, E. coli, and candida. “Terrific for diarrhea,” comments Aviva Romm. “There are no kind of Defluxions or Weaknesses but what it is of service,” writes John Quincy (1736).

### TASTE:

sweet, pungent • warm • stimulating, astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, atrophy, relaxation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Tired, weak, cold, with low immunity, hypoglycemia.
RESPIRATION
Cold, sinus congestion, flu, bronchitis, asthma; with chilliness and shivering.
Dull mind, difficulty breathing through the nose.
DIGESTION
Slow digestion with cold abdomen; abdominal spasm; parasites (oil).
Flatulence, nausea, looseness of the bowels, diarrhea, griping pains, abdominal cramping; worse from cold food; cholera.
Lowers the sugar spike after a meal; hypoglycemia, diabetes.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Nephritis.
FEMALE
Menstruation: early and profuse, blood of a bright color, extremities cold, pale complexion.
Postpartum: profuse flow, extremities cold, surface of the body pallid.
Menopause: pain and discomfort, chest pain, neck and back pain.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Rheumatism, cramping pain and swelling; worse from cold, damp weather.
Lower back pain, abdomen cold.
Coldness of the surface.
Sore muscles (liniment ingredient).
FEVER
Fever with chills (bark in hot wine).
WOUNDS
Stops bleeding.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Cinnamomum cassia is an inferior substitute for C. zeylanicum. The whole quills keep longer and have a better flavor; they are preferred over the powder. The oil is also used; it is very hot and must be diluted. Tincture, 1 dram dose (Blackwood).

### CAUTIONS:

Contraindicated during pregnancy.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Vasant Lad and David Frawley (1, 2), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (1, 2, 4, 5, 9), William Cook (5, 12), Finley Ellingwood (9, 16), Eva Graf, Jack Ritchason (4, 10, 12), dietary research, often confirmed (6), Alexander Blackwood (8, 9), Aviva Romm (14), Hildegard von Bingen (3, 14).

## Citrus aurantium var. amara. Bitter Orange.

Bitter orange peel contains bitter principles, flavonoids, volatile oils (including limonene), and vitamins C and B1. The flowers contain neroli essence, trace elements (manganese, cobalt, lithium, aluminum, phosphorus, and magnesium), bitters, and flavonoids.
Bitter orange has been long used in both European and Chinese herbal medicine. It is less commonly encountered in North American herbalism. In Europe the flowers, leaves, rind, and dried fruit are all used; in China the rind. It is bitter and cooling hence relaxing and sedative. It is used in nervousness, anxiety, insomnia, anguish, tachycardia, epilepsy, digestive spasms, dyspepsia, and aerophagia (swallowing air).

## Citrus aurantium var. dulcis. Sweet Orange Peel, Orange Oil.

Orange peel has long been used in herbal medicine, both East and West, as a carminative to stimulate the stomach. Although lemon, grapefruit, and some kinds of oranges are sour and cooling, the sweet orange is a stimulant. The pores in the skin are oil glands that contain the volatile oil of sweet orange (90–96% limonene). The peel is so heavily impregnated with oils that they can be considered interchangeable with the oil. Dietrich Gümbel gives an extensive account of the use of the oil.
The dried peel of the sweet orange has long been used in European medicine as a carminative to warm and stimulate the stomach. “The great use of the peel is in the tincture or infusion as a stomachic,” writes Sir John Hill (1740, 285). “It is for this purpose to be pared off very thin, only the yellow part being useful, and to be put into brandy or wine, or to have boiling water poured on it fresh or dry. If a little gentian and a few cardamom-seeds be added to this tincture, or infusion, it is as good a bitter as can be made; it prevents sickness of the stomach and vomitings, and is excellent to amend the appetite.”
Gümbel maintains that lemon oil acts on the posterior pituitary, whereas orange oil acts on the anterior pituitary. Sweet orange is warming and stimulating; the sour lemon, cooling and sedating. The effect of orange is to increase kapha. In other words, it promotes increased appetite and digestion, raises the blood sugar, and helps the cells plump up with water and nutrients. As a result, the skin is soft, full, velvety, and more sensitive. Although stimulating to digestive and dermic functions, orange causes relaxation and satisfaction. It also favors the soul-body connection, says Gümbel.

### TASTE:

sweet, sour, pungent

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Cough.
Bronchitis.
Lack of appetite (contraindicated in excess appetite).
Vomiting.
Scurvy.
Restorative after exercise (fresh orange juice with a pinch of sea salt).
Nervous debility, anemia, in children.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Fresh orange. Dried orange peel. Orange oil.

### CAUTIONS:

Overstimulates the stomach and appetite in people with hot, active digestion.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, John Hill (3–4), Swami Sada Shiva Tirtha (1–7), Dietrich Gümbel, David Winston.

## Citrus medica var. limonum. Lemon, Lemon Peel, Lemon Oil.

Lemon peel, oil from the peel, and lemon juice from the fruit, are all used in herbal medicine. The oil is secreted in little glands that are evident on the surface of the peel. The predominate oils are limonene (90%) and citral (3.5–5%). The peel and oil are sour and astringent, the juice more purely sour. Both contain vitamin C. The following closely adheres to Gümbel’s detailed account of the properties of lemon oil with indications from Ayurveda (Tirtha). Gümbel’s portrait of lemon oil is exceedingly similar to my picture of another sour and astringent herb, sumach (Rhus typhina, R. aromatica, R. glabra). It also fits my personal medicinal experience.
Traditionally, lemon is used in fevers and sore throats to alleviate heat. As lemonade it is used to cool during the hot summer months. It has also been used to relieve heat conditions in the respiratory, gastrointestinal, and renal tracts. Externally, it appears in many formulas for improving the health of the skin. Although lemon is highly acidic, its internal effect is alkalinizing, and it is a standard dietary item in an “alkalizing diet.” This is popular at present to antidote the acidifying diet of processed food.
Lemon oil stimulates the posterior pituitary, according to Gümbel, and therefore the secretion of antidiuretic hormone (vasopressin) and oxytocin. Hence, it tends to retain and concentrate the urine, regulate blood pressure, lower blood sugar levels, and contract the smooth musculature, including the whole gastrointestinal tract, gallbladder, gall duct, fallopian tubes, uterus, and blood vessels. The mind may also be concentrated and strengthened. Catabolism is increased, resulting in increased oxidation and need for blood sugar in the cells—hence it lowers blood sugar levels and is antiseptic and antitoxic. It stimulates both red and white blood cell production, and cell production in general. The oil is an “obstetrician” that can be used to increase and regulate uterine contraction at labor, or in overdue labor (Gümbel, 1993, 182).
I observed one case where Gümbel’s indications were verified after three weeks on lemon juice in water. A middle-aged man who urinated excessively at night for more than ten years returned to normal, while the urine pH went up, as predicted by the alkalinizing theory. He also experienced less sudden urging to urinate and less leakiness.
The direct action of lemon oil on the skin intensifies the epidermis and sensory tissues, increasing the sense of touch, pain, itch, warmth/cold, and pressure. It increases cell multiplication to strengthen and thicken the epidermis, and brings more blood to the capillaries to nourish the periphery. By increasing blood flow to the surface it improves nutrition and fluid flow through the connective tissue, hence strengthening elastin, collagen, and immune processes. It improves tanning and healthy response to sunlight (cf. Xanthoxylum, another member of the citrus family). It is indicated in weak hair, nails, and skin, thin, soft skin, sensitive, easily burnt skin, and dry skin (Gümbel, 1993, 185).
Buttermilk and lemon face lotion is an old domestic remedy for improving the complexion and skin. “Add 1 tablespoonful of fresh lemon juice to 1 pint of sour buttermilk and wash the face and hands with it every night at bedtime. Also saturate a rag and apply to the face and let it remain on all night” (Russell, 1911, 326).

### TASTE:

sour • cooling, drying • astringent (Gümbel, Tirtha)

### TISSUE STATES:

excitation, relaxation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
RESPIRATION
Sore throat (gargle with lemon juice in water).
Colds, fevers, influenza.
Bronchitis, asthma; decreases phlegm discharge.
DIGESTION
Thirst.
Digestive disorders.
Excessive appetite, craving for sugar, high blood sugar.
Heartburn (juice in water).
Indigestion, gas (juice in water, with baking soda).
Nausea, vomiting, indigestion, and mucus in the stomach (lemon juice and honey in water).
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Gallstones, lack of good bile flow.
Liver, spleen, pancreas; diabetes mellitus (peel, lemon oil).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Kidney stones, urinary gravel (juice in water with cilantro).
Urination, excessive, at night.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis, rheumatism, gout, neuralgia.
FEVER
Summer heat, sunstroke (with salt).
Fever; thirst, hot, dry skin (water with lemon juice).
SKIN
Hot, dry skin, sunburn.
OTHER
Scurvy; swollen, bleeding gums.
Scurvy; bleeding from lungs, GI tract, kidneys, uterus.
Lowered blood sugar levels and fat reduction due to alkalinization (lemon juice in water long term).
Insect bites, nerve pain, disinfectant (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Lemon oil. Lemon juice. Juice a fresh lemon into about a quart of water and drink throughout the day. Lemon peel. Lemon tea. “Pour a pint of boiling water on the sliced lemons and add a pod of red pepper, let stand half an hour, strain, sweeten to taste” (Ralph Russell, 1911, 321).

### CAUTIONS:

Peptic ulcer; do not take the juice with milk, mangoes, or tomatoes (Tirtha).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Swami Sada Shiva Tirtha (1–12, 14–20), Dietrich Gümbel, Ralph Russell (2, 12), Matthew Wood (6-confirmed, 13).

## Cnicus benedictus, Carduus benedictus. Blessed Thistle.

This wild thistle is native to rough, stony ground in southern Europe, but more widely cultivated, both for its interesting beauty and medicinal properties. It was held in high esteem in the Middle Ages, as indicated by the name St. Benedict’s thistle, which refers to the “patron saint of poison antidotes.” It can be used as an emetic to expel poisons from the stomach. Cnicus was also called holy or blessed thistle because it was so widely useful, especially in epidemic diseases—smallpox, chicken pox, and measles. It is a pungent bitter herb containing a wide array of constituents: bitter sesquiterpene lactones, flavonoids, phytosterols, tannins, volatile oils, lignan lactones, mucilage, and minerals (calcium, manganese, magnesium, and potassium).
Blessed thistle has a strong affinity to the liver, like its cousin milk thistle. However, it is not sweet and oily like that herb, but bitter and pungent. Consequently, it is useful in chills alternating with fever, or “intermittent fever,” which are particularly well treated by intense bitters such as cnicus that send a shiver through the system. Such chills, usually arising from influenza, malaria, or deep poisoning of the system, were associated with the liver and gallbladder in old-time Western medicine. This is because the chills run through the autonomic nervous system, which regulates the gallbladder and the digestive tract. The management of the bile is upset, so the chills coincide with symptoms of biliousness, indigestion, bitter taste, gallstone concretion, and jaundice. Cnicus settles chills and biliousness, increases biliary secretion, and can remove gallstones.
Bilious symptoms may also arise from stress on the liver coming through poisons or toxins that are difficult for the liver to break down. Cnicus is a remedy for active hepatitis (cf. milk thistle). Poisons cause what the old doctors called “congestive chills,” or deep chills and aching, sometimes with low-grade fever. I used Cnicus in a case of apparent hepatitis, where the complexion was moderately yellow, the woman was consumed by chills and fever, and the liver was painful and swollen. When she later went to get a blood test, no sign of hepatitis showed up, so we cannot be sure of the diagnosis. Still, it was an impressive cure.
In addition to acting on the liver, blessed thistle acts on all channels of elimination, making it a powerful blood purifier. This helps to explain its high reputation in the era of humoral medicine. It is a powerful diaphoretic that opens the skin to relieve fever and reset the healthy governance of the surface. By detoxifying the interior it reduces toxins that would be thrown to the surface in eruptive pox diseases. It also increases kidney function to remove edema and toxins. It is a pungent bitter carminative: small doses increase secretion from the GI tract, increasing saliva, stomach acid, pancreatic enzymes, and bile. Large doses act as an emetic to remove phlegm from the stomach and lungs. As a bitter remedy for intermittent fever, chills, fever, and toxic poisoning, it is analogous to Eupatorium perfoliatum (boneset).
In addition to the preceding properties blessed thistle acts on the veins. It is a “venous tonic when the venous drainage is obstructed from uterine, prostatic, or a congested pelvis” (Menzies-Trull, 2003, 549). It increases circulation to the brain, and is traditionally used to improve memory, poor concentration, headaches (especially those associated with the liver), and depression. The mild circulatory stimulation it exhibits assists the cleansing, detoxifying powers to cleanse the blood, lymph, and tissues. By its sanative and circulatory powers it cleans indolent, slowly healing, septic infections, wounds, and ulcers. It contains antimicrobial and antiseptic constituents (the bitter sesquiterpene lactones cnicin and artemisiifolin).
Samuel Westcott Tilke, an important herbalist in London in the early nineteenth century, introduced blessed thistle as a female remedy for puberty, menstruation, menopause, pregnancy, and lactation (Webb, 1916, 248). In my experience, it is especially well suited to conditions associated with excess androgen, the hormone most difficult for the liver to detoxify. Hyperandrogenism is the most common hormonal disorder in women and is frequent in young women, resulting in ovarian cysts, acne, PMS, and blood sugar anomalies (cf. Artemisia vulgaris, Marrubium, Lactuca, Lilium). It is an extraordinarily reliable galactagogue (Terry Willard).
All of these uses, save for Tilke’s gynecological additions, are found in the traditional literature. P. A. Matthioli informs us that blessed thistle “is a plant of great virtue; it helpeth inwardly and outwardly; it strengthens all the principal members of the body, as the brain, the heart, the stomach, the liver, the lungs and the kidney; it is also a preservative against all disease, for it causes perspiration, by which the body is purged of much corruption, such as breedeth diseases; it expelleth the venom of infection; it consumes and wastes away all bad humors; therefore, give God thanks for his goodness, Who hath given this herb and all others for the benefit of our health” (quoted by Maude Grieve, 1931, 796).

### TASTE:

bitter, acrid, pungent

### TISSUE STATES:

constriction, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Depression.
HEAD
Poor circulation to the brain; reduced memory, inability to concentrate; senility.
Dizziness, tinnitus.
Headaches associated with the liver and digestion; frontal.
RESPIRATION
Respiratory infection, weak lungs, colds, bronchial catarrh.
HEART
Angina.
DIGESTION
Poor appetite, weak stomach, mucus in the stomach (canker), fermentation, indigestion, gas (cold infusion; small doses).
Cramps (warm infusion).
Intestinal worms (powdered leaves).
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Liver congestion and swelling, with flu-like, intermittent chills, yellowish complexion, jaundice; hepatitis.
KIDNEYS
Edema.
FEMALE
Puberty: hormonal regulation in young girls.
Menstruation: cramps, pain, headaches associated with excess androgen.
Vaginal discharge.
Menopause: headaches associated with hormonal imbalances.
Pregnancy.
Lactation: one of the most reliable galactagogues.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Tightens tendons, sinews, joints.
Arthritis.
FEVER
Intermittent fever, chill/fever, malaria, influenza (cf. Eupatorium perfoliatum).
SKIN
Festering sores, septic wounds, ulcers.
Ringworm.
Cancer.
OTHER
Emetic.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Leaves, flowers, and seeds are all used. Large doses produce relatively painless emesis (hence its use to remove poisons taken at the mouth). Macerate 6 teaspoonfuls of the dried leaf in 1 pint of cold water; take 2 tablespoonfuls as an emetic and half a teaspoonful as a tonic (Cerney, 1976, 65). Cold infusions in smaller doses are tonic to the digestion, whereas hot draughts are diaphoretic in fever. Fluid extract and tincture are used.
John Quincy (1736, 111) writes in the London Pharmacopoeia, “Schrader observes somewhat very odd of it, viz., that if it be gather’d in the middle or beginning of June, it is very efficacious in curing fresh wounds; but that it has no such virtue if gather’d at any other time.”

### CAUTIONS:

Allergic reaction possible.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1–5, 7–10, 16–22), Samuel Westcott Tilke (1, 2, 3, 6, 12–15), Maude Grieve, Anita Ralph (4), Matthew Wood (8, 10, and 20-confirmed), Louise Tenney (8, 23), Christopher Menzies-Trull (3), Terry Willard (17-confirmed).

## Commiphora spp. Myrrh.

The resinous secretions of a small desert tree in Arabia and the Horn of Africa, long used in Old World medicine. Recipes from Assyrian texts date to 1700 BCE. Myrrh gum (correctly called an “oleo-gum-resin”) consists of oil soluble essential oils (about 5%), water soluble gum (about 60%, containing some carbohydrates and sugars), and a resin soluble in alcohol (about 35%). This means that the properties of myrrh gum can be altered by the method of extraction.
Myrrh gum is a strong stimulant and astringent that acts on the mucosa and skin. It is best known, from ancient times down to the present, as an external application in green wounds and putrefactive wounds. Samuel Thomson (1841) treats us to his own case. While breaking new land in Vermont, he cut a gash clean through his foot with an ax. His father, not knowing what to do, kept the wound open by putting a string through it (do not try this at home), put the boy on a sledge, and headed for the nearest doctor, several days away. Dr. Kitteridge was disgusted by the inept wound-treatment—the string caused the wound to putrefy and poisoned the blood. “Is he going to die?” asked the ashen father. “Of course he is,” the old doctor retorted sharply. “But not from this wound.” With myrrh powder the gash slowly healed.
For internal affections, myrrh is especially used for relaxed conditions of the mucosa and skin, to prevent hypersecretion and damp surfaces, and also in putrefaction. It is an important specific to cleanse rotten gums. The Assyrians used it to check excessive salivation and tonsillitis. Arab physicians recommended sucking on the little pieces of resin.
Myrrh gum is indicated by a pale, flabby, damp tongue. This symptom, with heaviness and indigestion, indicates relaxation of the gastrointestinal tract, with mucus in the stool; relaxation of the respiratory mucosa, with phlegm in the lungs; a full, congested feeling in the pelvis and clear vaginal discharge, reproductive relaxation, and so forth. Myrrh has long been used for a flaccid, relaxed condition of the muscles and mucosa of the womb. It is a “great Cleanser and Strengthner of the Womb,” wrote John Quincy (1736).
Some of our best palliatives for diabetes mellitus type II are astringent stimulants because these agents cut down on the secretion of urine through the kidneys and stimulate cell life and therefore blood sugar and insulin uptake. Hence, myrrh gum is a specific for diabetes in the Middle East.
Samuel Thomson used myrrh gum as a specific for arthritis.

### TASTE:

warm • stimulating • astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, relaxation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Endomorphic (kapha) constitutions with relaxation and depression; vascular enfeeblement, poor circulation to the extremities, depressed cell life.
Tongue pale, flabby, moist; mucosa pale, flabby, with profuse secretion.
RESPIRATION
Catarrh of the nasal passages, throat, pharynx, and ears.
Sore throat, tonsillitis, diphtheria.
Free secretion and phlegm in the lungs.
Chronic catarrh, “profound congestion” (Christopher).
Cough.
Bronchitis.
Asthma.
Tuberculosis.
DIGESTION
Bad breath; gum disease; canker sores; bleeding gums; wound in the mouth.
Atonic dyspepsia; indigestion, gas.
Chronic, atonic diarrhea, dysentery, colitis.
Mucus in the stools.
Worms (powder in capsules).
Pelvic engorgement, rectal fissure, hemorrhoids.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Mucus in urine.
FEMALE
Vaginitis, yeast infection, leucorrhea, discharge.
Menstrual derangements characterized by debility of the muscular fiber, “flaccidity of the womb,” amenorrhea, uterine tumors, infertility.
Parturition: deficient labor, helps pass the afterbirth following an exhausting labor.
Menopause.
Nipples, sore and abraded.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis.
SKIN AND EXTERNAL
Eczema.
Abrasions.
Skin and mucosa rotten, putrid, ulcerated, gangrenous (dissolved in water or wine).
Leg ulcers.
Boils, wounds, pimples, herpes.
Externally in tooth powders, mouthwashes, and gargles, and also as a mildly stimulating application to sluggish ulcers.
FEVER
Scarlet fever.
OTHER
Adult onset diabetes.
Hypothyroidism (uncertain medical evidence).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Used as a powder, or extracted in water, alcohol, or oil. Tincture of myrrh, 1–10 drops, internally or as a gargle.

### CAUTIONS:

Long regarded as safe. The absolute essential oil can cause skin irritation.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, James Snow and James Green (1, 2), Martin Watt and Wanda Sellar (2–13, 15, 16, 18, 19, 21, 23–29, 32), John Christopher (6–9, 11–13, 18, 22, 25), Louise Tenney (6–12, 16, 18, 22–25, 28, 30), Matthew Wood (31-not confirmed), 7Song (20-confirmed).

## Coriandrum sativum. Coriander, Cilantro.

Coriander seeds are one of the main flavoring agents in modern curry and chai mixes. They have long been used in Indian cooking. They have been used since the Middle Ages in the West, and are still used as a flavoring agent. Coriander leaf is known as cilantro, and has different uses.
The leaf (called cilantro) is cooling whereas the seed or fruit (coriander) is warming, giving the two parts of the plant opposite properties. Both are good general household remedies for simple problems in the digestive, respiratory, or urinary tracts. The fixed oil in the seed breaks down in the large intestine, so this plant has a stronger influence on that organ (Otto Masseurt). “It is claimed that it strengthens the muscles of the heart and the coronary system; certainly it corrects digestion” (Donald Law, 1973, 112). Cilantro is used as a “heavy metal detoxifier.”

### TASTE:

(leaf) bitter, pungent, sour • cool (fruit) bitter, pungent • warm • stimulating

### TISSUE STATE:

(leaf) irritation (fruit) depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
RESPIRATION
Allergies, hay fever, sore throat (leaf).
DIGESTION
Vomiting, indigestion (leaf).
Putrefaction in stomach and intestines (seed).
Acute or chronic indigestion (seed).
Bloating, soreness, hiccoughs, belching, flatulence, and cramps (seed).
Headache due to digestive trouble (seed).
Chronic indigestion with debility (seed).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Cystitis, burning urethra (leaf).
SKIN
Hot skin; urticaria, itch, rash, burns (leaf).
OTHER
Heavy metal detoxification (leaf).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Fresh juice of cilantro. Seeds whole or ground. Cold infusion. “Allow for lengthy steeping of the seeds, to get the full flavour do not boil the seeds” (Donald Law, 1973, 112).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Vasant Lad and David Frawley (1–2, 8–9), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (3–7), Otto Masseurt.

## Cornus officinalis. Cornel.

Cornel is a small tree native from Europe to China. It is not much used in the former area, but much in the latter. Henry Lu (1994, 134) explains the signature that led to the use of Cornus as a remedy for longevity and male sexual potency. The tree flowers in May (like our North American Cornus florida) but the fruit does not ripen until November. This suggested the idea of longevity through slow, steady development. Cornus thus came to be used, in combination with morinda bark, as one of the most important life extension tonics. It also developed a reputation as a remedy for male potency.
Cornus officinalis is a “yang tonic” that restores and retains important fluids. The fruit of the cornel is used. It is indicated for symptoms of kidney yang deficiency: loss of fluids, lower back pain, seminal emissions, dizziness, ringing in the ears, and insomnia. It is also indicated when a cold sweat results in exhaustion (Lu, 1994, 135).
Cornel would be considered an “elk medicine” in American Indian medicine, like its cousin, Cornus florida. That puts it in a class with staghorn sumach, Florida dogwood, and even the exotic lemon, all of which help retain fluids running too freely from the genitourinary tract. It is intensely sour and astringent (like sumach berry and lemon, both of which stop excess flux of urine and precious substances from the body). Like Florida dogwood, cornel acts on the liver and kidneys. Like staghorn sumach it restrains loss of perspiration and urination and stabilizes blood sugar.
I was visited by a woman in her mid-thirties who wanted to get pregnant with her new husband. She was dark under the eyes from adrenal exhaustion so I gave her Siberian ginseng, which cleared up the darkness and the exhaustion—an indication from William LeSassier. Next I saw her husband, who was suffering from exhaustion too, including sexual underactivity. The tongue was red, indicating some kind of fever, but he looked thin and weak. I gave him yarrow and cornel fruit. In two months’ time the woman was pregnant (since delivered of a baby girl) and, she reported, her husband was much more lively in bed.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

For most conditions cornel is decocted in water, but for insomnia it is macerated in wine, 20 grams to 5 cups of wine (Lu, 1994, 135).

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Constipation.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional Chinese medicine, Henry Lu, Dan Bensky and Andrew Gamble.

## Crataegus oxyacantha, Crataegus sp. Hawthorn.

The hawthorns are small trees native throughout the northern hemisphere. They have a long history of use in Arabic medicine, but were little known in European medicine until recently. In 1895 hawthorn was introduced to the world as a heart tonic. This usage has continued down to the present, with verification from modern research, so that hawthorn is at present a very popular remedy in Europe. It contains flavonoids, procyanidins, and numerous acids. These constituents are cooling, or “antioxidant.” More specifically, the flavonoid rutin is believed to act on the capillaries, reducing wear and tear—thus increasing the passage of blood and removing congestion associated with heat.
Hawthorn has been shown to lower unhealthy cholesterol and high blood pressure. It apparently does this by improving the deposition of lipids in the walls of the capillaries and in the red blood cells that are squeezed through them. This cuts down on the irritability of the capillaries, so that the blood passes more quickly and efficiently, thus reducing congestion and heat. Hawthorn has a normalizing effect on the cholesterols, probably decreasing high LDLs (bad cholesterols) and increasing HDLs (the good ones). This causes more efficient patching on injured capillary surfaces. How do we know this? These are deductions I have made from the effect that hawthorn has on the capillaries and skin.
How does a person like myself observe the capillaries? If the meaty parts of the palm, especially near the wrist, are red this indicates capillary congestion of red blood cells. If these fatty tissues are depressed with a finger and this is followed by blanching (the area stays white for a few seconds), then the indication is that there is heat and irritation in the capillaries with friction between the walls of the vessels and the red blood cells, so that the latter cannot flow through as quickly. This is a keynote indication for hawthorn. (If this condition occurs but the skin color is yellow this is an indication for wild cherry bark.) In addition, I have also observed that when hawthorn is needed there will often be a certain dryness on the backs of the hands and wrists, or elsewhere. This indicates poor secretion from the sebaceous glands, but more deeply it indicates that the cell walls are probably short on lipids.
Hawthorn not only has an affinity for the capillaries and cholesterol, but an elective affinity for the coronary circulation. Some people can feel the increase of circulation to the heart. What is interesting about this is that the coronary arteries are the site for the most deposition of LDLs because they are made up of triglycerides and it is believed the heart prefers this source over glucose for its sugar. Thus, the coronary arteries have a tendency to build up the so-called “bad cholesterols.” Hawthorn probably normalizes this deposition to the right value. Irritable capillaries, backup of red blood cells, and LDL cholesterol deposition all favor high blood pressure, and hypertrophy of the heart, as it overbuilds itself to pump the blood against these increased pressures. The practitioner should not, however, look to high blood pressure as a specific indicator because many people needing hawthorn have low blood pressure. A better indication is the redness and blanching in the palm.
Rudolf Weiss (1988), fairly representative of modern German literature, describes Crataegus as a cardiac tonic, reducing the likelihood of heart attack and relieving the subjective symptoms of discomfort in angina. It opens the coronary arteries and improves blood flow to the heart, strengthening the myocardium. Unlike digitalis, which directly strengthens the contractile movement of the cardiac muscles, Crataegus improves the nutrition, activity, energy reserves, and energy release of the heart muscle. Consequently, it has a beneficial effect in degenerative, age-related changes in structure. It is useful in cardiac arrhythmias. By opening the coronary circulation, Crataegus can antidote the effects of arteriosclerosis, lower high blood pressure, and increase the health of the heart muscle. The old eclectics add that Crataegus does not act on valvular disease—here they recommended cactus—but on the heart muscle.
Hawthorn also has a tonifying effect on the large vessels. My friend Jennifer Tucker, an herbalist in State College, Pennsylvania, passed on a beautiful case history. A woman came with her four- or five-month-old baby for flower essences. The baby, she explained, had a too small aorta and either was going to die or needed surgery, which it was unlikely to survive. She and her husband had chosen not to do the operation—the flower essences were for her to accept the eventual death of the little child. She didn’t want to get her hopes up, but Jennifer got her to give the baby hawthorn. At the next check-up the doctor exclamed, “What did you do to this baby?” The woman was frightened, but he quickly explained that the artery was now normal in size. Every once and a while, the mother brings the little boy by to visit “the herb lady.”
By reducing heat Crataegus lessens restlessness, irritability, anxiety, and nervousness that sometimes accompany cardiac conditions. It has been cited as a remedy for attention deficit by David Winston—an indication confirmed in my own practice. This is something we often find when there is heat, which has an agitating effect. The child (or adult) runs around and has trouble settling down and focusing. By relaxing the nervous system and heart, irregular pulse and arrhythmias are overcome.
In recent years hawthorn has been cited as a remedy for autoimmune disease. This goes hand in hand with its prominence as a remedy to fight heat and the excited tissue state, which is characterized by such overactivity. It reduces irritation of the skin, mucosa, and blood vessels. Deborah Francis, ND, has introduced it as an antihistamine to palliate the irritable mucosa in asthmatic cases while seeking a deeper, curative agent. I have not used hawthorn in this manner, but here is a case history from my practice showing how it worked in a bad case of childhood sinusitis. “We met you in July 2005 in Plum City by the trout pond,” I was reminded by a Wisconsin mother. Her son Jeff “was three years old at the time and had been diagnosed with chronic sinusitis at the age of twenty-three months by the MDs, who wanted to do major sinus surgery on him.” I was glad I saw the boy in person because I would not have guessed hawthorn by telephone. We both just happened to be in western Wisconsin right then. Jeff had the characteristic redness on the “cheeky” parts of the body—of which there are more on a three-year-old. We treated him with hawthorn (70%), elder (20%), and wild cherry (10%), the latter two reinforcing the former. “This seemed to work wonders on him, he was noticeably improved until he caught a cold earlier this summer [2006]. He didn’t get sinus infection this time, but his head is congested again and his breathing is very noisy at night and he is not getting a good night’s sleep again. He is once again waking up with bags under his eyes. His sleep apnea had stopped and he was waking up well rested when he was taking the tincture.” We sent off more, which again brought him back to normal.
Hawthorn reduces irritation and redness of the skin, and increases the ability of the skin to form a healthy scab. I remember one case in which a dose simply changed a small, red, irritable patch of skin into a scab in a few minutes. This reminded me of a tip passed on to me by an American Indian medicine woman, years before. She said, “There’s something in the thorn that causes the skin not to heal when it is pricked. But if you pick that thorn and prick a boil it will empty out and heal up.”
The story of hawthorn should probably begin with the digestion of lipids, rather than their usage. In traditional Chinese medicine Crataegus is used for sluggish digestion, especially of meat. As recently as the Renaissance, it was customary in Europe to use hawthorn berries with meat to make it more digestible. Hawthorn probably acts on the cells of the gut, through which digested lipids have to pass, since the digestate goes through cell walls, not between the cells. If the cell walls of the gut are slightly dry and lacking in their full measure of fats, it stands to reason they will not assimilate lipids as well.
Recently I gave Crataegus berry tincture to a four-year-old girl who had been badly damaged by a vaccine in infancy. She became autistic and her guts became riddled with sores and bacteria. I was led to this remedy by a slight “blanching” response in the capillaries of the hand, the preponderance of intense heat symptoms through the GI, the use of hawthorn as a specific for attention deficit (thank-you, David Winston), and by the folklore associating hawthorn with the fairies. It was not evident to either the mother or me that it was a particularly important part of the treatment strategy and it took some time to differentiate its effect from that of other remedies given. However, after two months it became clear to the mother that the hawthorn had a marked effect, so far promoting speech, attention, and interaction as to be remarkable. “I give a dose of one drop,” she related, “and within an hour she is speaking in sentences, abstract concepts, and correct tenses.” Normally she sort of mumbles to herself. However, “She gets completely constipated from one drop.” The mother believed that the hawthorn precipitated the mercury from the vaccine out of the tissues into the bloodstream, causing a die off of bowel flora. I suggested a fractional dose—a sip of a glass of water with one drop in it. I give this unfinished case as the book goes to press because of the radical improvement in this serious condition (autism), hoping that it may help others.

### TASTE:

(berries) sweet, sour • astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

excitation, atrophy, relaxation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
If the skin of the palms or fingers is red, press and watch for blanching (the area under pressure remains white for longer than normal).
Red cheeks.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Attention deficit in children and adults; can’t focus, can’t sit down and read; restless, irritable, nervous.
Insomnia.
RESPIRATION
Allergies; sinusitis, colds, bronchitis, asthma—curative or palliative.
HEART AND CIRCULATION
Senile heart, patients with degenerative changes of the cardiac muscle, or with coronary artery disease; anginal symptoms.
Hypertension; opens the coronary circulation and may reduce arteriosclerotic effects, if not the actual deposits.
Cardiac arrhythmias, mainly extra systoles and acute tachycardia attacks.
Weakness of the myocardium and heart problems after fevers, influenza, pneumonia, scarlet fever, acute arthritic attacks, etc.
Excessive perspiration following acute illness, with weakness of the heart.
Gray pallor of incipient heart attack.
Pulse rapid, irregular, intermittent, and small; a dose noticeably calms the pulse.
DIGESTION
Indigestion; food sits in the stomach, ferments, and causes heartburn; indigestion, constipation, atonic lower intestine.
Nervous stomach.
LIVER
Liver; symptoms not well developed.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Dropsy, stones in the kidneys.
SKIN
Skin red, dry, irritated; especially on the backs of the hands, wrists, and up the forearms; if it is the right remedy crataegus will bring oil into the area in less than 15 minutes from the first dose.
OTHER
Worse from excitement, exertion, and in a warm room; better from fresh air, quiet, and rest; great exhaustion from the slightest mental or physical movement.
Allergies, food allergies.
Redness under the nostrils, in severe cases down to the mouth.
Poultice of the leaves or berries to draw out embedded thorns, splinters, felons, and whitlows.
Thorn to break infected boil open.
Hypoglycemia.
Low blood pressure.
Inflammatory rheumatism.
Sore throat.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The berries were the part traditionally used. I don’t think there is a more beautiful flavor than tincture of hawthorn berry prepared in brandy. Pharmaceutical companies experimented with the use of the flowers and leaves, but they are not superior to the berries. Moderate to large doses are most beneficial. Twenty drops of the tincture, diluted with water, on rising and going to bed; treatment is long term (Weiss, 1988, 165).

### CAUTIONS:

“If it should cause nausea or fullness in the head then dose should be reduced” (Petersen, 1905, 79). This represents a homeopathic “proving,” since these are symptoms it cures.

### LITERATURE:

Finley Ellingwood (6–10, 15, 18), John William Fyfe (6–10,12), William Boericke (12, 18), Cyril Boger (12), Rudolf Weiss (6–10), Brent Davis (11), Chalid Ottway (16, 21), David Winston (3), traditional Chinese medicine (13), Matthew Wood (1, 3-confirmed, 5-confirmed, 11, 14, 17, 18-confirmed, 19, 20, 24-confirmed), Matthew Becker (2), Louise Tenney (4, 21–26), Deborah Francis (5), traditional Native American (22).

## Crocus sativus. Saffron.

This beautiful spring flower is native to Europe and Asia, but is grown widely as an ornamental. It is also cultivated for the stigmas, which are picked and used as a flavoring and coloring agent in cooking. They command a high price. Saffron contains a volatile oil (picrocrocin or saffron bitter), gum, wax, fat, albumin.
Saffron has long been used in domestic and professional medicine in Europe. The aromatic, bitter taste (Grieve) reveals that it is a stimulant and carminative. The fact that it is a warming remedy is reinforced by the growth habit, since it grows almost before the snow is off the ground. The oiliness of the bulb provides the plant with real insulation. As a warming stimulant crocus is used when the blood is congealed and clotted, in old bruises and menstrual difficulties, and when cancer develops from such blood. It also helps to bring the “bad blood” out of the system in eruptive fevers, where it brings the rash to the surface. “In our rural districts there is a popular custom of giving saffron tea in measles, on the doctrine of colour analogy” (Fernie, 1914, 456). The dangers of eruptive fevers are magnified by filth in the blood, which tends to internalize the fever, resulting in prolongation of the fever and damage to the internal organs.
The ability of Crocus to detoxify led to it being associated with crocodiles (“crocus-fearers”) and alligators, which feed on dead carcasses.
The hot tea has long been used in the early stages of eruptive diseases, particularly measles and scarlet fever. Dr. Finley Ellingwood (1918, 271) notes that “Saffron tea was long in good repute among the grandmothers” of the early nineteenth century “as an essential remedy to start newborn babes in normal health channels. It was thought necessary to encourage the action of the liver and to cleanse the intestinal canal and stomach. It was positively indicated, if the skin was yellow and in infantile colic.”
Saffron has a long history of use in professional medicine as a menstrual remedy, especially “when the blood is black, viscid, clotting, forming itself into long black strings” (Clarke, 1900, 607). “Boerhaave said that saffron possesses the power of liquefying the blood” (Fernie, 1914, 448).
The eclectic, J. W. Fyfe (1909, 493), gives an account rich in menstrual symptoms. “Crocus has a specific action on the uterus, and in amenorrhea, as well as in profuse bleeding, it exerts a corrective influence. In menorrhagia, especially when the discharge is clotted, it modifies the pain and restores normal menstruation, and in suppression of the lochia it is deemed useful. In chronic loss of blood from the uterus it is also said to be an efficient remedy.” Such excessively congealed blood indicates the need for a warming stimulant. It is also used on old bruises in an “indolent” phase, when healing will not set in, and on tumors (which often develop from badly congealed blood and bruises).
Crocus received a homeopathic proving that justified most of the traditional uses. It also brought distinctive mental symptoms, with alternation between violent rage and repentance, or laughter and tears, and as if between inebriation and sobriety. Clarke gives some case histories. “I once cured with a few doses of Crocus a young artist who had become subject to violent outbursts of rage in which he would take up a knife to throw at his mother, with whom he lived, and almost immediately after would be abjectly repentant. The household was on the point of being broken up when the trouble was completely removed by Crocus.” On another occasion, “in hospital, I happened to see a young girl who was really desperately ill with heart failure and valvular disease, in a fit of hysterical laughter. This made me think of Crocus.” The 30x homeopathic potency restored her to mental and physical health. This sort of instability of the emotional life is associated with the heart in traditional Chinese medicine—“heart not storing the shen.”
There are many associations between Crocus and the heart in English folk medicine. A popular name is Heart of Man (Cor hominis) or Philosopher’s Gold (Aurum philosophorum). Sir Francis Bacon is supposed to have written, “Saffron conveys medicine to the heart, cures its palpitation, removes melancholy and uneasiness, revives the brain, renders the mind cheerful, and generates boldness.” The syrup is a “slightly stimulating exhilarant” (W. T. Fernie, 1914, 447).

### TASTE:

sweet, pungent • aromatic

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, atrophy, constriction
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Inequality of the complexion; pallor alternating with redness; pulsating on one side of the face.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Great alternation of moods; violent rage and repentance, happiness and sadness, laughter and tears, exhilaration and despondency.
HEAD
Headache; especially in the forehead; vertigo.
Tongue; prominent papillae.
RESPIRATION
Cough, violent, dry, shaking (cf. Verbascum); bronchitis.
Expectoration of blood; tuberculosis.
DIGESTION
Stomach much disordered; heartburn, risings, qualmishness, nausea, bloating.
Colic and flatulence.
Distention of the stomach and abdomen.
Hyperglycemia and hypoglycemia.
Infantile jaundice and colic.
CARDIOVASCULAR AND BLOOD
Coagulation of the blood leading to heart disease.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Water retention.
Kidney stones.
FEMALE
Menstruation: amenorrhea.
Menstruation: pains in the lumbar region accompany.
Menstruation: congestive dysmenorrhea, discharge dark and stringy.
Uterine hemorrhage; blood dark and tarry; clotted and in strings.
Pregnancy: excessive fetal movement.
Pregnancy: sleeplessness and pain.
Postpartum: suppression of the lochial discharge.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Rheumatism, arthritis.
Back, coldness, heaviness, soreness, bruised feeling.
SKIN
Psoriasis.
Chafes, scalds, cuts, wounds, itch, bleeding.
Emollient; softens hard lumps and knots.
Boils, opens and heals.
Aches, pains, exhaustion (bath).
FEVER
Stimulating diaphoretic given in the early stages of eruptive fever, to bring out the rash.
Scarlet fever, measles.
Tonsillitis.
OTHER
Palpitation of the heart (circumstances not defined).

### PREPARATION, CAUTIONS, AND DOSAGE:

Large doses are uneconomical and can excite a homeopathic proving. “Excessive use of saffron proves harmful. It will produce an intense pain in the head, and imperil the reason. Half a scruple, that is, ten grains, should be the largest dose.
