In full doses this tincture will provoke a determination of blood to the head, with bleeding from the nose, and sometimes with a disposition to immoderate laughter. Small doses, therefore, of the diluted tincture, ought to relieve these symptoms when they occur as spontaneous illness” (Fernie, 1914, 449).
Tincture of the stigmas, 5–15 drops (Maude Grieve, 1931, 700). The homeopathic preparations have the same application as the herbal.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, John Henry Clarke (homeopathic and traditional; 1–7, 9, 12, 17, 18, 21), Maude Grieve (24), John William Fyfe (8, 14, 15, 16, 17, 19), Finley Ellingwood (11, 13, 23), W. T. Fernie (2, 18), Louise Tenney (8, 10, 13, 21, 22, 23, 24, 29, 30), Chalid Ottway (3, 14, 20, 22, 23, 25, 26, 27, 28, 31, 32).

## Cucurbita citrullus, Citrullus lanatus,
Citrullus vulgaris. Watermelon Seed.

Watermelon is one of the “four greater cold seeds” of Greek medicine. It was brought to the New World at an early date. One of my ancestors received a land grant for twenty acres on “watermilyn hill” in Hempstead, Long Island, in 1664. By this time the Spanish had already introduced it to the Southwestern Indians, who thus gained an important food and medicine.
The genus name Citrullus is correct today, but I have retained the old Cucurbita in order to allow comparison between different members of the melon family.
Watermelon is one of the most accessible remedies for heat and irritation of the kidneys. It is commonly eaten on hot summer days for its thirst-quenching, cooling effect. It cools partly by opening up the kidneys. The flesh is good in season, the dried seeds at other times. It is a cooling, sedative diuretic containing fruit sugars and acids.
The association of watermelon and the kidneys is quite ancient. Moses Maimonides learned to use cooked melon seeds to prevent kidney stones from Abu Merwan Ibn Zuhr. “He was one of the [great] experimentalists. His son related to me astounding things regarding his father and the latter’s travail and diligence in experimentation. I, therefore, see fit to mention them in his name” (Maimonides; Rosner, 1989, III:347). The affinity to the kidney is justified in another fashion by herbalist Julia Graves. “Cut a cucurbit crosswise and we have a semblance to the histological cut of the glomeruli,” she notes. John Hill (1740, 307) recommends “all the cold seeds in stranguries and heat of urine.”
“It seems as though it was only at yesterday’s meal that Grandfather warned us not to throw away the innumerable seeds,” writes New England herbalist and pharmacist Ben Charles Harris (1968, 166). “We were rewarded by having Grandfather relate to us some fable or bit of folklore pertaining to the herbs of our immediate neighborhood.” Harris gives precise directions. “Dip them via a metal strainer in a mild solution of cold soapy water. The seeds dry best if first they are rolled in a towel to absorb most of the moisture. To dry completely they are spread out on large sheets of cardboard.” The late Georgia herbalist Tommie Bass (Crellin and Philpott, 1990, II:450) said of the seed, “Old ladies and old men buy them by the pound” as a diuretic.
Dr. Finley Ellingwood (1918, 439) gives a short, neat account of this remedy. “The juice of the fruit is a diuretic with many individuals, producing a cooling sense of relief from heat or aching across the kidneys, or throughout the urinary apparatus. The seeds in the form of an infusion act promptly. In those conditions accompanied with a general sense of constriction, or backache from the passage of urates and phosphatic gravel, this remedy exercises a direct soothing influence, not only upon the renal organs, but upon the bladder, especially when the irritation persists, warding off subsequent inflammation. It is also very useful during the active stage of cystitis.”
Like some of the other remedies for heat and irritation of the kidneys, watermelon lowers high blood pressure. This fact is brought to our attention by Harris (1968, 166). “High blood pressure … has been helped to a marked degree by drinking teas of Watermelon seeds.” He points out the mechanism of action. “An extract of watermelon seeds contains an active ingredient called Cucurbocitrin which has been of great value in dilating the capillaries or very small blood vessels. By directing its benefits toward the kidney organs, the pressure upon the large blood vessels is reduced.” He quotes an old newspaper clipping in which a case of nephritis in an eight-year-old boy was kept at bay by watermelon seed tea.
The Southwestern Indians learned how to make syrup from watermelon flesh. This was used for food and medicine. Various anthropological and first person accounts were collected by John Heinerman (1994, 265–7). The syrup is a tonic nutritive for old people and convalescents. It cools fevers and strengthens and cools the lungs and gastrointestinal tract. One old wise woman recommended it for sour stomach caused by “eating too much of the white man’s food.” Heinerman adds that watermelon is beneficial for arthritis and gout, also for skin problems, all of which may be due to a too-acid blood from improper eating. Cherokee herbalist Sondra Boyd notes that it eliminates poisons and toxins. Herbalist Kathleen Maier recommends watermelon seeds in food poisoning with vomiting and diarrhea. Watermelon binds the stool but loosens the kidneys.

### TASTE:

(fruit) sweet, cold, moist

### TISSUE STATE:

excitation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Vexation, irritability.
Thirst, summer heat, sunstroke, fever.
DIGESTION
Canker sores, bleeding gums, sour stomach, diarrhea, food poisoning.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Heat and congestion across the lower back, in the region of the kidneys.
Irritation and constriction from gravel in the kidneys and bladder.
Burning urine, active stage of cystitis.
High blood pressure from congested, hot kidneys.
Nephritis.
Urination painful and scanty in infants.
Edema; as a diuretic in old age.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis, gout.
SKIN
Burning pains; skin problems, acne and rash.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The “infusion is preferable to alcoholic preparations” (Petersen, 1905, 79).

### CAUTIONS:

The flesh and seeds are both regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Kathleen Maier (3), Finley Ellingwood (4–6), John Hill (6), Ben Charles Harris (7, 8), Frederick Petersen (9), Tommie Bass (10), John Heinerman (11, 12).

## Cucurbita pepo. Pumpkin.

Pumpkin was one of the “four greater cold seeds” of Greek medicine, classified as cold and moist. However, it was not as important as watermelon and cucumber, also in this group. Today the seed is valued more for its fat and protein, which are nutritious, contain high amounts of zinc (deficient in the modern diet), and act on the prostate. In the nineteenth century the seeds were more famous as an anthelmintic to get rid of tapeworm. A protein, cucurbitan, is responsible for this action. It anesthetizes the worm, which is then removed with a gentle purgative. Results are variable, probably due to the uncertain concentration of this constituent in the seed (John Crellin and Jane Philpott, 1990, II:361). This is a case where, if there were enough need for it, a standardized extract would be beneficial. Pumpkin flesh is 95% water (cool and moist indeed), so less active medicinally.
Pumpkin seed is sweet, moist, and nutritive. As we have seen, it was one of the “four greater cold seeds” of Greek medicine, classified as cold and moist. It is very high in vitamins and minerals, especially zinc. Because this mineral is an important constituent of sperm, it has been used for some time as a “male tonic.” Because zinc is absorbed through the mouth and gums pumpkin seed should be chewed very well. The seeds are beneficial for prostate problems and a weak, irritable, or nervous bladder (Otto Wolff, 1991, 88–89). Dr. J. V. Cerney (1976, 73) goes so far as to cite them “for all urinary problems (spasm, scalding urine, etc.).”
Pumpkins were associated with the diet of the poor, both in England and America. “The poor people mix the fleshy part of the fruit with apples, and bake them in pies,” notes Sir John Hill (1740, 307). I guess those poor people came over to America, where pumpkin and apple pie is everyone’s favorite. Tommie Bass (Crellin and Philpott, 1990, II:360) noted that pumpkins were a staple of share croppers in the old South, and were excellent for “bad digestion.” The seeds are “the finest thing in the world for the prostate gland” and are diuretic (like watermelon seed, but less so) for the kidneys.
Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (1991, 124) classify pumpkin flesh as cooling and moistening, which follows the traditional designation for the seed. They recommend it for dryness in the stomach causing gas, bloating, and flatulence, burning, acidity, and hot flashes, worse from eating hot, spicy food. It has an action on hot flashes and night sweats—this suggests an hormonal and female use to supplement the male use. They also recommend pumpkin in chronic gastric flu, with upset stomach and fever, and in gastrointestinal complaints such as ulcer, gastritis, and ulcerative colitis. Also for heat and dryness causing spasms, irritation, and inflammation of the respiratory tract.

### TASTE:

sweet • cool, moist

### TISSUE STATES:

excitation, constriction, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
RESPIRATION
Heat and drying in the respiratory tract, causing spasms, irritation, inflammation (flesh).
DIGESTION
Dry stomach; gas, bloating, burning, acidity, and hot flashes, worse from hot, spicy food (flesh).
Gastric flu, upset stomach, and fever (flesh).
Ulcer, ulcerative colitis (flesh).
Tapeworm (seed; traditional).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Weak, irritable, nervous bladder.
Straining and scalding urine (cf. watermelon seed).
Scanty urine (cf. watermelon seed).
MALE
Prostate problems (fresh seed).
FEMALE
Hot flashes and night sweats (flesh).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The fresh seeds are usually recommended. Pumpkin seed oil can also be used.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, John Hill, Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli, Leonard Thresher (3, 7), J. V. Cerney, Otto Wolff.

## Cucurbita sativus. Cucumber.

Cucumber was one of the “four greater cold seeds” of Greek medicine. It was classified as cool and moist in the second degree. It is used in Western, Middle Eastern, and Ayurvedic herbalism. It appears to have properties similar to watermelon seed, another of the “greater cold seeds.”
Cucumbers “are excellent good for hot stomachs and livers,” writes Nicholas Culpeper (1653). “The juice of cucumbers, the face being washed with it, cleanseth the skin, and is excellent good for hot rheums in the eyes; the seed is excellent to provoke urine, and cleanse the passages thereof when they are stopped; neither do I think there is a better remedy for ulcers in the bladder than cucumbers; the usual course is to use the seeds in emulsions, as they make almond milk; but a better way by far (in my opinion), is this: when the season of the year is, take the cucumbers, and bruise them well, and distill the water from them, and let such as are trouble with ulcers in the bladders drink no other drink. The face being washed with the same water, be it never so red, will be benefited by it, and the complexion very much improved. It is also excellent good for sun-burning, freckles, and morphew.”
R. Swinburne Clymer gives an account of cucumber as a universal remedy. “It is questionable whether any other agent, either vegetable or fruit, is as cleansing as the cucumber. It is ideal for the entire system. Cucumbers should be eaten only with a small amount of salt and possibly lemon juice, and only as a fruit between meals. During fever stages, when a cooling, cleansing agent is desired, it should be run through a vegetable press, the juice then pressed out and given in teaspoonful or tablespoonful doses as one would a medicine. Its action … is on every organ of the body. It might well be termed a universal medicine” (Clymer, 1933, I:41). Bernard Jensen (1979, 149) also comments on the blood-purifying properties of cucumber. “They get rid of pus in the form of rashes, boils, pimples, and carbuncles.”

### TASTE:

sweet • cool, moist • slightly astringent
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
HEAD
Heat and discharges from the eyes.
Redness of the face; sunburn.
Thirsty in the summer (cf. watermelon seed).
RESPIRATION
Mucus and heat in the lungs.
DIGESTION
Stomach and spleen disorders; heat in the stomach (not well described).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Water retention.
Kidney stones.
Urinary tract infections, difficult or scanty urine.
Ulcerations in the bladder.
FEMALE
Fissures of the nipples (ointment from cucumber flesh).
SKIN
Acne, boils, carbuncles, rashes.
FEVER
Fever.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Used fresh as a vegetable or juice. Tirtha recommends cucumber eaten raw with lemon juice, pepper, and salt, which is quite similar to Clymer.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Nicholas Culpeper (1, 2), Swami Sada Shiva Tirtha (3, 4, 5, 7), Hakim Chishti (6, 9), R. Swinburne Clymer (11, 12), Bernard Jensen (11).

## Curcuma longa. Turmeric.

Turmeric is a spice native to the Spice Islands, long used in Chinese and Indian cooking and medicine. It has long been used in Western herbalism, but has enjoyed a renaissance in recent decades. It contains curcuminoids (antineoplastic, cholagogue, antioxidant), fixed oils, volatile oils, vitamin C, potassium, and sesquiterpene ketones.
Curcuma is a stimulant with an affinity to the liver, gallbladder, and digestive tract. As a stimulant to the liver it acts on both the catabolic side, to “purify the blood,” and on the anabolic, to create more bile. It is also thought to rebuild damaged hepatic tissue. By increasing the output of bile it promotes gallbladder function, clears intermittent heat and chills, fullness, constriction, heat and pain in the gallbladder region, and jaundice. While increasing bile production it also stimulates digestive secretions and is used as a carminative (like ginger and galangal).
By stimulating liver function and increasing the circulation to the periphery, turmeric is used to remove skin conditions associated with impure blood, coagulated blood, and depressed circulation. It also is used to remove gallstones, exciting contraction of the gallbladder. Samuel Henry (1814, 290) gives the following recipe. “Let the patient take near a teas-spoonful of the powder of turmeric root in half a pint of warm ale, with a small piece of fresh butter, about the size of a walnut, melted in it, shake the vessel well, and let him drink it as warm as he can bear, and soak his feet in warm water for fifteen minutes before going to bed, which will give him immediate relief. A certain gentleman, by making use of the above medicine, was relieved of his pains and voided a large quantity of gravel by stool, which was the means of restoring him to perfect health.”
Turmeric has an effect on the uterus that is quite strong, causing contractions for several hours. By stimulating the general circulation and uterine contraction it removes coagulated blood and promotes the menses. Samuel Henry gives the following directions. “For promoting the menses, pour a quart of boiling water on a tablespoonful of the powdered turmeric root, and let the patient take, for a few days before the full or change of the moon [or perhaps the beginning of the menses?], a teacup full twice a day, in the morning and when going to bed, sitting every night over the warm steam of catnip, pennyroyal, &c.”

### TASTE:

(rhizome) pungent, bitter • warm • stimulating (tuber) bitter, pungent • cool • stimulating

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
HEAD
Headache.
Conjunctivitis.
Toothache.
RESPIRATION
Bronchitis.
DIGESTION
Gastritis, heartburn, ulcers, nausea, vomiting.
LIVER
Hepatitis, viral.
Fullness, constriction, heat, and pain in the gallbladder region and jaundice.
FEMALE
Uterine inertia, heavy, clotted blood during the period; delayed labor (cf. Zingiber); contraindicated earlier in pregnancy.
SKIN
Scorpion bites, bruises with poor peripheral circulation, putrid ulcers and rashes that need to be brought to the surface.
FEVER
Intermittent heat and chills.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The usual dosage is 1 teaspoonful of the power blended in water or juice, or 1–1 1/2 teaspoonfuls of the tincture, 3–4x/day.

### CAUTIONS:

Turmeric is contraindicated in hyperacidity and ulcers from heat and irritation but is indicated in external or internal ulcers from putrefaction and depression. It should not be given in high doses to patients on blood thinners. It is also contraindicated during pregnancy.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, William LeSassier.

## Cymbopogon citratus. Lemon Grass.

Lemon grass is native to India, but widely cultivated in the tropics. It has long been used in Indian cooking and medicine. In the last century it has been adopted into Western herbalism and aromatherapy. It contains volatile oils, including citral (70–85%). All grasses contain high amounts of silica, which help the sheaths that keep their upright structure. Hence, they are medicinal for weakness of the connective tissue (cf. oatseed, couchgrass). Lemon grass also contains highly soluble vitamins A and D.
Lemon grass is sour, cooling, and astringent. Therefore, it combats heat and tightens tissues of the body. It acts particularly on the connective tissue, where structural and immune functions meet.
Gümbel (1993, 202) gives an excellent account, based on the use of lemon grass oil. He calls it “the connective tissue oil.” It strengthens the elastin fibers in the middle layer of the skin. This is the layer that has both structural and immune functions. Hence, it is beneficial in weaknesses of the connective tissue of the skin. He uses it in atrophic and “reticulated” skin, where the dermis is no longer able to swell and bind with water. He considers it a subcutaneous tightener. It also tightens breast tissue (cf. Alchemilla).
The connective tissue of the skin is also important for its immune function. It is here that immune cells react against external pathogens that are making their way into the body when the environment of the periphery is off. Lemon grass acts on the lymphatic capillaries and vessels draining away from the skin so it is useful in edema and lymphatic congestion, also clearing the periphery and making it less hospitable to foreign bugs. However, lemon grass also goes deeper into the lymphatic and immune system.
Gümbel writes that it acts on the spleen in a number of different ways. It increases the power of the spleen to destroy degraded red blood cells, it contracts smooth muscles of the spleen and intestines and activates peristalsis of intestines and lymphatic immune functions in the mucosa, glands, appendix, and so forth, and it acts on the pulpy connective tissue of the spleen, where blood gets into very close contact with lymphocytes, which comb the blood for impurities and carry out breakdown and removal of old red blood cells. It contracts the blood vessels generally. Going deeper into the inner workings of the immune system, lemon grass supports the function of the thymus gland.
Lemon grass is useful for sports injuries, sprains, internal bleeding, bruises, and dislocations. The oil is antiseptic, bactericidal, and antitoxic, because it stimulates oxidation and catabolism. In India it is used in cholera.

### TASTE:

sour • cooling • astringent

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Gastrointestinal spasm; colic, gas.
Diarrhea.
Lumbago (external in coconut oil).
Kidneys.
Dysmenorrhea, neuralgic.
Rheumatism, neuralgia.
Sprains, pains.
Fever.
Colds.
Ringworm.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Diluted oil; concentrated oil causes burning of the skin.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Swami Sada Shiva Tirtha (Ayurveda), Dietrich Gümbel (aromatherapy), Hilde Hemmes.

## Cynara scolymus. Artichoke.

Artichoke is a large thistle, and like most members of that distinguished group, it has affinities for the liver, gallbladder, and kidneys. It is popular in modern European medicine as a remedy for indigestion and congestion of the liver and gallbladder. Cynara contains flavonoids, bitter principles, tannins, polyphenolic derivatives, mineral salts (zinc, nickel, cobalt, manganese, and selenium), and enzymes.
I have not used artichoke, so my friend Julia Graves, a European practitioner, volunteered an account. “Artichoke is cooling, a cholagogue of the first rank, moistening, a digestive bitter, and also contains quite a bit of inulin (this in the edible bud). The leaves are nauseatingly bitter, to the extent that they aggravate vata (dryness). This might depend on when picked. The chokes are slightly to moderately bitter, slightly sweet, and release vata and pitta (dryness and heat) from the gallbladder. The leaves probably do, too, but they are too bitter for me to try.
“The chokes moisten the gallbladder, that is, increase the fluidity of the bile, and thus help it release, along with any thickened bile, definitely cooling the gallbladder, removing heat. I find artichoke cooling only to the gallbladder.” By moistening the tract and increasing the fluidity of the bile, artichoke relaxes and decongests the gallbladder, but not “in the way of a nervine”—that is, through relaxation of the nervous system. Rather, “in the way of removing irritation (pitta at home base). It feels as if it unplugs the common gall duct by remoistening the mucosa and increasing the fluidity of the bile itself. This is distinctly different from slippery elm, which remoistens the mucosa, but does not increase or liquefy the bile, nor cool the area.
“I read elsewhere (e.g., Richo Cech) that it is hepatoprotective and helps digest fats and increases the rate of sugar metabolism, thus indicated in overweight people. I do not feel that I lost weight when I ate one huge organic choke a day for eight days in a row as a cure. But then, I am not overweight.
“The signature is obvious: the cooking water turns gall green—actually blue sometimes, and the leaves are the most hydro-active I have ever seen—once powder dry, they will turn all moist and flexible again once the air moisture goes up. I have not yet seen this in any other herb.
“Jacque and I found that it reduces appetite, and does what we call windtreibend in Germany: it pushes flatulence down along the guts, forcing it to leave the body. It also increases the amounts we pee.
“On the mental level, it unplugs old, smouldering resentment, suppressed anger, or affects of shock with anger, moving the mind to a new level. I forgot what Jacque said he felt, but I felt almost sick after the first choke, as if something was sweeping out my gallbladder whether I liked it or not, and (I had had it for dinner) making me very alert and awake, unable to sleep. I found myself at the computer, writing a very long letter I had put off to write for about four years, when my God daughter had asked me to write down for her ‘how the story with my ex-boyfriend ended.’ I had had great aversion to writing this, still feeling resentful and unable to let go since unable to resolve. It just all poured out of me, an artichoke was washing out my gallbladder, and I found myself able to write everything clearly, objectively (yet not cold and distanced), and in doing so, letting it all go once and for all. As I dropped the letter in the mail box the next morning, I felt relieved of a huge burden. Artichoke cured me of my ex-boyfriend? Now, just three weeks later, I cannot remember anymore how crushed I still felt under the burden of the memories, and how I felt as if maybe I would never be able to resolve these feelings in this life. I told people I’d pay a fortune to never ever run into him in this lifetime again.
“This is why I am so impressed with artichoke, one of the most striking, bold, and incredibly beautiful members of the courageous thistle clan. It makes you strong and bold. As the Chinese say: the gallbladder is for courage.”
John Hill (1740, 65) comments, “I have known it alone cure a jaundice.” In traditional and European medicine, artichoke is used to remove gallstones and jaundice, increase digestion of fatty meals, decrease unhealthy cholesterol buildup and blood fats, reduce weight, decrease blood sugar levels, control arteriosclerosis and high blood pressure, calm the thyroid, and clear calcium and uric acid through the kidneys.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Hill recommends the root; today the leaves are used. “Harvest the leaves before flowering, drink an infusion before meals, two or three cups a day. Eating the choke alone without other food as a meal, perhaps with a bit of cold pressed organic olive oil is the most medicinally active way of eating it. The warmth of the cooked choke makes it better for vata people” (Julia Graves).

## Daucus carota. Queen Anne’s Lace, Wild Carrot.

Wild carrot or Queen Anne’s lace is native to Europe but widely naturalized in North America. It is the wild ancestor of the domesticated carrot, but has much more active volatile oils and therefore a medicinally different profile. Domestic carrot is largely used as a cooling poultice. Wild carrot has complex powers. It contains flavonoids, tannins, volatile oils, and an alkaloid (daucine).
Queen Anne’s lace is ranked among the “irritating diuretics,” due to a high content of stimulating volatile oils that pass off through the kidneys (cf. juniper). The oils also stimulate the mucosa of the respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts. It is probably partly due to its irritating qualities that it is an abortifacient. However, many members of the carrot family have this attribute (even celery). Therefore, a deeper influence through the hormonal system is likely at work here. Dr. John Christopher (1996, 84) considered wild carrot to be a “pituitary stimulant” and used it as the basis of his pituitary formula. Wild carrot would therefore seem to act down the endocrine cascade, on the thyroid, kidneys, and sex hormones. It removes water weight by stimulating the kidneys and melts away fat by stimulating thyroid output. It controls diabetes in appropriate cases. It influences the mineral content of the urine, hence it is a traditional remedy for gout and arthritis. It has been used to regulate fertility.
Many testify to the powerful diuretic effect of daucus. Dr. John Eberle (1834, 2:318) writes, “In my own practice I have derived the most unequivocal advantage from an infusion of these seeds in anasarcous swellings of the lower extremities. They have also been employed with success in the other varieties of dropsy,” sometimes including hydrothorax (water on the chest). “They hardly ever fail to produce a pretty copious diuresis.”
“For dropsy and all obstructions of the kidneys,” writes Derbyshire herbalist George Slack (1919, 120). “The author has cured a good many cases of pain in the back with an infusion of the wild carrot. One middle-aged female had a severe pain in her back, and made very little urine. She had tried many remedies, but to no avail. He made her a strong infusion of the herb, which she drank freely, and received immediate relief. In one week the pain was gone, and she was quite well. Those who suffer from pain in the back would do well to try it.”
The late north Georgia herbalist Tommie Bass used wild carrot seed for water retention in the belly, “beer belly,” hypothyroidism, and adult onset diabetes. He especially used it for men, but others have used it as a female remedy. (These indications are from Phyllis Light, who studied with Tommie Bass.)
As an “irritating diuretic” carrot seed is contraindicated in acute kidney inflammation, so instead it is used in inactive, torpid congestion of the lungs, abdomen, or kidneys. By stimulating urine it moves minerally deposits out of the tissues, so it has been classified as an “antilithic”—it removes excessive mineralization associated with arthritis and kidney stones. “Queen Anne’s lace is a specific for osteoarthritis,” comments herbalist Kim Dudley, of Canberra. “We had a steady stream of little old ladies coming in until we couldn’t get any more.” As a stimulating carminative it increases secretion in the gastrointestinal tract, removing flatulence and windy colic. Some arthritic conditions are improved simply by increasing the outflow of water from the joints.
An application of the roots of the domesticated carrot, fresh or boiled, has long been recommended as an application to nasty sores and ulcers. William Cook (1869, 399) writes, “Sores in which it seems impossible to arouse a healing process by ordinary means will usually improve at once under this application. The raw carrots are not to be continued after full vital action in the part has been established.” He recommends them in irritable ulcers and septic sores, phagedenic, cancerous, cachectic, malignant, scrofulous, tubercular, fetid, abscessed, and so on.

### TASTE:

pungent • warm • stimulating, oily

### TISSUE STATE:

depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Fatigue, loss of will power; lowered metabolic rate; hypothyroidism; adult onset diabetes.
Gray complexion.
Swollen, torpid mucosa.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Confusion of spiritual and sexual drives (flower essence).
Sudden, unprovoked change from playful to ferocious (in dogs).
HEAD
Night blindness.
RESPIRATION
Chronic cough; shallow breathing.
DIGESTION
Indigestion after meals, burping, flatulence, windy colic.
Thin or overweight; hard time assimilating minerals and oils; food ferments in the stomach; liver function low.
Constipated, stools hard and dry, with some periods of loose stool.
Thread worms (tea).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Urinates frequently.
Edema, stones, gravel, arthritic and gouty deposits.
Water retention and weight gain, “beer belly.”
Puffiness under the skin, water retention with the period, “baby fat,” especially in middle-aged women.
Water retention with backache, swollen feet.
Cystitis.
SEXUAL
Infertility, low sex drive.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Muscles tire easily; knotty, thin muscles; fibromyalgia.
Gout; minor aches and pains of arthritis, arthritic back pain.
SKIN
Irritable ulcers, septic sores, carbuncles, degenerate abscesses, fetid ulcers, malignant, cachectic, and scrofulous sores; helps cleanse and form granulation (boiled roots of the cultivated carrot).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The best preparation is made from the green seeds in a higher proof alcohol that extracts and preserves the volatile oils. Dose need not be very large. An infusion is made by pouring an ounce of water over a teaspoon of the dried tops or seeds.

### CAUTIONS:

I think of this as a moderately powerful remedy that should be treated with respect. It should not be used during pregnancy, even in small doses.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Nicholas Culpeper, Maude Grieve, R. Swinburne Clymer, William Cook (20), John Christopher, Robin Rose Bennet (fertility), Tommie Bass (14), Ben Charles Harris (11), Phyllis Light, Flower Essence Society (2), Yolanda LaCombe (5), Christopher Menzies-Trull (6, 19).

## Dipsacus sylvestris. Teasel.

Dipsacus sylvestris is native to Europe but widely naturalized in North and South America. It is used to tease the wool, but has little history of medicinal use until recently. On the other hand, Dipsacus japonica, the Asian teasel, has an old history of medicinal application. It is used to heal fractures, rheumatism, torn tendons, and bruises. However, it is described as acting on the “kidney essence,” which is the source of bones, and the “liver blood,” the source of tendons, so there is an idea that it has a deep, profound influence. The late William LeSassier introduced D. sylvestris into practice in North America as a substitute for the Eastern species and I learned from him to use it for catastrophic injuries of the joints and tendons. Later, when I was searching for a remedy for Lyme disease I remembered that teasel was said to act on the kidney jing. Syphilis is viewed as an attack on the kidney essence in traditional medicine (East or West), and since Lyme disease is due to a syphilitic spirochete, I surmised that teasel would be a possible remedy for this dreadful disease. Besides which, it acts on the connective tissue, joints, bones, tendons, and muscles, very much like Lyme. This has been verified in my practice and that of many others and in fact there is now medical research on the subject. I wish we had other “specifics” like teasel for dreadful diseases; this is one of the few. Teasel contains bitters, glucosides, inulin, and mineral salts.
Sir John Hill (1740, 365) is one of the few old British herbalists who offered an opinion about teasel. “The root is used: it is bitter, and, given in infusion, strengthens the stomach, and creates an appetite. It is also good against obstructions of the liver and the jaundice. People have an opinion of the water that stands in the hollow of the leaves, being good to take away freckles.”
Teasel is an important remedy for conditions where the muscles are torn, badly injured, or inflamed. Especially where the large joints (shoulders, hips) are torn and damaged. Where people have lost the ability to function—“had a use, but lost it,” as William LeSassier emphasized. In addition, it is a specific for Lyme disease; unfortunately, it only works in some cases (Matthew Wood).
Dipsacus japonica is used for broken bones, torn, disconnected tendons, weakness of the lower back, loins, and knees, vaginal bleeding, and prospective loss of the fetus (Lu, 1994, 209; see Bensky and Gamble, 1986, for references to the kidney essence and liver blood).

### TASTE:

bitter
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Large, bulky people who, when they throw out a joint, tend to really damage the tissues, due to the weight and momentum applied to the joint.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
“People who had a use, but lost it.”
HEAD
Ocular inflammation.
Excessive wrinkles on the face.
DIGESTION
Lack of appetite.
Parasites in intestine.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Jaundice.
FEMALE
Pregnancy: threatening loss of the child.
Postpartum: restorative for women who have had a Cesarean.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Complete or partial debility from inflammation and breakdown of the joints and muscles.
Lyme disease.
Severely damaged joints and muscles.
Broken bones.
SKIN
Eczema, psoriasis, acne, impetigo.
Reduces scars.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Teasel is a biennial so the roots are dug during the first year, or even during the winter. Dosage should be small. Chronic cases will aggravate after about two or three weeks. Persist in small doses (1–3 drops, 1–3x/day) for six weeks.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, William LeSassier (2, 10, 12), John Hill (5, 7), Henry Lu (Dipsacus japonica—8, 9, 13), Matthew Wood (1, 10, 11, 12, 15), Christopher Menzies-Trull (3–6, 14).

## Elettaria cardamomum. Cardamom Seed.

Cardamom is a member of the ginger family long used in the Old World as a warming stimulant. “It’s a lot like ginger but more aromatic and less hot” (Julia Graves). It contains starch, fixed oils, and volatile oils (up to 4%).
Like many aromatics, cardamom has a special affinity for the mucosa of the gastrointestinal and respiratory tracts. It is specific for damp, hot weather causing a cold stomach with diarrhea. It kindles the digestive fire.

### TASTE:

pungent, sweet • warm • stimulating, aromatic

### TISSUE STATE:

depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Clears the mind.
Colds, coughs, bronchitis, asthma.
Loss of taste, hoarse voice.
Cold, damp stomach; chronic indigestion with bloating, belching, gas, and hiccoughs.
Pressure in the chest from bloating in the stomach.
Belching, regurgitation, vomiting in children.
Indigestion from stress, tension, and nerve weakness.
Sickness from damp, hot weather.

### PREPARATION, CONTRAINDICATIONS, AND DOSAGE:

Buy whole cardamom. When removed from the pod and ground, the seeds rapidly lose flavor. The fresh seeds or seeds in the pods are used. Infusion, 2–3 pods per cup, keep on the lid and do not boil. Grind up the pods, mix with butter and honey to make an edible paste.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1–8), Vasant Lad and David Frawley (1, 2, 3, 6), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (4, 5, 7), Michael Tierra (8).

## Eleutherococcus senticosus. “Siberian Ginseng.”

“Siberian ginseng” is a member of the Araliaceae family native to northeastern Asia. We owe most of what we know about it to studies carried out by Russian researchers. It was proved in practice by Russian athletes who use it as a tonic to improve stamina. It is now widely used in the East and West. The name “Siberian ginseng” was introduced by commercial marketers to provide a handle for people already familiar with the ginsengs to grasp on to. Recently, the Food and Drug Administration, in the United States, has ruled that commercial interests cannot use this name, as it was not traditionally called ginseng. It contains eleutherosides (up to 0.9%), triterpenoid saponins, volatile oils (up to 0.8%), and polysaccharides.
Russian researchers believed that Siberian ginseng acts on the hypothalamus because it has a mild, normalizing effect throughout the endocrine system. It improves response to environmental stress, which is under hypothalamic regulation. It causes a gentle warming effect toward the periphery while reducing excess internal heat. Blood circulation and cardiovascular health are improved. Herbalist Barbara St. Dennis comments, “Siberian ginseng is particularly good for people who are overreactive. They are sensitive to temperature changes and suffer from exaggerated ups and downs—spiritual, emotional and physical. This can lead to adrenal exhaustion.”
Siberian ginseng thus appears to regulate the thyroid, which is better able to adjust to changing metabolic demands in the cells. Eleutherococcus is normalizing for both high and low thyroid, but especially the former. It decreases core heat but increases peripheral warmth. Blood sugar levels are stabilized. It also acts on exhausted adrenals, increasing cortisone secretion. “You can watch the circles under the eyes go,” notes herbalist William LeSassier, referring to one of the most prominent symptoms seen in adrenocortical exhaustion. I have found this to be a very reliable indication for eleuthero. The only other plant with this as a prominent symptom is Aralia racemosa, a close cousin with similar properties.
Scientific studies have proven that there is improvement in male sexual function and it is suspected that there is also improvement in female function, but this has not been proven. Barbara St. Dennis comments, “Women under stress are not sexually receptive, in case anybody hasn’t noticed. Siberian ginseng helps women be more receptive, relaxed, and gentle. Of course, I haven’t proven it scientifically!”
Herbalist Ellen Hopman says that Siberian ginseng is particularly suited to individuals who have been weakened by the “enervating influence of civilization.” They have not had to rely upon their bodies to build up stamina by facing the stresses of nature. It also gives such people a better grounding, or relationship to the Earth. It also works on people who have been weakened by prolonged stress, environmental, psychological, physical, and so forth. William Mitchell, ND (2003, 2), recommends it for nervous exhaustion, depression, flu, and convalescence, from disease or surgery.
Siberian ginseng can act as either a stimulant or a sedative, depending on the person. However, too much can cause overstimulation in a person who does not need it. Depleted people can use it for a longer time. Barbara St. Dennis gives some guidelines for use. “If an endocrine problem is suspected, first give Siberian ginseng and see what happens. Be patient. One dropperful, morning and night. It is wonderful for the elderly, give for as long as six months, especially in the winter. It is warming. Do not mix it with other herbs.”

### TASTE:

slightly sweet • fibrous or woody

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Brain fog, can’t focus, spaced people, not taking care of themselves.
Nervous exhaustion, “adrenal exhaustion.”
Headache.
Dizziness
Depression.
Insomnia, nightmares.
Dark circles under the eyes.
Hyper- and hypothyroidism due to hypothalamic stress.
Adrenaline burnout.
Poor appetite.
Angina, shortness of breath, heart palpitations, debility.
Sore limbs, arthritic pains.
Strengthens the periphery to hold in the sweat.
Sensitive to temperature changes; very cold in the winter.
Influenza.
Convalescence; from disease or surgery.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Comes to market as a powder or prepared. Can be too warming in summertime, hence best given during the winter months (Barbara St. Dennis). I use small doses, 1–10 drops, 1–3x/day. William Mitchell recommends 6 mg. per kilogram of body weight 2x/day, or 90 drops of the tincture 2x/day. He also recommends 30 drops in 1/2 teaspoonful of glycine, in a cup of hot water.

### CAUTIONS:

Regarded as quite safe, but like the other ginsengs it can be too warming or stimulating in some cases.

### LITERATURE:

Russian research, Ellen Hopman, Barbara St. Dennis, William LeSassier (7), William Mitchell (5, 16), Matthew Wood (7-confirmed).

## Equisetum arvense, E. hyemale. Horsetail.

Horsetail or shavegrass is an ancient, nonflowering plant that grows on wet sand and is therefore exceedingly high in silicon, from which sand is made. It is, as a consequence, known as “vegetable silicon” and used as a “silica tonic” for problems believed to be due to a shortage of this element.
Critics have raised the objection that the silicon in horsetail is inactive and therefore it is unscientific to use horsetail as a “silica tonic.” This is a materialist view of the situation. It is horsetail’s ability to take up silicon, with the tiny amount of biologically active silicon within it; that is the origin of its medicinal powers. Horsetail has the “intelligence” to deal with silica and share this “knowledge” with the organism in need of the substance. Additional criticism might be leveled against this idea from the fact that the human body, indeed the whole world, contains an overwhelming amount of silicon, or silica aluminates (sand); organisms therefore do not need silica supplementation. But again, this is too materialistic a thought. What is missing in the organism is the ability to use small amounts of this element in biological processes. This is what shavegrass provides.
Furthermore, such critics clearly have not used horsetail as a silica tonic. I have used this plant for twenty years as an analog of homeopathic silicon (Silicea) and as an analog of milky oatseed (also high in this element) and it has well served this purpose. Almost all the symptoms for which homeopathic silicon are prescribed, from nervousness to connective tissue weaknesses, are helped by horsetail.
Silica has the ability to externalize or bring substances to the surface. This is seen in its homeopathic provings and clinical applications, where it has been used to open a vent to release pus or bring foreign or dead material to the surface. It strengthens the surface, a fact that is well known to farmers. Chicken feed mixtures employ granite dust (high in silicon) to strengthen the shells of eggs. It strengthens structural material generally, in the cartilage, bone, and skin, and to lessen hemorrhaging. Rudolf Steiner (1975, 133) noted that silica promoted the transportation of materials to the periphery of the body. In fact, he looked upon horsetail as an example of a siliceous plant that had this curative capacity. As a consequence, horsetail is indicated by weak hair, loss of hair, split ends, weak nails, ridged nails, cracking nails, sore, irritated nail beds, hangnails, picking and biting of nails, nervousness, and insecurity. Horsetail also has a powerfully strengthening effect on the connective tissue, which requires large amounts of silicon. It is also a good antifungal for fungus growing under the toenails.
In addition, Equisetum has an affinity for the urinary tract (weakness of the mucosa, burning pains, cystitis). It is a nourishing diuretic, increasing both the watery and solid parts of the urine. Dr. Lyman Watkins (quoted by Rolla Thomas) writes, “We have found this remedy of value in males past middle life, with large mucous discharges from the bladder.” Rolla Thomas (1903) calls it “invaluable” in gravel and irritation of the urinary organs.

### TASTE:

earthen • cool

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Faint-hearted, yielding, nervous personality; lack of flint (cf. homeopathic Silicea).
Nervous habits, picks at things, chews nails, twirls hair.
Weak hair, split ends; weak, ridged, cracked nails; hangnails.
RESPIRATION
General susceptibility to allergies, due to weak mucosa of the upper respiratory tract; itching of the mouth, nose.
Chronic asthma.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema.
Chronic nephritis (2–3 cups a day).
Bladder infections.
Weak bladder with dribbling.
OTHER
Cool extremities; hands, feet; chills easily.
Broken bones.
Hemorrhage.
Dry skin.
Fungus under toenails (internal and external).
Improved healing from surgery in the aged.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

It is said that horsetail should only be picked when it is found growing in open sunlight, not in the shade. The stalks are picked when they are still tender, crushed to dry the water in the joints, and used by decoction. If the water is not removed, the herb may rot on drying. However, this water is perhaps the strongest medicinal part so an extract from the fresh plant in alcohol is an excellent preparation. Decoction: boil in a covered vessel for 15–20 minutes (infusion is not sufficient). Flavor with peppermint or lemon balm (Otto Wolff, 1991, 86). Tincture: I use 1–3 drops, 1–3x/day. A more common dosage is 10–25 drops.

### CAUTIONS:

Horsetail herb is “full of sand,” so to speak, and should not be taken internally. It must be decocted in water, tinctured in alcohol, or extracted, with the marc thrown away, or it can cause GI irritation.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (6, 8–14), Matthew Wood (1, 2, 3, 4, 14), Vasant Lad and David Frawley, Otto Wolff (7), Michael Moore (5, 6, 7, 8, 11, 12, 15).

## Eryngium maritimum. Sea Holly.

This is a European cousin of the American eryngo, or rattlesnake master. They are unusual members of the parsley family. Sea holly is native to the coasts of Europe, where it grows in sandy soil subject to watery and salty processes—a perfect signature for renal disease. One would presume that it has properties similar to its American cousin for there are similarities in the accounts. It contains mucilage, saponins, acids, resin, and minerals (sodium, potassium, silicon). “They are balsamic as well as diuretic” (John Hill, 1740, 167).
Eryngo “grows with us by the seaside,” notes Sir John Hill, “and is kept also in gardens because of its virtues. It is prickly like a thistle, and the whole plant appears not green, but whitish.” The root is the part used; “the best way is to take them candied; they are good against coughs, and weaknesses of all kinds. They have also caused noble virtues as a diuretic, and are good against the jaundice; for this last purpose, a decoction made from the fresh roots is best.
“In the olden times it was much more used than it is now, and was prepared in the form of a candy,” writes Richard Hool (1922, 80). This is an author who is extensively experienced with sea holly, and with whom it is well to consult on its properties and uses. “I have probably used the sea holly as much as, and possibly more than, any other practitioner.
“The complaints for which it may be employed are as follows: Coughs, bronchitis, asthma, consumption, liver affections, kidney diseases, yellow jaundice, stone and gravel, dropsy, rheumatism (acute or chronic), sciatica, nervousness, fits, palpitation of the heart, loss of vital force, and as an antacid for correcting bile or a general debilitated state of the tissues.
“Sea holly I have for a long time used in the crude state as an efficient remedy in the treatment of the disorders of the liver. In cases where the liver shows symptoms of sluggishness and allows large quantities of uric acid to accumulate, it has been proved to be an excellent remedy, when combined with wild carrot and made into a strong decoction, and given in 2-oz. doses four times a day. I have seen wonderful success in the removal of uric acid from the liver, and a complete correction of sluggishness, by the use of this prescription.” For jaundice he compounded equal parts of sea holly and barberry bark. It is also beneficial for “kidney disease, stone in the bladder, gravel, and dropsy.
“In all cases of coughs, bronchitis, asthma and consumption I have found sea holly exceedingly useful, either alone or combined with black horehound, bur marigold, and crushed ginger.
“In the cure of both acute and chronic rheumatism I have seen some of the grandest results it is possible to achieve by the use of sea holly alone, given” in infusion. He mentions the case of a man who was suffering from “chronic nervous rheumatism,” who was completely cured.
“Sea holly exercises a remarkable influence over the nervous system, giving tone and harmony of action, and awakening its latent energies to healthful activity. This peculiar stimulative property is of very great service in those cold and passive conditions which sometimes attend the development of strumous diseases [scrofulous swellings]; besides which sea holly has a special influence on the organs of generation, independent of its general constitutional influence, and for this reason it has proved of value in cases of prolapsus uteri, tendency to miscarriage, atony of the generative organs, sterility, and impotence—all of which have been cured by sea holly.” Hool considers the reputation of sea holly as an aphrodisiac that exhausts the sexual instincts to be an unwarranted supposition made by writers who have no experience with the plant but generalize from its uses.

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Hydrocephalus in children.
Coughs, bronchitis, asthma, tuberculosis.
Liver torpor, jaundice.
Kidney disease; stone in the bladder, gravel, and edema; uric acid retention.
Uterine prolapse, tendency to miscarriage.
Atony of the generative organs, sterility, and impotence.
Rheumatism, acute and chronic.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Richard Hool (2–7), Charles Abbott (quoted by Richard Lucas) (1).

## Eucalyptus globulus. Eucalyptus.

Eucalyptus is native to Australia, where it forms vast forests and savannahs. I remember seeing stands of horribly burned trees regrowing from deep inside themselves when I visited Canberra after the serious fires of 2001. I never saw such remarkable regeneration in any North American tree and it spoke to me of the tremendous healing power of this plant—possibly also, it should be used in burns. Eucalyptus is naturalized in California and other dry, warm climates around the world. The leaves contain flavonoids, bitters, resin, terpenes, and volatile oils (up to 3.5%). The oil produced from the leaves contains eucalyptol (80–85%).
Eucalyptus leaves, in infusion or tincture, are antiseptic and astringent. They are therefore suited to “relaxed mucous membranes with mucoid or purulent discharge.” Thus, in respiratory catarrh and infection, also in nephritis and cystitis, dysentery, vaginal discharge, cervicitis, and cervical erosion. A specific indication is a “pale coated, dirty tongue with halitosis.” It is also indicated in “coldness of the extremities with cold perspiration” (Menzies-Trull, 2003, 604). Compare with the cold symptoms given below from Rolla Thomas.
Eucalyptus oil is widely used as a medicated steam for pulmonary infection and congestion. It increases the absorption capacity of the mucosa of the respiratory lining and the capillary bed of the lungs, thereby increasing blood-building, general capillary diffusion, and cellular oxidation (Gümbel, 1993, 200). It is also antiseptic and, by increasing pulmonary efficiency, expectorant. It is widely used in sinusitis, bronchitis, asthma, and tuberculosis.

### TASTE:

aromatic • warm • stimulating

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Relaxed mucosa with mucus or purulent discharge.
Conjunctivitis.
Tongue coated pale and dirty, with bad breath.
Respiratory catarrh and infection.
Nephritis and cystitis.
Dysentery.
Vaginal discharge, cervicitis, cervical erosion.
Sensation of coldness and weight in the bowels.
Cold extremities.
Cold perspiration.
Perspiration during chill.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Internal dose: “in small dose, 10 drops in 4 ounces of water; or in ague, the larger dose of 10 drops to 1 dram” (Rolla Thomas).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Dorothy Hall, Ellis (Ellingwood’s Therapeutist, 1908, 2, 7, 20) (2), Rolla Thomas (8–11), Dietrich Gümbel, Christopher Menzies-Trull (1, 3–7).

## Eugenia caryophyllum. Clove.

Everyone is familiar with the clove buds used in cooking or the clove oil of dentistry and aromatherapy. I remember extracting the oil in organic chemistry classes in college. I thought, “This is just alchemy, but with modern names.” Clove oil is an old herbal medicine. The clove is harvested from an evergreen tree native to Indonesia and the southern Philippines. Either the clove buds or the distilled oil is used.
Cloves are a sweet, warming stimulant used for cold/depressed tissue states. Clove oil is spicy, stimulating, and heating. It is classified as hot and dry in the third degree. It stimulates the circulation, raises the temperature of the body, increases digestion and nutrition, and is antiseptic. The volatile oils exit through the liver, bronchi, skin, and kidneys, and as they move through them they stimulate and disinfect. “It is the most powerful of the aromatic and carminative herbs” (Christopher, 1996, 462).
In large, localized doses the oil burns the skin. For safe use it is diluted to 1–2 drops in a cup of hot water. In moderate doses it is a powerful anodyne and soporific causing profound sleep. Eva Graf called it “the herbalist’s sleeping pill” and an “extreme painkiller.” In small doses it is stimulating to the nervous system, heightens the senses, and is considered an aphrodisiac. Like some remedies for tissue depression and cold Eugenia raises low blood pressure and is contraindicated in hypertension.

### TASTE:

sweet, pungent • warm • aromatic

### TISSUE STATE:

depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Depression.
Insomnia.
Dizziness.
Earache.
Dry mouth, bad breath, deteriorated taste buds; opens the surface and draws out putrid material.
Toothache (rub oil on gums as a palliative).
Metallic taste in mouth in morning, on awakening.
Colds, cough, bronchitis, asthma; chills with infection; catarrh.
Laryngitis, pharyngitis.
Indigestion, gas, hiccough, sour stomach, vomiting, nausea (chew the cloves).
Abdominal pain, colic, flatulence, griping (poultice).
Mucus colitis, diarrhea, dysentery.
Abdominal cramps.
Pregnancy; vomiting.
Impotence.
Backache.
Rheumatism (external).
Low blood pressure; poor peripheral circulation.
Fever from putridity; temperature ups and downs; intermittent fever.
Heavy metal toxicity; especially from copper and aluminum (combine with basil).
Neuralgia (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The oil is most frequently used, 1–5 drops.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, John Christopher (5, 10, 11, 12, 14, 17, 18, 19, 21), Eva and Eugene Graf (1, 2, 5, 7, 13, 18, 19, 20), Vasant Lad and David Frawley, Julia Graves (2, 5).

## Eupatorium cannabinum. Water Agrimony, Hemp Agrimony.

This European native is similar to the North American gravel root. It grows at the edge of the water and the land, sends up a pink/purple flower, and has a bitter, pungent taste. In all these properties it resembles its American cousin—though it is even more bitter and pungent, I believe. It was not as important a remedy in Europe as is gravel root in the New World, but it probably possesses similar properties. The account by Maude Grieve (1931, 15) depends on that by W. T. Fernie (1914, 21). He relies on homeopathic provings. I was unsure whether these were provings of Eupatorium cannabinum or one of its American cousins, so it is unclear whether the information relates to the European plant directly or not. The following is from Grieve.
“Herbalists recognize its cathartic, diuretic and anti-scorbutic properties, and consider it a good remedy for purifying the blood, either used by itself, or in combination with other herbs. A homeopathic tincture is prepared, given in frequent small well-diluted doses with water, for influenza, or for a similar feverish chill, and a tea made with boiling water poured on the dry leaves will give prompt relief if taken hot at the onset of a bilious catarrh or of influenza.
“In Holland it was used by the peasants for jaundice with swollen feet, and given as an alterative or purifier of the blood in the spring and against scurvy. The leaves have been used in infusion as a tonic, and in the fen districts where it prevails, such medicines are very necessary. Country people used to lay the leaves on bread considering that they thus prevented it from becoming moldy.”

## Euphrasia officinalis. Eyebright.

Eyebright is a dainty flower native to cold, boreal regions of northern Europe. Analogous species are found in boreal regions of North America and are used interchangeably with the European species. They are members of the Scrophulariaceae family. The Euphrasias are becoming increasingly hard to obtain and should be considered endangered. They contain essential oils, tannins, glycosides, glucosides, resin, and bitter principles. The properties are particularly attributed to the tannins; it is used as a mild astringent principally for eye problems. Herbalist 7Song notes that eyebright is more cooling than ragweed, another remedy for the eyes and allergies.
“ ’Tis of great esteem in distempers of the eyes,” wrote Dr. John Quincy (1736, 69). From ancient times to the present, eyebright has been principally used in ocular diseases. It also acts on the mucosa of the upper respiratory tract. As an astringent it is drying and mucus-reducing. Astringents have an uncanny ability to improve eye problems, even those that seem structural (cf. bilberry).
“Eyebright constitutes a remedial agent of considerable usefulness in catarrhal diseases of the mucous membranes, both of the respiratory organs and the intestinal canal. In acute ‘cold of the head,’ when given in doses of ten drops every two or three hours, it will effect a cure. It is especially adapted to the colds of young children, and is promptly effective in the snuffles of infants.
“Euphrasia is a remedy for all diseases of the mucous tract with increased discharges. It is one of the most reliable specific agents for acute coryza, being indicated in the copious discharge of watery mucus. It controls cough, hoarseness, headache and earache supervening upon acute catarrhal affections. During or following measles, it is one of the best agents to control the inflammatory catarrhal conditions, and to prevent catarrhal aftereffects, as nasal catarrh, catarrhal ophthalmia, catarrhal otitis, etc. The more acute the condition, the better euphrasia acts. It is indicated in acute catarrhal conditions of the eyes, ears and nose, and where there is secretion of acrid mucus from the eyes and nose with heat and pain in the frontal sinus” (John William Fyfe, 1909, 531).
Here is a case history from my own files. A forty-something man who was normally healthy had, during the last year, become allergic to a number of things and developed a chronic sinus blockage with a little mucus in the lungs. Allergies were formerly seasonal; now all the time. A year ago the eyes became horribly sensitive with itchy, red conjunctiva; now the sinuses, chest, and ears are congested. Wakes in the morning with gunk in the eyes. Nose alternately running and plugged. Ears always feel filled with a fluid. Frontal nasal discharge. Dark around eyes, pressure in them. Tongue slightly red and pointed. Eyebright fit the symptoms like a glove, but I also had him take Easter lily because the mucus in the lungs seemed dried out. Within a month the symptoms were back to their chronic, manageable level.
Derby herbalist George Slack (1919, 110) relates, “The author’s eyes were once badly scalded, causing a mass of matter, which blinded him; an infusion of the eyebright was applied for a few days, sight was restored, and has been good ever since.”

### TASTE:

astringent • cool

### TISSUE STATES:

relaxation, excitation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
EYES AND NOSE
Rhinitis, sinusitis, free secretion of clear, watery mucus, irritable sneezing and tearing.
Perennial allergic rhinitis; mold, fungi, dust mite feces.
Sneezing and tearing on going out into the sunlight.
Conjunctivitis; bright red conjunctiva; irritated eyes with irritating free secretion from the nose; gummed up eyelids in the morning.
Catarrhal blepharitis.
Allergenic symptoms affecting the eyes (small, oft-repeated doses best).
Cataract, rheumatic choroiditis, corneal opacity.
SKIN
Hives.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

If not available, the homeopathic potency (3x, 6x), which has about the same properties, can be used instead of the bulk herb.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (herbal, homeopathic), William LeSassier (1), Billie Potts (3), 7Song (2, 8), Julia Graves (4).

## Filipendula ulmaria. Meadowsweet.

The old authors generally refer to this plant under the Latin name Spirea, but it has since been classified as a Filipendula. The second name ulmaria remains. Meadowsweet is a member of the rose family. It has the bitter, astringent taste of salicin and was the source after which aspirin was named (a-spir-in). It possesses similar properties, but is nontoxic and soothes the stomach, rather than inflaming it like aspirin. In addition to salicylates, it contains flavonol glycosides (including spiraeoside, rutin, hyperin, and kaempferol glucoside), so it is cooling, where aspirin is irritating.
“Meadowsweet is a true normalizer of a badly functioning stomach,” wrote Captain Frank Roberts, NIMH. “It regulates acidity and it rectifies alkalinity. This is the remedy which should replace all the alkalis now on the market. Meadowsweet, when made into a strong tea and a small cupful given every two or three hours, will be highly beneficial in all cases of fever, no matter of what kind, and in cases of dyspepsia caused through inflammation of the mucous surfaces and glands of the stomach. It will also be found not only to relieve, but eventually cure all cases of sour belchings, sour eructations, nausea, sickness and vomiting, waterbrash, swellings after meals or vomiting before meals, bile, or bilious attacks.”
Richard Hool (1922, 73) describes additional uses. “The value of meadowsweet in the treatment of gravelly affections depends more upon its alterative than upon its direct diuretic influences, as it is more effectual in the removal of uric acid deposits than of other calculous formations, although it is beneficially employed in almost all affections of the kidneys and bladder.
“Meadowsweet, if used for treatment of rheumatism, will also be found very highly beneficial either in the acute or chronic form, as well as in all cases of rheumatic fever, either of long or short duration, and which may have been caused by hepatic, or liver derangements or from exposure to cold or wet, or from damp clothes, starvation, or general derangement of the whole of the digestive organs, causing an overcharge and excessive retention of uric acid in the muscles and joints.
“By means of its anti-acid and astringent properties, it removes uric-acid deposits from the blood, muscles, and joints; expels the waste, morbid, and diseased particles of matter from the body, and by that process removes the inflammation and pains of the parts affected. It drives away the fever; restores the action of the liver, kidneys, and bladder; renews and tones up the powers of all the digestive organs by bringing them back to their normal condition; restores the appetite, strengthens the nerves and muscles, and when that is done the patient is restored to health and strength.”

### TASTE:

bitter • dry • astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

excitation, relaxation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
DIGESTION
Headache with indigestion.
Full feeling in the stomach, with nausea, mucus in the stomach, lack of appetite.
“Atonic dyspepsia with heartburn and hyperacidity” (Bastyr).
Irritation, acidity, of the stomach; abuse of antacids.
Peptic ulcer.
Diarrhea in young children.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Acute catarrhal cystitis.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Influenza, ache in the joints.
Arthritic pain in the extremities.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The dried, aerial parts are used to make a tea, or as a tincture. Dose in digestive complaints is about 2 ml of the tincture, 3x/day; in arthritis it is 120 drops, 3x/day (William Mitchell).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Frank Roberts (2, 3, 4), Richard Hool (8, 9), John Bastyr (3, 7), William Mitchell (5).

## Foeniculum vulgare. Fennel.

Fennel is a member of the Apiaceae family native to Europe, Africa, and Asia, although nicely naturalized in warm, dry, salty soils in California, Arizona, Mexico, Australia, and elsewhere. It has long been used in Greek, Arabic, Indian, and European medicine.
Fennel seed has been widely used as a carminative to improve digestion. As a moist, oily herb it lessens dryness and flatulence. It has antispasmodic properties and is a specific for colic, especially in infants. It thins unhealthy mucus and improves the secretion and health of the fluids. Hence, it is beneficial for the lungs. “Its relief of pain and release of mucus in stubborn cases of bronchitis are sufficiently well-known” in European medicine (Friedrich Husemann and Otto Wolff, 1982, 339). “Fennel breaks and expels wind; provokes urine and eases its passage; eases pain and breaks the stone in the kidney; stops hiccough; cools the stomach and increases the appetite; cleanses and clears the liver, spleen, and gall, and removes pain in the spleen; yellow jaundice; gout, and cramps. It is an antidote for poison, and it is good for shortness of breath; stoppages of the lungs; cleanses and regulates the blood; promotes the menses and eases the private parts” (Chalid Ottway, 1933, 195).

### TASTE:

(seed) sweet, acrid • warm, drying • diffusive, slightly oily

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, constriction
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Memory.
HEAD
Migraine, sinus trouble.
Matter in the eye, conjunctivitis (eyewash).
RESPIRATION
Hoarseness, coughs, congestion, bronchitis, asthma, emphysema.
Shortness of breath.
DIGESTION
Excess appetite, weight gain.
Digestive weakness in children and the elderly.
Colic and bloating in infants and children.
Slow digestion; halitosis, abdominal swelling, constipation.
Spasmodic digestion; hiccough, flatulence, abdominal swelling, intestinal spasm, irritable bowel syndrome.
Food poisoning.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Jaundice (large quantities).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Water retention; difficult or burning urination.
Kidney stones (large quantities).
Bedwetting.
FEMALE
Menstruation: deficient.
Pregnancy: morning sickness.
Lactation: increases (boiled with barley)
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Gout.
OTHER
Lowers cholesterol.
Insect, snake bites.
Convulsions, fits, in small children.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

“The seed and root are both used for medicinal purposes. Wild Fennel possesses stronger properties than cultivated” (Ottway, 1933, 195). The dried seeds are eaten raw after a meal or infused, 1/4 to 1/2 teaspoonful to the cup of just boiled water. Fennel seed can be used as a tincture, 3 drops on the tongue or 15 drops in water, 2–3x/day. Diluted oil of fennel can be massaged over the abdomen for constipation. The eyewash has to be made fresh, boiled for sterilization and cooled to a comfortable temperature (William Mitchell).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, John Bastyr (1, 3), William Mitchell (1), Hildegard von Bingen (2), Friedrich Husemann and Otto Wolff (3, 4, 8), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli, Vasant Lad and David Frawley, Louise Tenney (4, 6, 8, 10, 18, 21, 22), Chalid Ottway (5, 12, 13, 14, 16, 19, 21), John Hill (12, 14).

## Fragaria virginiana, F. vesca. Wild Strawberry.

Strawberries are members of the rose family native to the temperate northern hemisphere. The Latin name comes from either frigida (cool), fragrans (fragrant), or frango (I break), the latter referring to the traditional idea that the exposed seeds are a signature for stone-breaking. And indeed, strawberry is an excellent remedy for a too alkaline and stony body environment. The common name refers, not to straw, but to the strewn habit of growth. The leaves are the part most often used in herbal medicine. They contain fruit acids, leucoanthocyanin, vitamin C, catechins (all cooling), sugars, tannins, mucilage, fiber, and volatile oils. Also high in iron. The small wild fruit is associated in Ojibwe culture, via its shape, with the heart and womb.
Strawberry leaf takes its place among the members of the rose family that are cooling or refrigerant. This is indicated pharmacologically by the high content of fruit acids, catechins, and leucoanthocyanins, all cooling, supplemented by nourishing, lubricating sugars and mucilage. Of course, the cooling influence is evident to anyone who eats the fruit or drinks a tea from the leaf. It is especially indicated by heat, redness, swelling, and dryness in and around the eyes. It is also indicated when the fluids are dried out—lack of saliva, lack of appetite, dry throat and lungs, scanty, dark urine. It probably stimulates the salivary glands, like Trifolium.
Strawberry has other actions. R. Swinburne Clymer (1933, 96) emphasized the action of strawberry as a kidney stimulating diuretic. “Fairly rich in potassium and iodine but well supplied with sodium, the two elements which prevent or are necessary in the treatment of such symptoms as puffy, pale face and drowsiness, watery stools, vomiting of water fluids, dryness of tongue, stertorous breathing, tonsils covered with yellow, creamy coating. If a sufficient amount of sodium is supplied to the system in the beginning, these symptoms may never appear.”
The organ affinities of Fragaria are important. As a coolant it helps to soothe hyperacidity in the stomach (like Rumex). It apparently cools the small intestine as well, for Grieve recommends it for sprue, or allergies in the lymphoid gut tissue. Penetrating into the interior of the organism, strawberry reduces alkaline overdeposition. Thus it is used to reduce stones, arthritic deposition, and plaque on the teeth (here it is a specific according to Dr. Withering of foxglove fame). The cooling influence reaches into the liver and kidneys. As a remedy for allergies, we see that it increases protein breakdown in the liver. This, and the fact that it contains salts and reduces excess alkaline deposits, explains its powerful action on the kidneys.
Rudolf Steiner introduced strawberry as a liver remedy for high blood sugar problems—many of the sweet fruits have this value (cf. blueberry). The high iron level also indicates a possible liver connection via iron and bile metabolism. The many valuable uses of strawberry have not been entirely understood and utilized. We are prompted to give it more consideration by the fact that doctors in Withering’s time considered it an important medicine. Consumption of strawberries has been shown to reduce the rate of cancer in populations. The berry is used in Ayurvedic medicine to protect against viruses, herpes simplex, DNA damage, and cancer (Tirtha, 1998, 134).
I recently had a case where a woman was afflicted with severe reactions to mold, some of which was growing in her house. She had redness and itching in the eyes, redness, swelling, and wrinkling around the eyes (left worse), with sneezing and nose and sinus irritation. Nothing helped until I gave strawberry, when she was deeply soothed, cooled, and relieved.

### TASTE:

sweet • cool and moist • mucilaginous, slightly astringent

### TISSUE STATE:

excitation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
“Melancholy, faintings, and passions of the heart” (William Salmon).
Nervousness (in people and animals).
HEAD
Red, inflamed eyes; stye.
Swollen, red, dry tongue; with painful itching and swelling (caused and cured).
MOUTH, GUMS, TEETH
Plaque on the teeth.
Discoloration of the teeth (juice of the fresh fruit on the teeth for 5 minutes, cleansed with warm water and a pinch of bicarbonate of soda).
Spongy gums, periodontal disease, loose teeth.
Ulcers of the mouth, gums, and throat; sore throat (gargle).
Lack of saliva, no appetite, anemia, weakness.
RESPIRATION
Dry lungs.
DIGESTION
Diabetes, jaundice.
Swollen spleen.
Stomach acidity; bleeding from the stomach.
Diarrhea, obstinate dysentery (roots).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Kidneys; stones.
Bladder infections with urine that is alkaline.
Scanty, dark urine, passage painful, sharp, and scalding.
Imparts a violet-like fragrance to the urine.
FEMALE
Menses: irregularity, excessive bleeding.
Pregnancy: morning sickness, prevent miscarriage (cf. Rubus).
Postpartum: night sweats.
Lactation: increases production.
Vaginal yeast infections, with itch.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Gout (cf. cherries).
SKIN
Eczema and psoriasis (internal and external).
Hives from overconsumption of seasonal fruit (but it can cause this as well).
FEVER
Sunstroke, sunburn.
Fevers.
Convalescence after an illness; weakness, fragility (fruit, leaf tea).
OTHER
High blood pressure.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Infusion: 1 teaspoon of dried herb to 1 pint of water, steep 20 minutes, drink warm (Rogers).

### CAUTIONS:

More likely to cause allergies than many other members of the rose family.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1, 3–12, 14–29), William Salmon (1, 3, 8, 17, 28), William Withering (5), Maude Grieve (6), Linnaeus (24), Robert Dale Rogers (2), Louise Tenney (2, 14, 19, 20, 24, 29).

## Fucus vesiculosus. Kelp.

Kelp was originally best known as a weight reducer. It contains iodine, an essential element for the thyroid. By stimulating an iodine deficient thyroid it reduces weight and sometimes eliminates goiter. It was especially associated with torpid obesity with cold, clammy skin. From this its general use in hypothyroidism arose, but today most thyroid problems are not due to iodine deficiency, since this element is supplemented in the diet. Fucus is likely to often disappoint, if it is used for hypothyroidism. A more effective specific here is black walnut, with kelp to be used when there is nodularity in the thyroid (Phyllis Light).
Fucus contains high levels of polysaccharides, polyphenols, tannins, sterols, minerals (iodine, sodium, chlorine, phosphorus, iron, calcium, sulphur, silicon, potassium, magnesium, copper, zinc, manganese, and other trace elements), and vitamins (A, B complex, C, and E). It is demulcent and nutritive. It thins the blood and is useful in fatty degeneration of the heart. It cleanses the lymphatic vessels and kidneys, and has antiviral and antineoplastic capacities. It can be used externally as a liniment on rheumatic and arthritic pain.

### TASTE:

salty
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
HEAD
Headache.
THYROID
Goiter from lack of iodine.
Weight gain from inactive thyroid due to lack of iodine.
RESPIRATION
Asthma.
CARDIOVASCULAR
Vascular health.
DIGESTION
Digestion, poor.
Gas.
Colitis.
Constipation.
Morning sickness.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Gallbladder.
PANCREAS
Diabetes.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Water retention.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis.
Nervous disorders.
Neuritis.
FEMALE
Uterus.
MALE
Prostate.
SKIN
Complexion.
Wrinkles.
Eczema.
Fingernails.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Louise Tenney (1–4, 5–13, 15–22).

## Fumaria officinalis. Fumitory.

Fumitory is an old European medicine, little used in North America. The ashen white leaves, rising from the earth, inspired the name fumitory, or “earth smoke.” There is an old legend that this plant sprang, not from seed, but from vapor leaving the earth. In North America, the place of fumitory was taken by its cousin, Dicentra canadensis (turkey corn, corydalis). These medicines have a similar sphere of action in sluggish digestion and “toxic blood.” Since Dicentra is almost extinct due to overharvesting in the nineteenth century and loss of habitat in the Eastern Woodland forest, fumitory, a common inhabitant of fields and ditches in Europe, might be used in its place.
Fumaria is used in modern German medicine as a specific to open the gall duct and flush bile and pancreatic enzymes. This indicates that it has a specific action on secretin, a hormone from the stomach. In folk medicine, it is used more subtly to stimulate sluggish digestion, move the stool and urine moderately, and “cleanse the blood.” It appears to raise the metabolism, like Dicentra, and to act on low thyroid. It should be thought of when skin lesions from “bad blood” or thyroid deficiency are connected with sluggish digestion.
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Hysteria.
EYES
Conjunctivitis (eyewash; causes pain at first).
CARDIOVASCULAR
High blood pressure, arteriosclerosis.
DIGESTION
Sluggish digestion, food does not digest, sits; lack of enzymes and bile; constipation, hemorrhoids; especially when associated with “bad blood” or low thyroid.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Liver congestion; gallbladder congestion; gallstones, gallstone colic.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Rheumatism.
SKIN
Acne, dermatitis, eczema, associated with digestive disorder.
“Skin diseases of an eruptive character” (Neil).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Fumaria is difficult to dry and keep, hence is not used as a dried herb.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, William Salmon, James Neil (2, 8).

## Galium aparine. Cleavers, Lady’s Bedstraw, Goose Grass.

Cleavers is a member of the bedstraw clan (Galium) belonging to the Rubiaceae (coffee) family. It is native to Europe but extensively naturalized in temperate regions. It contains flavonoids and coumarins (which give it the sweet flavor), iridoid monoterpenes (including asperuloside, a laxative), and various alkaloids. The roasted mature seed heads make true coffee, complete with the caffeine.
Cleavers has a long history of use in folk medicine. It has strong affinities to the lymphatics, kidneys, and nervous system. The vanilla smell of the flowering plant indicates the presence of coumarins that thin the blood, but for this function herbalists usually turn to its cousins, madder (Rubia tinctoria) and woodruff (Galium asperula). Coumarins are not supposed to cross the intestinal membrane, but they probably have an influence by stimulating immune cells in the membrane. It is likely that the coumarins in cleavers assist in the penetration and breakup of fibrous tissue and calcifications in the body for which it is known. Cleavers has the ability to work calcifications, concretions, and fibrosities out of the tissues (muscles, lymph, kidneys). It has been used for the removal of gravel and suppression of urine. Herbs that remove urinary stones also usually remove calcifications in the system. Thus they are called “anti-lithics” in traditional medicine, a category that modern biomedicine does not acknowledge.
Cleavers is primarily used for lymphatic congestion. It is the main lymphatic remedy that is cooling. Red root is neutral whereas calendula is warming.
