Thus, cleavers is used in lymphatic conditions where there is evidence of mild warmth and inflammation. It also acts on the breasts. It is indicated in breast tissue that is generally fibrous, rather than tissue with scattered cysts or nodularities (red clover, Easter lily, scrophularia).
Samuel Henry (1814, 75) treats us to a case. “I was called to see an elderly lady, laboring under violent pain and suppression of urine. After necessary evacuations [catharsis to remove water via the bowels], I took a large handful of the leaves of this goose grass, which was gathered fresh from the meadow, and poured three pints of boiling water on them, and ordered a tea-cup full sweetened with honey, every half hour until I returned from visiting a patient near by. In about three hours, when I was returning, her husband came to meet me, and with the greatest satisfaction declared that the life of his wife had been saved by drinking the tea.” So successful was the evacuation by the kidneys that Henry had to antidote the cathartic influence on the bowels with an anodyne—that is, an opiate, to mildly paralyze the colon.
Cleavers also acts on the nervous system. This could be guessed from the fact that the ripe seed heads contain caffeine. From the standpoint of the doctrine of signatures there is also the point that the long, slender stems with little seed heads on the end look like nerves with their terminal nerve bulbs. These little swollen bulbs look a lot like the swellings on the skin caused by neurofibromatosis. These signatures were pointed out by Tis Mal Crow one day while we were out in my back yard looking at a cleavers patch.
Cleavers is a “deer medicine,” a category in Native American herbalism associated with the nerves. Deer like to sleep in patches of cleavers (it makes them smell nondeerish) and the doe will sometimes have her fawn in such patches. (I know of two rural persons who had the opportunity to observe this firsthand.) From this, cleavers and bedstraw passed into usage, both in Europe and North America, as a birthing remedy, though Specific Indications: are lacking. I used it once to restore stamina after an exhausting labor. The long, sharp-edged stalks remind one of the finely articulated legs of the deer. These signs indicate that cleavers is beneficial for the nervous system.
Although most herbalists use Galium for the lymphatics and kidneys, I have gotten on a different track, using it for the nerves. I have had many remarkable cures and palliations with cleavers in the neurological sphere. It is beneficial in “gatherings of the nerves” and inflammation of the nerve endings (neurofibrositis). It is indicated for oversensitivity of the nerve endings, tickling and itchy skin. It is a specific in Dupuytren’s contracture and Morton’s neuroma, when the tendons tighten up under the middle fingers or toes. (Here also consider frankincense.) Long after I had confirmed these indications in my own practice I was delighted to find that one of the old authors had discovered the same property. Rolla Thomas, one of the eclectics, notes the specific indication: “nodulated growths or deposits in the skin or mucous membranes.”

### TASTE:

sweet, earthen • cool and moist • aromatic

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, irritation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Sensitive nervous system; skin tickles and itches easily.
Stagnation of water, in the lymphatics, kidneys, bladder (any or all); poor elimination of effete material; calcifications and fibrous tissue.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Nervousness, sympathetic excess.
Fussy, irritated, unsatisfied, “not myself.”
HEAD
Dry eyes.
Sore tongue.
LYMPHATICS
Infectious mononucleosis.
Lymphatic swellings under and behind the ears; in newborn babies.
Underarm odor.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema, swelling of the hands and feet.
Bladder infections with fever, heat, and straining; nonspecific cystitis, urethritis extending to kidneys, testicles.
Passes white sand through the kidneys.
Cloudy urine.
FEMALE
Fibrocystic breasts with many cysts, fibrous tissue.
MALE
Male sexual system; prostate inflamed, enlarged, difficulty passing water; hydrocele, orchitis; after venereal infections.
Irritability of the vas deferens and seminal vesicles; soreness of the testicles on rising.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Neurological disease; Dupuytren’s contracture, Morton’s neuroma (gathering of nerves under middle fingers and toes), swollen and hot.
Cramps up legs, walks with a limp (cf. Vinca).
SKIN
Skin eruptions; febrile exanthema, scarlet fever.
“Nodulated growths or deposits in the skin or mucous membranes.”
OTHER
Epilepsy.
Bleeding (cf. Galium verum).
Cancer. “It is of great service in cancer. Give tincture 30 to 60 drops mixed with milk, four times a day, and see the miracle” (Yadubir Sinha).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The stems should be collected in the spring and early summer, when they still have a vanilla-like flavor, before the seeds appear, and should be preserved fresh in alcohol—brandy brings out the sweetness. Dose of the tincture: 1–3 drops, 1–3x/day. If picked for use as a tea it must be dried immediately to prevent the flowers from going to seed and to preserve the sweet taste and the medicinal properties. One method is to pick it on a hot day and dry it in a hot greenhouse (Matthias and Andrea Reisen). It should be decocted in cold water all night because hot water dissipates its virtue (John Skelton). The ripened seeds can be roasted to make a coffee that is equivalent to the genuine article (Maude Grieve)—est probatum (Matthew Wood). In long-term buildup of fibrous tissue, cleavers may need to be persisted in for a year or longer, though immediate results will be evident. Sometimes it will only palliate.

### CAUTIONS:

Cleavers is considered to be entirely nontoxic.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Matthew Wood (1–5, 14, 17, 20), A. W. and L. R. Priest (19), Maude Grieve (3), Dorothy Hall (15, 16), Christopher Menzies-Trull (6, 7, 9, 15, 16), Edwin Hale (homeopathic uses), Gideon Lincecum (Indian uses), Maria Treben (21), Julia Graves (12, 13), David Winston (18), Yadubir Sinha (23), Rolla Thomas (20).

## Galium odoratum. Sweet Woodruff.

This little bedstraw is native to woodland in Europe, but can be grown as an ornamental and is naturalized in North America. It has an ancient, rather than modern, reputation in medicine, and is known as a “witch’s remedy.” In Germany it is called waldmeister, or “master of the woods,” presumably in reference to its occult powers. It has a sweet, aromatic scent that betrays the presence of coumarins, also found in other members of this family. In addition it contains flavonoids, anthraquinones, tannins, and iridoid alkaloids.
As a blood thinner, sweet woodruff breaks up blood stagnation resulting in varicose veins, phlebitis, and thrombophlebitis. It probably also acts through the liver by decongesting the venous blood supply, which is double in this organ (portal circulation to bring in toxic blood as well as the regular venous drainage). Hence, it is used in hepatic pain, biliary obstruction, jaundice, gallstones, constipation, diarrhea, and indigestion. Here a poultice is traditionally used on the liver and gallbladder. It is also beneficial for renal colic, kidney stones, and menstrual congestion and cramping. As a nervine (cf. Galium aparine) it is beneficial for menstrual cramps, panic, insomnia, restlessness, neuralgia, paralysis, migraine, and itching skin, and for wounds and cuts. It increases milk flow.

## Galium verum. Lady’s Bedstraw, Yellow Bedstraw.

Properties are similar to cleavers (Galium aparine) and sweet woodruff (Galium odoratum). John Hill (1740, 84) writes, “This herb is little regarded, but it has very great virtue; it should be gathered when the flowers are not quite blown, and dried in the shade. An infusion of it will cure the most violent bleedings at the nose, and almost all other evacuations of blood.”

## Ganoderma lucidum, G. tsugae. Reishi Mushroom.

The ling zhi mushroom (G. lucidum), native to Asia, has been introduced into Western herbalism from traditional Chinese medicine in the last thirty years. It is best known by its Japanese name, reishi. One common North American species, G. applanatum, is popularly known as “artist’s conk.” The most popular and spectacular of the North American ganodermas is G. tsugae. All members of this family can be used interchangeably, although commerce prefers the first and last.
The Chinese name ling zhi is derived from the pictographs for “shaman praying for rain” (ling) and “tree fungus” or “substance used to concoct an elixir of immortality” (zhi).
Ganoderma is a very important addition to the Western herbal pharmacopoeia, so I have included it despite my general prohibition against mushrooms in this book. It contains polysaccharides, phytosterols, terpenes and triterpenes, and protein (adenosine, a component in the ATP cycle). It reduces autoimmune excess (heat), but also builds up exhausted, cold people, rebuilds the adrenal cortex, regulates blood clotting, and protects the liver.
Ganoderma tsugae is a beautiful sight to behold under any conditions, but my friend Christine Dennis, herbalist (OHA), of Port Burwell, Ontario, related an especially remarkable sighting. She and her father came across a rotted but still standing hemlock tree in the forest covered with dozens of reishis (G. tsugae). The beams of the setting sun ran through millions of golden red spores that burst out from the fungi in a rhythmic pulsation. “The mushroom is breathing,” exclaimed her father. Perhaps it was some such sighting that inspired the shamans to make their elixir of immortality.
Reishi is a tonic to the parasympathetic side of the nervous system (rest, relax, eat, digest, sleep, dream), and to the adrenal cortex—the “fat of the kidneys” that supports parasympathetic function. More particularly, it balances and builds the “pro-inflammatory” side of the adrenal cortex—that is, the “mineralocorticoid” side that supports the inflammatory side of the immune response. It does not act on the “anti-inflammatory” or glucocorticoid side, which suppresses the immune response. Thus, it reduces autoimmune excess. Don Babineau, who has collected more of the golden red Ganoderma tsugae than anyone I know, says, “It looks like fire and it restores fire to the system.”

### TASTE:

meaty, salty

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, excitation

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Sympathetic excess; nervous exhaustion; adrenal burnout.
Allergies.
Hypertension; inhibits cholesterol synthesis.
Congealed blood; inhibits clot formation, platelet aggregation.
Nerves and muscles; pain, spasm.
Chronic degenerative conditions.
Arthritis.
Myasthenia gravis.
Autoimmune disease; Sjögren’s syndrome.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Although Ganoderma lucidum is the officinale species in the Chinese pharmacopoeia, several species grow elsewhere that can be used in its stead: G. oregonense, G. applanatum, G. tsugae, G. capense, G. japonicum. The officinale reishi grows on oaks and chestnuts. I have seen it growing on oak trees along busy streets in New York City. G. tsugae grows on hemlocks (tsuga) and the applanatum on maples.
The whole fruiting body is used. It requires hot water to release some of the active ingredients, so reishi is frequently cooked for an hour, then preserved in tincture, used as a tea, or combined with a good stock containing soup bones and tonic herbs and frozen for future use. Dose of the tincture: 1 teaspoon, 1–3x/day. Dose of the decoction: 1/10 ounce per cup to 2 ounces per quart, 3x/day. Many people prefer processed products in order to ensure the proper content of active ingredients in the mushroom.

### CAUTIONS:

Regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional Chinese medicine, Christopher Hobbs, Terry Willard, Kate Gilday, and Don Babineau.

## Gentiana lutea, Gentiana sp. Gentian.

The officinal gentian (G. lutea) is native to high meadows in Europe and Asia. It has many cousins, all of whom are very bitter, and are used interchangeably. Gentiana lutea contains iridoid monoterpenes (bitters), sugars, pyridine alkaloids, xanthone derivatives, and traces of volatile oils.
Gentian is an intense bitter that stimulates the taste glands, appetite, digestive secretions, and bile production. Through stimulation it strengthens the innervations of the enteric brain, or autonomic nervous system presiding over the digestive tract and gallbladder. It has long been used to stimulate secretion of digestive enzymes, hydrochloric acid, and bile. “It is the great bitter and stomachic of the modern practice,” noted Sir John Hill (1740, 188); the taste is improved by combination with dried orange peel. Closer to our time, John William Fyfe (1909, 551) writes, “Gentian is an excellent stomach bitter. It increases the appetite and aids digestion, accelerates the blood current, and acts generally as a stimulant and tonic. It constitutes a medicament of usefulness in debilitated conditions generally, and through its corrective influence the stomach and bowels are often brought up to a normal state. Slow and feeble digestion are often improved by this agent, and in fermentative dyspepsia it is employed with much advantage.”
Gentian was much used as a medicament in intermittent fever or chills alternating with fever. This condition is usually associated with malaria, though today when it appears it is merely a symptom of influenza. The alternating chills and fever weaken the autonomic nervous system, through which the chill is conducted, leading to exhaustion of the other centers governed by the autonomic—the digestive tract and gallbladder. Hence it is an old remedy in weakness of the digestion. William Cullen, the great eighteenth-century writer on materia medica, “never knew it to fail in intermittents” when given with an astringent like oak gall or tormentil (Eberle, 1834, 1:292). American physicians did not find gentian reliable in this condition generally, but there is a considerable difference in climate between Scotland and eastern North America.
Dr. John Eberle (1834, 1:292) gives Specific Indications: and contraindications for the constitution that benefits or regresses under the influence of gentian. I do not know if these indications originate with him; probably they are much older, for they resemble Greek medical concepts. “Persons of an irritable or excitable habit of body, or of a sanguineous temperament, do not, in general, bear the gentian so well as the columba, from its greater tendency to heat and irritate the general system.” However, it is indicated in persons who are “torpid” and “phlegmatic,” with a “torpid and debilitated state of the digestive organs.”
Dr. Edward Bach introduced gentian flower essence for people who are making progress, then suffer a setback and doubt their remedy, the healer, and so on. This valuable indication works for the flower essence, the herb, or the homeopathic preparation. In another instance, when the client starts to ask repeatedly if the remedy is going to work, and needs continual reassurance—even when they already feel better—give gentian. Most irritating are the people who back off when the gut-level instinct of the healer kicks in and is certain about the approach. Give the remedy then and there to reassure these autonomically challenged individuals (Matthew Wood, Jeanne Ohearn).
The person needing this remedy shows an inability to connect with their gut-level instincts. They mistrust their instincts so they worry and look for confirmation from outside sources but usually jump to the wrong conclusion because they mistrust themselves. Later on this inability to connect with life makes them feel discouraged and depressed, as well as doubt-ridden and uncertain. Sometimes they feel outright panic, hence it is serviceable in anaphylactic shock. It is also suited to stronger people who try to stir up emotions, create anger, and get other people to doubt their own instincts (Edward Bach, Dorothy Hall, Matthew Wood). In Appalachia a piece of gentian root is carried to increase energy and strength (Jack Ritchason, 1995, 95). This is an appropriate usage if one understands the affinity of the plant for the autonomic and the gut-level instincts, from which energy and strength arise.
Samuel Henry (1814, 140) used “American gentian” or horse gentian (Triosteum perfoliatum) as a substitute for the European. Other American gentians, especially Sabatia, were used by other physicians.

### TASTE:

bitter, acrid

### TISSUE STATES:

constriction, atrophy, torpor
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Persons who doubt themselves.
Torpid, phlegmatic persons, with stagnant, debilitated digestion.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Persons who do not trust their gut-level instincts; afraid to follow instincts, the advice of the therapist.
Hysteria, panic, anaphylactic shock.
Lack of faith, discouragement.
Patient is recovering, but experiences a setback and becomes nervous, doubtful, and discouraged.
Intense anger; wants to upset and discourage others.
DIGESTION
Acute gastrointestinal inflammation; jaundice, diarrhea, dysentery.
Lack of appetite; digestion slow or feeble; food lies heavy on the stomach; heartburn; gas; constipation; worms.
Atonic dyspepsia, with mental and physical depression, general debility, and exhaustion.
Anal itch.
Intermittent fever; with jaundice, diarrhea.
“Sense of depression referred to epigastric region, and associated with sense of physical and mental weariness” (1–5 drops) (Rolla Thomas).
FEMALE
Vaginal rashes and discharges.
OTHER
Increases secretion and assimilation in the aged and convalescent.
Convulsions.
Bee stings with anaphylactic shock (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The root is used as a tea, tincture, or extract in diluted or very small doses because of its intense bitterness. It is often combined with other substances to lessen the taste. For example, one-quarter ounce each of orange peel, quinine bark, liquorice root, gentian root, and cloves, pour over them a pint and a half of boiling water, and steep until cold. Strain off and preserve. Dose: 1 tablespoonful 3x/day. “The author has cured many cases of dyspepsia with this remedy after others had failed” (Slack, 1919, 115).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (8–10, 12, 15, 17), John Eberle (2), Edward Bach (1, 3–6), Matthew Wood (1–6, confirmed), Matthew Becker (7), John William Fyfe (8–10), Rolla Thomas (13), Sebastian Kneipp (9), Jack Ritchason (4, 9, 11, 14).

## Geranium robertianum. Herb Robert.

This small member of the cranesbill clan is native to Europe and naturalized (or possibly even native) to New England and the eastern Canadian shield. It has properties similar to Geranium maculatum. John Hill (1740, 139) comments, “It is to be observed that Nature seems to have set her stamp upon several herbs which have the virtue to stop bleedings; this and the tu[t]san [Hypericum androsaemum]. The two best remedies the fields afford for outward and inward bleedings, become all over as red as blood at a certain season.”

## Geum urbanum. Common Avens, City Avens.

Avens was one of the great herbs of European folk tradition, used not only as a medicine but as a protection against evil spirits and moths and even as a model for architectural devices found in medieval churches. This usage indicates that avens was thought to possess spiritual and occult properties. Geum urbanum is a common, scruffy, weedy representative of the genus, habituated to cities, that has spread from Europe to North America and elsewhere. The root has an aromatic smell and taste like its wild American cousin, G. rivale. It is traditionally classified as an astringent, nutritive tonic, antiseptic, and antiperiodic.
“In modern herbal medicine avens is considered useful in diarrhea, dysenteries, leucorrhea, sore throat, ague, chills, fresh catarrh, intermittent fevers, chronic and passive hemorrhages, gastric irritation, and headache,” writes Maude Grieve (1931, 74). Some of these indications are confirmed and enlarged by Nalda Gosling (1981, 26). It is used in ulcerative and catarrhal colitis in combination with agrimony—a Rosaceae cousin with many of the same powers. The hot infusion is considered appropriate in the early stages of influenza, with runny mucus and loose diarrhea (cf. another cousin, Rubus canadensis). The cold infusion is taken for debility and lack of appetite after an exhaustive influenza. It promotes appetite and assimilation (cf. Rubus canadensis) and probably autonomic timing and strength (cf. Agrimonia).
To continue with Maude Grieve: “The infusion is considered an excellent cordial sudorific at the commencement of chills and catarrh, cutting short the paroxysm, and the continued use of it has restorative power in weakness, debility, etc. Its astringency makes it useful in diarrhea, sore throat, etc. It is taken, strained and cold, in wineglassful doses, three or four times a day.
“The powdered root has been used both in America and Europe as a substitute for Peruvian bark and has frequently been found to cure agues when the latter has failed.” The use of agrimony, potentilla, and avens for malarial agues is very ancient. John Hill (1740, 68) testifies, “I have known [avens] alone to cure intermittent fevers, where the [quinine] bark has been unsuccessful.” And in America, Samuel Henry (1814, 26) called Geum urbanum “really an excellent substitute for the Jesuit bark [quinine], in the cure of intermittent fevers, dysentery, chronic diarrhoaes, wind colic, affections of the stomach, asthmatic symptoms, and cases of debility.” He uses it between the chills. Michael Moore (2003, 40) calls Geum spp. “one of our better dysentery remedies.”

### TASTE:

sweet, pungent • astringent

### TISSUE STATE:

depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
RESPIRATION
Laryngitis, pharyngitis.
Influenza with free secretion of mucus and loose stools.
Exhaustion after influenza.
DIGESTION
Aphthous ulcers (canker sores) (cf. Agrimonia, Rubus, Rhus typhina, Rosa, Salvia, Hydrastis, Coptis, Geum triflorum).
Gingivitis, halitosis.
Peptic ulcer, dyspepsia.
Diarrhea, dysentery; enteritis, colitis, ulcerative colitis, hemorrhoids.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Cystitis.
FEMALE
Vaginal discharge.
Menorrhagia.
OTHER
Intermittent fever; epidemic fever.
Insect bites.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Grieve (1931, 74) relates an old custom, probably dating from the period when avens was considered to have occult properties. “The root should be dug up in spring; some of the old physicians were so particular on this point that the 25th March was fixed for procuring the root (and it was specified that the soil should be dry). At this time the root was said to be most fragrant. It loses much of its odor in drying, so must be dried with great care, and gradually, then sliced and powdered as required, as they are less likely to lose their properties in this form than when kept in slices.” March 25 was the ancient New Year’s Day. In the north the ground is still often covered with snow and no avens would be visible.
“The infusion or decoction is made from 1/2 oz. of the powdered root or herb to 1 pint of boiling water, strained and taken cold. The infusion is the most grateful, but the decoction may be made much stronger by boiling it down to half. The simple tincture is made by pouring a pint of proof spirit on an ounce of the bruised root and macerating it for fourteen days and then filtering through paper. Two or three teaspoonfuls of this tincture in any water vehicle, or in a glass of wine, are a sufficient dose.”

### CAUTIONS:

All the avens are generally regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Christopher Menzies-Trull (1, 4–12), Maude Grieve (2, 3, 7, 11), Nalda Gosling (2, 3, 7), Michael Moore (6, 7, 9, 10).

## Glechoma hederacea. Ground Ivy, Creeping Charley.

Ground ivy is native to Europe, but widely naturalized in temperate North America. It contains volatile oils, sesquiterpenes, hydroxy fatty acids, caffeic acid derivatives, flavonoids, and minerals (iron, copper, iodine, phosphorus, and potassium).
Considering how little we use ground ivy today it is interesting to note Dr. John Quincy’s comment in the 1736 London Pharmacopoeia that it is “mightily used both in the Shops and common Prescriptions” and is “prescribed in almost all Distempers of the Lungs.” Alma Hutchins (1992, 1) calls it “one of the most wonderful of all herbs.”
Ground ivy has been traditionally used as a remedy for the inner ear—humming sounds, ringing noises, and hardness of hearing (Gerard, 1633). This usage has been confirmed in modern times. Glechoma also seems to act on the middle ear. It is effective in head colds, when the tubes of the middle ears are involved, or in any chronic respiratory congestion where the problem began with blockage in the Eustachian tubes. This indication was discovered by myself and Lise Wolff, herbalist (AHG), who also adds that there may be a kidney weakness associated with the pattern. I treated one case of chronic bronchitis in a fifty-six-year-old woman that she dated to a middle ear infection beginning at age eight. It took a month and a half under Glechoma, but the bronchitis completely cleared up and has not returned in five years. An older author who seems to have been thinking along the same lines was Donald Law (1973, 26): “The juice can be used to wash out the ears.” And Alma Hutchins (1992, 1) says, “The fresh juice snuffed up the nose often takes care of most deep-rooted, long-established headaches.”
The latter author considers it useful in GI problems, where there are griping pains and gas, sores in the mouth (gargle), and in jaundice from stoppage of the gallbladder or liver, and congestion of the spleen and lymphatics. “It also regulates the heartbeat by making the blood more fluid.” She also recommends it in nervous problems like sciatica, hip gout, arthritis of the hands and knees, and in endocrine conditions, like premature aging.
W. T. Fernie (1914, 228) notes, “In the Organic Materia Medica of Detroit, U.S.A., 1890, it is stated, ‘Painters use the ground ivy (Nepeta glechoma) as a remedy for, and a preventative of lead colic.’ ” (This is a discarded botanical name.) Maude Grieve (1931) picked this up, and Euell Gibbons mentioned it. Contemporary herbalists such as Kate Gilday and David Winston have confirmed this use in practice. There is speculation and some experience to suggest that it removes other heavy metals such as mercury, and perhaps petrochemical pollutants as well.
Ground ivy is a traditional cancer remedy. The plant in late spring or early summer is covered by weird growths that look like cancerous tumors, though they are actually insect damage. I consider it indicated in cancer cases when the tongue looks like “chopped meat” in the middle—that is, when heat is disorganizing the tissues.

### TASTE:

acrid, bitter, “metallic” • cool, dry • aromatic
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Depression.
HEAD
Headache.
Styes, sore eyes (external).
Deafness (external, in ears).
Mouth ulcers.
RESPIRATION
Acute head colds; especially with congestion in the tubes of the ear, middle ear infection.
Nasal congestion.
Chronic respiratory problems, congestion in the lungs; especially when there has been middle ear congestion.
Hot, wet coughs, with bubbling in the chest; it can aggravate other coughs.
DIGESTION
Dyspepsia.
Gastrointestinal catarrh.
Colic, bloating, gas.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
“Bloody and foul urine.”
FEVER
Spasms resulting from fever.
SKIN
Abscesses, tumors, gatherings.
Skin eruptions, scurvy (external).
OTHER
Cancer and diseases of intense heat; when the tongue is dark red in the center, red elsewhere, the center breaking up like “beefsteak.”
Pulls out the soft heavy metals such as lead, mercury, aluminum.
Babies; teething, colic, spasms, restlessness (decoction every few minutes until child is quiet).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The herb can be picked any time of the year, even pried out of the snow, but it is probably at its peak during flowering, in May and June. Dr. John Christopher recommends an infusion of the dried herb, 1 ounce to 1 pint of water, sweetened with honey, and drunk cold, as a cooling beverage. The leaves, stems, and flowers are easily extracted fresh in alcohol. Dose of the tincture, 3–30 drops.

### CAUTIONS:

Generally regarded as safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Tommie Bass (1, 2, 3), Samuel Henry (2), Alma Hutchins (1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 12, 15), John Christopher (3, 4, 6, 8, 10, 15, 16, 17, 18), Lise Wolff (6, 8), Maude Grieve (14, 18), Thomas Fleming, Ralph Russell (12, 19), John Hill (13), Euell Gibbons (18), Kate Gilday (18), W. T. Fernie (18), David Winston (9, 18), Matthew Wood (6 and 8-confirmed, 17).

## Glycyrrhiza glabra. Liquorice.

“Its virtues are very great,” writes Sir John Hill (1740, 237). Liquorice has been an important staple of the apothecary trade and herbal medicine in the Orient and the West for many centuries. The sweet taste masks the flavor of other agents. It was also thought to help disparate ingredients mix together more uniformly in a compound prescription. It is a sweet mucilage that is useful for lubricating, cooling, and sedating the mucosa in coughs and stomach ulcers. Modern research demonstrates that liquorice contains steroids that enhance the operation of the adrenal cortex. It is used to enhance the effect of cortisone, but it also enhances aldosterone and can therefore lead to high blood pressure, and a saltier, drier, hotter system, if one is not careful.

### CAUTIONS AND CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Not to be used when high blood pressure is present.

## Gnaphalium uliginosum. Marsh Everlasting, Marsh Cudweed.

This everlasting is native to Russia and North America, but was only used in the Old World, the more interesting rabbit tobacco taking its place in the New World. Marsh everlasting was used by Russian folk healers for many hundreds of years before attracting the attention of Soviet doctors in the 1930s. It is the best researched of the everlastings from the scientific standpoint.
No plant I am aware of makes such an immediate impression upon the throat as sweet everlasting. In this it is similar to Agastache (anise hyssop), which makes such a marked impression on the nose and sinuses. The throat is warmed, stimulated, astringed, and yet slightly favored with a mucilaginous coat. Cudweed has long been a traditional remedy for quinsy or tonsillitis and sore throat. Plinius mentions this use. It remains to this day a folk remedy for sore throat in Kent, England, where it abounds, according to my friend Anita Ralph, MNIMH.
Although marsh everlasting never achieved widespread popularity in European medicine, it was an important remedy in Russian folk medicine, where it is known as the Immortal. Modern Russian research has shown it to be well suited to the treatment of hypotonia (passive venous congestion), where the blood collects in the veins, due to a lack of cardiovascular tone, resulting in venous disease, high blood pressure, and stress on the right side of the heart. The loss of potassium with fluids explains many other symptoms such as the neuromuscular spasms.

### TASTE:

slightly bitter, sweet, and pungent • warm • astringent, mucilaginous, and aromatic

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, relaxation

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Pale asthmatics
Nervous excitability, insomnia.
Headache; specific for migraine.
Quinsy, tonsillitis (gargle).
Asthma.
Gastrointestinal problems with colic and spasms; internal bleeding from intestine.
Passive venous congestion; high blood pressure; stenocardia, heart palpitations.
Diarrhea, dysentery (in vinegar).
Kidneys; loss of fluids; diabetes.
Excessive menstrual bleeding.
Fresh cuts with profuse bleeding.
Old running sores and ulcers.
Dermatomycosis.
Wet eczema.
Burns; chemical and thermal.
Balding; to prevent (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The aerial parts or flower tops are collected during flowering (July, August). They need to be carefully dried in the shade to prevent spoilage. They extract well in water, alcohol, and oil. Officinal Russian dosage, infusion: 1 tablespoon (15 grams) to 1 cup (250 ml); steep for 10 minutes, strain. Drink 1 cup, 3x/day. Decoction: 2 tablespoons (30 grams) to 1 cup (250 ml); boil for 15 minutes, cool for 45 minutes, strain. Drink 1/2 cup, 3x/day after meals. May be taken for up to 1–2 months.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Dioscorides (8), Plinius (4), Igor Zevin (Russian research and folk usage, 1–16).

## Harpagophytum procumbens. Devil’s Claw.

This plant is an unusual native of the deserts of southern Africa, where it is a traditional remedy among the Bushmen and adopted into use by the white settlers of the region. The roots look like grappling hooks, or for that matter like the hand of a person afflicted by rheumatoid arthritis, for which this plant is most famous. Devil’s claw is used as a bitter tonic for gastrointestinal complaints and as a blood cleanser. It is used mainly for dyspepsia, meteorism, and gallbladder problems with or without pancreatic involvement. It is also used for arthritis, low back pain due to spondylosis, rheumatoid arthritis, neuralgia, and headache. It relieves pain and improves mobility in the joints. It has also reduced cholesterol, triglycerides, and obesity. Devil’s claw contains iridoid glycosides, phytosterols, unsaturated fatty acids, triterpenes, flavonoids, and free acids (Rudolf Weiss, 1988, 266).

## Hepatica spp. Liverwort.

Hepatica grows in shady woods in the northern temperate region. It has a slight history of use in European medicine. It was not much used in America, but enormous amounts passed through herbal commerce in the nineteenth century, so it probably was an ingredient in some patent medicine. Although it must have been overpicked, it is still a common wildflower. Let us not, however, overpick it again.
Dr. John Quincy (1736, 138) gives a good account of the European species in his London Pharmacopoeia. It “grows near springs, wells, and watery places, very low, almost like a moss, and puts out flowers about June, not unlike stars. This is wonderfully commended by Schrader, in those disorders of the liver which arise from too much heat; and he says ’tis good in the jaundice, hectics, gonorrheas, and fevers.” This describes conditions not unlike hepatitis. “Some are fond of it because of the resemblance it bears to the liver and spleen in its leaves, but that signature philosophy is now exploded. It is not much in the shop dispensations; but frequently prescribed in antiscorbutic and medicinal ales and drinks in spring time.” It is little used today in European medicine, if at all.

## Hibiscus spp.

Hibiscus flowers have long been used in Middle Eastern and Ayurvedic medicine. They are high in vitamin C and are used for colds and flus. They also act on internal organs, especially where heat is bothering the kidneys and genitals. The flowers are used to make a sour, cooling tea in the summer (sometimes combined with rose hips). The hibiscus is considered a sacred plant in India.

### TASTE:

sour

### TISSUE STATE:

irritation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Irritation of the arteries and capillaries.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
In women who don’t feel good with their sexuality (flower essence).
RESPIRATION
Colds, flus, coughs, and fevers.
DIGESTION
Overheated liver, stomach.
Diarrhea from an irritated colon.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema from hot, congested kidneys.
Painful urination; cystitis.
FEMALE
Uterine irritation and bleeding; menorrhagia.
Dysmenorrhea; cramping with bleeding.
OTHER
Venereal diseases; toxins in the blood.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The dried flowers are used by infusion or tincture.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Flower Essence Society (2), Vasant Lad and David Frawley (3, 5, 7, 9, 10), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (3, 4, 6, 7, 8).

## Humulus lupulus. Hops.

Hops is a vine native to Europe but grown and naturalized in North America. It is a member of the marijuana family. The female flower (strobile) contains bitters (up to 30%), tannins, ammonia (hence the “urinous” smell), and volatile oils. The fruits contain resins, pectin, tannins, bitters, volatile oils, and rutin. The bitter strobiles have been used since the Protestant Reformation to flavor beer.
Hops has an intense acrid/bitter flavor, indicating that it is relaxant and antispasmodic. It also possesses a salty flavor that suggests an affinity to the water/solid balance in the kidneys. On drying, acrid valeric acid develops, so the properties of hops are related to valerian—that is, it is a smooth muscle relaxant. It is suited to conditions where there is sleeplessness, pain, twitching, and tremors associated with exhaustion from mental, emotional, and nervous strain and overexcitement. “Hops have five basic effects on the body,” writes herbalist Michael Moore (2003, 133). “As a sedative, bitter tonic, antispasmodic, anodyne, and antibiotic.”
The use of a hops pillow for insomnia “was regarded as superstitious nonsense until a physician prescribed a hop pillow for King George III in 1787 with excellent results,” writes Richard Lucas (1981, 131). “The hop pillow has proved to be of remarkable value for tense, nervous people who are unable to sleep.” Abraham Lincoln also used the hops pillow.
Hops is suited to people with intense personalities and drives, mental strain, and strong emotions (hatred, anger), the overexercise of which results in nervous exhaustion, nervousness, insomnia, and worry. The person is too strong above, and too weak below (cf. Verbena, Capsella).
Daniel Gagnon, herbalist (AHG), characterizes hops as a remedy for “the abused stomach.” Michael Moore (2003) relates it to the “nervous stomach.” It normalizes the secretions, whether too high or low (cf. meadowsweet). Half a beer, roughly, increases digestive secretions, whereas a whole beer decreases them. When gastric secretions are diminished, food does not digest well, the head pounds, and a person can’t sleep.
“Its chief field of action, however, is to allay irritation associated with wrongs of the male reproductive organs. It is frequently of service in cystic irritation, causing frequent urination, and is quite efficient in chordee. It has long been valued in nocturnal seminal emissions, being one of the few agents which actually do good service in that affection” (Frederick Locke, 1895).
Hops is used by fomentation or hot poultice on boils, skin irritations, spasms, and especially pain of the lower back. It can be used in a bath before bed, to reduce insomnia, especially in people who find the smell of the hops pillow repulsive (Michael Moore, 2003).

### TASTE:

acrid, bitter, salty

### TISSUE STATES:

constriction, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Red face; flushed face.
Active cerebral hyperemia.
Overexcitement leads to nervous exhaustion, debility, neurasthenia, and pain.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Personality “very intense.”
“Blows up” easily with anger.
Mental strain, worry.
Mental excitement, nervousness, wakefulness, sexual preoccupation, anger, hysteria, alternating with drowsiness, sleepiness, dullness, heavy head.
Tremors, twitching, tension; with heat and nervous exhaustion.
Delirium tremens, mild attacks.
Insomnia (hops pillow, bath).
Agoraphobia.
Chest pain, with anxiety.
HEAD
Toothache.
Nervous headaches, facial pain, cerebral hyperemia, earache, occipital headache.
RESPIRATION
Strep throat, middle ear infection, earache (alcohol soluble parts).
Bronchitis, whooping cough.
DIGESTION
Lack of appetite, lack of saliva and digestive juices.
Appetite excessive; bolts their food, big bites.
Emotions (hate, anger) and nervousness cause stomach acidity, indigestion, diarrhea.
Stomach; exhaustion, irritation, indigestion, flatulence, gastritis.
Intestines; flatulent colic; colic of children.
Dyspepsia due to faulty starch digestion; after a meal food ferments instead of digesting; with restlessness and brooding over troubles.
Mucus colitis; slack, free secreting bowels with lack of tension; fermentation, unpleasant eructations.
Tension blocking digestion; hot, tense, irritated, dry conditions in the gastrointestinal tract; abdominal cramping; irritable bowel syndrome.
“Dyspepsia, with restlessness and a disposition to brood over troubles” (Scudder).
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Gallstone colic; with jaundice, spasm, pain.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema.
Urinary incontinence; nocturnal emission; priapism.
Tension in bladder or uterus; excessive sexual desire, bedwetting, and nocturnal emissions; calculi.
Cystitis, with irritation, frequent urination.
FEMALE
Dysmenorrhea; painful uterine affections.
Female sexual irritation.
Parturition: a bottle of hopped beer before delivery is used to calm the mother (not to be used during pregnancy).
Lactation: brings down the milk.
Lactation: mastitis.
Menopause.
MALE
Male sexual irritation; nocturnal emissions; excessive desire; premature ejaculation; excessive preoccupation; priapism; acute prostatic disease.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Muscular tension, drawing pains, and twitching.
Backaches and menstrual pains of the neurasthenic.
SKIN
Abscess; to ease the pain and bring them to a head (external).
FEVER
High fever with nervous excitement and tendencies to delirium.

### PREPARATION, CONTRAINDICATIONS, AND DOSAGE:

Dorothy Hall (1988, 183–88) should be consulted for a description of the important differences that occur depending on the treatment of the hops. Generally, the longer the drying, the more relaxing it becomes to the nervous system, whereas the shorter the drying the more bitter and stomachic it will be. Dosage can also change the action. “Small doses are advised in nervous anxiety while larger doses in insomnia and where sedation is required” (Menzies-Trull, 2003, 651).

### CAUTIONS:

Food use, generally regarded as safe in small doses, but prolonged use of hopped beer may have deleterious effects.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Dorothy Hall, John William Fyfe (7–9, 14, 17, 20–21, 31, 33, 34, 37), Kim Dudley (4, 11, 12), William Boericke, John Scudder (22, 23, 25), Frederick Locke (3, 8, 31, 39), Daniel Gagnon (14), Brent Davis (23), Paul Bergner (1), Matthew Alfs (5, 7, 18, 19), R. Swinburne Clymer (37), Louise Tenney (14, 16, 25–28, 41), Ina May Gaskin (33), Christopher Menzies-Trull (2, 6, 8, 10, 13, 19, 26, 31, 34–38), Thomas Reilly (3, 7, 14, 21, 29, 31, 37, 39). An important paper was published in the Journal of the American Medical Association (1906) by Dr. Thomas C. Reilly, of New York City.

## Hypericum androsaemum. Tutsan.

“A very singular and beautiful plant, and of great virtues,” writes Sir John Hill (1740, 375). “It grows in our woods and under hedges, but not very common: It is kept in many gardens” in Great Britain. “The whole plant, in autumn, frequently appears of a blood-red color.” This may have suggested its use as a wound remedy. “The leaves are an excellent cure for fresh wounds. Scarce any thing is equal to them. The young and tender ones at the tops of the branches are to be chosen; they are bound upon the wound, and they stop the bleeding, and perform a very speedy cure. I have had very late and very singular instances of the effects of this herb. Many of the common plants are celebrated for this virtue, but the effects of this is surprising.”

## Hypericum perforatum. St. John’s Wort.

St. John’s wort is a native of Europe, where it has probably been used since prehistoric times as a medicine and religious plant. It has long been associated with the little people, fairies, witches, saints, and others. It is an important wound medicine. Of late it has attracted interest as an antidepressant.
The taste of St. John’s wort is difficult to define but it is certainly sweet, oily, warm, and dry. It definitely qualifies as “balsamic”—that is, an oily plant with volatile oils that are soothing, calming, and curative. As a sweet, oily tonic it improves digestion and metabolism. It builds tissue tone, helps the liver process complex toxins (including pharmaceutical drugs), and slightly strengthens the eliminative faculties—skin, kidneys, colon, and lungs. It is a stomach normalizer applicable in both hyperacidity and hypoacidity. It is suited to weakness of the digestive nerve reflexes and the enteric brain, or animal instincts in the gut. Like other balsams (cf. Melissa), St. John’s wort has a powerful effect on the nervous system. It is best known as a first aid remedy for injuries to nerves—and is suited to injuries to parts rich in nerves (eye, fingertips, spine) where there are sharp, shooting pains, inflammation along nerves, acute sensitivity and pain, blood poisoning from injuries to fingers and toes (red stripes up the extremities), and clonic spasms and convulsions from inflamed nerves. Hypericum was considered a specific for tetanic convulsions in homeopathy. It is one of the most important herbal pain-relievers. (If it does not work, try Xanthoxylum, which is suited to even more intense, tormenting pain.)
St. John’s wort is much used in the traditional medicine of central and eastern Europe. It is a fixture of Russian herbalism and medicine (which are not separate in that country). Russian indications are therefore extensive. It is considered to be a “liver remedy” and thus a “detoxifier.” We get some of these indications from Alma Hutchins and Fred Siciliano, OMD. The latter studied with Sydney Yudin, a Russian trained doctor of botanical medicine. Hypericum with Aloe powder (the purgative) is a deep detoxifier, according to Hutchins (1992, 258). Watch the urine: “Whole flakes of morbid matter are sometimes washed away with it.” St. John’s wort is particularly called for in cases where the innervations of the digestive tract, the autonomic, are weak and there is tension from a toxic liver—“liver overpowering the spleen” (Siciliano).
St. John’s wort and wood betony were the two most important remedies for psychiatric problems in the Middle Ages—what were then called the ill-effects of witchcraft and demons. Both plants strengthen the enteric brain, seat of the instincts, and it is in this manner that they “drive away evil influences”—the person gets stronger, so as not to fall under the domination of untoward people. Today, Hypericum is used for depression and anxiety. The exact mechanisms are uncertain, but it does not seem to be an MAO inhibitor.
St. John’s wort has taken a long time to become a popular herb in America. Samuel Henry (1814, 248), an early botanical practitioner of New York City, used St. John’s wort for “promoting urine, curing of ulcers, immoderate flowings of the menses, diarrhea, remove lowness of spirits, helps hysterical, hypochondriac, and maniacal disorders.” However, it did not catch on until introduced through homeopathic sources, when it was used as a wound remedy for injuries involving nerves. Then it attracted attention, early in the AIDS crisis, as a remedy for retroviruses. It was catapulted to fame as a remedy for mild depression, however—note Henry’s mention of “lowness of spirits.” It is widely used for this at present, despite attempts to discredit it and falsely suggest that it is an MAO inhibitor that interacts with blood pressure.
William LeSassier saw St. John’s wort as a “bladder relaxer.” It is for “emotions that influence the bladder. Over urination; burning, hot urine; stressful; easily scared; adrenal overwork.”

### TASTE:

sweet, oily • warm and dry • balsamic

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, constriction, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Anxiety, depression, hysteria, insomnia.
Insomnia in children afraid of the dark.
Depression in the wintertime.
Weakness of gut-level instincts, inability to trust instincts.
Frail, older people, discouraged, depressed, in pain.
Chronic fatigue, mental burnout.
HEAD, BRAIN, SPINE
Concussion to brain or spine; like “fireworks in front of your eyes.”
Meningitis; stiff neck.
Gum disease, offensive breath (mouthwash, 1 tablespoonful/cup, simmer 10 minutes, cool, strain, gargle before meals, 3x/day).
RESPIRATION
Cough, bronchitis.
Bleeding from the lungs, tuberculosis, anemia.

Asthma.

Stomach normalizer; hyperacidity and hypoacidity.

Suppressed urine.
Ulcerations of the urinary tract.
Pus in the urine.
LYMPH/IMMUNE

Sharp, shooting pains along nerves.

Deep wounds; heals from the inside out.

Corns.

### PREPARATION, CONTRAINDICATIONS, AND DOSAGE:

The flowers are picked when they are dry (not moist) from midsummer onward, preferably on a long, hot, dry summer day. They can be used by infusion, tincture, oil, or salve. Can cause sensitivity to light—sunburn.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional eastern European remedy, homeopathic sources, Flower Essence Society (2), Matthew Wood (3, 4, 41), Julia Graves (1, 2, 3, 7, 41), Alma Hutchins (7–14, 17, 19, 20, 22, 25–29, 31–37, 42–44), Fred Siciliano (15–18, 21), Jack Ritchason (6), William LeSassier (23–24).

## Hyssopus officinalis. Hyssop.

Hyssop is a bitter, pungent mint, long used in Mediterranean medicine but easily grown in gardens elsewhere. It is related to or identical with the hyssop of the Bible, of which it is said: “Purge me with hyssop and I shall be clean.” It had economic and social importance as a sacrifice at the Jewish Temple, as a fumigant in pagan ceremonies, and as a household cleaning agent (cf. Thymus). Scholars have identified another plant as the hyssop used in the Temple as a fumigant, but as Willie Southall notes, it is the main plant growing out of the Weeping Wall, the remnant of the Herodian Temple.
The ancient use of hyssop as a general remedy for fever and bronchial infection is justified, from a modern perspective, by the fact that mold growing on the leaves produces penicillin. From the traditional standpoint, however, hyssop was valued as a diaphoretic that opened the pores to allow the exit of fever. Hyssop has a smell like skunk, which tips us off that it will be deeply penetrating, opening pores and passageways deep inside the body, as well as in the skin, releasing heat through the skin, bringing cooling, lubricating, cleansing fluids into interior organs, dredging blood and fluids to remove heat and congestion, bringing pathogenic heat to the surface and out through the skin. It is indicated when mucus is hardened from heat baking down the fluids. It acts deeply on heat in the deep tissues and viscera. Richard Hool (1922, 37) gives a good account of this herb.
“In hyssop, when employed for the cure of continued or slow fever, we have one of the finest remedies that we can ever use, especially when children are the sufferers.” The old-fashioned term “slow fever” refers to conditions where the nerves are affected so the reactions and movements are slow, numb, and weak. “You will not find its equal when made into an infusion with 1 oz. of hyssop to 1 pint of boiling water, stewed for 15 minutes, and 1 wineglassful being taken every hour during the day. It will be found to have permeated the system, and its stimulating properties to have relieved the dryness of the mucous surfaces lining the stomach and bowels. It will cause diaphoresis or moisture of the skin to appear on the whole surface of the body, and will relieve the kidneys and bladder by means of its diuretic properties, and owing to its mild, aperient action the bowels also will be relieved.
“Scarlet fever, measles, typhoid fever, spotted fever, and inflammation of the bowels in children may be treated in the same manner as above stated. I remember in the year 1868 fever was extensively prevalent in Bolton, and I had a sister who at that time was attacked with the continued or slow fever, which is supposed to last any length of time from three to four to eighteen or twenty weeks. My father and mother were on the point of calling in a doctor, but I persuaded them not to do so, although my sister was in a very dangerous condition. I prevailed on them to give her plenty of hyssop tea and sponge her body down with vinegar and hot water every day, and keep a good fire in the room.” In two weeks she was entirely cured.
“In case of a cough, bronchitis, and asthma, hyssop made into a syrup with sugar, honey, or black treacle, will be found beneficial, as it relieves the dryness of the mucous membranes of the bronchial tubes, and so allays the inflammation and removes the obstructing phlegm preventing plasticity of the blood, and thereby congestion of the lungs and heart. Its action in pneumonia is most beneficial owing to its diaphoretic property.” When an old coating of mucus is dispersed from the linings it is replaced by a new coating that is full of rich immunological and healing substances, so that expectoration of old mucus alone has a healing effect. This is a general principle (not limited to hyssop), pointed out by John Reddon, herbalist (OHA), of Toronto.
“In case of sore throat it may be used in the same manner as stated for coughs, and the throat gargled with the infusion or tea three or four times a day.” It prevents “swelling of the throat and glands of the neck, and keeps the stomach in good condition, causing a regular evacuation of the bowels.
“Its action on the stomach by means of its bitter, aromatic, odorous principle gives tone to the muscular parts of action, and thereby causing better absorption of the fluids taken into the stomach. By that means it produces the better digestion and assimilation of food, and at the same time removes the fetid or bad-smelling breath.
“It keeps the kidneys and bladder in good condition, so that the water can be voided through its natural channels, and so prevent inflammation or Bright’s disease.” Hyssop is also known as a remedy to open obstructions to the liver and to treat jaundice.
Herbalist Willie Southall, of Los Angeles, developed the use of hyssop based on the biblical references such as “Cleanse me with hyssop and I will be cleansed,” and so on. He has made soaps, bath oils, lotions, and all kinds of products, and contributes to our knowledge of the applications of hyssop. In a similar vein, Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1973, 152) recommends hyssop for poisoned animals. “An infusion of hyssop plant given morning and evening is highly effective as a remedial agent in all forms of poisoning.” She attributes this knowledge to the animal world. “Foxes are said to seek out this plant when poisoned.” The little critters are evidently better read in the Bible than we generally suppose.
David Dalton (2006, 39) developed a profile for the mental state associated with hyssop as a flower essence that may owe something to the biblical concept. He recommends it for people who feel guilty, feel undeserving of happiness, fear punishment, fear pleasure, and engage in self-sabotage. He also cites it for relationship to the stomach, food assimilation, and mineral deficiency.

### TASTE:

pungent, bitter • warm • diffusive

### TISSUE STATE:

depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Fever with dry, hot skin.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Feels guilty, undeserving of happiness, fears punishment, fears pleasure (flower essence).
HEAD
Lice (external).
Eye and ear ailments.
Fetid breath, due to putrid digestion.
Tonsillitis, sore throat, swollen glands of throat and neck.
Hoarseness.
RESPIRATION
Deep-seated respiratory infection with fever, hardened mucus, harsh, dry cough, hot, dry skin.
Colds, congestion, bronchitis, pneumonia, asthma, pleurisy.
Coughs, irritable, harsh, wheezing, chronic.
CARDIOVASCULAR
High blood pressure.
DIGESTION, LIVER
Deep-seated heat in stomach and intestines, with mucus, putrefaction, worms.
Liver; hepatitis, jaundice.
SPLEEN, LYMPHATICS
“Spleen problems” (Tenney).
Swollen glands.
KIDNEYS
Kidneys not passing enough water, Bright’s disease, edema, infection.
FEMALE
Amenorrhea.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Muscular ache; fibromyalgia (external).
Epilepsy (probably in convulsions from fever).
Infantile paralysis.
SKIN
Skin, cuts, bruises (external; application of fresh leaves).
FEVER
Skin hot and dry in fever (infusion).
Continued or slow fever; especially in children (infusion).
Scarlet fever, measles, typhoid fever, spotted fever, inflammation of the bowels in children (infusion).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The leaves and flowers are removed from the woody stalks and extracted in alcohol or water. Dose: 3–15 drops. Infusion or decoction.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, David Dalton (2), Willie Southall (13-confirmed), Louise Tenney (3, 4, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 18, 19, 21), Richard Hool (1, 5, 6, 8, 9, 12, 15, 16, 20, 22, 23, 24), Al Tamimi (13).

## Illicium verum. Star Anise.

Star anise is a small tree in the magnolia family native to southern China and Vietnam, where it is cultivated for its culinary, medicinal, and pharmaceutical uses. The dried fruit and seeds within are the source of the volatile oil that is the medicinal part. It should not be confused with anise seed (Pimpinella anisum). The oil or a decoction or tincture is used as a sweet, warm, pungent carminative, antispasmodic, bronchial expectorant, and diuretic. Over the years I have used it as a specific for congestion in the maxillary sinuses and it has always worked surprisingly well. The seedpods look like sinuses.

### TASTE:

pungent, sweet, warm

### CAUTIONS:

Not to be used with small children and not to be confused with Pimpinella when the latter is given to children for digestive problems.

## Inula helenium. Elecampane.

Elecampane is a member of the composite family native to Europe but grown and occasionally naturalized in North America. It prefers low, moist, fertile ground, in the shade or open. It was anciently classified as hot and dry in the third degree, which means that it opens the pores to release sweat, thins the fluids to drive them out through the pores and channels of elimination, rekindles the internal fire to drive waste products to the surface, and decongests stagnant fluids in the body. It is one of the most antiseptic and antibacterial herbs. It is so sanative that it has been used as a surgical dressing. Late-nineteenth-century research showed that even diluted elecampane (1 to 10,000) will kill bacteria and especially the bacillus of tuberculosis (Frederick Locke, 1895; Maude Grieve, 1931, 281). Elecampane contains high amounts of the starch inulin (up to 44%), mucilage, resin, sterols, bitter terpenoids and sesquiterpenes, and volatile oils (up to 4%).
“Hardly any plant has more virtues,” writes Sir John Hill (1740, 165). Elecampane is a warming, stimulating, pungent, aromatic bitter that permeates the bronchial tree. It resolves bacterial infection, reducing heavy, thick, green mucus down to yellow and eventually to white or clear mucus as it sanitizes the lungs. It is specific to yellow and green mucus, indicating bacterial infection. The removal of the layer of old, adhesive mucus allows for the secretion of a new layer of thin, clear mucus that is impregnated with immune factors. Meanwhile, the bitters protect the stomach against indigestion caused by mucus that is swallowed. Very typically, the person needing elecampane (often a child) swallows the mucus. It is suited to old, lingering, infected pulmonary conditions that slip into asthma—also to recent-onset bronchitis in children, with gushing mucus, swallowing of mucus, when the cough reflex will not descend deep enough to bring out the big clump of mucus.
After giving Inula the cough descends deep enough to bring forth this mucus, which can come gushing out. Some practitioners misunderstand the positive effect of this “ripening” of the stage of the disease, thinking that elecampane causes the infection to go deeper, rather than causing the cough to go deeper and bring up the locked up mucus.
Elecampane is sometimes called “scabwort.” I always thought this name referred to its use for healing scabs, but I learned from herbalist Jennifer Tucker, of State College, Pennsylvania, that her teacher, Evelyn Snook, used elecampane for proud flesh, where the scab refuses to form. I have since had the opportunity to verify this. This leads us to an additional insight about elecampane. Not only is it suited to old infections in the lungs, where the mucus is green or yellow from the presence of bacteria, but it has a facility for “getting the job done” when there is a lingering, stuck infection that will not yield to treatment. It not only cleans up the bacteria that have taken residence in the part, but corrects the condition of the deteriorated tissue upon which the critters thrive.
Brent Davis, DC, of Nashville, Tennessee, suggests that elecampane has a special influence on the lymphoid tissue in which white cells are embedded—found in the adenoids, tonsils, small intestine, liver, and elsewhere. The white cells are needed to protect passages coming in from the surface, to digest proteinaceous food, and to process toxic materials. Davis especially points to the lymphoid tissue in the gut. He recommends elecampane in poor absorption through the lymphatics surrounding the small intestine, leading to poor tissue nutrition. There are probably also problems with unhealthy bacterial colonies in the colon—elecampane is a vermifuge.
I had a personal experience that supports the idea that elecampane acts on the lymphoid tissue of the throat. Since childhood I suffered from swollen glands and frequent infection in the right side of the throat. One day I took a huge bite of elecampane (I should have known better). Although I quickly spit it out, for the next week I had swelling, heat, and pain in the throat, but after that my throat was in much better shape. I also used occasional cups of sage tea, which cleanses the throat.
The large quantity of inulin contained in the plant (and named after it) is indicative of the fact that Inula is nutritious and rebuilding in old, worn-out, exhausted, and broken-down states, with poor nutrition and assimilation. (Specifically, in Greek medicine, it supports agglutination, which helps the nutrients conjoin to the tissues, and helps the lips of the wound come together to prevent proud flesh.) Inulin is a starch that does not trigger insulin but instead provides blood sugar to the muscles and extremities, where the cells take up sugar under the influence of a different hormone (IGF, or insulin-like growth factor). William Salmon (1710) recommended elecampane to rebuild a consumptive and Dorothy Hall speaks of it for thin, hunched, pale persons with obvious poor nutrition. Very often, these are people with poor immunity. Dr. John Christopher (1976, 374) describes it in a similar fashion: “malnutrition, rickets, glandular insufficiency, nervous debility, weakly children, poor digestive powers, muscular weakness, and convalescence.” On the other hand, as a powerful stimulant elecampane acts on damp, phlegmatic kapha people with stagnant lymphatics.
Salmon (1710, 332) used the distilled oil. “It has all the virtues of the liquid juice, essence, pouder, electuary and spirit, and may be given in place of all or any one of them, being much more powerful than them all. I have had great experience of it in facilitating the birth, and bringing away the after-birth. It powerfully provokes the terms in women where they are stopt; cures vapors and hysterick fits. There is nothing better against coughs, colds, catarrhs, hoarseness, wheezing, shortness of breath and difficulty breathing; as also the colic in stomach or bowels, than this oil. You may give it from six drops to fourteen or twenty, dropping it first into white sugar, and mixing them well; and then mixing all with a glass of good, generous wine, and so drinking it off. It opens obstructions of reins and bladder, and provokes urine powerfuly.”
Yet another set of uses are brought out by Al Tamimi (d. 980) in his Al Murshid. “Juice of the elecampane in bee’s honey prepared with spices and muscat [Myristica fragrans,] which is called a king’s drink [potio regia,] is beneficial for the elderly and those with moisture. It dissolves the moisture of the superfluities, is salutary for pains in cold joints, strengthens the stomach and the heart, increases the appetite and stimulates the libido” (quoted by Moses Maimonides; Rosner, 1989, III:331).
I recently saw a case in which I gave a middle-aged woman elecampane for chronic respiratory infection with yellow and green mucus. Not only did it help the sinuses and lungs but the ventricular fibrillation she had went away. She then had a dream in which she had mucus around her heart. She also felt an increase in libido. That was pleasant, though elecampane did not cure the problem she came to me for: lack of energy.
Elecampane is considered to be a heart remedy in Ayurvedic medicine (David Winston). I recently used it with success in an older Afghan refugee who suffered from terrible pain in the heart, with great sadness and grief, as a result of leaving his homeland. The cardiac doctors did not consider him to have a heart problem. He also suffered from an occasional yellow discharge from the sinuses. I gave him Inula, 3 drops, 3x/day. It warmed his hands and feet, took the pain out of the heart almost from the first day, and improved his emotional state.
The indication “torn away from one’s home,” causing grief and suffering, comes from Jennifer Tucker. She uses it for women who have had to follow their husbands to new locations for work and feel torn away from home. The legend is that when Helen was kidnapped by Paris the plant sprang up where her tears fell. Afterward the plant was known as “Helen of the campagna”—elecampane.
An old name for Inula is “elfwort.” Possibly this plant was used to treat “elfshot”—wasting and preoccupation caused by being shot by an elfin arrowhead. “Elves were supposed to live under the plant,” observes Donald Law (1973, 49) rather tentatively.

### TASTE:

acrid, bitter, pungent (Christopher, 1976, 374)

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, stagnation, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Thin, pale, hair lifeless, extremities cold, eyes dull, hunched posture.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Depression, poor concentration, memory lapses.
Homesickness, “torn away from home.”
Helps the disposition.
HEAD
Weak, dim eyes; cataract.
Fastens loose teeth; keeps the gums from putrefaction; lessens tooth decay.
RESPIRATION
Hoarseness; throat swollen, extreme pain.
Diphtheria (small, repeated doses).
Acute bronchitis, especially in children; the cough cannot descend deep enough to bring up the mucus; afterward the mucus gushes out, is swallowed, and creates indigestion.
Any respiratory infection where the mucus causes indigestion.
Chronic postnasal drip, with thick, yellow, green mucus; drips, irritates the throat and stomach.
Old, chronic bronchial and sinus infections, with yellow to green mucus.
Chronic bronchitis turning into asthma.
Sore spots around the lungs; stuffiness in the chest.
Shallow breathing, sighing, shortness of breath.
Persistent cough with substernal pain.
Tuberculosis.
Whooping cough.
DIGESTION
Loss of appetite, poor assimilation, poor lymphoid tissue in gut, malnutrition.
Gastric atonicity, catarrhal condition of the mucosa.
Wind and spasm in the stomach and intestines; colic.
Prickings and pain in the side from swollen spleen.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema; stoppage of urine; stone, gravel; prevents the return of swelling to the foot after a cure of edema has been effected.
Bladder and urethra; cystitis, catarrh.

Endometriosis.
Amenorrhea, delayed menses.
Expels the afterbirth, prevents putrefaction, convulsions, and fits.

Cramps, convulsions.
Paralysis of the nerves.
SKIN
Dry patches on the skin; itching, scabs, and sores.
Proud flesh, the wound does not form a scab.
FEVER
Fever; restores capillary circulation after exposure to chill.
Fever; infection deep, septic, putrefactive, malignant; resulting in convulsions, fits, nervous damage.
OTHER
Snake bite.
“Malnutrition, rickets, glandular insufficiency, nervous debility, weakly children, poor digestive powers, muscular weakness, and convalescence.”

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Traditionally, the roots are used in European medicine, but the flowers are used for the same bronchial symptoms in China. The dried roots are available in commerce, “but it is for most purposes better to take that fresh out of the garden” (John Hill, 1740, 165).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Plinius, William Salmon (5–7, 10, 11, 12, 14, 16, 17, 20–22, 27–31, 34, 35, 36), Andrew Lange (2), Jennifer Tucker (3, 33), Evelyn Snook (33), Dorothy Hall (1, 2, 15), Matthew Wood (9–14, 24), Christopher Menzies-Trull (8, 13, 16, 18, 19, 23, 25, 26), Louise Tenney (4, 6, 19, 21, 24), Brent Davis (19), John Christopher (6, 8, 12, 15, 18, 23, 24, 27, 32, 33).

## Lactuca virosa. Wild Lettuce.

Wild lettuce is a member of the aster family. The officinal species is Lactuca virosa, a native of Europe. Prickly lettuce (L. scariola) and the native American L. canadensis can be used in its place. All are powerful sedatives or relaxants. Wild lettuce contains terpenoid bitters and resins, sesquiterpene lactones, coumarins, acids, alkaloids (a trace of hyoscyamine), flavonoids, and a trace of opiates. I claim, almost in its entirety, credit for developing the following profile for wild lettuce.
Lactuca relaxes tight muscles and settles insomnia. It is indicated for stiff, sore persons with painful muscles, especially in the lower back. The pulse is slow and hard. This pulse type was produced in the original homeopathic provings in the 1830s and I have confirmed it dozens of times in practice, as has my friend Julia Graves. It indicates “cold blockage” or “internal cold,” as it would be termed in Chinese herbalism. This is cold that has invaded the body, causing hardness, stiffness, and pain. It is the opposite of “deficient cold,” which arises from a lack of internal heat; the pulse is slow and deep.
Wild lettuce is one of the bitter/acrid remedies that disperse tension caused by penetration of cold. Cold often constricts the pores of the skin. However, in this case the cold goes to a deeper level, anchoring in the muscles and articulations, resulting in stiff, sore muscles, lack of bend-ability, soreness in the lower back, and shallow respiration from tight muscles across the chest. Sometimes there is slight evidence of the heat that is being blocked by the cold. Heat rises; in the case of Lactuca it produces red margins of the eyelids, allergies, and facial acne.
Lactuca is one of the best remedies in existence for teenage acne, or any acne where the face is involved and there is a rough, cystic form with deep cysts slowly coming to the surface; the whole causing unsightly roughness and scar tissue. It will clean this up from the inside, reducing the acne on the face for several weeks. Then a new crop—the cysts from below—boil up at about the third week and cause a new outbreak. After that the face clears up and even old scars seemingly disappear because the heat, swelling, and waste products in what appear to be scars are dissipated. Meanwhile, the hard, slow pulse softens.
I have a note from a now forgotten source to “treat wild lettuce like a drug” because “overuse can rob one of motivation.” This is exactly what lactuca, in small doses, is for. I call it the “street person remedy.” It is for people with a complete lack of motivation. Before this stage is reached, however, it is a remedy for negative-thinking. This came out in the homeopathic provings. Lactuca people sit and think about the worst possible scenarios that could happen and that keeps them home, brooding over imaginary problems and injustices (John Henry Clarke). I have often verified this symptom in practice. It may go along with the shallow breathing.
So far we have been discussing lactuca from the standpoint of an “energetic” model involving cold, but the symptoms can also be accounted for from another perspective. Deep chills, acne, and frustration can also be associated with high androgen levels. Androgen is hard for the liver to break down and is one of the causes of “bad blood” and skin eruptions. High levels occur in teenagers, both boys and girls, as they enter sexual maturity and their bones grow—androgen stimulates bone growth. Lactuca is one of the great remedies for teenagers (“acne, frustration, lack of motivation”), along with phytolacca (“lazy, apathetic teenagers”). One would associate high androgen levels with a high sex drive—we certainly see this in teens—but high levels do not translate automatically to a successful sex life. Indeed, they are often associated with frustration and the need to make changes in life.
Androgen stimulates the immune system, the sex drive, and the liver, but it can overstimulate all of these functions, resulting in chills alternating with fever (or just chilliness and stiffness), frustration, and liver and skin problems. High levels of androgen are a particular problem—and a very common one—in young women, causing polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS) and infertility. I have twice used lactuca for infertility with success. The indication was the slow and hard pulse. It was only years later that I realized lactuca had a hormonal effect. In one case the woman was pregnant within five days; in the other, less than a month. From an energetic perspective we would blame the infertility on cold in the uterus, or even “cold in the liver meridian.”
This view of wild lettuce, as a specific for high androgen, is a product of my clinical experience, and has not been explained pharmacologically. Indeed, my theory of treatment for hyperandrogenism, which revolves around intense bitters, is based on clinical experience. However, I offer it as the fruit of experience, to be accepted or rejected by additional study. Wild lettuce is in a group of remedies for high androgen that are intensely bitter, including blessed thistle and white hoarhound. There are, however, remedies for this condition that are not bitter, like Easter lily—in my experience another great acne remedy. And speaking of hormones, one client who was taking it for her back pain reported that it lowered her blood sugar levels.
Samuel Henry (1814, 475) considered Lactuca virosa to be very effective in dropsy—but was it actually the naturalized L. scariola?—Henry is poor with his botany. “I have found an extract prepared from the expressed juice of the leaves, gathered when in flower, of great efficacy in the cure of the dropsy.” He gave 10–12 grains/day, in four doses, increasing the dose until the diuresis started. “They agree very well with the stomach, quench thirst, proved gently laxative, powerfully diuretic, and somewhat diaphoretic.” He also gave a tea of “cohush” or Caulophyllum. The wife of a judge in Ramapo, New Jersey, was “laboring under an ascites,” which was getting worse. He left some Lactuca and Caulophyllum and returned to New York City. “Two days after, I asked the judge how his lady was; he told me, to my surprise, that his wife was at work in the kitchen, and that after using the medicine I had left she had made whole pots of water.”

### TASTE:

bitter, acrid • warm, moist

### TISSUE STATES:

constriction, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Lack of motivation; hard experiences; deadened, stiff, cold.
Negative-thinking; train of thought habitually ends in negative possibilities and outcomes.
Insomnia, restlessness.
Excitability in children.
Teething in children (tea).
FACE
Redness of the margins of the eyelids, with general cold; allergies.
Facial acne, cystic acne, scar tissue.
RESPIRATION
Coughing, whooping cough, bronchitis, asthma.
Tension across muscles of the chest; constricted, shallow breathing.
Constant tickling cough.
MALE
Blows to the testicles.
Inflamed prostate.
FEMALE
Dysmenorrhea.
Infertility.
Lactation.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Tension and tightness of the lower back.
Cold extremities.
SKIN
Warts, moles, cancers (juice, external).
Pulse slow and hard.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The leaves are harvested in late spring or early summer, before the flowers come out. Extract fresh in alcohol. Dose: 1–3 drops, 1–3x/day. Low homeopathic potencies can also be used. Large doses induce sleep.

### CAUTION:

Lactuca can cause sleepiness; caution before driving. Always warn clients they can get sleepy while driving when on wild lettuce.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, homeopathic provings (2, 19), Matthew Wood (1, 2, 6–9, 11, 14, 15, 19), Ralph Russell (5), Julia Graves (19-confirmed), Tommie Bass (18), Christopher Menzies-Trull (4).

## Lamium album. White Archangel, White Deadnettle.

This dainty member of the mint family is native to Europe and central and northern Asia. One cousin (Lamium amplexicaule) is widely naturalized in North America, and others are grown as ornamentals. I never thought any of them looked like a nettle until I was in Britain, and saw the nettles over there (Urtica urens). Some of them do look like deadnettle. Lamium flower is used in European folk medicine primarily as a female remedy. It contains flavonol glucosides, mucilage, amines (histamine, tyramine, methylamine), saponins, tannins, and volatile oils. It is chiefly valued as an astringent and diuretic.
“The flowers are the only part used,” writes Sir John Hill (1740, 62). “They are to be gathered in May, and made into conserve. A pound of them is to be beat up with two pounds and a half of sugar. They may also be dried. They are excellent in the whites, and all other weaknesses.” Lamium flowers are indicated in young women who are suffering from weakness from a nutritive cause—hence the use of sugar. Precisely the same indications are given two centuries later by Dr. Rudolf Weiss (1988, 314). He cites Lamium as a traditional German folk remedy for vaginal discharge in young, asthenic girls. That would be thin, atrophic, vata women. He adds, “It is often possible to get very good results in these persistent cases, particularly if another tonic such as yarrow is added.” The use of archangel has declined in Britain, but is still established in Germany. It is virtually unknown in North America.
Herbalist George Slack (1812–93) of Derbyshire gives the following account of the more general applications of the archangels—red, white, and yellow. “Good for the spleen, whites, floodings, bleeding of the nose, spitting blood, or any kind of hemorrhage; it is very effectual in healing green wounds, bruises, and burns” (Slack, 1919, 104).

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Insomnia.
Nosebleed.
Inflammation of the mouth and throat.
Catarrh of the upper respiratory tract.
Cough, bronchitis, pleurisy.
Stomach; gastritis, bloating, flatulence.
Swollen spleen.
Intestinal hemorrhage.
Urinary and vaginal catarrh.
Menstruation; excessive bleeding during periods, breakthrough bleeding, cramping, PMS, vaginal discharge.
Leucorrhea of young, asthenic girls.
Inflamed prostate.
Arthritis, gout, sciatica (poultice).
Skin, mild, superficial inflammation (external).
Anemia.
Hemorrhage (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

“The flowers are used medicinally; they have to be pulled from their calices one by one” (Weiss). This is probably the reason why this herb has not been commercially exploited. Infusion of the herb; tincture.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, John Hill (2, 10, 11, 16), George Slack (2, 7, 16), Rudolf Weiss (11), Christopher Menzies-Trull (1, 2, 4, 5, 7–13, 15), Thomas Fleming (3, 4, 5, 6, 14).

## Lavandula officinalis, L. angustifolia, L. spica, L. vera. Lavender.
