Lavender is a perennial mint native to dry, chalky mountains in the western half of the Mediterranean. Generally, mints are relaxant to the nervous system and lavender follows this guideline. Because of their high volatile oil content, many mints are warming and stimulating (basil, rosemary, sage, thyme, and others), but lavender is a cooling stimulant. It contains volatile oils, triterpenes, coumarins, and flavonoids. Lavender must have been in use long before the ancient Greek authors on materia medica flourished. Dioscorides, Galen, and the Arabic physicians give it prominence and its virtues have been appreciated to the present. The aromatic flowers are used, or the oil obtained from them.
Lavender is a cooling relaxant suited to spasmodic and tensive conditions involving the nerves, muscles, and neurovascular system. It has long been used for psychological tension, migraine headaches, spasms, and paralysis, as well as neurological symptoms from insect and adder bites. In headache the oil is put on the temples. A survey of a variety of sources, from different periods and places, demonstrates a similar view of the action of lavender on the mind and nervous system.
John Quincy (1736, 73) writes of the flowers, “There are no diseases of the head in which they are not employ’d, under some form or other; they are also very grateful and cordial to the stomach. In all paralytic affections they are reckon’d to give place to nothing; and used in loss of speech they do wonders.”
Rudolf Steiner (1975, 134) says that lavender is for “negative states of the soul.” As he explains it, the “astral body” grasps the physical body too tightly. This results in spasms, tension, paralysis, and nerve-wasting. Steiner’s “astral body” is essentially identical to the autonomic nervous system, which relays impressions felt by the unconscious. Thus, he is describing conditions where unconscious experiences are causing the autonomic to clamp down too intensely.
Herbalist Barbara St. Dennis recommends lavender as a mental calmative. It opens up the mind so that some of the contents can be moved out. It is as if the mind grasped its contents too tightly. She recommends it in combination with chamomilla for hyperactive children. “The world is moving towards the lavender condition,” she observes. “What used to be called obsessional thinking is now normal. People are mentally full. They are addicted to a stress-filled life.”
Australian herbalist Dorothy Hall (1988) recommends lavender for people who can’t sleep because their mind is too active. (Paradoxically, it also wakes up people who are feeling sleepy.) She makes light of the fact that lavender is associated with cleansing (lavar—to clean). She says that lavender is indicated in people who feel unclean, or have too high standards. These people are driven, suffer from overwork and too intense self-application, with headaches, tension in the neck and shoulders, drooping of the head from fatigue (cf. Verbena hastata).
Lavender is used in southern Europe for snake bite. (We should keep in mind that the European adder is hardly as poisonous as the many dangerous snakes of North America.) The little flower head as it raises up has been likened to a snake’s head and the plant is sometimes even called “asp lavender.” Gascon herbalist Maurice Mességué remembered how the hunting dogs, when he was a boy, were treated with lavender when they were bitten by asps.
Lavender is traditionally used as an essential oil rather than as an infused or tinctured herb. An interesting account of the oil is given by Dr. Dietrich Gümbel. He takes a somewhat different view of lavender than most other authors. He explains the calmative effects of lavender by the assertion that the oil stimulates the development and extension of the capillary network. This increases the surface area through which the blood flows into the tissues. It thus promotes a decongesting and cleansing effect that is cooling and relaxing. This influence is especially felt on the skin, nerves, and liver.
In comparison to other authors, Gümbel places unusual stress on the action of lavender on the liver. As the portal vein arises from the intestines and ascends toward the liver it ramifies into finer veins until it forms a complex of vessels that support and bathe the hepatic cells in the venous blood full of the toxins that they must metabolize. In order to stay alive these cells need to receive a constant and ample supply of life-giving arterial blood to provide oxygen and dilute the toxic venous blood. Thus, the expansion of the capillary bed cools the liver and keeps the hepatic cells from being overwhelmed. Another author who mentions the association with the liver is Rudolf Weiss. He classifies lavender as a mild hepatic that should be used in combination with more active agents.
Gümbel further suggests that lavender enhances conversion of glycogen or sugar stored in the liver, to glucose. This makes stored sugar more available for use throughout the body. Thus lavender has a glucagon-like effect—the opposite of its cousin rosemary, which has an insulin-like effect. By opening the peripheral circulation lavender brings the blood and blood sugar to the tissues for nourishment. This helps to explain its action on neurasthenia, asthenia, wasting, and weakness—vata. However, wasting can also be due to nerve spasm, which lavender also releases, so the effect works through both relaxant and nutritive approaches.

### TASTE:

cooling, aromatic, antiseptic

### TISSUE STATES:

excitation, constriction, atrophy, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION
Tension, irritability, nervousness, anxiety, panic, depression, nervous exhaustion; especially in vata types.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Overconscientious, perfectionist, detail-oriented, picky.
Depression and anxiety connected with an irritable bowel.
Insomnia; mind too active to sleep.
Exhaustion in people who do too much meditation.
HEAD
Dizziness, fainting.
Severe headache, migraine, neuralgia of the head (a drop of oil on the temple).
Migraine from detail work, at the end of the day, at the end of the work week.
Head droops from fatigue.
Tension in the cervical vertebrae and trapezius muscles; neck and shoulders.
DIGESTION
Loose teeth, bad breath (mouthwash).
Nervous dyspepsia with flatulence, cramping, nausea; vomiting.
Faulty appetite, cramp in the stomach, intestinal gas and colic.
Motion sickness.
RESPIRATION
Influenza, bronchitis, asthma, whooping cough.
Pneumonia (a few drops of the oil in water).
CARDIOVASCULAR
Palpitations.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Painful arthritic joints; worse from overexertion (external, foot bath).
Weakness of limbs, paralysis (external).
SKIN
Skin; burns, vesiculation with redness.
Poisoned wounds, snake bites (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The oil is most frequently used. It is applied to the skin and should never be taken internally. An infusion of the flowers or flowering herb may be used internally.

### CAUTIONS:

Research demonstrates that the slight estrogenic effect may not be appropriate for teenage boys.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Dietrich Gümbel, Rudolf Weiss, Dorothy Hall (2, 4, 8–10), Julia Graves (1, 5-confirmed), Flower Essence Society (5), Christopher Menzies-Trull (3, 11, 14, 17), Pam Fisher (16), John Quincy (19), Maude Grieve (7, 20), Maurice Mességué (21).

## Leonurus cardiaca. Motherwort.

Motherwort is a mint native to Europe, but has become a common weed in parts of North America, especially around human habitation. The leaves, which have a nauseously bitter taste, are the part used in herbalism. It has properties similar to the other bitter mints such as lycopus and scutellaria; all three are for hyperthyroidism. It contains bitter iridoids, diterpenes, and alkaloids, flavonoids, caffeic acid, lauric acid, oleic acid, glucosides, resins, tannins, and volatile oils. The alkaloids have a mild vasodilating effect.
The name motherwort indicates a traditional association with the “mother” (uterus) and motherhood. It has long been used as a sedative in nervousness associated with female hormonal changes. It also acts on the heart somewhat more strongly than some of the mints and is used as a “heart tonic” and a specific in nervousness with heart palpitations. It has been credited with an organ affinity to the heart since early times. This may be based on the doctrine of signatures: the rhythmic distribution of the flowers on the stalk suggests the heart or pulse. This remedy also has an influence on hyperthyroidism, often associated with cardiac palpitations and nervousness. Culpeper says that “Venus owns this herb and that it is under Leo,” meaning that it is a female remedy with an affinity to the heart. It was from the British herbalists and botanical physicians that the cardiac properties of leonurus spread to American practitioners (A. Dawes, MD, Ellingwood’s Therapeutist, 1908, 2, 6, 24).
The medicinal actions of motherwort probably depend on the intense acrid bitters, which may calm the sympathetic nervous system. It is a remedy for nervous restlessness, wakefulness, disturbed sleep, and anxiety. It opens the skin (motherwort is used as a diaphoretic), releases fever, cools, mildly stimulates the colon and kidneys, and relaxes the uterus. The aromatic bitters were noted in Greek medicine to act particularly upon the uterus. It is indicated in retention of the afterbirth “from any cause, amenorrhea from cold [spasm]; dysmenorrhea, with morbid nervous excitability, and hysteria.” A special indication is “harassing bearing down pains” (Ellingwood, 1918, 483).
Ellingwood was one of the best gynecological/obstetric authors in the late eclectic tradition. He writes, “Prof. John King regarded motherwort as superior to all other remedies in suppression of the lochia,” or retention of the afterbirth, “giving it internally and applying a fomentation of the herb over the lower abdominal region. The author has used it with excellent results.” It is also used as an oxytocic to induce labor, though it is used throughout pregnancy, safely, to calm nervous tension and palpitation.
Herbalist Matthew Becker notes that motherwort drains excessive heat from the upper part of the body associated with a red face, excessive emotionality (not anger), hyperthyroidism, heart palpitations, and high blood pressure. It is especially appropriate for women who are nervous, anxious, move too quickly to thoughts, emotions, or actions that are not thought out. Herbalist Barbara St. Dennis says that motherwort is especially appropriate in menopausal women with “that freaked out look,” nervousness, and anxiety.
Herbalist William LeSassier gave an unusual constitutional indication for motherwort. He associated it with a hollow, caved-in chest, odd shapes in the sternum, and scoliosis. It also has an affinity to the spine. It is indicated for “spinal irritation” and “pain in the pelvis and lumbar regions in females” (Ellingwood). It is somewhat tonic and nutritive, perhaps a bitter tonic that stimulates secretion, digestion, and metabolism. Hence it is indicated in anemia, cachexia, debility, and nervous weakness.

### TASTE:

bitter, acrid • aromatic

### TISSUE STATES:

constriction, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Hollow, caved-in chest, odd shapes in the sternum, scoliosis.
Chronic diseases with wakefulness, restlessness, disturbed sleep, spinal irritation, neuralgia of the stomach and head.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Red face, excessive emotionality (not anger), heart palpitations.
Big, staring eyes.
Freaked out expression in menopausal women; nervousness, anxiety.
HEAD
Headache, insomnia, dizziness, from peripheral circulatory tension.
Hyperthyroidism, goiter.
DIGESTION
Feeble digestion, general nervous debility.
Indigestion connected with nervous irritability and weakness, hormonal problems, spinal pain.
CARDIOVASCULAR
“Feebleness of the heart” (Neil).
Heart palpitations; during pregnancy, menstruation, menopause; associated with uterine disease, amenorrhea, cachexia, and an irritable, excitable, enfeebled state of the nervous system.
Precordial pain with palpitation.
High blood pressure.
Hyperthyroidism with heart symptoms; tachycardia, heart palpitations (cf. Lycopus, Melissa).
KIDNEYS
Edema associated with nephritis; clears protein in the blood and urine.
Bloody urine from the passage of stones.
FEMALE
Vaginismus (Leonurus sibiricus).
Cramps, chorea, convulsions, nervousness from irregular or delayed menses.
Pelvic inflammatory disease.
Puberty: menstrual irregularity and nervousness.
Menstruation: cramping, delayed menses, amenorrhea, nervous weakness.
Premenstrual nerve tension and muscular rigidity, with painful cramping, suppressed menses, tension, heart palpitations, back pain and spasm.
Spasms and harassing bearing down pains, and morbid sensitivity from uterine disease, pain in the pelvic and lumbar regions in females; suppressed lochia from any cause.
Postpartum: expels the afterbirth; strengthens the postpartum uterine contractions stimulated by lactation; “can be used from the first to the fourth or fifth day to help clarify drainage and prevent uterine infection, particularly when accompanied by an uva ursi or manzanita sitz bath” (Michael Moore).
Menopause: heart palpitations, nervousness, anxiety, hot flashes.
FEVER
Fever, colds, chills, influenza; with nervous involvement, debility, exhaustion.
Weakness and debility following fever or menopausal hot flashes; with heart palpitations and nervousness.
Spasms, fits, seizures; after fever has dried out the fluids (cf. Verbena hastata).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Numbness in the limbs.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The entire herb is harvested after it has flowered. Dry for use by infusion or extract fresh in alcohol. Very bitter. I use small doses: 1–3 drops, 1–3x/day.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

“Not appropriate during pregnancy, and although often useful in menopause, it can increase flooding for some women, in which case it should be withdrawn, usually in favor of bugleweed.” “Employed directly after childbirth, it may contribute to bleeding” (Michael Moore, 2003, 169).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Matthew Becker (3, 13), Barbara St. Dennis (4, 5), Finley Ellingwood (2, 8, 9, 11, 18, 20–27), William LeSassier (1), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (6, 7, 12, 14, 15, 16, 19, 29), A. W. and L. R. Priest (7, 11, 14), Michael Moore (14, 17, 18, 21–24), James Neil (10), A. W. Kuts-Cheraux, John Christopher.

## Levisticum officinale. Lovage.

Lovage is an old European folk remedy, little used at the present time, but mentioned in all the older books. It is a member of the parsley family (Apiaceae) native to low, salty ground in Europe, but easily grown in many other locations. It contains a lot of salt (noticeable in the taste of the leaves, similar to celery), volatile oils, some bitter terpenes, coumarins, sterols, resin, and gum.
Lovage contains much salt, which explains its strong diuretic capacity. It helps mop up cardiac and renal edema, increasing the elimination of fluid and minerals. “The salt makes it emollient in indurative diseases, such as gout, rheumatism, kidney stones, spleen and liver ailments” (Husemann and Wolff, 1982, 340). It also warms and stimulates the digestive viscera, so it is a “strengthener of the stomach, and by its detersive virtues also help against the jaundice and obstructions of the spleen” (Quincy, 1736, 83). It counters sluggish lymphatic circulation (Dan Kenner and Yves Requena, 2001, 110), warms the abdomen, and promotes healthy menstruation.

### TASTE:

salty, slightly bitter, slightly warm
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
HEAD
“Migraine resulting from deficient kidney activity” (Husemann and Wolff).
Conjunctivitis (eye drops).
RESPIRATION
Tonsillitis.
Otitis, earache; inflammation and swelling (warm oil dropped in the ear).
Catarrh.
DIGESTION
Aphthous ulcers (mouthwash).
Loss of appetite, dyspepsia, gastritis, flatulence.
Flatulent colic, intestinal spasm.
Spleen and liver problems.
CARDIOVASCULAR
Cardiac edema.
KIDNEYS
Edema, calculi, enuresis, pyelonephritis, glomerulonephritis, albuminuria, proteinuria.
Urinary tract infections, cystitis, urinary retention.
FEMALE
Asthenic women.
Muscle spasm, dysmenorrhea.
Amenorrhea and menostasis.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Gout, rheumatism.
SKIN
Foul-smelling sweat associated with renal disease.
Dermatoses; acne on the back; seborrhea; psoriasis.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The rhizome, roots, leaves, and seeds have been used in traditional medicine.

### CAUTIONS:

The irritating volatile oil can cause aggravation when there is active inflammation in the urinary tract. Members of this family generally should not be used during pregnancy.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Nicholas Culpeper (2), Christopher Menzies-Trull (3, 5–8, 12, 13), Friedrich Husemann and Otto Wolff (1, 4, 9, 11, 12, 14, 16, 17), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (11–16, 18).

## Lilium candidum. Madonna Lily.

Madonna lily grows wild in Lebanon, but is widely cultivated and sometimes naturalized elsewhere. The fresh and dried bulb has been used in Greek and Arabic medicine from the most ancient times. It was still an important female remedy in Britain when Maude Grieve wrote in 1931. However, Madonna lily has fallen out of use in English-speaking herbalism. The analogous Chinese Easter lily may be used in its place. The former is warm and moist, while the latter is slightly cool and moist.
The bulb of Madonna lily contains soluble polysaccharides, starch, mucilage, tannins, stearin, and glycosides. The bulb and petals contain unusual amounts of boron. The following portrait of Madonna lily has been developed by my friend Julia Graves, who now lives in “Madonna lily country” (Normandy), where it is still used in folk medicine.
Madonna lily bulb and petals are traditionally used as a mucilage, to moisten surfaces, and as an emollient, to break up hard swellings. It is used externally for ulcers, inflammation, boils, red skin, burns, and injuries. A poultice of the bulb boiled in milk is used warm on frostbite. A bulb baked in the oven is squeezed and put on an abscess. A warm poultice of cooked bulb is put on bruises. Madonna lily flowers are extracted for 6 hours in brandy then applied to wounds. For ear pain, use 3 drops of oil made by cold infusion in olive oil. Inflammation of the beds of the nails is treated with a warm poultice made from cooked bulb (Dr. Jean Rivolier and Caroline Rivolier, Secrets et Vertus des Plantes Médicinales, cited by Julia Graves).
Julia Graves relates: “Here in Madonna lily country, they collect the petals and dry them to wrap them around wounds, saying that it will prevent infection and pus formation and speed up the healing (purity being really the biggest keynote of this plant maybe). My friend told me that when she was a child, her friend sprained her ankle and the grandma took macerated Madonna lily petals to apply as a poultice. She does not remember, however, what it was macerated in.”
Madonna lily bulb is used internally for gynecological problems, according to Grieve. Because it is mucilaginous and emollient, it is used to dissolve cysts of the skin, breasts, uterus, and ovaries. Nicholas Culpeper (1653) cited it for “swellings in the privities.”
Julia Graves differentiates the mental states between Madonna lily and Easter lily: “The former has more sensitivity, bordering on clairvoyance.” She has often verified the indications I found long ago, that this is a remedy for people who feel a great conflict between the sexual and the spiritual, or are too sexual or too spiritual. She further notes that these lilies have a powerful feminizing element, making one more receptive and intuitive, and reducing fear about being vulnerable or feminine.
“The odor of the first buds of lilies, and indeed the odor of the flowers, makes a person’s heart joyful and furnishes him with virtuous ideas,” notes Hildegard von Bingen (1998, 22).

### TASTE:

“mildly bitter and unpleasant flavor” (Fleming, 1998, 937) • warm, moist • mucilaginous

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Bronchial catarrh.
Menstruation: pain and hemorrhaging.
Cysts.
Bruises, wounds, inflammation, abscesses, frostbite.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Flower petals in brandy, bulbs in oil.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Nicholas Culpeper, Maude Grieve, Julia Graves, Thomas Fleming.

## Lilium longiflorum. Easter lily.

Easter lily (Lilium brownii, L. longiflorum) has a long history of use in traditional Chinese herbalism. It was one of the cardinal remedies used by Zhang Zhongjing, the great reformer of the third century CE, one of the greatest herbalists of all time. The white, demulcent rhizome is the part usually available in commerce in the West but the flowers are also used in China. I have long used the flowers, soaked in water, in the sun, like a flower essence, preserved in alcohol.
Easter lily is classified as a remedy for “yin deficiency” bronchitis in Chinese herbalism. That would be a condition where lack of fluids causes heat. However, this is an oversimplification of an extensive tradition. Zhang Zhongjing (c. 200 CE) describes the use of lily bulb in Synopsis of Prescriptions of the Golden Chamber. In fact, he named a disease pattern after the plant: “lily bulb syndrome.” He writes: “The patient wants to eat, but is reluctant to swallow food, and is unwilling to speak. Or he prefers to lie in bed, yet cannot lie quietly due to reslessness. He may want to walk about, but soon becomes tired. Now and then he may enjoy eating certain delicacies, but at other times he cannot even tolerate the smell of food. He may feel either cold or hot, but without fever or chill. He also has a bitter taste in his mouth and passes reddish urine. No drugs appear able to cure the syndrome. After taking medicine, acute vomiting and diarrhea may occur. The disease ‘haunts’ the patient, and though his appearance is normal, he is actually suffering. His pulse is somewhat speedy.”
Lily bulb syndrome is especially common after inappropriate use of powerful diaphoretics, emetics, or purgatives have driven out the fluids of the body. These drugs are banned by Zhang Zhongjing until the yin is restored. If the deficiency is due to diaphoretics lily bulb is combined with a remedy to moisten the skin and lungs (Anemarrhena), after emetics it is combined with egg yolk, and after purgatives with talc and hematite. Many other systems can be damaged. For a complete account of the use of lily bulb by Zhang Zhongjing refer to Synopsis of Prescriptions of the Golden Chamber, translated by Luo Xiwen (1987, 44–49).
Alternatively, lily bulb syndrome is said to occur after cold has caused a disease and subsides to leave a pathogenic heat that produces the “yin deficiency” bronchitis of modern Chinese literature. When there is a lack of yin or fluids either there is a lack of mucus or the mucus gets dried out. In lily bulb syndrome it is the latter; therefore, some practitioners have qualified the description of the syndrome from “yin deficiency” to “stagnant mucus.” The phlegm is often “baked” and stuck in the periphery of the bronchial tree, and difficult to raise. With this there may be fever with a red face.
My own experience with Easter lily is extensive and complementary to the above, but also considerably different. I originally used Easter lily as a substitute for Madonna lily (Lilium candidum), which was an important female remedy in Western herbalism until quite recently (cf. Maude Grieve). Nicholas Culpeper informs us that it is a remedy for “swellings in the privities,” and this, with the Eastern association with stagnant mucus, suggested cysts. Easter lily turned out to be a wonderful remedy for cysts of the ovaries, breasts, and skin. It also turned out that it was a tremendous remedy for infertility. Quite a few women have gotten pregnant after using Easter lily. Further reflection and experience led to the conclusion that it was a remedy for mucus obstructing the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and uterus, and that it increased fertility by removing mucus that kept the egg from descending or implanting. The menstrual blood was often stringy and brown from the passage of mucus after taking Easter lily. This also turned out to be an excellent indication for taking the remedy. In addition to cleansing the female tract, I found from extensive clinical experience (dozens of cases) that Easter lily will correct unsightly acne in young women, especially when it is on or under the cheekbones or zygomatic arch, and when the skin is elsewhere clear and beautiful, like the petal of the Easter lily flower.
I was also led to understandings about this remedy through the doctrine of signatures. The flower suggests purity and yet intense sexuality. Thus, I started to use the flower essence for conflicted feelings about sex and it proved to be a wonderful remedy in this capacity and has been much verified by many others in the flower essence field. My friend Phyllis Light has used Easter lily several times for calming excessive sexual desire in both women and men. One can readily see the similarity between the conflicted feelings recorded by Zhang Zhongjing and those I have recorded, though I didn’t know about the classical Chinese tradition at the time.
I believe Easter lily is suited to androgen excess. This would account for the intense sex desire, often with frustration and conflicted feelings, acne, polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS), and infertility. I was informed by British herbalists working from their own experience that high androgen is a cause of mucus in the female tract (cf. Marrubium). This fit my own experience—though I had never thought it out that far.
Easter lily provides some of my favorite case histories, complete with delightful evidence in the form of offspring. One of my friends had a case where the woman had been told she would never bear children. She was surprised (and angry) a few months after starting Easter lily to find herself in a family way. Here is a case history from a recent case. A forty-year-old woman had been suffering from ovarian cysts that would rupture midcycle to produce internal bleeding with great pain. The problem dated back to the birth of her first baby at age twenty-seven, or to gallbladder removal at that time. She had one more child and four miscarriages. Finally, several doctors told her she would not get pregnant again due to the large amount of scar tissue in the ovarian region. Although the symptoms did not occur every month, one time she almost died, and numerous times she suffered from extreme pain and internal bleeding. She always knew when a bad cycle was approaching because she would have a lot of stringy, clear, thick, slippery mucus before ovulation, followed by brownish, stringy discharge afterward. If the cyst ruptured she had internal bleeding with pain that was more intense than usual and lasted for many days. Her monthly pains radiated from one ovary (not necessarily the one involved) around her back, sometimes to the other ovary, and were sharp, causing her to double up and walk hunched over. During the bad months she felt backache and extreme fatigue. She began Easter lily flower essence, 1 drop, 1x/day, and had well-regulated periods for six months. The pain was completely relieved or tolerated without drugs. After that she became pregnant and had a little boy. I could hear him in the background when she ordered a second bottle three years later. Herbs are so beautiful!

### TASTE:

slightly cool, moist • mucilaginous

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Grief, sadness, depression; can’t receive love, can’t let go.
Sex drive intense, conflicted.
Yawning, can’t concentrate.
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Clear, luminous skin, marred by acne under the zygomatic arch.
RESPIRATION
Chronic bronchial infection with congestion, redness of the face, fever.
Congestion of mucus, lingering, in the bronchial tree or sinuses, after active infection has subsided to a low grade.
FEMALE
Cysts in the breasts, ovaries, skin; soft, movable cysts; change location and shape from period to period; worse from coffee.
Menses and ovaries painful.
Infertility; from mucus in the fallopian tubes and uterus; the egg is unable to descend and implant, leading to polycystic ovarian syndrome (PCOS).
Brownish, stringy, mucoid vaginal discharge or menstrual blood.
FEVER
Fever.
OTHER
“Pulse somewhat speedy.”

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Flowers are collected in midsummer and tinctured in alcohol or made into a flower essence. The tincture of the flowers is quite active and can cause emotional and physical upset, so the best doses are small: 1 drop, 1x/day for 10–14 days before the period (Matthew Wood). If the bulb is used, soak overnight until a foam leaves the bulbs, remove them, and use per decoction by itself or with other agents (Zhang Zhongjing).

### CAUTIONS:

Usually regarded as entirely nontoxic.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional Western herbalism (7), traditional Chinese medicine (5, 6, 11, 12), Matthew Becker (1, 3), Matthew Wood (1, 2, 4–10), Julia Graves (confirmed: 1–2, 7–10), Phyllis Light (confirmed: 2), David Milgrom, DC (9), Lise Wolff (9).

## Linaria vulgaris. Toadflax.

Linaria is a member of the Scrophulariaceae family native to Europe, but widely naturalized in temperate North America, so that its orange and yellow flowers are commonplace near the calcareous gravel along roads. It contains flavonoids, citric acid, tannins, mucilage, sugar, sodium, and an alkaloid.
Toadflax is an acrid bitter, hence useful to stimulate liver function and gallbladder secretion, and an astringent, thus adding a tonifying element. The movement of fluids is furthered by the presence of a small amount of diuretic salt and the whole effect is directed toward cooling by the flavonoids. The liver and gallbladder are relaxed by the use of a warm infusion, which increases bile secretion when necessary or reduces bilirubin levels in the blood (jaundice) from congestion. The increased bile in the intestine lubricates the stool. The use of toadflax for hemorrhoids indicates passive venous congestion in the portal vein, pressing back from the liver and gallbladder.
“For liver stress from poor diet, or malabsorption, toadflax helps decongest and repair. Likewise in alcohol excess, or post-hepatitis exhaustion, it helps detoxify and soothe the liver. For this it combines well with dandelion and yellow dock root; and is useful in all spleen and liver enlargements and inflammations” (Robert Dale Rogers, 2000, 100).
Toadflax also contains a “weak saline” element, according to Maude Grieve (1931, 816). This makes it diuretic and it is used for edema, gravel, cystitis, prostate inflammation, and difficult urination. “It was at one time in great reputation among herb doctors for dropsy.
“The fresh plant is sometimes applied as a poultice or fomentation to hemorrhoids, and an ointment of the flowers has been employed for the same purpose, and also locally in diseases of the skin. A cooling ointment is made from the fresh plant—the whole herb is chopped and boiled in lard till crisp, then strained. The result is a fine green ointment, a good application for piles, sores, ulcers, and skin eruptions.”
Dr. J. G. Rademacher spoke highly of the use of linaria for knotted bunches of hemorrhoids, and for phlebitis. He also introduced it for inflammation of the vessels of the eyes. He recommended decoction of Linaria for headstrong, willful children with inflamed eyes, where one is often not even able to examine the eyes—“so I do not need to explain why it is so useful in these cases.” Keep the compress moistened and over the eye.

### TASTE:

“weakly saline, bitter, and slightly acrid” (Grieve)

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Headstrong, willful children, with inflamed eyes.
Red, inflamed, swollen vessels in the eyes.
Swollen glands, spleen disorders, scrofula.
Liver congestion; jaundice, gallstones.
Constipation.
Hemorrhoids, knotted bunches.
Edema, gravel.
Cystitis.
Prostatitis.
Sciatica.
Phlebitis.
Bubonic plague, lymphadenitis.
Postpartum: expels placenta.
Dermic ulcers, macules, sores.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The flowering herb cut just above the ground. Decoction, infusion, tincture. Salve, compress.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, J. G. Rademacher, Maude Grieve, Robert Dale Rogers.

## Linum usitatissimum. Flaxseed, Linseed.

Flaxseed contains up to 45% fixed oils (omega-3 and -6, alpha-linolenic acid, which metabolizes to prostaglandins), resins, phosphates, lignans, sterols, phytoestrogens, triterpenes, cyanogenic glycosides, and mucilage up to 10%. Prostaglandins are hormone precursors that contribute to the regulation of blood pressure, clotting, heart rate, vascular dilation, and immunity.
Ground flaxseed produces an oily meal that is an excellent nutritive tonic to lubricate the colon, remove constipation from dryness and atrophic weakness, increase bile production and fat absorption, and build the lipid economy of the body. It also acts on the respiratory membranes to soothe dry, irritable cough, stuck water (pleurisy, pneumonia) in a generally dry, debilitated, even deteriorative condition. Going deeper, flaxseed nourishes the muscles and tendons, making them supple and limber (Dorothy Hall), also the bones and reproductive organs (Vasant Lad and David Frawley). Today it is also used for its positive cardiovascular effect.
Externally: “The ground seed, when mixed with boiling water, makes a thick mush which is excellent for use in poultices. Any herb may be added for this purpose, such as smartweed, alum bark, granulated hops, mullein, or any of the other herbs recommended for use in poultices. These herbs mixed and used as a poultice with flaxseed make one of the best poultices for all kinds of old sores, boils, carbuncles, inflammations and tumors” (Jethro Kloss).

### TASTE:

sweet, pungent • warm • oily, astringent

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Eyes (poultice).
Allergies.
Sore throat.
Lungs; chronic bronchitis, pleurisy, asthma, pneumonia; soothes cough.
Gastritis, enteritis, irritable bowel syndrome, diverticulitis, dysentery, diarrhea, colic, spasmodic colitis.
Chronic constipation.
Kidney stones; lubricates the passage; nephritis.
Tendons, muscles; keeps them flexible and supple (external).
Dupuytren’s contracture; thinning of pad of the hand and contracture of tendons in palms near fingers (external).
Contracture of the extremities (external).
Tightens loose tendons; fingers, joints, neck (external).
Burns, after the initial injury, while they are weeping plasma (external).
Burns, scars, and wounds that cause contracture (external).
Eczema, psoriasis, dry, itchy skin (dietary supplement for essential fatty acids).
Abscess, boil (external).
High blood pressure, high cholesterol, atherosclerosis.
Systemic lupus erythematosus.
Strains, sprains (poultice).

### PREPARATION, CONTRAINDICATIONS, AND DOSAGE:

May be congesting if there is a torpid condition causing the constipation instead of debility, dryness, and atrophy. Freshly ground meal, milk decoction, paste. Does not store well after grinding; the oils quickly turn rancid and age to become the linseed oil used in painting. Millet and flaxseed porridge is a good low carbohydrate, high protein food (Dorothy Hall). It should not be heated during preparation.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Jethro Kloss, Vasant Lad and David Frawley, Dorothy Hall (4–9).

## Lonicera spp. Honeysuckle.

Honeysuckle is a member of the Caprifoliaceae family (which also includes elder) and is represented throughout the northern hemisphere by many species that have naturalized throughout the world. The flowers are very sweet and cooling. They are considered an important medicine in China. Also used in Arabic medicine, they are little known in Western herbalism.
Lonicera japonica is the officinal species in Chinese herbalism; it is widely naturalized in North America. The flower buds are the part used; they are classified as sweet and cooling and are used to reduce heat in the body, counter toxic heat, cool the blood, and disperse wind and heat. Symptoms include carbuncles, contagious fever with skin eruptions, dysentery, and sore, swollen throat. Research shows that honeysuckle can protect the liver, inhibit influenza and mumps, reduce fat in the blood, and kill bacteria (Lu, 1994, 189).
“Honeysuckle is good for the lungs; asthma; the spleen; provokes urine; aids childbirth; cramps; convulsions; palsy; colds. Ointment of honeysuckle will clear the skin of morphew; freckles; sun burn and other skin discolourings” (Chalid Ottway, 1933, 202). The root, leaves, or flowers are used in Arabic medicine.

## Lycopus europaeus. Gypsywort.

This plant is used in Europe as a substitute for the American bugleweed (Lycopus virginicus), on the same indications. It is one of the most invasive plants I have ever come across. The association with gypsies should probably be considered a racial slur.

## Malva neglecta, M. sylvestris. Low Mallow, Cheeses.

This common weed can be used as a substitute for marshmallow (Althaea officinalis), though it is not usually considered as powerful. A simple poultice of the widely available leaves are used in folk medicine on infected finger wounds, skin inflammation, insect bites, and bee stings (Menzies-Trull). It draws out inflammation and pus, and is therefore beneficial in teen acne, infected and cystic acne, and boils (Hall). It is also used in traditional country medicine to make a cough syrup. This is excellent for irritable, dry coughs of the larynx and trachea in children (Jane LaForce). It is a mild laxative mucilage for the bowels. William LeSassier recommended it for leaky gut syndrome.

## Marrubium vulgare. White Hoarhound.

White hoarhound is a very bitter member of the mint family like its namesake, black hoarhound. Both are covered by a slight down like a “hoarfrost,” hence the name. The spelling “horehound” is incorrect but far more common. White hoarhound has a light green color, whereas black hoarhound is darker green. The former is used primarily on the lungs, the latter on the digestion. Marrubium contains a bitter (marrubiin), tannins, flavonoids, vitamin C, quercetin, sterols, mucilage, alkaloids, volatile oils, potassium, magnesium, calcium, and iron.
White hoarhound is one of the half dozen grand old cough remedies of European folk and professional medicine, sharing that distinction with such herbs as elecampane, coltsfoot, mullein, and marshmallow. It is difficult to imagine treating respiratory conditions without this powerful committee of pulmonary herbs, though they are improved by the addition of North American plants such as wild cherry, rabbit tobacco, and lobelia.
Marrubium is an old domestic remedy principally used in colds, coughs, and fevers. As a bitter it promotes secretions that expel “abundant thick expectoration” (W. T. Fernie, 1914, 251). This allows for the secretion of new mucus, which contains new immune cells and secretions. Again and again, we see that herbs work, not by “killing germs,” but by changing the environment and allowing the body to do the germ-killing. Meanwhile, the mild tannins astringe and restore the tone of the boggy mucosa. Simultaneously marrubium opens the skin, relieving congestion by bringing fluids out through the periphery. It is useful in nonproductive coughs, pertussis, bronchitis, asthma, and tuberculosis where the catarrh is dried out and adhesive.
Marrubium is indicated in the first tickle of a cold or cough in the back of the throat (Dorothy Hall). This is then followed by increasing soreness and, at some point, an increase in salivation. The saliva wets the tongue on the sides (the liver and gallbladder area in Chinese medicine), flows down the throat, and irritates the larynx and bronchi. It is not often appreciated that excess salivation, like postnasal discharge, can cause a respiratory condition. This is also the case with red clover (Matthew Wood). Later on the excessive secretions dry out, leading to lack of appetite, digestive dryness, dry, harsh coughing, adhesive mucus, and liver and gallbladder sub-secretion. William LeSassier explained the pattern in a different way. White hoarhound is indicated where cold stagnation in the GI causes a backup that discharges through the respiratory membranes, resulting in mucus buildup and coughing. “Aching and fullness is felt through the bottom of the chest,” he remarked. “The liver may feel hard and cold. Typically, some emotional thing sets off the respiratory problem. It is suited to all sorts of different kinds of coughs.”
William Cook (1869, 556) writes, “A cold infusion creates a warm impression through the lungs, favors the ejection of viscid mucus, and sustains the vocal organs in congestion and hoarseness; but it is not a suitable agent to use in dry and irritable coughs, and patients with a tendency to spasmodic asthma often suffer a sense of suffocation on using it. Large quantities sometimes act [as a laxative] on the bowels.” Actually, dry coughs can respond to hoarhound. “A warm infusion acts moderately toward the surface, secures a slight perspiration, promotes the menses where they have been obstructed by recent exposure, relieves hysterical symptoms, and sometimes acts on the kidneys.”
London herbalist Paula Grainger informed me that some British herbalists were using marrubium for infertility associated with excess androgenism. This condition can cause mucus to interfere with the descent of the egg through the fallopian tubes and its implantation in the uterus (cf. Lilium longiflorum). I also associate excess androgenism with cold, chills and fever, and cold in the liver; this imbalance is often treated by bitters—cf. mugwort, blessed thistle, wild lettuce. I have found white hoarhound to be useful in thin women who gain abdominal fat in their forties and fifties from high androgen. It can materially reduce the abdominal fat deposition. This type of weight gain can be associated with diabetes type II.
Kenner and Requena (2001) cite marrubium as a bitter, pungent remedy that is cooling. This is in line with the Chinese idea that bitters are cooling, but is incorrect. Western tradition, from classical Greek to modern times (see Cook, above), considers it to be warming. This is clear on taking some of the leaf in the mouth. Marrubium is a stimulant—that is, a hot remedy.

### TASTE:

bitter • warm • astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

stagnation, relaxation, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Debilitated condition of the nervous system.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Emotional sensitivity, upset, hysteria.
RESPIRATION
Respiratory conditions associated with emotional problems and constipation.
First tingles of irritation in the throat or nose, in acute head colds.
Viscosities in the eyes and ears (drops).
Hoarseness, irritation and congestion in the throat, laryngitis, tracheitis, loss of voice, chronic sore throat.
Thick, viscid, heavy phlegm (combine with orris root, Nicholas Culpeper).
Chronic catarrh, chest colds, coughs, croup, sinusitis, bronchitis, asthma, pneumonia, pertussis, dyspnea.
“All sorts of different kinds of coughs.”
Dry, unproductive cough; with poor appetite and sluggish digestion.
Pain and aching through the bottom of the lungs.
Senile asthma.
Excess salivation at the beginning of an upper respiratory tract infection.
DIGESTION AND LIVER
Catarrhal gastritis and enteritis.
“Cold, hard liver”; jaundice; hepatitis.
Constipation.
Worms; associated with poor tone, secretion, and circulation of the mucosa.
Bacterial enteritis.
HEART
Arrhythmia, tachycardia.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema.
FEMALE
Problems associated with excess androgen.
Amenorrhea; associated with cold, with venous congestion.
Infertility from mucus in the fallopian tubes and womb (cf. Lilium).
Parturition: warms the uterus, eases labor.
Postpartum: assists expulsion of the afterbirth.
Menopause: weight gain in the abdomen in thin women due to androgen.
FEVER
Fever with lack of perspiration.
Intermittent chills and fever, influenza, malaria.
SKIN AND WOUNDS
Dermal ulcers.
Dog and adder bites (traditional).
Shingles.
OTHER
“If the plant be put in new milk and set in a place pestered with flies, it will speedily kill them all” (Fernie).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Infusion of the dried herb. One teaspoonful to 4 ounces of boiling water, cover and steep for 10–20 minutes, sweeten with honey or molasses to increase the soothing property (Dewey).

### CAUTIONS:

Extended use can cause hypertension, especially in the elderly (Michael Moore). Can cause irregular heartbeat in some people (Dewey). Large doses are emetic and laxative. Not appropriate in some pregnant women.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Nicholas Culpeper, John William Fyfe (1), Dorothy Hall (4), William LeSassier (2, 3, 9, 11, 15), Dioscorides (22), Wilhelm Pelikan (12, 13, 14, 19, 22), Chalid Ottway (5, 7, 8, 24, 25), William Cook (2, 6, 25), Christopher Menzies-Trull (5–8, 15–17, 20, 22, 27, 28), Matthew Wood (13, 21, 26), Paula Grainger (23), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (18, 19, 20, 22), John Hill (22), W. T. Fernie (32).

## Medicago sativa. Alfalfa, Lucerne.

Alfalfa is a clover native to central Asia, long cultivated for animal feed. It is highly alkaline and will grow well only on alkaline soils. It has deep foraging roots that pick up minerals far beneath the surface of the ground. Alfalfa contains bitters (including alkaloids), coumarins, isoflavonoids (including phytoestrogens), protein, vitamins (including A, B1, B2, B3, B6, B12, C, D, E, F, K, and U), and minerals (including calcium, potassium, iron, and phosphorus).
Many herb books report that the medicinal effects of alfalfa depend upon the large amounts of vitamins and minerals it contains. However, John Heinerman (1994, 1) notes that a scientific writer actually states, “No other herb … shows so many fluctuations in its vitamin and particularly in its mineral contents as does alfalfa.” The medicinal properties more likely depend on the strong alkalinity of the herb. Many problems are due to acidity. Alkalization sedates the nerves and muscles, removes acids from the digestive tract and internal environment, improving digestion, reducing putrefaction in the gut, and cleansing the internal fluids and tissues.
Bernard Jensen (1979) gives an account of alfalfa as a remedy for the gastrointestinal tract. He combines it with peppermint to make a tonic for the stomach. With its complex and opposite flavors, bitter and sweet, alfalfa is both stimulating and regulating to stomach secretion, like meadowsweet. Jensen also recommends alfalfa, in tablet form, as a remedy for the colon. The fibers exercise the muscles of the intestinal walls and are slightly laxative. They build up the tone and remove toxic accumulations, bacteria, and pockets (diverticulum). He recommends 4–8 tablets at each meal, chewed or broken up. In addition, he suggests use of remedies to increase digestion and decrease toxic gas (probably caused by the wrong flora in the colon). He suggests goldenseal as a good herb to use with alfalfa—it nicely improves the digestive secretions he is looking for. This method, Jensen says, will take about a year to rebuild a run-down gastrointestinal tract. It is suited to ulcers of the surface as well as of the mucosa.
Australian herbalist Dorothy Hall (1988) gives an account of the constitution calling for alfalfa. These people are busy, on the go, impatient. They are constantly aware of the passage of time and frequently complain that they don’t have enough of it. The nervous system is irritable. They usually suffer from poor protein digestion. This leads to irritation of the digestive tract, with acid buildup and secretion of mucus to coat the irritated tissue. There may also be difficulty metabolizing protein, leading to internal inflammation, acidity, catarrh, and allergies. The alfalfa person is unusually sensitive to insect stings, resulting in swellings, red welts, itching, and irritability.
Richard Lucas (1981) gives a good account of the applications of alfalfa in arthritis, rheumatism (fibromyalgia), rheumatoid arthritis, and back pain.

### TASTE:

sweet, bitter, earthen • cooling

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, torpor
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Busy, conscious of the clock, on the go, “not enough time.”
Nervousness, irritability, insomnia.
HEAD
Itchy scalp.
RESPIRATION
Acute affections of the upper respiratory tract; nosebleed, hay fever, allergies, sore throat.
Chronic, debilitating cough of tuberculosis, pertussis; as a nutritive support.
DIGESTION
Poor appetite, bad breath, tooth decay, acidic saliva, fermentation of digestate.
Acid taste on the tongue; in the elderly.
Stomach acidity, bloating, distention, pain.
Intolerance of rich, proteinaceous foods.
Peptic ulcers.
Flatulence, catarrh in the colon, rectal itching.
Diverticulosis (bowel pockets); constipation; sluggish bowels; rectal itch. Thought to prevent colon cancer by cleansing the bowel.
Colitis.
HEART, KIDNEYS, AND BLADDER
Heart disease, high blood pressure.
Excess water retention, weight gain.
Gravel.
FEMALE
Pregnancy: morning sickness.
Lactation: promotes milk quantity and quality.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis, bursitis, rheumatoid arthritis, gout, muscle cramps.
Pain, swelling, and stiffness in joints, back.
SKIN
External ulcers that are not resolving; putrid sores; proud flesh (cf. Inula).
Skin: irritable, itching, rashes, changing location frequently; reacts badly to insect bites.
Boils.
OTHER
Hemostatic; too much bleeding or too little; preventative for strokes.
Anemia.
Supportive “when taking sulfa or antibiotic drugs or when fasting” (Gagnon).
Convalescence; to buffer the blood from nitrogenous acids caused by tissue breakdown.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Large doses are most commonly used (Lucas). The herb is often used in tablet form (2–4 with meals). Dose of the tincture: 15–30 drops, up to 4x/day (Daniel Gagnon). The tea liberates the calcium for absorption through the stomach and higher GI, bypassing the parathyroid-controlled usual mechanisms for calcium absorption in the gut, thereby making it easier to get calcium into the system (Michael Moore).

### CAUTIONS:

Alfalfa frequently causes mild side effects, including bloating, gas, and diarrhea, when it begins to work on the system. Long-term use can cause reactivation of quiescent SLE (systemic lupus erythematosus) (Talalaj and Czechowicz).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Alexander Blackwood, Ann Wigmore, John Christopher, Dorothy Hall (1, 2, 3, 7, 22), John Heinerman, Bernard Jensen, Christopher Menzies-Trull (4, 5), Daniel Gagnon (13, 25, 26), Richard Lucas (19, 20), Michael Moore (27).

## Melaleuca alternifolia. Tee Tree Oil, Cajeput Oil.

This agent is a tree native to Australia. It was known in the nineteenth century as cajeput oil. It was largely forgotten for a long time in the West, then reentered the limelight and under the name “tea tree” or “melaleuca” became something of a fad herb in the closing decades of the twentieth century. It is high in volatile oils, sesquiterpenes, and monoterpenes.
Melaleuca is a powerful antiseptic, antibiotic, antiparasitic, and antifungal. It can be taken internally only in very small doses. Dorothy Hall (1988, 114) recommends several drops to a flat teaspoonful, at most, diluted in water or fruit juice, up to once a day. This is for parasites, amebic dysentery, candida, and the like. However, usually melaleuca is used externally in a cream, gel, or oil (up to 80% strength to prevent skin sensitivities) for abscess, acne, wounds, tinea pedis, ringworm, pediculosis, dermal parasites, warts.
It must be remembered that “killing critters” is not the best way to deal with any of these infestations. True cure comes from changing the environment, so that it does not play host to microorganisms in the first place. For instance, I recently saw a case of ringworm on the backs of the hands and up onto the wrists. The skin was cool and clammy, indicating an excess of watery perspiration, but it was coarse from a lack of oil. I gave sweet leaf (Monarda fistulosa) to reverse the perspiration, and hawthorn berry (Crataegus spp.) because it increases oil on the backs of the hands and wrists. The condition was quickly cured. This woman could have put melaleuca on until she was blue in the face; it would not have cured because the underlying condition was the source of the problem, not the critter.

## Melissa officinalis. Lemon Balm.

Melissa is a member of the mint family native to Europe but widely cultivated for its pleasant lemon scent and taste. It is one of the few aromatic mints that is sour and cooling. It occupied a position of considerable importance in European domestic medicine for the last several centuries. Until recently, many households had a bottle of klosterfrau melissengeist or “spirit of melissa” on hand as a basic nerve sedative. Melissa contains flavonoids, triterpenes, and volatile oils (including citronella).
Lemon balm has a sour taste, as its name indicates—it is one of the few sour mints. Like most sour plants, it is cooling and sedative. It combines this property with the typical nerve-calming powers of the mint family to make a strong, but safe and simple sedative. These powers are much more marked when the plant is tinctured fresh. A tincture of fresh melissa should be on the shelf in every household as a general sedative.
Melissa is a general remedy in some cases and a specific in others. Although it will generally sedate most people, it is particularly suited to conditions of sympathetic excess, hyperadrenalism, or hyperthyroidism. It is especially indicated when these tendencies are associated with the stomach or heart. Heart palpitations, atrial fibrillation, high blood pressure, and even aneurysm have found a treatment in melissa. It is sometimes combined with Lycopus in hyperthyroidism or is specific by itself. Keynote symptoms include sweaty palms (an indication for nervousness or sympathetic excess), a rapid or superficial pulse, and an elongated, pointed, red tongue.
In addition to being cooling, sedative, and calming, melissa is diaphoretic, so it is applicable in fevers with nervousness and in the fevers of young children. It is also used in nervous depression following exhaustive fevers and for convalescence when the digestion has been long irritated.

### TASTE:

sour • cool

### TISSUE STATE:

excitation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Nervousness, depression, anxiety, panic attacks, insomnia.
Debility and weakness of nervous origin.
Nervous headache (cold infusion).
RESPIRATION
Spasm in the upper respiratory centers, convulsive coughing, asthma.
THYROID AND HEART
Anxiety and palpitations; hyperthyroidism.
Heart palpitations, atrial fibrillation, high blood pressure, associated with nervousness.
Pulse rapid or superficial.
DIGESTION
Elongated, pointed, red tongue.
Dyspepsia, upset stomach, flatulence.
Nervous indigestion (hot tea after a meal).
Nausea and vomiting.
FEVERS
Recent colds and fevers (hot tea, to promote perspiration).
Acute febrile conditions in children.
Depression following exhaustive fevers and long irritation of the digestion.
FEMALE
Conception: said to promote.
Menstruation: deficient and painful.
Leucorrhea.
Menopause: hot flashes (combine with peach leaf).
Excessive sexual excitement.
MALE
Impotence (“a sovereign remedy … from times of yore”).
SKIN
Burns, blisters, herpetic sores, stings, and burns; burning, nettling pain (external).
Sweaty palms.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

“Fresh balm is much better than dry, for it loses its fragrancy in drying” (John Hill, 1740, 70). The tea or tincture should be made from the fresh leaves. Dosage of the tincture can be small (1–3 drops) to large (10–30 drops). The oil can also be used. Extremely Safe.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Humbart Santillo, Rudolf Weiss, Nalda Gosling (14), W. T. Fernie (3, 10), Ben Charles Harris (12), Friedrich Husemann and Otto Wolff (4, 16, 17), Wilhelm Pelikan (11, 14, 19), Sondra Boyd (18), Matthew Wood (7, 8, 22), Donald Law (20).

## Mentha piperita. Peppermint.

Peppermint is an old culinary and medicinal herb native to the Old World but widely naturalized and used in North America. It is usual among the mints for its nervine properties, but unusual because of its paradoxical hot and cold properties. It has a cool taste, but stimulating effects. It possesses many active ingredients, of which the most important are flavonoids, acetic acid, tannins, resin, gum, triterpenes, and essential oils (menthol, 50–75%). The menthol is often used separately in liniments.
Peppermint preparations “have been popular from the days of our grandmothers for the relief of colic in the bowels, or for the stomach-ache of flatulent indigestion,” writes W. T. Fernie (1914, 3).
Peppermint tastes cool but has a stimulating and warming aftertaste. This paradox helps to explain its physical properties. Peppermint is traditionally used as a remedy for fever and chills, or an admixture of heat and cold. It is also used to reduce “wind” in the intestines, which can arise in combination with cramping, often a symptom associated with chills. Thus, the primary uses of peppermint in fever, chills, flu, and indigestion are explained by its taste. Scientific medicine and herbalism explain the action of peppermint by the fact that menthol is antispasmodic and analgesic. Thus, it settles the stomach and opens the sweat pores and peripheral circulation through relaxation.
Peppermint “cures the cholic often almost instantaneously,” wrote Sir John Hill (1740, 264). It “is mostly used for flatulence and wind colic; but may be employed for other sudden pains and crampings through the abdomen” (William Cook, 1869, 563). It is contraindicated in hot, dry conditions of the colon, writes Finley Ellingwood (1918, 280). “In painful bowel complaints with inflammation—pain on pressure, tongue dry, with reddened tip and edges, peppermint should not be given.” Rather, it is indicated “where the food tends to ferment in the stomach and bowels, it may be given in doses of three to five minims in capsules, as an antiseptic to prevent fermentation and promote digestion.”
Peppermint is traditionally used in colds and fevers brought on by sudden exposure to cold. Yarrow, elder, and peppermint comprise a time honored formula for colds, fevers, and flus. “Osiander states in his textbook, Popular Remedies, 1838, that he found mint tea to be exceedingly valuable in the treatment of influenza. He used mint tea during the terrific grippe epidemics of 1838 and 1836” (Harris, 1972, 144).
Peppermint and menthol are highly active substances that can artificially overrelax the nerves and muscles. Eva Graf (1978, 188) notes that peppermint can relax the peristaltic action of the intestines so that they don’t have the tone they should. This can happen merely from taking the tea daily for three or four weeks. She observes that this effect will usually reverse if the tea is stopped. Because of this it is not appropriate to take peppermint right after a meal, when the peristalsis needs to be active. She uses spearmint, instead of peppermint, in treatment of infants, because of its milder action.
Although its properties are primarily cooling/relaxing, peppermint has a secondary warming/stimulating effect. This travels through the lymphatics of the body, which are a subtle pathway not so easily observed as the nerves and muscles. Through this mechanism, peppermint reaches into the depths of the organism and then out to the periphery, where we first noticed its activities in fever, flu, and stomachache.
Eva Graf (1978, 188) noticed the lymphatic application of peppermint. She considered it to be a specific for congested lymphatics in gathered breast in mastitis. Dr. Dietrich Gümbel (1993, 205) credits peppermint oil with the ability to move the waters of the body through the slow-moving lymphatic ducts and interstitial spaces between tissues, where there is very little pumping action. Thus, it increases the capacity of absorption of the lymphatic organs in the mesentery surrounding the intestines. By improving the uptake of digestate, alimentary stasis is relieved. Cooling fluids and nourishing food are brought into the body. By increasing the flow of water through the lymphatics peppermint has a cooling, moistening effect on hot, dry conditions in the interior and periphery of the body. (It is not, as mentioned above, indicated for hot, dry conditions of the gut, however.) Reaching the largest center of the lymphatic system, peppermint oil stimulates the spleen, improving the breakdown of old red blood cells, combing the blood for impurities, and increasing white cell production. Thus, it ultimately has a restorative, regenerative influence on the immune system.
As the absorptive effect of peppermint oil moves out to the periphery, it relieves hot, dry conditions of the joints and skin. It moves more fluids into the hard-to-reach connective tissue, which lies outside the normal circulation of the blood. This not only strengthens connective tissue, improving its “swellability,” but improves the immune function, which is vested in this tissue. It acts on the corium, the middle layer of the skin, the connective tissue anchor of the dermis, making it more flush with water, increasing the function in the immune cells in this area. It is therefore, according to Gümbel, a remedy for dried-up, atrophic skin, especially that injured by the effect of light, and for dry, irritated joints. By increasing the immune capacity of the periphery, peppermint becomes a remedy in influenza.
Dr. J. V. Cerney (1976, 72) records a dramatic case. “Mary McK. had had heartburn for years. She complained of acid coming up from her stomach to cause a burning sensation in her throat. She continued to have this discomfort despite all the prescribed anti-acids and other potions. The problem didn’t interfere too much with her activity but it did make her feel miserable day after day. She got to the point where she didn’t want to eat any more because food made her feel worse. This pyrosis, or burning, simply got worse and when I saw her the burning in her throat was already interfering with her speech. Her throat was inflamed and swollen. I’d never seen a case go so far. In fact, I suspected it was more likely to be a drug allergy than pyrotic in origin. I started her on peppermint tea. The first three days there were no appreciable results. Then a mashed fresh peppermint leaf poultice was placed on her tummy. That’s when results made themselves apparent. I started her on soft foods and within days she was her smiling self,” and remained on a diet and regular doses of peppermint tea.

### TASTE:

pungent • at first warming, then cooling • stimulating

### TISSUE STATES:

constriction, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Nervousness, insomnia, dizziness, fainting.
RESPIRATION
Increases respiration and oxygenation.
DIGESTION
People who are drowsy after a meal.
Headaches associated with digestive tension.
Mouth sores, sore throats.
Stomach pain, nausea, vomiting, heartburn, flatulent colic, belching, hiccough.
Spasmodic pain in the bowels, griping, diarrhea with abdominal pain.
Gas and bloating after a meal.
Palpitation from indigestion and gas.
Gallbladder colic.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
“Scanty secretion of urine, frequent desire to pass water” (Rolla Thomas).
FEMALE
Menstruation: colic, cramp.
Pregnancy: indigestion and morning sickness (tea, after the first trimester).
Lactation: mastitis.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Muscle tension, spasm, cramp (oil, externally; or poultice).
Spasms and convulsions in infants (small internal doses).
Hot, dry joints.
SKIN
Shingles, herpes (cf. Melissa, Tilia, Prunus serotina, homeopathic Ranunculus bulbosus).
FEVER
Colds and flus of the chilly variety and fevers of the hot type.
Measles; brings eruptions to the surface.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Infuse dried or fresh mint leaves, 1/4 to 1/2 teaspoonful per cup, in boiled water; serve hot, warm, or cold. Peppermint oil is used as a digestive aid. Dose of the tincture: 10 drops to 1 dram in 4 ounces of water (Thomas).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Flower Essence Society (3), J. V. Cerney (6), Rolla Thomas (12), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (20), Eva Graf (15).

## Mentha pulegium. European Pennyroyal.

See Hedeoma pulegioides, American Pennyroyal, in the volume on American plants.

## Mentha spicata. Spearmint.

Spearmint has properties similar to those of peppermint—it is a stimulant and antispasmodic—but it is more gentle. It contains less menthol, and more carvone, also found in dill and caraway (Michael Moore, 2003).
Samuel Thomson (1841, 682) comments that spearmint “expels wind from the stomach, and promotes perspiration. It is an excellent article to settle the stomach, and stop vomiting.” Wooster Beach (1849, 664) used it especially for “suppression of urine” and “gravely affections, etc.”
Michael Moore (2003, 236) renders a perfect and artistic account of spearmint. “The main value of spearmint is its almost complete lack of toxicity, allowing even the sickest person to tolerate the tea. It is this feebleness that makes it so useful. Soothing to the stomach and a mild diuretic and diaphoretic, it has mild vasodilating characteristics, imparting a pleasant, warm glow to the drinker.”
Eva Graf (1978, 119–20) did not use peppermint for infants because it was so powerful, so she used spearmint. “It seems to work more with the stomach than with the whole intestinal tract. It also has antispasmodic qualities.… You would be better able to use spearmint right after eating than peppermint. Spearmint seems to help stimulate digestive enzymes and work with digestion of protein in a really positive way.
“Spearmint seems to be really effective for people who need to cool out emotionally. In that sense, it is for those acute times. It can be used in hysterias, for children who are upset, in any of that type of situation.”
Spearmint, or yerba buena, is the traditional tea given to birthing mothers in New Mexican and Hispanic usage, notes Moore (2003). It is mildly astringent and toning in postpartum.

## Menyanthes trifoliata. Bogbean, Bog Trefoil, Buckbean.

Bogbean is a member of the bogbean family. It enjoys a circumpolar distribution, hence is found in North America and Europe, where we find it in cold, northern bogs. Its uses come to us from European folk medicine. It is “an herb better known by the common people than among the apothecaries, but of great virtue,” writes John Hill (1740, 93). The leaves and roots are used; they contain numerous anthraquinones, bitter alkaloids, phenols, and tannins. This well explains the long use of this plant as a digestive tonic.
Buckbean, with its impressive collection of bitters, purgative anthraquinones, and tannins, is perfectly poised to stimulate a sluggish digestive tract and stagnant lymphatics conjoined with clogged kidneys and skin. The “main influence is expended on the glandular structures, promoting the flow of bile and urine, acting fairly on the bowels and skin, and in large quantities sometimes proving emetic, as boneset does. The principal use made of it is as a tonic in company with alterants for such maladies as dropsy, scrofula, jaundice, and general biliousness. Considerable doses will so effectually purge the liver, gall cyst and bowels, at the same time sustaining the strength and the outward circulation, that it is a quite popular remedy for intermittents” (Cook, 1869, 565).
Menyanthes is believed to stimulate both the sympathetic and parasympathetic branches of the autonomic, thus normalizing imbalances in the nervous regulation of the digestion that would especially result from malaria or intermittent fevers (alternating chills and fever). It opens the lymphatic drainage, releasing pelvic lymphatic congestion. It also improves secretion of the skin, which takes a burden off the kidneys, and stimulates the kidneys themselves to remove waste products. Thus, it is “the most serviceable of all herbal tonics” (Fernie, 1914, 36). “With this remedy the author has cured some cases of scrofula and rheumatism of very long standing” (Slack, 1919, 106). Recent research has shown that it has a possible influence in glomerulonephritis.
“Bogbean has a long history of use in rheumatism. The bogbean person often has pain in the kidney region that may later affect the legs, knees, and feet. There may be heat and swelling in the joints, that are improved with movement. Gouty rheumatism is a classic bogbean!
“Sciatica pain becomes worse as the body congests and stagnates with old acids and waste. Water retention is reduced by making the lymphatic system more efficient.
“In the negative state, the bogbean person cannot climb the stairs. Repeated trips to the chiropractor only give temporary relief. They need to walk to keep the lymphatics moving and the kidneys eliminating. In this state, they crave meat and yet are not thirsty, a sure recipe for kidney damage. Pain killers simply overload them more [and leave the basic condition untreated], resulting in kidney stones, backache, sciatica, and muscle pain” (Prairie Deva, quoted by Rogers, 2000, 135).
Buckbean is used for shingles. A working man “had an eruption of painful little blisters along his lower ribs on his right side. They followed in a line along his ribs and became agonizingly painful to the point where he couldn’t do his job.” He was completely cured using buckbean leaf tea, 1 ounce to a pint of boiling water, 4 tablespoonfuls 3x/day (J. V. Cerney, 1976, 67).

### TASTE:

bitter

### TISSUE STATE:

stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Mental confusion, lack of humor, aversion to small amusements (flower essence).
HEAD
Migraine, associated with digestive stagnation.
Eyes; “early stages of amaurotic paralysis of the retina” (3–4 drops of the tincture in cold water).
RESPIRATION
Wheezing, asthmatic congestion.
DIGESTION
Dyspepsia, chronic, sluggish digestion, constipation.
Hemorrhoids.
Cold abdomen and extremities.
Swollen glands, sluggish lymphatic drainage.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Edema.
Glomerulonephritis.
FEMALE
Menstrual irregularities; pelvic congestion.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Chronic arthritic and rheumatic conditions; fibromyalgia, gout, lumbago; achy joints (internal, external).
SKIN
Dry, flaky skin, eczema, psoriasis (combine, Solidago, Mahonia).
Herpetic pain, festering sores, shingles.
FEVER
Intermittent and remittent fevers; regular or irregular chills and fevers.
OTHER
Epilepsy (1 wineglassful, 3x/day).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

“The leaves of Buckbean are to be gathered before the stalks appear for flowering, and are to be dried; the powder of them will cure agues, but their great use is against the rheumatism: for this purpose they are to be given for a continuance of time in infusion, or in the manner of tea” (John Hill, 1740, 94). Cold infusion: 1 teaspoonful of fresh or dried leaf in a cup of warm water, set overnight. Dose: 1 fluid ounce every 3–4 hours (Rogers). Tincture: fresh roots and leaves. Powdered root or leaf: 1–3 grams as a tonic. “Small doses tone and larger ones cause drainage. Go slow!” (Rogers). Infuse roots in wine. Root oil: collect fresh, pulverize, cover with an equal quantity of olive oil, sit warm for a week with daily shaking, strain and use for arthritic and muscular pain (Rogers).

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Aggravates acute diarrhea and irritates colitis due to excitation.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, W. T. Fernie (3), Richard Hool (7), Robert Dale Rogers, J. V. Cerney (14), Prairie Deva (1), John Hill (12).

## Momordica charantia. Bittermelon.

Bittermelon is a member of the pumpkin family widely grown in the tropics as a food and medicine. It is native to Asia, but has been introduced to Africa and tropical America. It is used in Chinese and Ayurvedic medicine for cold, depressed conditions of the tissues. It contains such strong antitumor properties that it is used both to fight cancer and as an abortifacient in China. Recent studies have confirmed its traditional use in Ayurveda to treat type II diabetes. It contains an insulin-like substance (charantin) that keeps the sugar in solution in the blood longer and helps the cells pick up more sugar. It sensitizes the cells and the sense of taste to sugar. It is the original, organic source of the protease inhibitors used to fight AIDS. I have seen the original patents myself, which name the plant.
At an early date bittermelon was brought to the New World. Dr. William Salmon (1710, 42) testifies to this use from his sojourn in the “West Indies,” which at least included South Carolina. “We in the West-India’s in this case, where the constipation is great, give a strong decoction of the bitter Gourd, or bitter Squash made in water, which effectually does the cure at once, if given in the beginning of the disease: pulp of Colocynthis here [in London] will do the same.” He is referring to colic or bellyache with constipation.

### TASTE:

bitter and pungent

### TISSUE STATES:

tension, depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Type II diabetes.
Kidney stones.
Intestinal worms, parasites.
Diarrhea.
Constipation with colic.
Anemia.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Juice of the fresh immature fruit is used—the mature fruit is poisonous. The dose is 2 ounces a day.

## Nepeta cataria. Catnip.

Catnip is a mint native to Europe but widely naturalized in North America around old farms and homesteads. It possesses compounds that break down into nepetalactone, a substance similar to the valepotriates found in valerian (hence the acrid flavor) and essential oils (carvacrol and thymol, typical of the mint family).
Catnip is relaxant with both stimulating and cooling properties (cf. peppermint). It relaxes muscle spasm, especially in the smooth muscles of the intestines, and opens the pores of the skin to promote diaphoresis and release fever. It is especially indicated for infants and children, although it is also useful for adults. It is specific for infantile colic and fever (which often occur at the same time). The infant flexes the legs upward. The tension, pain, and anxiety are accompanied by a “pressing upward” sensation from the stomach. However, the symptoms mostly go downward (cf. lemon balm, where the symptoms go upward from the stomach). In adults catnip is beneficial for hiatal hernia, menstrual headache, cramp, and colic.
A mental profile for catnip has been developed by several practitioners. William LeSassier explains that it is for the “quiet child” who never says anything, “internalizes stuff, and is closed down.” An Irish practitioner notes that it should be thought of for children “when trouble is suspected at home.” Elisabeth Janos collected folklore to the effect that it is beneficial for hyperactive children. It has a reputation for children who are mean or cruel. Here we might think of its influence on cats—catnip brings out their cruelty.

### TASTE:

acrid, pungent • moist, cool • resinous

### TISSUE STATES:

constriction, depression, excitation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Infants; nervous, fretful; intestinal colic, constipation, and fever.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Children; “the quiet child,” internalizes experiences; trouble in the home; overactive, nervous, restless, mean-spirited.
“Worry worts.”
“Held in people with pent-up tension; especially tension held in the stomach.”
HEAD
Nervous headache; menstrual headache; sleeplessness.
Swollen under the eyes.
DIGESTION
“Tends to internalize stress to the stomach.”
Congestion, pain, and anxiety in the stomach; pain up to the vagus nerve.
Pain in the stomach, mimicking heart attack.
Anxiety attacks felt in the stomach, rising upward.
Hiatal hernia; sensation of extreme pressure upward against the diaphragm; hiccough.
Intestinal colic; draws the legs up for relief.
Colicky gas pains, relieved by burping, belching.
Spasm, pain ascending from the stomach or uterus.
Motion sickness (mild palliative); nervous to be on airplane.
“Does sedate HCl production, eases an over acid stomach caused by nerves.”
“Balances coffee jitters.”
FEMALE
Menstrual cramps (combine with leonurus).
Lactation: sweetens the milk.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Lessens fatigue from muscular exertion.
SKIN
Skin irritation; hives, measles, fever, acne, scarlatina; brings rashes to the surface.
OTHER
General first aid for infants and children.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The herb is gathered before flowering and prepared fresh as a tea, or fresh in tincture. It loses properties on drying. To make a poultice boil the fresh leaves (covered), thicken with corn meal (Daniel Cobb, 1846, 2:231).

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

“Do not use for extended periods of time with small children. It has an almost narcotic effect when overused” (Darryl Patton, Tommie Bass).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1, 2), Jack Ritchason (6), William LeSassier (2, 3, 4, 7–13, 15, 16, 17, 21), Elisabeth Janos (2), John William Fyfe (1, 12, 14), Rosari Kingston (2), Tommie Bass (5, 15), Aviva Romm (18).

## Nigella sativa, N. damascena, N. arvensis. Black Cumin.

The ancient Egyptians used black cumin as a medicine and food, both of which practices persist in the Middle East today—it is a common ingredient of curry. Galen recommended nigella. Mohammed extolled its effects above all other herbs, saying, “Black cumin is good for all ailments except death.” This is verified by the fact that it is an immune normalizer, useful for both lack and excess of reaction to stress. It is a member of the buttercup family, an annual native to the Mediterranean and Middle East, but easily grown in gardens. The seeds are high in essential fatty acids that are the source of a pressed oil that has long been used in medicine. It belongs to the same pharmacological group as evening primrose, black currant, and borage, all of which are high in essential fatty acids (EFAs). Each, however, has somewhat different properties.
Black cumin seed was classified by the Greek physicians as hot and dry in the third degree, meaning it warms up the center to drive humors, toxins, and heat to the surface, thins the humors to improve their passage, and opens the skin to release them. Hence, it was traditionally used (1) as a warming, stimulating, and bitter carminative to raise the digestive fire, (2) as an anti-inflammatory, and (3) as an expectorant, or mucolytic. Remedies in this category are also beneficial when there are putrefaction and toxins in the bloodstream. In addition, it was used externally as an emollient to soften hardness of the skin.
In modern terms black cumin is an immune normalizer, which means that it can act on both “hot and cold,” on inflammation and lack of reaction. Thus, it balances the inflammatory suppressant side of the adrenal cortex associated with high cortisone (high blood sugar, digestive heat, impotence) and on the other it controls excessive immune reaction associated with high aldosterone and androgens (hyperactivity, allergies, asthma, gum disease, food allergies, inflammatory and degenerative arthritis, skin eruptions).
The hard little black seeds are a signature for the kidneys, if we follow the Chinese designation of color and organ. They resemble gromwell seeds, also an old specific for kidney stones, and are used for that purpose.

### TASTE:
