warm • oily

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, stagnation, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Hyperactivity in children.
Depression associated with PMS and hormonal changes.
Forgetfulness of old age, difficulty concentrating (combine with myrrh).
Fainting spells.
Insomnia (strong decoction of the seed).
HEAD
Loss of hair and dandruff (external).
Headache, associated with hormones.
Eyes irritated, itchy, watery; with headache, nasal congestion.
Eyestrain (external, on temples).
Ears; middle ear infections (internal, external).
Gum infection.
RESPIRATION
Respiratory allergies and susceptibility (begin treatment in January and continue throughout the season); sneezing, frequent colds, constant runny nose, hay fever, conjunctivitis, flu, colds (internal; inhalation).
Sinusitis (roasted seed; tied in a cloth and the odor inhaled).
Bronchitis, stubborn, annoying, hacking cough, whooping cough, shortness of breath, bronchial asthma, sore chest (internal; inhalation; syrup; foot bath for prevention).
DIGESTION
Indigestion, gastritis, flatulence, sharp, severe gas pains after eating (with milk and honey).
Diarrhea, with significant weight loss.
Hemorrhoids; itching, bleeding (cream).
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Inflammation of the liver, gallstones.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Kidney stones, difficult passage of urine, dropsy (pain relief and cure).
FEMALE
Premenstrual syndrome, menopausal complaints, headaches with hormonal component and depression.
MALE
Impotence (mix with honey and pepper).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Arthritis and rheumatism; inflammatory and degenerative conditions; stiffness, swelling, pain, redness.
“Frequently recurring, inexplicable aches” (Schleicher and Saleh).
SKIN
Varicose veins, varicose ulcers (oil, 5 drops in water, 4–5x/day).
Skin diseases; acne, eczema, psoriasis, fungus, nettle rash, itching, impetigo, urticaria, warts, neurodermatitis, lice, parasites (internal and external).
Fistula, ulcers (external).
Contusions, bruises, effusions of blood, wounds (external).
Insect bites.
OTHER
Diabetes type II.
Deafness (powdered seeds).
Exhaustion, convalescence (combine with royal jelly).
Candida.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The seeds or the pressed oil of the seeds. Often used externally.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Large doses of the seeds have been used to bring on the menses, so black cumin should be used with caution in pregnancy. It is a member of the Ranunculaceae (buttercup) family, which includes several abortifacients.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Sylvia Luetjohann (The Healing Power of Black Cumin), Peter Schleicher and Mohamed Saleh (Black Cumin: The Magical Egyptian Herb for Allergies, Asthma, and Immune Disorders), Galen, Chalid Ottway, Hakim Chishti.

## Ocimum basilicum. Basil.

Basil has been used since the most ancient times as a culinary herb and medicinal plant. It is sacred in India (Ocimum sanctum) and taboos about its use are common in the older Western literature. The name basilicum (Greek basilicon) derives from the Greek word for a dragon (basilisk) or a king (basileus). It removes heat and toxins from the bloodstream, liver, portal circulation, and intestines. It could be said that it rules the fiery basilisk. It contains flavonoids, polyphenolic acids, triterpenes, ursolic acid, volatile oils, mucilage, and vitamins A and C.
The pungent volatile oils help basil open the skin and lungs, relax the digestive tract, and purge the blood of heat and toxins. It detoxifies through the skin by diaphoresis and removes mucus from the lungs, so it is serviceable in colds, flus, and acute respiratory conditions. It stimulates the appetite and digestion, removing stagnant food and flatulence. It also penetrates to the deeper regions of the organism to detoxify the blood, liver, portal circulation, spleen, and intestines. “Basil appears to dissolve and liberate for excretion, toxic lipophilic compounds which are stored in body fat deposits. It especially neutralizes past (and long past) damage to the body from recreational marijuana use” (Brent Davis, DC). It can also be beneficial for removing heavy metals (Eva Graf).
Basil was traditionally used to detoxify from cannabis in India and the Middle East. This fact was noted by Brent Davis. He began to use basil with great success in patients with long histories of marijuana consumption. The typical patient suffers from a fear that their health is permanently compromised, about which they feel a great deal of emotional distress. They are suffering from “neurological disorganization.” The nervous system is incapable of self-correction, despite the right spinal manipulation, the patient is gradually getting worse, and they suffer a lack of musculoligamentous tone, or “ligamentous looseness,” so that their bodies cannot hold manipulations. In addition, Davis recommends basil as a detoxifier for removing medical and chemical drugs that sequester themselves in fat cells.
Nervine effects are paradoxical. Research by French doctors M. Cadéac and A. Meunier showed that basil first stimulates, then relaxes, the brain and nervous system. This is demonstrated in traditional herbalism: basil is used in Mediterranean countries before bed, for insomnia, and on waking, to enhance alertness (Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli). It seems to work on both the sympathetic and the parasympathetic sides of the autonomic nervous system—an unusual and valuable fact. Hence it has been called an “adaptogenic tonic” for the autonomic (Christopher Menzies-Trull). Likewise, it can be either cooling or heating. This explains its use for tuning up the gallbladder and helping in recovery from influenzas and intermittent chills/fever, where the periodic chills wear out the nervous system and gallbladder reflexes. It also improves the operation of the liver.
The improvement in bone and connective tissue healing and in immunity suggests that it may improve androgen function. Low sex drive is a problem in chronic marijuana use. John Hill (1740, 75) writes, “No simple is more effectual for gently promoting the menses, and for removing those complaints which naturally attend their stoppage.”

### TASTE:

pungent • cooling and heating

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, torpor
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Depression, melancholy, nervous tension.
Insomnia (bedtime tea for sleep).
HEAD
Headaches associated with cold, indigestion, nervous tension.
Earache.
Poor eyesight.
RESPIRATION
Head cold, sinus infection, sinus headache, sinus drainage, acute fever.
Hay fever, allergies, pollen allergies (1–2 teaspoonfuls of basil tea in apple cider).
Catarrhal accumulation in the lungs; cough, whooping cough, croup, asthma.
DIGESTION
Catarrh in the stomach; vomiting; enteritis; parasites.
Flatulence.
FEMALE AND MALE
Suppressed menses (hot tea).
Low sex drive.
Low milk production.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Broken bones, take a long time to heal.
Rheumatism (external, palliative for the pain).
FEVER
Influenza (intermittent fever/chills); aftereffects, residual cold, wiped out, exhausted after, lingering (basil tea).
Fever, intermittent (chills/fever); with autonomic disorder; constantly yawning and stretching during the chills; spasms in the extremities, violent electric-like shocks in the nerves, violent chills and shivering.
Fever; thirst increases with rise in temperature, flashes of heat from face, eyes, mouth.
Fever; scanty sweat in axilla, neck, shoulders, and chest.
EXTERNAL
Snake bites and insect stings (external).
Fresh basil juice as a direct application on fungal lesions on the skin.
OTHER
Results of past use of marijuana; including neurological disorganization; complex hormonal dysregulation, infertility; extreme aggravation of premenstrual syndrome; confounding symptoms associated with chronic structural complaints, instability; ligamentous laxity.
Hypoglycemia.
Heavy metal toxicity (combine with cloves).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The mature flower head contains the most complete representation of the properties (Brent Davis). The infusion is made from a pinch of the dried or bruised fresh tops. Pour a cup of boiling water over a teaspoonful of the herb and steep for 15 minutes. Flavor with honey or lemon. The tincture can also be used. The dose is 5–60 drops, 3x/day. Oil of basil is used to build up the cortisol side of the adrenal cortex.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Despite Nicholas Culpeper’s assertion that basil will grow “scorpions of the mind,” it is usually considered an edible food.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Jethro Kloss (20), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (3), Donald Law (10), Hakim Chishti (1, 5, 10, 15, 16), Eva and Eugene Graf (6, 7, 14, 16, 22, 24), John Hill (11), Brent Davis (22), Ben Charles Harris (9, 23), Yadubir Sinha (Ocimum sanctum: 17–19).

## Olea europaea. Olive.

The olive tree has been used since antiquity for food, fuel, and medicine. The oil is an extremely important food product and is also used in herbalism as a base for oils, salves, and creams.
“This oil was very extensively used by the Roman physicians as an external application in the cure of diseases. Celsus, Galen, and Aëtius were much in the habit of employing oily frictions; and have left us some excellent observations in relation to the cases in which they are applicable” (John Eberle, 1834, 2:460). Such “frictions” or warming massages “have a considerable tendency to excite the activity of the absorbents”—that is, lymphatics. They were especially used for watery accumulations such as dropsy, ascites, and anasarca. The oil lubricates, softens, nourishes, and unkinks. Still widely used as a massage oil.
The most common internal use of olive oil today is as a “gallbladder flush” to remove gallstones. This method is mentioned as far back as the late nineteenth century, both in Europe and North America (Fernie, Ellingwood). The usual method is to eat apples for several days (or fresh apple cider), then drink a cup of olive oil with some lemon juice squirted into it, and lie down on the right side for several hours. The apple pectin is supposed to soften the stones (then why not just use apples for several months?). The olive oil stimulates the gallbladder. The lemon juice makes the olive oil palatable. Many people claim this works, though some say that the “gallstones,” under study are just congealed olive oil!
The following account points to a deeper relationship. Here olive oil is used to unkink the pyloric sphincter. When this sphincter opens, to let food out of the stomach, it sends a hormonal signal to the gallbladder to release bile. The fact that olive oil works on the pyloric sphincter indicates that it would simultaneously work on the gallbladder.
W. T. Fernie (1914, 368) writes: “In cases of ulcerated stomach, or contraction of its further outlet (the pylorus), a wineglassful of olive oil taken before meals will prevent the severe pain which otherwise follows on eating; and with most patients the accompanying dilation of the stomach disappears completely. From eight to nine ounces should be thus taken in all every day. In two instances this plan of treatment, when tried as an absolutely last resource before operation, yet proved successful; and the patients, who had become reduced to a shadow, began forthwith to regain flesh, insomuch that within two months they were almost cured.”
The leaf has also been used and, in the last decade, has been reintroduced into modern usage. Properties are somewhat different from the oil, since the latter is nutritive and lubricating whereas the leaf is highly astringent. It has remarkable properties as an antiviral, antibiotic, antifungal, and antiparasitic. It can kill or inhibit more than one hundred pathogenic microorganisms (HIV, anthrax, tinea, chicken pox, chlamydia, cholera, common cold, diphtheria, Epstein-Barr, equine encephalitis, influenza, helicobacter pylori, herpes, giardia, Lyme disease, malaria, rubella, viral meningitis, mumps, pneumonia, rabies, salmonella, staph, strep, thrush, candida, warts, and others). It was used for malaria with some success. It also has a strong effect on the cardiovascular system. Unfortunately, differential symptoms for olive leaf have not been developed, so it is not well established which kind of mumps, pneumonia, herpes, ulcer, and so forth, it will act upon. Dr. Bach used olive bud for exhaustion.

### TASTE:

(oil) oily (leaf) bitter • astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

(oil) constriction, atrophy (leaf) relaxation, depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

(oil)
INTERNAL
“Strengthens the gums and tightens the teeth” (Ottway).
Spasm of the pyloric sphincter (drink olive oil).
Hepatic insufficiency (oil slows digestion so the liver is not swamped).
Gallstones, gallstone colic (preventative; 1 teaspoonful in the morning).
Constipation, habitual (mild laxative; 1–3 teaspoonfuls/day).
Colitis.
EXTERNAL
Renal colic.
Bedwetting of children (friction with olive oil over the kidneys).
Joints, tendons, muscles (externally, nutritive, lubricating; cf. linseed oil).
Uterine colic during parturition (1 teaspoonful in a cup of hot milk).
Swelling of knee after injury (rub oil in clockwise direction, twice a day).
Swelling of the abdomen, legs, feet (external, friction).
Burns, insect bites (oil).
Boils (staph infections) in young athletes, due to inordinate stimulation of the sweat glands.
(leaf)
Hypertension, vasoconstriction.
Angina pectoris.
Diabetes.
Diarrhea.
Fever.
Wounds, sores, ulcers, with putrefaction and discharge.
Herpes, shingles.
(root)
Diabetes (shaved olive roots, olive leaves, 1 teaspoonful daily in tea).

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Neither oil nor leaf is known to have toxic effects in short- or long-term use.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Jean Palaiseul (3–10, 13, 15–20), John Eberle (5), Chalid Ottway (1, 5), W. T. Fernie (2, 14), Juliette de Bairacli Levy (22).

## Ononis spinosa. Restharrow.

This is a common weed in Europe and has a good reputation in European folk medicine, but is little known in North America. It contains flavonoids, isoflavones, tannins, triterpenes, sterols, and volatile oils.
Restharrow is a very powerful diuretic, increasing urinary output up to 20%. It removes calcified deposits, like a number of diuretics. Thus it is useful in stones and arthritis. Because it contains flavonoids it will be cooling to the genitourinary tract.

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Aphthous ulcers (canker sores).
Cystitis.
Urinary calculi.
Arthritis, gout.
Hemorrhoids (external).
Ulcers and wounds (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

“The root is to be taken up fresh for use, and the bark separated for that purpose. It is to be boiled in water, and the decoction given in large quantities” (Hill, 1740, 317).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Christopher Menzies-Trull.

## Origanum majorana. Sweet Marjoram.

This sweet, perfumy mint has a long history of medical usage in the Old World. It is suited to high-strung persons who push themselves, are exhausted, but cannot relax; to obsessive thinking, particularly about romantic matters; and to irritative conditions of the respiratory tract and digestion (Boericke). Sweet marjoram improves the downward movement of the digestive tract and prevents putrefaction (Elliot and de Paoli). It was at one time used in sneezing powders (Grieve).

### TASTE:

sweet, spicy • warm

### TISSUE STATE:

depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

High-strung persons who push themselves, are very tired, but cannot relax.
Mental tiredness and tension.
Unrequited love, romantic infatuation, obsessive thinking on the subject; relieved by jogging.
Headaches associated with nervousness, exhaustion, respiratory and digestive problems.
Upper respiratory tract disorders accompanied by much sneezing (Chomel).
Irritable cough.
Wheezing, oppression, mucus (Matthioli).
Digestive spasm, belching, stomach cramps, colitis.
Amenorrhea, dysmenorrhea.
Sprains, bruises, strains (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Infusion of the bruised fresh or dried leaves, a pinch to a cup, steep.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (1, 2, 4–8), William Boericke (3).

## Origanum vulgare. Wild Oregano, Wild Marjoram.

Wild oregano is an Old World mint, easily naturalized in North America. It is analogous in taste to its American cousin Monarda fistulosa, and probably also in properties. It is likewise used in fever, candida, and tinnitus. Wild marjoram contains flavonoids, triterpenoids, vitamins A and C, and volatile oils.

### TASTE:

pungent • warm • diffusive

### TISSUE STATE:

depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Tinnitus.
Colds and flus with chills and shivering.
Respiratory tract infections with profuse mucus and chilliness.
Halitosis; putrid digestion, abdominal swelling, hiccough, belching.
Dyspepsia, gastritis, spasm, colic.
Candida.
At the commencement of measles, used to bring out perspiration and the rash, to lessen the course of the disease.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Infusion of the bruised fresh or dried leaves, a pinch to a cup, steep 5 minutes.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1–4, 6), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (2–4), Maude Grieve (6).

## Osmunda regalis. Royal Fern, Buck Horn Brake, Backache Brake.

This beautiful, regal fern is almost unused in modern herbalism, but after many years of experience with most of the plants in this book I will testify that it is highly useful, at least for one thing: lumbago. Osmunda is mentioned by John Gerard (1633), Nicholas Culpeper (1653), John Quincy (1736), Beal P. Downing (1851), Finley Ellingwood (1918), and Maude Grieve (1931). It is sometimes known as tussock, or boar tusk root.
Osmunda has an earthen, mucilaginous, and astringent taste. Culpeper called it an herb of Saturn because it is drying and hardening. As an astringent it checks excess fluid losses, as an astringent with earthen salts it hardens the bones and reinforces the mineral content of the tissues, while the mucilage soothes, nourishes, and softens the mucosa. Grieve (1931) remarks that “the actual curative virtues of this fern have been said to be due to the salts of lime, potash and other earths which it derives in solution from the bog soil and from the water in which it grows.”
Osmunda is an old remedy for the defective bone growth of infancy and the spinal deterioration of old age. “It passes with some almost for a specific in rickets,” writes John Quincy (1736, 103). Beal Downing (1851), writing from the wild American frontier a hundred years later, calls it the “only and absolute cure for rickets.” Finley Ellingwood (1918) recommends it for osteoporosis and subluxations of the spine. Maude Grieve (1931) mentions that it has a common name, “lumbago brake.” I have found it excellent for lumbago.
As a mucilaginous emollient and astringent Osmunda is beneficial for the skin and mucosa. “Its soothing influence upon mucous surfaces seems to be remarkable,” writes Ellingwood (1918). “It is most in esteem for restraining the whites in women, and strengthening the womb,” continues Quincy. His contemporary, Sir John Hill (1740, 173), writes, “A decoction of the fresh roots promotes urine, and opens obstructions of the liver and spleen; it is not much used, but I have known a jaundice cured by it, taken in the beginning.” The mucilage makes it of some value in acute and chronic bronchial irritation.

### TASTE:

earthen • cool • mucilaginous, astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

relaxation, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Persons suffering from bone disease, bone malnutrition, rickets, weakness, tendencies to fluid losses (diarrhea, leucorrhea, frequent urination), demineralization.
Rickets, “the best and only absolute cure.”
Lumbago, weak back, weakness of the muscular structure of the back, incipient disease of the spinal vertebrae, osteoporosis, subluxations.
Large-boned persons.
RESPIRATION
Chronic irritation of respiratory mucosa.
DIGESTION
Diarrhea and dysentery; from local irritation; with acute or protracted fevers, with great weakness, in convalescence after such fevers; in poorly nourished patients.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Frequent urination.
SEXUAL
Decreased sexual desire.
Female weakness, severe leucorrhea, menopausal problems.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Sprains, bruises, clotted blood, broken bones, bones out of joint.
Hernia.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The heart or middle part of the root is the traditional source of the medicine. These are gathered in the latter part of summer. Prepared in a fresh infusion they yield a mucilaginous substance that quickly forms into a jelly-like consistency. This is preserved in brandy (Downing, Ellingwood). However, a more ecological preparation is just as good: soak the leaves in water in the sunlight for about 3 hours to extract the minerals. After 3 hours mucilage increasingly dominates the extract (Wood). Samuel Henry (1814, 28) credits “polypody” with the same properties and calls it “backache brake.” It is indicated in “violent coughs,” has “cured the most inveterate lumbago,” and is “an effectual cure for the rickets in children.” I suspect he has misidentified polypody with osmunda.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

None known for internal use. Except in a few locations, osmunda is usually found in sparse populations and should be judiciously collected from the wild. However, I have seen areas where it grew unmolested for miles and miles along northern rivers.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1, 9), John Gerard (9), Nicholas Culpeper (6, 9), Beal P. Downing (1, 2, 7, 11), Finley Ellingwood (1, 2, 3, 4, 7, 9), Maude Grieve (3), Matthew Wood (1, 3-confirmed), Ariel Rose (4, 8).

## Paeonia officinalis, P. albiflora, P. lactiflora. White Peony Root.

Peony (P. officinalis) was one of the mainstays of Greek medicine and remained popular in European medicine all the way to the nineteenth century. It is less popular today but its Chinese cousin (P. albiflora) has been introduced into the West and is now extensively used. There is very little difference between the two. The double flowered red specimens are considered officinal in Western phytotherapy. The flowers and roots have both been used. Peony root contains flavonoids, anthocyanins, and tannins. We should not suppose that the native North American or California peony (P. californica, P. brownii) has identical properties to the others (Michael Moore, 2003).
Peony is one of the three members of the buttercup family that have a powerful action on the female hormonal system. Interestingly, each has a different effect. Pulsatilla acts strongly on hypothalamic-pituitary regulation, cimicifuga on estrogen deficiency, and peony on estrogen excess. For herbal gynecology and obstetrics these three members of the buttercup family are indispensable. I call them the “three sisters.” Yet, all of them have considerable other powers outside this sphere.
Peony root is indicated when there is cramping and heavy bleeding. This is usually associated with an estrogen excess profile. It pacifies heat and is specifically indicated by the appearance of acneform sores on the chin, themselves usually a sign of inflammation in the ovaries and estrogen excess or intolerance to estrogen. It was used in Greek medicine in obstetrics to relax the expectant mother, reduce cramping, and encourage labor. After the birth it was used to stop fits and seizures in children.
Galen mentions the ancient custom of tying a peony root around the neck of a child to prevent epileptic seizures. Although a rationalist, highly skeptical in such matters, Galen tested this method on an epileptic boy and found it curative. Several of my students have verified this usage with children, adults, and even dogs. William Salmon (1710) notes that the decoction or extract works just as well as hanging the root around the neck. John Quincy (1736, 79) notes that “women highly esteem necklaces made of it to hang about children’s necks, for convulsions, and difficult breeding of the teeth.” Today, small children would not be given necklaces.
In traditional Chinese herbalism white peony root is classified as bitter, sour, and slightly cooling. It is indicated in irregular periods, heavy bleeding, and menstrual cramping. It is also used in night sweats; when there is “deficient blood,” yang floats to the surface and causes heat and sweating. For this reason it is also used in fever with spontaneous sweating. It is suited to liver stagnation or stagnant qi symptoms with hypochondriac pain, breast distention, and irregular menses. It is also used for muscle spasms and pain of hands and feet, abdominal pain and tenesmus or straining in dysentery. Headaches and dizziness caused by “overactivity of liver yang” come under its purview. It is contraindicated when there is cold or deficiency of yang.
Peony is an excellent remedy in middle-aged, menopausal women who fit the estrogen excess profile: plump, red, flushed, warm. It is believed to help the liver break the “bad estrogens” down into the “good” ones. It is also indicated in young women who are oversensitive to estrogen and the heat it produces (estrus is Greek for heat).
William Coles, in Adam in Eden (1657), notes that peony buds look like a head, complete with sutures, and that it is a brain or head remedy. He then associates this with epilepsy. I would add another association. Because peony is a cousin of black cohosh, so useful in whiplash and spinal injury, I thought it would be beneficial in brain injuries; sure enough, in the two cases so far, it has proven helpful, restoring one woman to nearly normal after being quite debilitated.

### TASTE:

(root) bitter, sour • moist, cool

### TISSUE STATES:

(root) irritation, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Women; middle-aged, estrogen excess, plump, red-faced, with menopausal flushing.
Women; young, often red-haired, aggravated by estrogen; acneform sores on the chin; worse at ovulation and before the period.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Nightmares.
HEAD
Head injuries (cf. Acorus calamus).
Headache, dizziness; aggravated before the period.
Blurred vision, eyelid twitching, eye irritated.
Acneform sores on the chin, worse before the period; facial redness.
RESPIRATION
Whooping cough in children, with seizures.
DIGESTION
Abdominal pain and spasm, in dysentery.
Hemorrhoids (external).
FEMALE
Menstruation: fullness, heat, redness, discomfort before the period.
Irregular periods with cramping and heavy bleeding.
Menopause: fullness, heat, redness, hot flashes; helps the liver break down the “bad estrogens” into “good” ones.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
An ancient specific for epilepsy and seizure that has been verified. The fresh root is tied to a string and hung around the neck; it is beneficial for about a month, or until the root dries up completely.
Seizures; in children during teething.
Spasms in the hands and feet.
FEVER
Fevers with heat and fluid loss, cramping, seizures, easy sweating (cf. Verbena hastata).
Night sweats and heat.
OTHER
Diabetes mellitus.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Pick the tubers growing beneath the “eyes” and replant the latter to perpetuate the plant. The domestic peony can be used. The fresh root hung around the neck is the best method of use for seizures, since the body is exposed to a constant, small dosage through the vapor rising from the drying root. When the root dries out, use another. Tea or tincture in small to moderate doses.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional Greek and Chinese (3, 4, 5, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12, 13, 14, 15, 18), Galen (14), homeopathic provings (3, 8), Kim Dudley (19), Matthew Wood (1, 2, 4, 7, 8, 11, 13), Christopher Menzies-Trull (6), Isla Burgess, Samuel Henry.

## Panax ginseng. Red Ginseng.

Chinese or Korean ginseng is warming and moistening, whereas American ginseng is cooling and moistening. It is the great legendary plant of Eastern herbal medicine, a status that is justified by its extraordinary range of medicinal constituents and wide applicability. It is a member of the Araliaceae family, other members of which include American ginseng, Siberian ginseng, Tienchi ginseng, spikenard, and wild sarsaparilla. Most of these remedies have subtle endocrine activity, but all in different directions. Panax ginseng contains a rich assortment of sterols, triterpene saponins, volatile oil, polysaccharides, mucilage, vitamins B1, B2, and B12, and pantothenic acid.
Ginseng has been classified as a general “yang tonic,” meaning that it restores the latent heat and energy of the organism. An excessive dose in a hot person, or on a hot day, can cause hyperactivity and overheating. However, where there is a lack of heat and energy, ginseng is indicated. For this reason it is especially used for older men, whose energy is not what it once was. It is most specific in neurasthenia or nerve debility, where prolonged anxiety, nervousness, insomnia, heart palpitations, nervous stomach, addictive behaviors, sexual exhaustion, and depression have contributed to a condition of exhaustion, debility, and weakness, perhaps with weight loss. Such conditions wear out the cortisol reserve, which is required to provide enough blood sugar to support all this activity. Hence it is useful in hypoglycemia (cortisol opens the receptor sites for glucagon), which frequently afflicts those suffering from nervous exhaustion. When there is too much reliance on insulin due to the lack of glucagon (which provides blood sugar between meals), we end up with triglyceride formation, high LDL cholesterol, hypertension, weight gain, and diabetes type II.

### TASTE:

sweet, warm

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Anxiety, insomnia, tension, depression, addictions, nervous exhaustion, neurasthenia (cf. Avena sativa).
Memory loss.
RESPIRATION
Colds, infections.
Asthma, tuberculosis.
HEART
Heart palpitations.
High cholesterol and triglycerides; high blood pressure.
Heart failure.
DIGESTION
Stomach tension and fullness, with inability to digest food.
Peptic ulcers.
MALE
Low sperm count.
Low sexual desire.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Enuresis.
Renal failure; edema.
OTHER
Alcoholism.
Diabetes mellitus type II.
Hypoglycemia.
Convulsions.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

There are many different preparations of red ginseng. Consult a text specializing in this plant.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Not indicated for hot, active persons, or in hot weather.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional.

## Petroselinum sativum. Parsley.

The name Petroselinum comes from the Greek, meaning “celery of the rocks.” The growth habit amid the rocks is a signature in traditional herbalism for a kidney or stone-breaking remedy. Parsley is one of the five “opening roots” of traditional Greek/Arabic medicine, used to remove obstructions to the movement of blood, lymph, sweat, urine, stool, and menstrual blood, along with celery, fennel, butcher’s broom, and asparagus. All of these are salty plants. All but the last two are in the Apiaceae or carrot family. Parsley contains flavonoids, fixed oils, mucilage, vitamins A and C, coumarins, minerals (iron, calcium, sodium, trace elements), and volatile oils (including apiol, also in celery).
Parsley leaves have a slightly bitter, crispy, and salty taste that indicates the presence of minerals and sodium that increase secretion. Sodium draws water into areas that are dehydrated and mineralized, breaking up hardness. The buildup of sodium in the internal waters of the body (up to a point) strengthens diuresis and drives out stagnant waters through rejuvenated kidneys. Too much sodium collects excessive water. As a normalizer, parsley thins congealed fluids and breaks stones. The mild bitterness increases digestion in the stomach and raises the internal heat. Hence, parsley is warming, nourishing, thinning, and opening. It contains fixed and volatile oils and has an effect on oil systems and the steroid hormones made from lipids.
William Salmon (1710, 812) writes that parsley “is peculiar against the cholick in the stomach and bowels, expells wind, opens obstructions of the liver, spleen, reins and womb, gives ease in the strangury, provokes urine, is said to break and expel the stone, gravel, sand and tartarous slime; is profitable against the jaundice, dropsy, and falling sickness, provokes the terms in women, facilitates the birth, and resists and expels poison.”
Parsley is the herb “par excellence” for the kidneys and bladder, writes Culpeper (1653). It has been found to be almost infallible as a general remedy for bladder infections. I knew an elderly gentleman who claimed to have “cured” (his word) more than eighty seniors with an infusion of the fresh leaves of parsley. His method: buy a bunch of parsley in the grocery store, cut it in half, chop one-half fine and infuse in 3 cups, to be taken 3 cups a day for two to three weeks. Samuel Hahnemann, the founder of homeopathy, considered drop doses of parsley tincture to be specific for gonorrhea with discharges of slime or mucus in the urine.
“In addition to being a kidney cleanser, parsley supplies properties that maintain the proper function of the thyroid and adrenals,” writes Dr. J. V. Cerney (1976, 120). “It maintains the well-being of the smaller blood vessels (venules, arterioles, capillaries). Of particular note is its effectiveness in the treatment of weak eyes and other diseases of the eyes.” He also recommends it for menstrual irregularity. This usage was introduced by French doctors in 1849. Contemporary herbalist Billie Potts considers parsley to be one of the most important but overlooked female medicines. Dr. William T. Fernie (1914, 376) says it is “beneficial for women who are irregular as to their monthly courses due to ovarian debility.” Dr. Elliot Abravanel (1982, 14) recommends parsley leaf tea as a general tonic for the adrenals.
Stewed in lard, parsley root “makes one of the best-known ointments for erysipelas, inflammation, etc.” (Slack, 1919, 115).

### TASTE:

(seed) slightly bitter and salty • aromatic, oily, mucilaginous

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, torpor, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION
Weak, anemic persons.
HEAD
Dizziness.
Eyes, hot and swollen; blepharitis (inflammation of the margins of the lids); conjunctivitis, with discharge (external).
RESPIRATION
Allergies, hay fever, mucus, colds, coughs, asthma.
GLANDS
Scrofula, swollen glands.
Mastitis; breasts hard and swollen, with retention of milk (external).
DIGESTION, LIVER, GALLBLADDER
Lack of appetite, bad breath, poor digestion.
Gastrointestinal pain, spasm, cramp, flatulence, gastritis.
Liver and gallbladder “problems”; jaundice, gallstones.
Colic in children.
Constipation.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Dropsy, scanty urination; especially following scarlatina and other eruptive diseases.
Retention of urine, nephritis, and cystitis, especially when the urine is thick, irritating, and painful.
Glutinous slime in the urine, as in gonorrheal discharge.
Cystitis; urethritis when the urine causes a scalding sensation.
FEMALE
Weak, anemic women with scanty periods.
Very severe cases of dysmenorrhea (tincture of the seed in vinegar).
Menstrual irregularity.
OTHER
Colliquative sweating of tuberculosis; profuse perspiration during severe fever (seed).
Arthritis, gout.
Low blood pressure.
EXTERNAL
Acne, eczema, erysipelas, inflammation.
Contusions.
Insect stings.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

“Leaves, roots, and seed are all used: the leaves are weakest, the roots are next in strength, and then the seed” (Salmon). The root and leaf are commonly made into tea. The oils are extracted or distilled from the seed to produce “apiol.” William Salmon differentiates between the properties of the alcoholic, vinegar, and oily preparations of the seed.

### CAUTIONS:

Like many members of the carrot family parsley stimulates uterine contractions (though not very strongly) and its use is therefore limited in pregnancy. The seeds have been used as an abortifacient. The distilled oleoresin of the seed can cause dizziness, fainting, and paralysis, followed by fatty degeneration of the liver and kidneys (Grieve, 1931).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1–24), William Salmon (6, 17), Samuel Hahnemann (14), John William Fyfe (17), Maude Grieve, Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (16), Billie Potts, William T. Fernie (18), George Slack (22), Juliette de Bairacli Levy (8, 15).

## Pimpinella anisum. Anise Seed.

Anise seed has long been employed in European folk and professional medicine. It is a member of the Apiaceae family, usually compared with its cousins dill, fennel, and caraway, all of which are used as carminatives in European cookery and medicine. Anise seed needs to be differentiated from star anise (Illicium spp.), which has a similar taste but is more active and comes from a completely different botanical family. Anise seed contains flavonoids (including quercetin), rutin, sugars, malic acid, iron, sodium, and volatile oils (including anethole).
Anise seed has a sweet, fragrant, mildly warm/spicy flavor. It is moistening, nourishing, emollient (softening), warming, anodyne (pain-relieving), and moderately antiseptic. Like other members of the apiaceae it calms and builds the nervous system, acting especially on the digestive and respiratory spheres through both the nerves and mucosa. It is the most sweet, moistening, and nourishing of the apiaceae carminatives, and is indicated in conditions where the digestive tract is dry, atrophic, or spasmodic.
The most important use of anise is as a carminative for spasm and bloating in the stomach and intestines. An ancient name for anise was solamen intestinorum (comforter of the bowels). Today it is classified as a parasympathetic relaxant. That says much the same thing, since the parasympathetic largely runs the stomach and intestines. Anise is an important traditional remedy for wind, bloating, spasm, sluggish digestion, and lack of appetite.
Anise is also highly valued for breaking up catarrh or heavy phlegm from the digestive and respiratory tracts (Harris, 1972). “It has a special influence on the bronchial tubes to encourage expectoration, particularly in children,” writes W. T. Fernie (1914, 26). “For infantile catarrh, after its first feverish stage, aniseed tea is very useful.” He quotes several adults who found relief from asthma with the essence of anise seed (“anisette”) in water. He used it for “bronchitis, with attacks of spasmodic asthma,” with “barking like Cerberus,” and sleeplessness. One patient reported, “I have been bronchitic and asthmatic for twenty years, and have never known an alleviative so immediately efficacious as ‘anisette.’ ”
In addition to preventing bloating and gas, Rycharde Banckes (1525) informs us that the principal uses of anise in his day were to unbind stoppage of the liver and blocked humors. Probably because of its high oil content it acts on the liver and gallbladder. Phyllis Light uses anise seed to decongest the gallbladder when there is phlegm occluding the bile passages and gallstones are released mixed with a lot of phlegm.
Anise has a mild anodyne property, which makes it pain-relieving in harsh, painful coughs, gastrointestinal colic, and menstrual pain. The oil of the seed can produce stupefaction, tremors, and seizures, so it evidently causes in concentrated doses what it cures in small doses. It also promotes prolactin and lactation but seems to reduce estrogen. As a parasympathetic relaxant we should associate it with the kapha or parasympathetic-dominant constitution suffering from stress, exhaustion, and lethargy. Herbalist Laurel Dewey (1999, 76) paints a beautiful picture of the character of the plant, matching this kind of personality. Anise seed takes a long time to come to maturity, she observes. It requires a growing season of at least 130 days. Thus, it may be associated with a steady, slow, purposeful character. She also detects a susceptibility to perfectionism, which may explain the nervousness that sometimes afflicts what sounds like an otherwise overly relaxed constitution.

### TASTE:

sweet, pungent • warm, moist

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Nervousness, insomnia; associated with digestive problems; “the restlessness of languid digestion” (freshly ground anise seed in hot milk for insomnia).
HEAD
Migraine accompanied by nausea.
RESPIRATION
Infantile catarrh; coughing and mucus in children; after the fever has subsided.
Tickle in the back of the throat.
Colds, respiratory infections; whooping cough, tracheitis, pneumonia, asthma.
Hard, dry, painful, spasmodic coughs with phlegm difficult to raise, dried out.
Bronchitis, with attacks of spasmodic asthma; “barking like Cerberus.”
Emphysema.
Aerophagia, hiccough.
DIGESTION
Bad breath; poor appetite; fermentation of food in the stomach and bowels; with colic.
Indigestion with weakness, nervousness, flatulence, tension, hiccough, belching, nausea, vomiting, abdominal spasm, griping, colic, pain.
Gallstones; stones mixed with mucus.
Colic in infants and young children; safe for young babies (do not confuse with star anise, Illicium spp.).
Cholera.
KIDNEYS
Scanty urination.
FEMALE
Menstrual cramping associated with high estrogen levels.
Vaginal discharge; “white humors from the uterus” (Al Tamimi).
Low libido (women and men).
Lactation: increases milk production and prevents colic in the baby.
SKIN
Pediculosis and scabies (external).
Increases perspiration.
OTHER
Epilepsy, convulsions; in infants and children.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Infusion: the seeds must be freshly ground as they quickly lose their properties. Simmer 4 ounces in a closely covered pint of water for 2–3 minutes, steep for 10 minutes, strain, and sip slowly (Dewey). Once a day for three weeks, rest for ten days, repeat the course for three weeks, with rest, and again. If used more frequently, anise can oversedate and slow the metabolism (Elliot and de Paoli). Tincture of the whole seed: 9–12 drops a day is in the “normal range”; for pain and spasm take a lower dose, 1–2 drops, 6x/day (Kenner and Requena, 2001, 203). Topical salve: 5 drops of anise seed oil in 1 tablespoon of base oil, rub on spasmodic abdomen (Dewey).

### CAUTIONS:

The seed (or fruit) is considered fairly safe, but should not be used during pregnancy. The oil can irritate the skin (Dewey). Taken internally, the oil can be toxic, irritating the stomach, kidneys, and nervous system, resulting in stupefaction, tremor, renal damage, epileptiform seizures, and miscarriage (Fernie). Do not confuse with star anise (Illicium spp.), which has had toxic effects in children.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, W. T. Fernie (1, 3, 5, 6, 7, 11), Jack Ritchason (8), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (9, 11, 15, 18), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (10), Laurel Dewey (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 10, 16, 18-confirmed, 19), Phyllis Light (12), Al Tamimi (17), Christopher Menzies-Trull (15, 17–20, 22), Rudolf Steiner (21).

## Plantago majus, P. lanceolata. Plantain.

This little plant is as useful as it is common. Plantain grows in soil that has been heavily compacted by foot or other traffic. The name comes from the Latin planta, a foot, in reference to its appearance and habitat. It is native to Europe but widely naturalized and known in many places as “whiteman’s footprint.” In China Plantago asiatica is known as “plant before cart” (Lu, 1994, 153). Although science falls short of an explanation, it is clear from watching where plantain grows and what it survives that it possesses extra power to draw water and nutrients out of compacted ground. This is a signature, indicating that it can “draw” foreign substances out of the body. In fact, plantain is one of the best “drawing agents” in Western herbalism. It contains flavonoids and acids (including vitamin C), allantoin, mucilage, sugars, fixed oils, tannins, bitters, and minerals (potassium and calcium).
Plantain is cooling, moistening, softening (emollient), and astringent. It was classified as drying in Greek medicine because it is able to draw out and close up pus and infection and stop outflows of water and blood. However, it is also soothing to irritated membranes and it is one of the best drawing agents we possess. It is excellent for a dirty wound, to draw out the dirt and infection and leave it in a clean state, and for boils and abscesses.
Another name for plantain is “snakeweed,” due to the fact that the little seed stalk sometimes resembles a snake’s head, and also because it is used as a drawing agent to draw out venom from a snake bite. Here we turn to an old physician. Dr. Henry Taylor (1860, 274) writes that treatment of a rattlesnake or cottonmouth bite should be prompt. If a surgeon is present, the flesh around the bite should be cut away or burned. “But best of all is the application of plantain.” Bathe the wound with a strong decoction. “After this, mix the decoction of the plantain with the slippery elm bark and a good quantity of sweet oil; apply this constantly as long as there is any swelling or inflammation.” Take olive oil to keep the gastrointestinal tract moving. The late Anishinabe herbalist Keewaydinoquay pointed out that plantain had a special affinity for venom of all kinds. It is an important remedy for the bites of snakes, bees, mosquitoes, and poisonous spiders.
As a drawing agent I find plantain particularly useful when there are abscesses around the teeth, inflamed roots of teeth, or old lingering infections after root canal surgery. It is a specific here and it will almost never fail to draw out the pus and stop the infection, sometimes even saving the tooth where it was thought to be lost. I have seen this in dozens of cases and considered it a personal discovery, but I note a comment by Yadubir Sinha (1962, 118): “In pains of any kind in teeth and gums, apply this remedy 4 times a day with cotton and see the miracle.”
Plantain can be quite helpful in bronchial infections and pneumonia. It cools and moistens the mucosa, but also astringes and draws up, so to speak, mucus and water from the lungs. It is indicated when there is irritation in the trachea and bronchi, with an irritable cough. It also strengthens the kidneys—it is a “nourishing diuretic” rather than a “forceful diuretic,” as William LeSassier would express it, meaning that it increases renal output by building up the kidneys rather than forcing them to work and weakening them. In China the seeds of Plantago asiatica are used as a kidney tonic (seeds are considered to have a greater affinity to the kidneys in Chinese herbalism). It is used for edema, water in the lungs, inflammation of the kidneys, gonorrhea, and associated low back pain. This is fairly analogous to the Western usage.
The seeds of another relative, Plantago psyllium, are used to provide fiber (the husks) or mucilage (the seeds) in contemporary Western medicine. The roughage stimulates the bowel (irritates it in sensitive people), whereas the mucilage coats and soothes. The fiber is believed to get into the circulation and improve conditions of the vasculature. Plantago majus herb has somewhat analogous properties. It cools and moistens the bowels. The leaves of Plantago asiatica are used for bacterial enteritis, diarrhea, and hemorrhoids. The nutritive qualities of plantain are utilized in the East for wasting and retarded development of infants due to deficiency of growth hormone and androgen. In the East it is also used for cooling and reducing dampness.
Plantain was one of the most important coolants used in European Renaissance herbalism. It was especially used when heat was associated with scurvy. It contains vitamin C. “It cures the Scurvy in a hot habit of body, heals the excoriations of the stomach and other bowels, stops catarrhs, and all sorts of fluxes in man or woman. It stops also all sorts of hemorrhages whether internal or external, the overflowing of the courses in women, spitting and pissing of blood, and therefore is of excellent use in the curing of a phthisis [tuberculosis], or ulceration of the lungs” (Salmon, 1710, 873). Culpeper says it is specific for a “hot womb.”
Plantain is considered to be either a snake medicine or a frog medicine in American Indian practice. One of my students observed a frog eat the leaf after being bitten by a spider. The same observation comes from India, though here the victim was a toad (Rogers, 2000, 16). It is a Native American remedy for Bell’s palsy. The signature for the nerves is in the fibers that run through the leaves.

### TASTE:

bitter, earthen • cool, moist and dry • fibrous, mucilaginous, astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

irritation, atrophy, relaxation, depression (especially putrefaction)
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
EYES
Inflammation of the eyes; conjunctivitis; blepharitis; bloodshot eyes; dry, red eyes (warm poultice on the unopened eye, followed by eyewashes of the infusion).
Poor vision, light sensitivity, cataract.
Sore eyes from tearing, sore nostrils from blowing the nose (external).
EARS
Earache; facial neuralgia.
Catarrhal deafness (combined with nettle juice).
TEETH, GUMS, AND MOUTH
Abscessed teeth; infected root canal, before or after surgery (mouthwash).
Thrush in children.
RESPIRATION
Sore throat; chronic tracheitis.
Upper respiratory and chest infections; colds, bronchitis, pneumonia.
Bronchitis; dry cough, sounds as if the person were trying to cough out a fiber caught in the throat or chest.
Pneumonia; pulls water and mucus up from deep in the lungs in old, infected cases.
Fibrosities in the lungs; fibrosing alveolitis, emphysema, tuberculosis.
A specific for smoking cessation; can make cigarettes not taste good and cleanses the lungs after smoking.
DIGESTION
Diseases of the gastrointestinal mucous surfaces, when there are pinching or colicky pains.
Stomachache, diarrhea, hemorrhoids (infusion in milk).
Constipation (seeds soaked overnight, similar to psyllium seed).
Colitis, diarrhea, dysentery.
Constipation, hemorrhoids.
LIVER
Hepatitis, jaundice.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
“Affections of the kidneys, Bright’s disease, ulceration and inflammation of the kidneys.”
Edema.
Bladder infections, urinary difficulty.
Incontinence in children and the aged.
FEMALE
Vaginitis, vaginal discharge (internal, or douche).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Lower back pain.
Sprains and strains (external).
Neuralgia.
SKIN
Inflammatory affections of the skin, when there is pricking, itching, or burning pain.
Dirty wounds, open sores, boils, abscesses, burns, skin irritations, sunburn, poison ivy (external).
Acne, impetigo, eczema.
OTHER
Reduces cholesterol.
Hemorrhage; external or internal, especially from ulcerations.
Insect bites, snake bites (external).
Bell’s palsy (one-sided paralysis).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

“The whole plant can be used (roots, stems, leaves, and seeds), either in syrup, infusion, decoction, or fluid or solid extract. It can be ground up and made into pills” (Hool). In acute situations pull up the plantain leaves, chew, and place on the part. Juice the leaves for use fresh during the summer; dry for use in the winter. Dose: 1 cup, 1–3x/day. Extract fresh in alcohol; dose: 1–10 drops internally, 1–3x/day. Salve, cream, or poultice.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Plantago asiatica is contraindicated in men with seminal emissions due to kidney deficiency (Lu).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1–11, 13–20, 22, 23, 24, 25, 26, 27, 28, 29, 30, 32, 33), modern research (12, 31), homeopathic literature (4, 13), Matthew Wood (6, 10, 11), Yadubir Sinha (6), Richard Hool (7, 22), Jack Ritchason, Robert Dale Rogers (1, 5, 12, 15) Tommie Bass (24, 31, 32), Rolla Thomas (14, 28), Henry Lu (Plantago asiatica—1, 8, 17, 19, 21, 22, 24).

## Polygonum aviculare. Knotweed.

The Polygonums are a subdivision of the rhubarb family (Polygonaceae). There is considerable variation among them. P. aviculare is mucilaginous and cooling, P. bistorta is warming and intensely astringent, P. hydropiper is warming and stimulating, P. persicaria is warming and mucilaginous. The constituents of these plants are often intolerant of heat and respond best to cold water infusion overnight.
Knotweed proper (P. aviculare) is a common weed native to Europe but widely dispersed throughout the world. It is, perhaps, the most common of the knotweed clan. It contains mucilage (which is easily noticed on scrunching a leaf), flavonoids, sugars, acids, tannins, coumarins, and a little soluble silicon.
Knotweed “is used almost exclusively in folk medicine as an expectorant and secretolytic for coughs and bronchial catarrh, and further as an adjuvant in pulmonary disorders (like other silicic acid-containing drugs), as well as against night sweats in patients with tuberculosis. In folk medicine, the drug is also employed as a diuretic [see below under P. hydropiper], a haemostyptic in certain kinds of bleeding, and for skin conditions.
“In pharmacological experiments, knotgrass extracts have been shown to be active ACE (angiotensin converting enzyme) inhibiters” (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994, 387), but hypotensive properties have not been proven in practice. The flavonoid fraction inhibits thrombocyte aggregation. There are no known side effects or cautions and it is counted among the edible wild plants.

## Polygonum bistorta, P. bistortoides.
Bistort, Snakeweed.

The name bistort comes from the Latin for “twice bent,” referring to the bent shape of the root, which looks like a snake folded back upon itself, or a blackened intestine. Bistort has been used for a variety of conditions manifesting a low, depressed tissue condition, including snake bite, plague, cholera, typhoid, colitis, bleeding, and hemorrhoids. Maude Grieve (1931, 106) writes, “The older herbalists considered both the leaves and roots to have ‘a powerful faculty to resist poison.’ Combined with the bitter flag root (calamus), the root was used to cure intermittent fever and ague.” They also mention polypi, spongy gums, measles, plague (epidemic disease), smallpox, jaundice. William Salmon (1710) gives a formula for smallpox and eruptive fever: bistort root, angelica, zedoary, cinnamon.
In addition to its stimulating properties, bistort is an astringent. It is one of “the strongest astringent medicines in the vegetable kingdom and highly styptic and may be used to advantage for all bleedings, whether external or internal, and wherever astringency is required. The powder, or decoction, of [the roots], will stop all fluxes of the belly, and is one of the safest remedies known for overflowings of the menses” (John Hill, 1740, 87). “Although its use has greatly been superseded by other astringents of foreign origin, it is of proved excellence in diarrhea, dysentery, cholera and all bowel complaints and in hemorrhages from the lungs and stomach, and is a most effectual remedy for bleeding from the nose and exceedingly useful in dealing with hemorrhoids. It is used—as a medicine, injection and gargle—in mucous discharges, as well as for hemorrhages” (Maude Grieve, 1931, 106). It removes “canker” or mucoid coating from the intestines (John Christopher, 1976, 178), renewing the tissues as it removes old mucus and allows the secretion of new mucus with its increased immune properties.

### TASTE:

pungent • warm • astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, relaxation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
DIGESTION
Putrefaction in the alimentary canal and passive bleeding from the mucosa.
Loose teeth, gum disease, sore, ulcerated mouth (mouthwash).
Diarrhea, dysentery, cholera, typhoid, colitis; massive fluid loss.
Parasites causing bleeding.
Straining at stool causing bleeding.
Hemorrhoids.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Incontinence.
FEMALE
Profuse menstrual bleeding.
Leucorrhea.
Amenorrhea.
Dysmenorrhea.
FEVER
Intermittent fever, eruptive fever; measles, formerly used in the epidemic diseases and smallpox.
WOUNDS
Bruises, with black tissue.
Cuts, bleeding.
OTHER
Diabetes.
Hernia.
Insect strings, snake bites.
Drawing agent (bistort and comfrey powder, worked into a muck); on joint injuries, broken fingers, sprained joints, abscesses (Michael Moore).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The root is dug at the end of winter, “when the leaves begin to shoot,” and dried for use as tea (John Hill, 1740, 87). The tincture is made from the fresh or dried root. Dose: 30–50 drops (Christopher).

### CAUTIONS:

“The use of this root may be continued without danger, till it effects a perfect cure” (John Hill, 1740, 87).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1), John Christopher (2, 3, 4, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 14, 16, 17), Maude Grieve (12), John Skelton (2, 3, 8), Marie Southworth (13), Dorothy Hall (4, 5, 6), Louise Tenney (15), Michael Moore (18).

## Polygonum hydropiper, Polygonum spp. Water Pepper, Smartweed, Arsesmart.

There are a number of hot, peppery knotweeds native to the edges of wet spots in Europe and North America. Throughout the world they have earned similar names. The English “water pepper” and the Latin hydropiper are analogous to the Aztec A chilli (water chile). The Aztec herbal of 1552 clearly shows a knotweed under this name, though it is misidentified by the translator (Gates, 2000, 65). This plant was used in a formula for lesions on the foot. A cousin called mamaxtla (“many-jointed leg”) was used as a diuretic “against difficulty in urination” (Gates, 2000, 60). This Aztec name is similar to the Latin polygonum, “many joints.” The various water peppers are used interchangeably.
Water pepper grows on the margins of wet spots that dry out in the summer, filling in the bare earth left as the water retreats. Thus, it has the same signature as gravel root and hydrangea, showing the perfect balance of water and solid. In European folk medicine water pepper is the specific for taking away gravel, the way gravel root and hydrangea are used in North America. The hard little black seeds are also signatory of kidney stones. Dr. William Salmon (1710, 45) gives arsesmart extensive treatment under this subject. It is a “specifick,” he writes, “known by manifold and huge experience to be a peculiar plant against gravel and stone, whether in reins or bladder.” He preferred the distilled water, which “has cured to admiration when all other things in the World has failed, and so great things have been some hundreds of times experimented of this simple Distilled Water, which seem to be even Miraculous, it performing what much reputed Medicaments cannot do: I write not this from my own Experience only, which in this case is very considerable, but we have also the suffrage of very great and learned men, as to the same thing.” Dr. John Hill (1740, 64) corroborates, “The right arsesmart is an excellent medicine in obstructions of urine, in the gravel and stone. And in jaundice and beginning dropsies it has done great cures. The juice of the fresh-gathered plant is the best way of giving it.”
The second signature of water pepper is that it is “many jointed,” or like a “many-jointed leg,” per the Aztec nomenclature. Thus, it is used for arthritis. Very often kidney stone remedies have this double function, because it is thought that they regulate the balance of water and solids in the body as well as the kidneys. Thus, they are called “anti-lithics” in the old literature. My friend Paul Red Elk knew of an old, now-deceased medicine man who used smartweed to break up hard arthritic nodularities in the joints.
The “knot signature” has also inspired the use of knotweed as an antidote to magical curses made by tying knots in strings. Thus, William Salmon (1710) notes in passing, “Some use it in the transplantation of diseases, and removing of incantations.”
The third major property of water pepper is its stimulating, pepper quality. It is used like many plants of this nature (cf. Cinnamomum, Erigeron, Achillea, Capsicum), to stop hemorrhage, diarrhea, and watery discharge. It normalizes conditions of excess or deficiency of flow, especially from the kidneys. Dr. John Christopher (1996, 181) writes, “It produces a sensation of heat in the stomach, increases the force of the heart, raises arterial tension, promotes surface warmth and the secretions of the bronchial, renal, and uterine areas.”
The final notable property of water pepper is that it causes burning itching of the rectum, hence the name arsesmart, or more politely, smartweed. This indicates its use in hemorrhoids with burning in the rectum—I have verified this application.

### TASTE:

pungent • hot • stimulating

### TISSUE STATE:

depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
RESPIRATION
Colds, coughs.
CARDIOVASCULAR
“Increases the force of the heart, raises arterial tension.”
DIGESTION
Sore mouth, spongy gums (cold water infusion of the fresh leaves as a gargle).
Diarrhea, dysentery, cholera.
Flatulent colic, pains sharp and lancinating, intermittent, of a severe, griping character.
Hemorrhoids, rectal itching, “inflamed stage of the piles” (Cobb).
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Specific for kidney stone formation and colic (cf. Hydrangea, Eupatorium maculatum).
Depression of kidney action following the active stage of fevers and inflammations (cf. Solidago, Petroselinum, Galium).
FEMALE
Suppression of menstruation, chilly sensations in back, tensive pain, pain of legs.
Amenorrhea due to atony, exposure to cold.
Excessive menstrual flux.
Atonic hemorrhage following abortion or miscarriage.
Persistent, low volume, atonic bleeding of menopause.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Hard, arthritic nodularities.
SKIN
Harsh, inactive, dry skin; suppression of perspiration.
OTHER
Diabetes mellitus type II; increases circulation and perhaps cell metabolism.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Fluid extract, 1–15 drops; in hot tea for fever and suppressed menses. Polygonum pennsylvanicum can be used as a substitute. Smartweed is an “accelerator” for other herbs, according to the late Tommie Bass (Darryl Patton).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1–10), Daniel Cobb (6, 11, 12), William Salmon (7), John Christopher (1, 2, 3, 4, 7), Rolla Thomas (9, 15), Robert Dale Rogers (3, 12, 13), Paul Red Elk (14, 16).

## Polygonum persicaria. Lady’s Thumb, Peach-leaved Knotweed.

This is not a widely used member of the knotweed family, but it ought to be better known. It has several interesting qualities. The black mark on the leaf (like a lady’s thumbprint) almost demands our attention and indicates a remedy for depressed or even necrotic tissues, while the almost repulsive slime and smell of the tincture are quite unique.
Indications for lady’s thumb have been worked out by my friend Lise Wolff. She says that it is indicated when there is a dark spot in the center of the tongue or a dark line down the central crease. This usually corresponds to depression of the digestive powers or stagnant blood involving the spine. The characteristic stomach emotions are present. The person worries, is a worrier, and the stomach is “all tied in knots.” Food allergies are common. The membranes are dry and digestion is poor. There may be a history of unresolved mononucleosis (cf. baptisia, helianthemum). Old bug bites remain and have a purple-reddish appearance, indicating stagnant, inflamed conditions of the blood. Vaginal dryness is also possible. It is a remedy for infertility because it eliminates stagnant blood from the uterus and increases secretion. Stagnant blood in the lower spine and pelvis. Cancer when there is a black spot in the middle of the tongue.
A friend of mine had cancer of the colon, removed by surgery. She had a big black spot in the middle of the tongue. I gave her tincture of lady’s thumb and the spot disappeared. She did many other things as well. Neither the spot nor the cancer has returned. That was five years ago.

## Potentilla anserina. Silverweed.

The potentilla clan has properties similar to those of their cousins in the rose family, the agrimonias and geums. Most of these plants are astringents for relaxed tissues that (ironically) are associated with a tense mental, emotional, or physical background. Dr. Bach taught us about the mental indications for agrimony and I have found that they are largely true for the potentillas as well: tension hidden behind a facade.
Silverweed contains tannins (up to 25%), various flavonoids and related structures, organic acids, resins, gums, and essential oils. Empirical experience in modern phytotherapy suggests that it is spasmolytic, but no pharmacological evidence for this has yet been found (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994). The name “silverweed” refers to the furred leaf. The short, fine hairs make it silver in appearance and are insulative, carrying the plant into frigid zones. Silverweed is native to Europe but extremely widely naturalized in temperate to subarctic locations, from northern Canada to New Zealand, Argentina, and Lapland.
“Country folk often call it Cramp Weed,” writes Dr. W. T. Fernie (1914, 472). “Silverweed tea is excellent to relieve cramps of the belly; and compresses, wrung out of a hot decoction of the herb, may at the same time be helpfully applied over the seat of the cramps.” Maria Treben included silverweed among her small but potent group of healing herbs. Robert Dale Rogers, writing in northern Alberta, also speaks of it highly. It is the Potentilla of choice for the German E Commission. William Salmon (1710) classified it as cool and dry in the second degree.

### TASTE:

(roots) sweet, cool, and astringent
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
COMPLEXION
Pigmentation mask over cheeks and bridge of nose.
DIGESTION
Gums; spongy and bleeding; scorbutic; loose teeth.
Mouth and throat; sores and ulcerations; relaxed uvula (gargle).
Esophageal and stomach ulcers.
Nervous stomach.
Cramping of the stomach and intestines.
Diarrhea and constipation alternating.
Diarrhea, in children (root, combine with blueberry leaf and Irish moss).
Dysentery.
Hemorrhoids, bleeding (external).
Gallbladder; chronic inflammation; jaundice.
KIDNEYS
Gravel and stone.
FEMALE
Menstruation: dysmenorrhea of spastic origin.
Leucorrhea, yeast, or bacterial infection (douche).
FEVER
Inflammation and fever caused by infection.
Intermittent fever (leaves).
SKIN
Acne, freckles, sunburn.
Shingles (in vinegar or wine).
Wounds, eczema.
Ulcers, external.
OTHER
Metabolic disorder: diabetes, goiter, obesity.
Lockjaw (tablespoon of the herb boiled in milk or water, drunk as hot as possible).
Epilepsy.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Infusion of the dried leaves and flowers: pour 1 cup of boiling water over 3 teaspoons, cover, and steep for 10 minutes. Dose: 1–3 cups/day. Decoction of the dried, chopped roots: Simmer 2 tablespoons in 1 pint of water for 20 minutes. Dose: 1–2 ounces, 1–3x/day. Tincture: 2–4 ml, 3x/day (Rogers).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Maria Treben, Robert Dale Rogers (1, 8, 18), Maude Grieve (2, 3, 4, 10, 11, 12, 16, 17, 22), Norman Grainger Bisset and Max Wichtl (13), Russian research (21), Juliette de Bairacli Levy (23), W. T. Fernie (1, 6, 10, 17).

## Potentilla erecta, P. tormentilla. Tormentil, Septfoil, Cinquefoil.

Tormentil is perhaps the most “fierce” looking of the potentillas. The leaves are deeply serrated so that they look like a tortured hand—though in this potentilla there are usually five main leaves and two small ones, so that it is sometimes called “septfoil” instead of “cinquefoil.” It is a northern European plant, naturalized to North America, so it was not named by the Greek and Latin authors, but it has all the properties of the cinquefoils of antiquity (including the magical ones). It is the most tortured looking of the whole family, and therefore bears the signature of stress and pain that runs through the potentillas, agrimonias, and geums. The root is an astringent, with some sugars, and a touch of mucilage.
Tormentil is “a very common wild plant, but very pretty, and of great virtue,” writes Sir John Hill (1740, 370). “The root is the part used, and it is best dried; it may be given in powder or decoction; the powder is excellent against the bleeding of the piles, bloody stools, and the overflowings of the menses.… The root is cordial as well as astringent, and operates a little by sweat. This decoction is therefore very serviceable in fevers, attended with purgings.” The leaves are also powerful.
Tormentil has the same psychological properties as agrimony, and can be used in the same way. Even the magical properties are similar: both are excellent for correcting problems having to do with hierarchical organizations (employer/employee problems) or interference in one’s work. This is in fact the single most reliable indication I know in herbalism: tormentil or agrimony has never failed in any instance I am aware of to resolve employment, boundary, or legal tensions. Place a leaf or spray some tincture on the documents, boundary, or work space involved. It removes “enchantments” designed to interfere with one’s work.
Beyond this, tormentil possesses all the medicinal properties listed under agrimony. However, it is more astringent and contains some mucilage, so it is more beneficial in diseases of the guts, for which it has long been used. In a medieval monastery in Scotland, famous for its medical cures, archeologists found the drainpipes full of tormentil mixed with worms. Dr. John Bastyr used tormentilla for all types of diarrhea, 60 drops, 2x/day (William Mitchell, 2003, 70).
One time I was out in the country visiting some friends and one of them had an asthmatic attack. She was tortured to capture her breath, a statement Dr. Bach made about agrimony. I looked out in the yard for some kind of remedy, found some Potentilla erecta, and gave it to my friend. It quickly relieved the attack—it did not cure the chronic asthma.
William Salmon (1710, 185) gives a use based on the doctrine of signatures: “If the hands be often washed therewith, and it is suffered every time to dry in of it self, without wiping, it will in a short time help the palsie, or shaking of them.”
Cinquefoil “is not much used, which is a pity, for it is very powerful,” writes Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1973, 48). “I value this herb very much.” She uses it in epilepsy and hysterical nervous disorders, syringed into the nostrils for sinus infections, externally on sore mouth and gums, as a gargle for inflamed and ulcerated throats, and on wounds “of all kinds.”

## Primula veris. Primrose, Cowslip, Heaven’s Keys.

The common primrose “is the most widely known of our English wild flowers,” writes Dr. Fernie (1914, 413), “and appears in the Spring as its earliest herald,” while we in America are reduced to watching it grow in pots in the early spring. The name primula comes from the Latin primus, first. It is native to low ground in northern Europe, where it has a reputation as a medicine. It is little used in North America. Primula contains flavonoids in the flowers, saponins in the rhizomes, and is astringent throughout.
Primula is a nervine, “being extremely calming to the nervous system” (Juliette de Bairacli Levy, 1973, 55). It is traditionally used as a sedative for spasmodic coughing where there is sensitivity and pain. It is also beneficial for spasms in the GI tract, nervous excitability, hyperactivity, restlessness, irritability, headache, migraine, panic attacks, anxiety, insomnia, tremors, chorea, and convulsions. “Cow Slip gives beauty and gloss to the skin; removes spots, blemishes, or wrinkles; sun burn; freckles; headaches caused by heat and wind; vertigo; frenzy; falling sickness; convulsions; cramps and nervous conditions; palsy and pains in the back or bladder, and urine” (Chalid Ottway, 1933, 153).

### TASTE:

(herb) bitter

### TISSUE STATES:

excitation, constriction

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Nervous excitability, hyperactivity, restlessness, irritability, anxiety, insomnia, panic attacks (flowers, herb).
Migraine, headache; caused by nervousness, heat, and wind (flowers, herb).
Vertigo, frenzy, epilepsy (flowers, herb).
Colds, catarrh, tracheitis, bronchitis, asthma, pertussis (dried rhizome).
Pains in the bladder, on urination (flowers, herb).
Fibromyalgia, lumbago, sciatica (flowers, herb).
Skin spots, blemishes, freckles, wrinkles (herb).
Sunburn (flowers, herb).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

“If you allow leaves or stalks to be mixed in with the flowerheads this will make the taste more bitter, and you will have to use more sugar” (Donald Law, 1973, 112). Infusion of the flower heads: take a small cupful sweetened with honey, morning and night. “In a concentrated form, cowslip is an anaesthetic” (Juliette de Bairacli Levy). Flowers in infusion: 5–6 parts to 100 parts of water (W. T. Fernie). Soak 1 pound of cowslip herb in 2 quarts of white wine for 48 hours, filter, and add 2 pounds honey. 1 tablespoonful, 3x/day for adults, 1 teaspoonful for children (Chalid Ottway).

### CAUTIONS:

Cultivated varieties can cause dermatitis (Fernie).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, W. T. Fernie (1, 2, 4, 6, 7), Chalid Ottway (1, 2, 3, 5, 6, 7, 8), Norman Grainger Bisset and Max Wichtl (4), Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1, 2, 3).

## Propolis. Bee Secretion.

Bees gather resins from tree buds (especially poplars), regurgitate it, and use it as a cement in the construction of their hive. Propolis also has an antibiotic and antiviral effect, so that as bees enter and leave the hive they are dusted with a protectant to keep the hive safe from contagion.
Adopted into human use, it is said to be a “natural antibiotic.” However, I find that the important application is in dust and mold allergy, for which it is a great specific. It has a fine cleansing effect on the mouth and throat, being a specific for laryngitis. If it does not work almost instantly, it is not the right remedy. Descending the respiratory tract, it is a specific for a hot, raw bronchitis. “During the monsoon, when everything is wet and we would get a raw bronchitis 1 drop of propolis would stop it” (Julia Graves). Just a drop on the tongue disperses volatile oils through the nose, sinuses, throat, and bronchial tubes that has a soothing and antimicrobial effect. It has a similar helpful effect on the digestive mucosa and is useful for ulcers. Entering more deeply into the organism, propolis seems to have an immune-boosting effect, increasing white cell production and stimulating the thymus.

### TASTE:

pungent, bitter • warm • resinous

### TISSUE STATE:

depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Specific for dust and mold allergy.
Mouth and gums; bad breath, sores, ulcers.
Laryngitis; instantly produces the voice.
Tonsillitis.
Hot, raw bronchitis; pneumonia.
Gastric and duodenal ulcers.
Arteriosclerosis, high blood pressure.
Prostate problems, cystitis.
Shingles, herpes.
Dry eczema, psoriasis.
Acne, abscesses, wounds, bruises, burns, warts, ulcers.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Tincture, 85% alcohol, 1 drop on the tongue, as needed; the propolis vaporizes and can be felt throughout the respiratory tract (Wood).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Matthew Wood (1, 2, 3, 5), Russian research (2–11).

## Prunella vulgaris. Self-Heal, All Heal.

Self-heal is a member of the mint family that is predominately mucilaginous. It is native to Europe and Asia, where it has long been used in traditional medicine. It is now widely naturalized in North America. It was adopted into practice by the American Indians but was little used in the herbal practice of lay and professional doctors in nineteenth- and early-twentieth-century America.
Self-heal leaves easily work up into a mucilage, so that it can be picked and placed on a wound. It draws out heat and infection. The old authors considered it to be one of the most reliable woundworts available. The flower looks like a mouth and throat, hence the traditional European usage in tonsillitis. An American Indian friend took this as a signature that it is a drawing agent (looks like the mouth of a “sucking doctor”). In traditional Chinese herbalism it is used to clear heat from the liver. The late William LeSassier considered it a very important remedy for building and nourishing the kidneys. It improves diuresis, not by actively pumping the kidneys, but by strengthening them. He used it especially when there was dental decay combined with kidney trouble. This is perhaps a condition caused by inability to deal with protein properly, resulting in excess protein waste products (urates) burdening the liver and the kidneys, precipitating with calcium to weaken teeth and bones. He preferred the traditional Chinese medicine explanation: the kidneys strengthen the bones and teeth.
Gerard (1633) says, “To be short, it serveth for the same that the Bugle serveth; and in the world there are not two better wound herbs, as hath often been proved.”

### TASTE:

moist • mucilaginous

### TISSUE STATES:

irritation, atrophy

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

People who do not feel like they will ever recover (flower essence).
Dental problems, tooth decay, with kidney weakness.
Sore throat.
Heat in the liver.
Kidney weakness.
Wounds, fresh or infected. Chop up the leaves into a mucilage, poultice until they fall off, put on more leaves until the wound is clean.
Skin discolorations and lesions; insect bites; eczema (external).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The flower tops are used in Chinese herbalism, the flowering herb in European herbalism. Infusion, tincture, oil, or salve.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (European, Chinese, Native American), Flower Essence Society (1), William LeSassier (2–6), Julia Graves (1, 7).

## Prunus americana, P. domestica. Wild Plum, Domestic Plum.

Plum is native to the New and Old Worlds. The genus has been cultivated (and crossed) for the perfection of their fruit, which are used fresh or dried in the form of prunes. The latter are, of course, a popular laxative. In other regards, however, plum (wild, cultivated, European, American, or cross-bred) is not used to a great extent in Western herbalism. Yet, plum is related to some important and useful remedies, and has given “hints of potential.”
Chalid Ottway (1933, 256), probably drawing on Middle Eastern sources, gives a short account of the remedy. He used the dried plum, or prune. “Plum is good for thirst; sick feeling; vomiting; stops bleeding; clears obstructions; cleans and heals cuts and wounds; kills and expels worms; sore mouth; burns; throat; breaks stones in kidney and eases passage of urine; cools the stomach.” He regards it as cooling and moistening, and under the dominion of Venus, which agrees with Culpeper.
“Lately a Chicago physician whilst officiating at a reformatory, found that the boys behaved themselves much better when taking prunes in their diet than at any other time. These act, he supposes, on certain organs which are the seats and centres of the passions” (W. T. Fernie, 1914, 479). Peach leaf and hawthorn, close relatives, are likewise sedative for children and the former is traditionally given by the Cherokees to children who need discipline. Fernie classifies plum as cooling and slightly laxative.
The late Georgia herbalist Tommie Bass used wild plum bark for one purpose, for which he considered it something of a specific—asthma. I saw a case of asthma that would not respond to any other remedy I tried except plum bark. In this case it was a cultivated European plum that worked best. The use of plum bark for asthma is quite established by tradition. Hildegard von Bingen (1998, 113) recommended plum bark or leaf for a “dry cough.”

### LITERATURE:

Hildegard von Bingen, W. T. Fernie, Chalid Ottway, and Tommie Bass.

## Prunus armeniaca. Apricot.

The name apricot is of Sumerian derivation, and appears in the most ancient herbals known. Apricot kernel and oil has been used in place of peach kernel or leaf and sweet almond oil, which it closely resembles in property.

## Prunus persica, Amygdalus persica. Peach.
