Scoliosis, if the problem is in the tendons.
OTHER
“Gangrenous tumor or cancer of the uterus.” 5 drops, 3x/day (Sinha).

### PREPARATION, CONTRAINDICATIONS, AND DOSAGE:

The leaves are collected in late summer; they can irritate the skin and mucosa. Tincture, 1–3 drops (Hall, Sinha). Not to be used during pregnancy.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Samuel Hahnemann (6, 15), Temple Hoyne (7, 11, 14, 17), Robert Cooper, homeopathic provings and clinical experience (3, 5, 9, 10, 12, 16), Dorothy Hall (1–4, 8, 13), Yadubir Sinha (19), Julia Graves (18).

## Salix alba. White Willow.

This is the European willow, now much cultivated and naturalized in the New World. Willow bark is famous as a source of salicylates, the “active ingredient” in aspirin. As such it is used for fever, headache, and arthritis. Unlike aspirin, the mix of salicylates and natural ingredients found in willow does not irritate the stomach and cause ulcers. White willow has been used for passive hemorrhages, atonic menstrual bleeding, loose stool, and chronic and curdy diarrhea. It is a Bach flower remedy. Properties are similar to those of Salix nigra (see the companion volume of The Earthwise Herbal on New World plants).

## Salvia officinalis. Sage.

Sage is a member of the mint family native to the Mediterranean region but much more widely grown in gardens. There are numerous varieties and species of Salvia, many of which have the same properties as the officinalis. The great white sage (Salvia apiana), native to California, can be used in place of garden sage. It is more stimulating and warming, but less astringent. On the uses of different sages it might be well to quote William Turner (1551, 275): “Dioscorides maketh but one kind of Sage, but … now are there found more kinds, the which, though they differ one from another much in roughness and smoothness, in greatness and smallness, and in diversity of colours, yet in my judgment do agree in one virtue and property.” The leaves are the part used in herbalism; they contain fixed and volatile oils (including thujone), tannins, and bitters.
Sage is astringent, oily, and slightly warming, so it acts on three tissue conditions—relaxation, atrophy, and depression—or a combination of them. It possesses virtues almost too numerous to comprehend under a single heading. Michael Moore (2003, 228) came to the same conclusion as I have about this genus. “Our sages are complex plants, with a variety of physiologic effects,” he writes, and he considers a complete rundown of constituents “hopeless.” When we have two herbalists as different as Michael Moore—a pharmacological genius, if I may term him such—and myself—an “energetic” herbalist—coming to the same odd conclusion, we see evidence that there is a great underlying bedrock of Truth in herbalism that can be discerned by experienced students of the field, no matter what their philosophy or background, because herbs are consistent—their properties do not change with the philosophy. However, as Moore points out, the properties of sage do change with the method of preparation. The hot tea brings out the aromatics and is therefore useful as a stimulant to sweating, salivation, and internal secretion. The cold tea will decrease secretions, including sweating, salivation, mucus production in mouth, throat, and lungs, and lactation—it is the great medicine for curbing milk production. The lukewarm tea is bacteriostatic and astringent, hence beneficial in sore throats.
Where do we start? Perhaps best with the most simple use of sage in cooking as an ingredient to render fatty meats more digestible. Because it contains small amounts of bitters and oils, sage mildly stimulates the gallbladder to greater activity, releasing bile to improve the digestion and absorption of fats and oils. Bile also facilitates the eliminative function of the bowels, while the increased uptake of fats and oils improves the lipid economy of the body. Lipid nutrition and elimination seem to be improved all the way to the cellular level. Steroids and sex hormones dependent on fats and oils are probably better produced and transported to do their work. The lipid and water content of the body is built up; hence sage combats drying and aging. The skin and the tendons are strengthened.
A keynote symptom indicating sage is withered, dry skin and withered tendons. When we go to find the tendons they seem to have disappeared. The skin is strengthened by sage to retain fluids; therefore, it is used in night sweats where the body is losing fluids by perspiration and for dry, withered skin. “In people with little tendency to sweat it will act to stimulate perspiration, and in those who sweat heavily, it will act as an antiperspirant” (Husemann and Wolff, 1982, 331).
Sage has an old reputation in gum and throat problems, canker sores, sore throat, swollen glands, and hoarseness. It is nearly a specific for a sore throat. Here its astringence comes into play. “It has an austerity upon the palate,” writes John Quincy (1736, 72), “and makes a very good gargarism; especially if it be sharpen’d with a little acid.” Sage tea, sweetened with honey and lemon, is an old specific for sore throat, and is quite delicious as a drink. Here the more stimulating Salvia apiana can be substituted if available.
Sage is sometimes used, like a number of astringents (bilberry, raspberry, bayberry), for cataract.
Sage tea is not uncommonly used for simple fever, especially where there is a sore throat. “It makes an excellent liquor, after the manner of tea, in fevers, and is mighty grateful and cooling, with the addition of a little lemon-juice, which with the red sage makes it of a delightful colour” (Quincy, 1736, 72). It acts deeply when there is stress on the nervous system from fever. It is an old remedy for fevers with reduction of consciousness, delirium, drowsiness, and lethargy. However, it is also used in the opposite kind of conditions, where there is restlessness, spasm, and convulsion associated with low, prostrating fevers. Sage can preserve the nervous system when it is under profound assault by viral, bacterial, and febrile diseases. It is indicated when these diseases cause lethargy and drowsiness, and is contraindicated in restlessness and tension associated with high fever and activity.
The use of sage in convulsion and shaking is established all the way back to the school of Salerno. This symptom is homeopathic, since prolonged use of sage can cause convulsions. Problems are prevented by not using it in large, prolonged doses.
One aspect of European sage that is little known is its capacity to thin the blood and remove coagulated blood. Here it shows the same sort of properties as the Chinese sage, S. miltiorrhiza. It reduces clotting in the blood vessels without releasing dangerous clots that would cause heart attacks, strokes, or thrombosis. I was first made aware of this fact from the lecture notes of Eva Graf (1978). Subsequently I have found S. officinalis serviceable in a number of cases where there was a dangerous coagulation of blood. One individual had clots on his arms where his father grabbed him to save him from being swept out to sea forty years previously. These and newer, life-threatening clots were both removed. A woman with very complex problems didn’t want to take blood thinning drugs because of the side effects. Sage served the purpose. She also found that it removed a lipoma, when administered externally—here the affinity to oil processes is shown. Another woman with a hemangioma had dark, prominent, unsightly veins that were greatly reduced in prominence by the external use of sage. Eva Graf used sage when veins were prominent, dark, and prone to ulceration and hemorrhage.
It is likely that sage has an influence on the endocrine system. It reduces appetite, sexual desire, and lactation, which are controlled by the hypothalamus. Because the hypothalamus is more or less the master gland of the endocrine cascade, reading the blood to alter hormonal secretions, sage must therefore act deeply on the endocrine system, and thus on the regulation of temperature and liquids in the body, as well as reproduction. Anthony Godfrey, ND, of Toronto, comments that sage “regulates virtually all fluids—blood, lymph, milk, sweat, urine—to maintain a healthy balance.”
Alabama herbalist Phyllis Light tells us of a very important hormonal usage for sage. She says that it is specific for the transition from fertility levels of estrogen to post-menopausal levels. In other words, it helps the adrenal cortex take over the manufacture of sexual hormones as the gonads atrophy. I have often used this valuable insight with success many times. It is specific for drying out at menopause, or andropause. Look for drying out of the tendons and skin.
Herbalist Amy Mynel provided one of my favorite case histories for sage. A thirty-six-year-old man, thin and elegant, was always thirsty, drinking water, but drying up. “I feel like I’m turning into an old man,” he said. Very fine gravel was passing in the urine. Dry, withering, and soreness in the penis. Tendons tight, dry, rigid, tense. Copious, very frequent, clear urination. Liquorice and sage were given. After two months, no gravel was passing, urination was normal, tissue was more flexible and less dry, and he felt much better.
While Samuel Thomson (1841) was on Nantucket Island a raging intestinal fever broke out that caused an epidemic of encephalitis in children. The last remaining child in a family that had lost four children already pleaded with her parents to let the herb doctor treat her, rather than the others, who had failed. By the time he arrived she was unconscious, but he brought her safely to recovery with infusion of sage leaf.

### TASTE:

pungent • warm • oily, astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

relaxation, atrophy, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Older persons with dryness of the skin, hair, mucosa, tendons; drying up of secretions.
Weak, debilitated persons, sensitive persons, children, old people; prone to colds, flu, coughs, which begin with dryness, soreness of the throat; with weak digestive powers, bloating, and flatulence.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Mental lethargy, depression, melancholy, poor concentration, loss of memory; “dull and heavy spirit” (Matthioli).
Typhoid and brain fevers with nervous tension, restlessness, insomnia or drowsiness, lethargy, unconsciousness.
HEAD
Loss of hair, fading hair color, dandruff (external rinse).
Running sinuses, postnasal drip, colds.
Gums, sore, bleeding, ulcerated, inflamed, receding; canker sores; inflammation of tongue.
RESPIRATION
Throat sore, dry, ticklish; sometimes descending into the lungs, resulting in dry, irritable cough, followed by secretion of mucus, which dries out, resulting in mucus difficult to raise; voice loss; weak and spasmodic cough reflexes.
Sore throat, swollen glands, tonsillitis, pain on swallowing, laryngitis (tea with honey).
Palliative for tuberculosis; with night sweats, emaciation, swollen glands.
Muscular tension and pain in the upper body, shoulders, and chest; associated with lung conditions; after continuous coughing.
DIGESTION
Appetite feeble; stomach “weak and windy” (small doses) (Child).
Atonic digestive mucosa with increased secretion, or lack of secretion.
Indigestion with weakness and debility; difficulty digesting fats and oils, gas.
Stomach dry, with bloating and flatulence.
Pain in the side.
Parasites (powdered leaves, taken freely in molasses).
Chronic diarrhea, dysentery, with debility.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Increased secretion of urine with low specific gravity; with debility; cystitis.
FEMALE
Excessive or deficient sexual desire; infertility; dryness, lack of vaginal secretion; lack of bleeding, amenorrhea.
Pregnancy: morning sickness.
Postpartum: mother’s grief when the baby dies.
Lactation: dries up milk; contraindicated during lactation.
Breast cysts.
Menopause: helps the body switch from ovarian, fertility levels of estrogen to adrenal, maintenance levels; menopausal hot flashes and night sweats.
MALE
Excessive or deficient sexual desire, sexual debility, spermatorrhea.
SKIN
Excessive, debilitating perspiration, skin soft and relaxed, with enfeebled circulation to the extremities, which are cold; feet sweat and are cold. Contraindicated when the night sweat is preceded by hectic fever and dry, harsh skin.
Skin dry, withered, like a sage leaf; lichenification.
Insect bites; wounds, bleeding.
Cold sores, herpes.
Lipoma.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Tendons dry, weak, malnourished, flaccid, or tight.
Pains in limbs and joints; arthritis; worse from cold and damp.
Epilepsy, palsy, shaking, trembling.
CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM AND BLOOD
Deterioration of the veins; bleeding, varicosities; in diabetes and other diseases (cf. Chinese red sage, Salvia miltiorrhiza).
Blood stagnation; removes clots and coagulation without causing strokes, etc.; improves circulation and cardiovascular function.
Veins blue/black.
Hemangioma (cf. Sambucus, Aesculus hippocastanum).
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Gallbladder colic.
FEVER
Brain fever.
Flu; intermittent fever and chills.
Headache accompanying measles and eruptive fever.
Hectic fever; exhausting night sweats (fast morning and night, drink cold sage tea continually).
OTHER
Gravel.
Yeast infection.
Ulcers.
Excessive salivation.
Pregnancy: to prevent premature delivery (Agrippa von Nettesheim).
Insomnia; “a cupful of the tea taken at bedtime” (George Slack).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

A pinch of the fresh or dried leaf (1–3 grams) is infused in a cup of steaming water. Add honey and lemon for flavor. The tincture or fluid extract of the fresh or dried leaves can be used in doses of 3–30 drops, 1–3x/day.

### CAUTIONS:

Sage is not recommended for prolonged dosage (longer than three weeks). It probably also has a too-drying effect when used for prolonged periods. Even in short duration, sage should not be used during lactation, unless the mother desires to dry up the milk.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Sage is not appropriate in active, excited conditions.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1–10, 13–21, 23, 26, 27, 28, 29, 33, 34), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (2, 3), Samuel Thomson (4, 40), Tommie Bass (5), Matthew Wood (1, 4, 6, 19, 24–27-confirmed, 29, 30, 36-confirmed, 37, 38, 39), William LeSassier (11), Elena Ixcot (23), Phyllis Light (24), O. Phelps Brown (27), John Scudder (26), William Coles (27), John William Fyfe, Eva Graf (34–36), Anthony Godfrey, Louise Tenney (3, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 16, 40, 41, 42, 44, 45), William T. Fernie (41, 46, 48), Lydia Child (12, 17, 42, 43), Agrippa von Nettesheim (47), George Slack (48).

## Salvia rubrum. Red Sage.

The English herbalists use red sage somewhat differently from ordinary sage. Richard Hool (quoted by William Webb, 1916, 217) writes, “It is useful for fevers of every kind, measles, sore throat, quinsy, laryngitis, diphtheria; obstructed menstruation (amenorrhea), flooding (menorrhagia); colds, coughs, bronchitis, and inflammation of the lungs; constipation, indigestion, biliousness and liver complaints. Red sage is the single remedy we know for quinsy, diphtheria, and laryngitis. The infusion of one ounce to a pint of boiling water can be used in these conditions both as a medicine and as a gargle. The dose, internally, is from one wineglassful to half a tea cupful as often as required, or according to the severity of the case to be treated.
“It is used as a poultice for sore breasts or other inflamed gatherings. It allays nervous excitement, and is considered a useful medicine in typhoid fever given in small and frequently repeated doses. A cup of the strong infusion is good to relieve nervous headache, and when it is inveterate combine with wood betony and add a few drops of tincture avena if available.”

## Sambucus nigra, S. canadensis. Elder.

Sambucus nigra is the black elder native to Europe, whereas S. canadensis is the black-berried elder native to North America. They can be used interchangeably, though the Old World species possesses a more subtle flavor and yields the better wine. S. racemosa, commonly found in North America, should not be used. Elder is one of the two or three primal remedies of the European tradition. Charlemagne (c. 800 CE) decreed that an elder be planted in every yard in his realm, to be a “medicine cabinet” ready for immediate use. Thus it became a staple of peasant medicine in Europe. Elder was perfectly suited to humoral medicine since it opens all the channels—causing diaphoresis, emesis, purgation, diuresis, expectoration, and menstruation—also building and detoxifying the blood.
Elder was sometimes called the “Hylde Moer” or “Elder Mother.” It was said that the “Little Elder Mother” dwells in the tree, or her dominion can be entered through the tree. The Elder Mother is the “Queen of the Underworld” (a different dimension associated with the earth) or “Faery.” Thus, it is considered perilous to fall asleep under an elder tree, and cradles were never to be made from its wood, because kidnapping human souls is a faery sport. The result is a “changeling,” what we would now call an autistic child.
It was believed that Christ was crucified on elder wood. After his death he went down into the Underworld. Judas was believed to have hung himself on an elder tree. I have been informed that elder does grow all about the temple mount in Jerusalem, a city as close to the Underworld, perhaps, as it is to God.
In Iceland, where the faeries are still taken very seriously, they are known as the “huldafolk,” meaning “hidden people.” Thus, the name elder is derived from hulda, not eld (fire), as given in most sources (cf. Grieve, 1931). The name Sambucus, on the other hand, comes from the sambuka, or pan-pipes made from its hollow stems. It is said that the most haunting music is made from elder wood. So the tree is also associated with Pan, the Lord of the Underworld.
The North American Indians placed offerings by each plant when picking them for use, whereas the medieval European peasants addressed the entire green world each spring by placing offerings to the Little Elder Mother at the foot of the elder in hopes of good medicine and crops. Later, at the feast of the Assumption of Mary (August 15), the herbs were blessed by the priest and the Queen of Heaven. Today people take it for granted that they can use natural resources without showing gratitude.
All parts of the elder are used in different ways, though the flowers and berries can be used to represent all the major uses in a slow-acting, safe presentation. The berries contain flavonoids, cyanogens, and some volatile oils. The flowers contain volatile oils of a buttery nature (due to the presence of fatty acids), coumarins, bitters, flavonoids, sterols, mucilage, and tannins (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994, 449, 447). Thus, elder flower is cooling, immune-building, slightly blood-thinning, soothing, mildly stimulating (but not very warming), and slightly tonic (alterative and astringent). The numerous components almost justify the ancient idea that elder can do anything. Elder is a member of the honeysuckle family, which includes several cooling agents (Lonicera, Viburnum trilobum).
Elder flowers, used fresh, are a stimulating diaphoretic, bringing the blood to the surface, strengthening the periphery, and bringing forth a sweat in pale, bluish persons with weak peripheral circulation. The dried flowers are more active as a sedative diaphoretic, reducing heat, opening the pores, and dispersing the blood in people with dry, irritable, red skin of the cheeks of the face and “cheeky” parts of the body. There are tendencies to fever and eczema from the closed pores. Both actions—stimulating and sedative—are present together and therefore this plant has a normalizing action.
Sambucus is constitutionally indicated in persons with dry, red, irritable skin of the cheeks with a blue cast across the nose or by the eyes, or prominent blue veins or blue, swollen edema. In newborn infants when the skin is dry, closed, red, and irritated and there is a blue swelling across the root of the nose. Another indication is full, slightly puffy skin, mottled red, blue, and white, especially visible on the arms and legs. Elder opens all the tubes of the body (skin, lungs, colon, kidneys, blood vessels), improving oxygenation, blood flow, perspiration, and elimination by the colon and kidneys (indications from acupuncturist Chris Hafner).
Elder is relaxant, removing spasmodic cough, spasm in the stomach, and intestinal colic. It is an excellent remedy for croup and spasmodic coughing in children with the appropriate complexion. The child wakes in the night, can’t breath, turns blue, or lies awake at night with the croup. It is remedial for convulsions and epilepsy. The leaves are used fresh or dried externally on a boil to disperse heat and regulate blood flow. The young leaves have antifungal properties (external use).
The bark was used to make an emetic drink. A large draught was taken all at once. Taken slowly, it acts through the lymph, colon, and kidneys, causing movement of fluids, loosening of the stool, and urination. In the old sources, such as Nicholas Culpeper, it is said that elder bark cut away upward is emetic; scraped downward is cathartic. Generally, these uses are more heroic and it is better to get the colon and kidneys working by taking a preparation of the flowers over a longer period than a large draught of the semi-toxic bark.
The berries have much the same properties as the flowers, but they are also considered to be tonic and blood-building. Hence, they are used in anemia. “The juice of the berries, boiled down with a little sugar, or by some wholly without; and this, when it comes to the consistence of honey, is the famous rob of elder, good in colds and sore throats” (John Hill, 1740, 165). In large doses, this juice is purgative (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994, 450).
Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1973, 140) reports that elder sticks can be used to make lightweight splints for broken bones. This is an old gypsy remedy, still in use “on sheep farms in the West Country.” She gives an extensive account of the advantages of this method.
Elder was so widely used at one time that Dr. Martin Blochwitz, of Haina, wrote a book, Anatomia Sambuci (1629), entirely about the folk and professional uses of the little tree. It remained in print in various translations, including the English, as late as 1675. Extracts from the original text are quoted by me in The Book of Herbal Wisdom.
Modern research has shown that elderberry kills many kinds of influenzal viruses and is effective in shortening bouts of influenza.

### TASTE:

(dry flowers) sweet, sour, slightly acrid • moist, cool • sedative, stimulating

### TISSUE STATES:

(dry flowers) irritation, constriction, depression (berries) irritation, constriction, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
The great infant remedy; for infants with blue, pale swelling across the nose, red, dry, irritated skin of the cheeks; “opens the tubes and pores,” lubricates the skin, improved respiration, digestion, and kidney function.
Children with marbling of the skin and respiratory problems; croup, ears full, respiratory problems, skin problems, eczema.
Full bodied persons, especially women, with red-blue marbling of the flesh on the arms and legs; with colic, water retention under the skin, lower back pain.
Old persons with edema; blue, swollen ankles.
Conditions where there are both stagnant fluids and blood, hence, pale, blue swelling; or where the skin is dry, harsh, red.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Children with strong, sanguine personalities, who pay little attention to directions, “poorly socialized,” classified as hyperactive.
RESPIRATION
Congestion of the respiratory tract with restriction of breathing.
Upper respiratory system, postnasal drip, allergic shiners under eyes.
Wakes at night, can’t breath, turns blue.
DIGESTION
Intestinal colic and constipation.
LYMPH/IMMUNE
Swollen spleen (berries).
CARDIOVASCULAR AND BLOOD
Anemia (berries).
Hemangioma (cf. Salvia, Aesculus hippocastanum, Plantago).
FEMALE
Menstruation problems; fibroids.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Dropsy following scarlet fever and other eruptive fevers.
SKIN
Tissues full, flabby, as if containing water.
Diseases of the skin, when the tissues seem full; epidermis separates and there is abundant serous discharge that forms crusts (weeping eczema).
Indolent ulcers.
Eczema; skin harsh, dry, red.
Tinea, ringworm, rashes, scabies (young leaves).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Sprained wrists and ankles; blue and swollen.
Tight tendons.
FEVER
Fever; harsh, dry, red, irritated skin; of cheeks, arms.
Influenza, achy feeling.
West Nile virus (a cup of elderberry wine, 3x/day).
Immunity in winter; to improve (6 berries in water, once a day, in the fall).
EXTERNAL
Poison ivy, poison oak (Sambucus nigra, Bastyr).

### PREPARATION, TOXICITY, AND DOSAGE:

Black elder (Sambucus nigra) and Canadian elder (Sambucus canadensis) can be used interchangeably. Red elder (Sambucus racemosa) grows on uplands in North America, flowers in the spring, and produces red berries. All the elders are somewhat toxic (large doses are cathartic) but the last variety has actually killed children making blow guns from the stalks and should not be used in herbal medicine. Many parts of elder have been used, but the flowers are safest, best tasting, and reflect the overall properties of the plant well. They are collected in midsummer (Sambucus canadensis) and dried for use as an infusion or extracted in alcohol or used fresh. The berries are also good tasting and commonly used in herbal commerce. An infused tea of elder flower (sometimes with lime flowers) is used in fever (Bisset and Wichtl). Dose of the tincture: 10–20 drops in 4 ounces of water (Rolla Thomas).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Martin Blochwitz (Anatomia Sambuci, 1629), Samuel Hahnemann (homeopathic provings: 9), Chris Hafner (1, 4, 5), Julia Graves (1-confirmed, 12), Aviva Romm (8), Kim Dudley (13), Dorothy Hall (10), Matthew Wood (1-confirmed, 2, 3, 5-confirmed, 6, 21–23, 25: reported from rural Minnesota), Jennifer Tucker (26), Rolla Thomas (16, 17, 18), John Bastyr (27).

## Sanguisorba officinalis. Salad Burnet.

Salad burnet, a member of the rose family, has a dainty and beautiful flower. The leaf tastes like cucumber and is somewhat astringent. It is used primarily as an astringent on mucosa of the respiratory, gastrointestinal, and genitourinary tract, in gum disease, diarrhea, ulcerative colitis, irritable bowel syndrome, hemorrhoids, excess menstrual bleeding, and erosion of the cervix. Also used externally on wounds. Research shows that the root contains tannins, flavonoids, saponins, and volatile oils. Neil (1998, 31) recommends the wild over the garden variety in efficacy.

## Sanicula europaea. Sanicle.

This common European wildflower vies for consideration as one of the most important but completely forgotten woundworts of the Middle Ages, as Peter Holmes would amusingly express the situation. It is a member of the Apiaceae family. Sanicula contains bitters, saponins, tannins, volatile oils, calcium, silica, and allantoin. Since it does not grow in my area I must rely upon the European authors for information about its healing properties. Friedrich Husemann and Otto Wolff (1982, 336) compare it to arnica as a general woundwort. Richard Hool (William Webb, 1916, 212) compares it to coltsfoot, comfrey, marshmallow, and mullein, so he thinks of it more as a mucilage or emollient.
“The sanicle is popularly employed in Germany and France as a remedy for profuse bleeding from the lungs, bowels, womb, and urinary organs; also for the staying of dysenteric diarrhea. The fresh juice of the herb may be given in tablespoonful doses” (W. T. Fernie, 1914, 467). Its healing property extends into the inner body, where it acts on the viscera or more vegetative functions, and less on the nervous system, so much the focus in other umbelliferae. “In cases of diseases of the chest and lungs, spitting of blood, scrofula, ulcers and tumors, or internal abscesses and ulcerations, there is no plant superior to wood sanicle. It is useful in sore or ulcerated mouth and throat” (Hool, quoted by Webb, 1916, 212). Hool combines it with marshmallow and mullein for ulceration of the lungs or stomach.
“In injuries to the physical body, it shows the etheric body [vegetative, cellular processes] the right paths by which it can counteract the inflammation and the chaotic tendency of the blood process with its formative forces” (Husemann and Wolff, 1982, 337).

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Inflammation of mucus surfaces.
Aphthous ulcers (canker sores), tonsillitis, gingivitis.
Catarrhal conditions of the respiratory tract; cough, chronic bronchitis, spitting blood, pneumonia, tuberculosis.
Gastroenteritis, ulcers, inflammation, diarrhea, dysentery, hemorrhoids, flatulence.
Metritis, menorrhagia, vaginal discharge.
Intermittent fevers.
Skin ulcers, burns, cuts, wounds.
Varicose veins.
Bruises.
Erysipelas, rashes.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

“Used alone, one ounce of the dried herb or root, simmered in 1 1/2 pints of water down to one pint, and strained, can be taken in wineglassful doses four or five times a day as a general strengthener of the bronchial tubes and lungs” (Hool).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Friedrich Husemann and Otto Wolff, Richard Hool, W. T. Fernie.

## Saponaria officinalis. Soapwort.

This common native of Europe, widely naturalized in temperate regions, is a member of the clove family. It contains high levels of saponins and like other such plants it is used as an expectorant to remove catarrh from the upper respiratory tract. Traditionally, it was also used for skin conditions, rheumatism, and urinary tract problems (Bisset and Wichtl, 1994, 453).

## Satureja hortensis. Summer Savory.

“There be two kinds of Savorie, the one that endureth winter & is of long continuance: the other is an annual or yearly plant, that perisheth at the time when it hath perfected his seede, and must be sowen againe the next yeare, which we call Sommer Savorie, or Savorie of a year” (John Gerard, 1633). The savories are small mints used in European medicine. They are warm and relaxing for the respiratory and gastrointestinal tracts. They have a long history of use in colds, respiratory infections, bronchitis, asthma, gastritis, and colic of the stomach and intestines. They contain volatile oils (including thymol) and some tannins. Although very similar, they are not entirely interchangeable. Summer savory is more sweet and less hot, whereas winter savory is more hot and drying.
John Gerard (1633) writes, “Sommer Savorie is not full so hot as Winter Savorie, and therefore saith Dioscorides, more fit to be used in medicine: it maketh thin, and doth marvellously prevail against winde: therefore it is with good success boiled and eaten with beanes, eason, and other windie pulses, yea if it be applied to the belly in fomentation, it foorthwith helpth the mother, proceeding of winde”—that is, uterine cramping.
Summer savory is used as a warming and nourishing stimulant for conditions of asthenia, with deficiency of the endocrine, adrenocortical, and sympathetic systems. It is a stimulant to the cerebral cortex (useful in dementia), to the adrenal cortex, the ovaries and testicles (tardy development, sterility, impotence, frigidity), and “one of the strongest essences for building up a weak immune system” (Dan Kenner and Yves Requena, 2001, 235).

### TASTE:

pungent and sweet

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, atrophy, tension
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Asthenia.
Retarded development.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Dementia.
RESPIRATION
Respiratory infections.
Bronchitis.
Asthma.
DIGESTION
Chronic gastritis.
Spasm of stomach and intestines.
Chronic diarrhea.
Chronic colitis.
SEXUAL SYSTEM
Stimulates the testicles and ovaries; sterility, impotence, frigidity, developmental retardation.
OTHER
Mycosis, “most types.”

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (1–12).

## Satureja montana. Winter Savory.

“Winter Savory will grow either from seed or slips. It is a woodier and more bushy plant than the Summer Savory, and flowers contentedly among the stones of a rockery. Thus have I seen it in the Old Physic Garden, Chelsea. Gardeners still have faith in the virtue of its leaves and sprigs to cure wasp and bee stings” (Frances A. Bardswell, The Herb Garden, 1911, quoted by W. T. Fernie, 1914).
John Gerard (1633) writes, “Winter Savorie is of temperature hot and drie in the third degree, it maketh thinne, cureth and clenseth the passages, to be brief, it is altogether of like vertu with Time.” It is more pungent and hot, whereas summer savory is sweeter, and therefore more nutritive. Because it is hot and dry in the third degree, winter savory is used to warm up the interior to drive impurities to the surface, thin fluids and toxins to drive them out, and open the pores to remove them. It is applicable in cold, moist conditions where there may even be putrefaction. Thus, it is indeed much like thyme.

### TASTE:

pungent and astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, relaxation

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, John Gerard, Frances A. Bardswell.

## Silybum marianum, Carduus marianus. Milk Thistle, St. Mary’s Thistle.

The older literature uses the name Carduus marianus. This is still preferred in homeopathy and has recently been resuscitated in botanical literature as well. Milk thistle has a long history of use in European folk medicine. It was used for a hot, swollen liver or spleen, jaundice, respiratory problems associated with a congested liver, to break and expel kidney stones, increase urination, dispel dropsy, and cleanse the blood.
After a period of obscurity the use of milk thistle was revived in the early nineteenth century by Dr. J. G. Rademacher. He was the first to determine that the active ingredients were found in the seed coat. His work was translated into English and his indications were enlarged upon in the late nineteenth century by J. Compton Burnett. Dr. Windelband of Berlin recorded 145 out of 190 cases of varicose veins cured by its use. This application was further developed by the American eclectics.
In the twentieth century the active ingredients of Silybum were analyzed and it was found to contain antidotes to powerful hepatotoxins. It is a specific in the liver damaged by poisons such as Amanita verna, A. phalloides, or alcoholism. It has been reintroduced through modern phytotherapy. However, we must turn to the older literature for the specific indications and a sense of the context within which it best operates.
The sweet, oily seed indicates that milk thistle will be suited to dry cases, which is indeed what we find. Dry skin, hard, swollen, knotty veins, and constipation with dry, hard stool are often present. An excellent account of the traditional uses of milk thistle is given by Yadubir Sinha, an Indian homeopath who uses herbal mother tinctures. “Carduus acts principally on the liver. It is beneficial in hepatic congestion due to cold or alcoholic drinks. When the action of the liver is sluggish, the secretion of bile is insufficient and causes jaundice, the bowels constipation, motion clay coloured and the tongue foul with a feeling of languor and general debility. It works like a magic in cirrhosis of the liver due to alcoholic habits and cures all the complaints dependent on it, viz.: gastric derangements, vomiting of blood, jaundice, scanty urination, dropsy, etc. It removes all ailments such as torpidity and induration of liver, gall stone colic, inflammation of the hepatic region. In any disease if constipation is prominent, give Carduus 1 [first homeopathic dilution, 10% of mother tincture] in one drop doses intercurrently with the indicated remedy. It will give tone to the liver, remove constipation and aid in rapid cure of the case.
“Carduus exerts a curative action also on the spleen, in conditions similar to those of the liver. The diseases of the two organs are concurrent. In such cases Carduus should be alternated with Ceanothus Americanus 1 [mother tincture].” Both these organs are much dependent on the portal vein and the venous circulation in general. This remedy acts strongly on enlarged and hardened varicose veins. The eclectics speak of the arteries as being ropey. “Chronic congestion and cirrhosis of liver give rise to piles. In these conditions Carduus should be administered.”
Rademacher first discovered the utility of Carduus in cases where there was serious lung disease adjacent to serious liver disease—probably abscesses on the liver discharging through a fistula into the lungs—a condition seen before the advent of antibiotics.
“Dr. Ambers advocates for the use of Chelidonium 1 and Dr. Birjosky lays stress on the use of Carduus 1 in gall-stone colic and affirms that constant use of Carduus prevents recurrence of gall-stone colic and formation of bile-stones and thereby cures gall-stone colic permanently.”

### TASTE:

sweet • oily

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Dull, heavy head, despondency, with coated tongue, lack of appetite, nausea.
Asthma.
Purulent discharge from the lungs, with adjacent swollen liver.
Liver swollen, sore, painful; jaundice.
Liver damage from alcoholism, especially beer.
Influenza with liver complications; sudden onset, chills, diarrhea (cf. Cnicus).
Specific antidote to poisoning with Amanita verna and related mushrooms.
Gallbladder swollen, painful; gallstone colic.
Edema associated with liver and kidney problems.
Constipation; stool hard, knotty, difficult to expel, black, hard, like balls, in knots, like clay, without bilious coloring.
Hemorrhoids, bleeding, burning.
Varicose veins, hard, rope-like.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Tincture, fluid extract. In emergency (mushroom poisoning) give continually in small amounts. It acts incredibly quickly. In chronic cases “give it for a long time as it acts slowly,” 2–5 drops, 3–4x/day (Petersen, 1905, 69).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, J. G. Rademacher (3), J. Compton Burnett, Finley Ellingwood, Rudolf Weiss, Yadubir Sinha.

## Solidago canadensis, S. virga-aurea. Goldenrod.

Goldenrod is an extremely widespread and common plant in the northern hemisphere. The New World contains somewhere between forty and sixty species, depending on who is doing the counting, whereas Europe is home to a single native species, Solidago virga-aurea. The ubiquitous American representatives have now spread to Europe. Of the latter, the common Solidago canadensis is considered to be the officinal medicine. The European native is preferred in Europe. Most goldenrods seem to be interchangeable. Goldenrod contains flavonoids (including quercetin and rutin), tannins, triterpene saponins, bitters, and essential oils.
The root, leaf, and flower of the Solidago is predominately bitter and pungent, leading to the traditional use as a carminative to stimulate secretions from the stomach. The flavonoids make it cooling as well as stimulating or warming, so it has been used on infected wounds that need to be simultaneously cooled off and stimulated. The aromatic essential oils carry it into the respiratory sphere, where it is used for allergies or old, stuck cases after influenza has left a low-grade bronchial infection. It is particularly important as a kidney remedy, stimulating the kidneys to greater activity following stressful illnesses or even psychological stress. It acts somewhat like an alterative on the skin and scalp, a specific indication being scabs and sores on the scalp and legs. Due to the affinity to the kidneys it has a greater association with the legs—edema, skin sores—than the upper body. There is some history of use as a female medicine, both in Europe and North America, but these indications have not been fully developed.
“This herb is a very old and good kidney medicine,” commented Dr. J. G. Rademacher in the early nineteenth century. It was likewise used as a kidney remedy in North America. Rademacher used it in persons who were recovering from illness when the urine turned dark, exhaustion set in, and it was evident that the kidneys could not process the waste products from the disease. A similar picture is painted by our twentieth-century folk herbalist, Maria Treben. She relates how the kidneys help us work out our emotions and if they become exhausted they slow down, the back and feet are tired, and the urinary output is altered. This theme is further developed by Dr. John H. Clarke (1903), a homeopath—the uses are the same in homeopathy and herbalism. “The grand keynote of this remedy lies in the condition and the action of the kidneys and the quality of their secretions. Diseases arising from or complicated with defective action of the kidneys are very likely to be benefited by Solid. Pain and tenderness in kidney region; pains extending from kidneys to abdomen, bladder, and down limbs; urine dark, red-brown, scanty, thick, voided with difficulty; albumins, mucous, and phosphatic urine.” It helps the body excrete larger protein particles (7Song).
The exhausted kidneys of Solidago can go either way, toward a dry, irritable skin with dark, scanty urine (Matthew Wood) or toward swelling with copious, light urine (William LeSassier). Very often the skin on the legs is dry and scaly, with scattered scabs and crusts. The scalp is likewise affected. Goldenrod opens the pores of the skin on the legs and scalp, sometimes even inducing a temporary perspiration when first used.
Solidago is bitter, warm, and pungent, a combination ideally suited for use as a carminative—that is, for stimulating and increasing digestion. William LeSassier recommended it as a carminative tonic for a “cold stomach,” especially in the winter.
Goldenrod has produced and cured conjunctivitis. The symptoms are well described by William Boericke. It is indicated for reddened, glistening conjunctiva that look like the person had just stepped out of chlorinated water. It is excellent for many kinds of allergies where these symptoms appear—usually also with the “tired kidneys” and tired feet. It is a superlative remedy for cat allergy, either in small herbal or low homeopathic doses (Matthew Wood).

### TASTE:

(leaf) bitter, pungent

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, torpor, depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Tired, worn out, can’t process the issues of life, feet tired; “Where’s the nearest chair?”
Allergies; conjunctiva red, glazed; eyes watering, nose itching; specific for cat allergies.
Acne in sheets of small pimples on the face.
Enlarged, wet, boggy tongue.
“Cold stomach,” inactive digestion, flatulence after the meal.
Urine dark, scanty; or clear, copious.
Dry, scaly skin, or edema and swelling.
Old purulent conditions of the lungs, mucosa, and skin.
Exhausted, tired lower back, tired feet, exhausted kidneys.
After acute disease clears, when the urine gets dark and scanty, the kidneys are exhausted and incapable of completing the cure.
Bladder irritation, early stages, with bacterial infection, sweat.
Edema of the legs with sores and purulence.
Dry, scaly patches on the scalp and legs.
Old, inflamed, purulent, gangrenous wounds.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The roots, leaves, and flowers have all been used. The leaves are harvested before flowering. They are preferred as a stomach tonic. The flowers are harvested in the fall and extracted fresh in alcohol for use in respiratory and renal conditions. Dose: 1–3 drops, 1–3x/day for allergies. Larger doses can be used but can cause conjunctivitis. Solidago virga-aurea is officinal in Europe; S. canadensis in North America.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, homeopathic (William Boericke, J. H. Clarke), J. G. Rademacher (10), Maria Treben (1), Matthew Wood (1–3, 13), William LeSassier (4, 5, 11).

## Spiraea ulmaria. Meadowsweet.

Refer to the entry under the modern name, Filipendula ulmaria.

## Stachys officinalis. Wood Betony.

Refer to the entry under the old name, Betonica officinalis.

## Stachys palustris. Woundwort.

Woundwort is a rather coarse, ill-smelling mint that is native to roadsides and edges of fields near low ground. Native to Europe, it is naturalized in North America and utilized by the Indian people. Its introduction into medicine comes rather late and it is still not widely used—but it lives up to its name.
Members of this family had an ancient reputation as wound medicines, hence the common name. The fixation of this species as the officinal woundwort owes to a curious coincidence. John Gerard, a famous London surgeon and the author of The Great Herbal (1633), recounts how he was traveling to Kent to visit a friend when he happened upon a farm laborer who had a huge gash in his arm that was bleeding profusely. He had been cutting hay in a field with a scythe. Gerard offered to help, explaining that he was a surgeon from London. The country fellow immediately took a dislike to the city gentleman and proclaimed that he knew a better remedy and didn’t need the help of any high and mighty London surgeon. He went over to the edge of the field, grabbed some of this obscure plant, and put it on the cut. Gerard, who was already shocked by the display of bad manners, was even more shocked to see the bleeding stop almost immediately. Even the best medicine of the received tradition could not rival what he saw. Humbled, he inquired after the plant and was shown Stachys palustris. Up to that time this plant was simply known as a woundwort, but Gerard, to commemorate the event, named it “clown’s woundwort.” He thought the farm laborer was a clown for refusing help and being rude about it, but it is perhaps possible that the name could describe a famous surgeon who was quite full of himself. Experimentation showed that clown’s woundwort was far better than the standard medicine, made from lemon balm. Gerard gives several case histories where it healed quickly, including one where the trachea had been severed.

## Stellaria media. Chickweed.

Chickweed is native to moist, shady spots in yards and woods, often around the bottoms of trees. It is mild in flavor and is used as a mild salad green to counter the bitterness of other spring greens. In addition, it has long been utilized in folk medicine. It is one of those very simple and ubiquitous plants that can be applied to many conditions.
This innocuous little plant proves once again that Nature’s pharmacopoeia need not be poisonous and is always common and close at hand. In the most simple things we find the greatest virtue. Jethro Kloss writes: “It heals and soothes anything it comes in contact with.”
The properties of chickweed are so numerous that they are hard to define, but we may start with the observation that this moist, cool, shade-loving little green denizen is lubricating, nourishing, cooling, and antifebrile. William LeSassier said it is “first and foremost a nutritive.” It helps to assimilate nutrients. Chickens love it and it makes them very healthy, he observed. “As a poultice it can’t be beat.” It is applied externally on boils, rashes, outbreaks, and eczema for its cooling and cleansing properties. It is a cooling diuretic so it is beneficial in “any form of internal inflammation” (Kloss).
Chickweed not only subdues heat and lubricates dry conditions, but also regulates water levels and drives off excess dampness and fats. These actions show that it stimulates both sides of the metabolism, building and breaking down, not only through the liver but also through the endocrine system. Thus, it is used to lose weight, not just short-term water weight, but long-term deposits of fats. It is an outstanding remedy for lipomas (fatty tumors), as well as general weight loss—facts to which I can attest from personal experience. At the same time, chickweed acts deeply on the waters of the body, decongesting the lymphatics and clearing waters through the kidneys, driving off local pockets of water in the lungs or elsewhere, but also preserving and balancing the water levels in the body. In short, there is no area in the “alterative category” (liver, lymphatics, endocrine, kidneys, skin, intestines, lungs) it does not touch.
Because of this wide range of application, it is difficult to define specific indications, though the presence of lipoma and excess fat is always a good indication. Water regulation is another key, as Rosemary Gladstar pointed out in a lecture. “Chickweed is very powerful. It cures through nourishment, especially helping the kidneys to regulate water. People with kidney problems need a lot of emotional support and help. The hairiness on the leaves indicates that it preserves water.”

### TASTE:

moist, cool

### TISSUE STATES:

irritation, atrophy

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Thin, malnourished, weedy constitution.
Overweight, hypothyroidism, poor lipid metabolism, high cholesterol, arteriosclerosis.
Sore eyes (poultice).
Deafness.
Ulcerated, inflamed throat and mouth.
Bronchitis, pleurisy, coughs, colds, hoarseness, asthma.
Excessive appetite.
Inflammation or weakness of the stomach and bowels; colitis, diarrhea.
Constipation, complete obstruction.
Hemorrhoids.
Peritonitis (cf. Eupatorium maculatum).
Lactation: mastitis (internal and external).
Swollen testis (poultice).
Cellulite.
Rheumatism, arthritis.
External application on inflamed surfaces; scalds, burns, boils, abscesses, skin diseases, rashes, eczema, itching, erysipelas, burning and itching genitals, wounds.
Lipomas, fatty tumors, watery deposits.
Blood poisoning (internal and external).
Itching skin (salve).
Drawing agent for foreign bodies in the skin, with infection and pus formation (salve).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Dried or fresh, the leaves are prepared by infusion or decoction. Steep a heaping teaspoonful in just-boiled water for half an hour. For constipation, boil 3 heaping tablespoonfuls of the fresh herb in a quart of water, until a pint is left; take a cupful warm every 3 hours or more often (Kloss). Bathe the surface with the decoction or poultice; follow with a salve. External application may need to be persistent, for as long as six months, to bring about a cure (Richard Hool, 1922). The tincture is as good as the tea (Rosemary Gladstar).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Jethro Kloss (9), Richard Hool (1), Rosemary Gladstar, Maude Grieve (2), Lise Wolff (17), Dorothy Hall (20).

## Symphytum officinale. Comfrey.

Comfrey is one of the most famous of medicinal herbs, though a shadow has passed across its career in recent years. Discovery of the presence of pyrrolizidine alkaloids has added a warning to its use. These agents can be hurtful to the liver. Hence, comfrey has been banned in some countries and considered dangerous in most of the rest. However, some strains and parts of the plant do not contain the culprit and external use is still entirely safe. Comfrey is a member of the borage family native to Europe. The medicinal ingredients include allantoin (a cell proliferant), alkaloids (CNS activity), mucilage, tannins, sterols, resins, volatile oils, gums, and triterpenes.
This old European folk remedy has been shown to possess a substance, allantoin, which causes cell growth, hence the explanation for some of its remarkable healing properties. Comfrey stimulates growth when the system has been traumatized and is, perhaps, having trouble regenerating on its own. It is excellent for delayed bone-healing, abrasions and injuries to the skin and mucosa, and as a general rejuvenative. In addition, it is mucilaginous, mildly astringent, and slightly bitter, suiting its use as a tonic on the surfaces of the body. It tones and heals injured skin and mucosa. It also is a drawing agent and will often draw stagnant or toxic material out of injured joints, muscles, and tendons. It probably increases movement of fluids into the joints (which lie just beyond the range of direct blood circulation), and is perhaps regenerative to cartilage and bone. It has long been used to heal broken bones and repair injured joints.
As an astringent mucilage comfrey has been used to correct erosive discharges from the intestines, in diarrhea and dysentery, spitting of blood in tuberculosis, leucorrhea in weakly females, ardor of urine. The roots correct “salt sharp serum,” comments Samuel Henry (1814, 81).
There are several problems with comfrey. The pyrrolizidine alkaloids may be dangerous for persons with weak liver function, though most of this is speculative. The leaves and roots are strongly mucilaginous and slimy, but the leaf is better tolerated by the body and contains less pyrrolizidine. Another problem, seldom mentioned in the literature, is that comfrey causes exuberant growth, even overgrowth, of tissue, so that it can cause a callus on a broken bone or callus the skin. It causes growth “from the top down,” like goldenseal, rather than from the inside up, like yarrow, plantain, calendula, boneset, and St. John’s wort. Hence it needs to be worked with carefully and not exuberantly. In two instances I am aware of it caused the lips of the vagina to grow together in infant girls on which it was used for diaper rash, requiring surgical intervention. In another case it caused a torn tissue to form a tough coating without bringing the lips of the wound together, requiring subsequent surgery. I have never seen it used (by itself) to heal bone without causing an overgrowth or callus, even after a few days. One of my apprentices was holding the plant in her left hand and a chopping knife in her right; the next day her left hand was calloused.
The following rather interesting material is from a lost source (but see symptom number 5 below, from Dorothy Hall). Comfrey flower essence is beneficial for head injuries. It helps to “repattern the brain.” It is indicated in epilepsy, insomnia, long-standing rapid eye movement, and autism.

### TASTE:

mucilaginous, slightly bitter, astringent • cold, damp

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, relaxation

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Bloody discharges from weakened mucosa (lungs, intestines, bladder).
Odd blood diseases; abnormal white cell production; leukemias in children, thalassemia.
Delayed development and deformities.
Delayed puberty.
Brain injury, apparent death, no activity in brain (comfrey can revive).
Alzheimer’s.
Gum disease.
Allergies, hay fever, sinusitis.
Chronic cough; bleeding from the lungs; pleurisy, pneumonia, asthma, emphysema, tuberculosis.
Debilitated, chronic gastric mucosal irritation.
Digestive inflammation, colitis, diarrhea, ulceration, bloody stool.
Breast inflammation.
Kidney stones.
Repetitive use problems in joints, sprains, strains (poultice).
Back keeps going out of joint (Eva Graf); decreased bone density in the lower lumbar spine; chronic lumbosacral instability, where the vertebrae rotate out all the time, soreness of the fourth and fifth lumbar process (Brent Davis).
Severe spinal injury.
Arthritis, bursitis (hot poultice).
Muscle spasms (external).
Rheumatoid arthritis; early onset.
Difficult reunion of broken bones; facilitates bone repair, nutrition, and strengthening.
Burns, bruises, cuts, boils, abscesses, bites (external).
Develops calluses on fingers, palms, and soles; good for dancers, needle-workers, etc.
Dark, horny skin; from retention of dead skin cells.
Cancer following an injury at the site; especially in breasts, chest (10 drops, 4x/day; Yadubir Sinha).
Psoriasis and eczema (green leaf tea, 1 quart a day).
Leucorrhea.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Root and leaves.

### CAUTIONS:

Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids, which can cause liver damage. Leaves contain less. Use externally, in small doses, and when the liver is healthy.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Not to be used during a normal, healthy puberty (Dorothy Hall). Do not use to increase bone growth until the bones are set in the right place (Matthew Wood).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Dorothy Hall (2–6, 16, 19), Eva and Eugene Graf (15, 25), Aviva Romm (18), Brent Davis (15), Yadubir Sinha (24), John Hill (26), Samuel Henry (26).

## Tanacetum parthenium. Feverfew.

Refer to the entry under Chrysanthemum parthenium.

## Tanacetum vulgare. Tansy.

Tansy is a member of the aster family native to Europe but widely naturalized in North America. It was once used as a bitter tonic at Easter, to cleanse the system. It is “a rather harsh remedy” (William Cook) that is not pleasant to use. I was once driving in the country with a girlfriend, picked a single shoot, put it on the dashboard for decoration, but immediately removed it when my companion starting having a severe uterine spasm. As soon as it was out the window she was back to normal. It has been used to bring on suppressed menstruation and even as an abortifacient, but it is both dangerous and inefficient in moderate doses. This remedy could perhaps be used in very small or homeopathic doses. The oil is used as a local stimulant on sprains, strains, and even torn tendons.

## Taraxacum officinale. Dandelion.

Dandelion is, of course, an extremely common resident of the temperate regions, originally native to Europe and Asia. It was imported to North America as a spring green. Dandelion is a member of the Asteraceae or composite family. It contains flavonoids, polysaccharides, sesquiterpene lactones, triterpenes, sterols, carotenoids, and potassium (up to 4.5% in the leaves).
“This is the celebrated plant in which the common people of every land have faith,” wrote the nineteenth-century New Zealand herbalist James Neil (1998, 77). Dandelion has an old reputation as a “spring tonic” and “blood purifier.” The tradition of eating dandelion greens in the spring is a widespread European practice that, imported to the New World, has not yet completely died out. Although best known for its general properties as an alterative, dandelion also possesses virtues of a highly specific nature, though these more definite indications are not well known. It is indicated when heat descends deeply into the tissues, thickening fluids, slowing down drainage, inflaming the deeper tissues, and even infecting the bones. We find it of use occasionally in a severe acute fever, but more often it is called for in cases that develop slowly; it is a remedy to cool excess heat and thin and disperse surplus fluids. (Note the case history given under “Cautions,” where it was too drying.)
The tongue provides a specific indication that leads to its effective use in these cases. Taraxacum is especially indicated where there is a mapped or geographic tongue. This indication comes to us from the homeopathic provings of Samuel Hahnemann and was further developed by some of the later homeopaths. “Tongue covered with a white film; feels raw; comes off in patches, leaving red, sensitive spots” (Boericke, 1927, 633). Sometimes the film is yellow, sometimes the mapping is at the top or at the bottom, and sometimes it is so burned off that only remnants remain around the edges. The color of the tongue body is just as significant. It is dark red, indicating a sort of deep, internally established heat, although there may be spots of intense red showing off local inflammation. Sometimes there is dark red in the lips too. At the same time they are usually somewhat dry. After giving dandelion the tongue will usually turn from dark red to pink red, indicating that the heat is coming up from the depths but that a sensitivity to heat remains.
If we understand the significance of the mapped tongue, we understand the process to which dandelion is remedial. This indication would be associated with “phlegm fire” in traditional Chinese medicine. The basic idea is that heat is baking down fluids while thickened fluids are blocking the outward escape of the heat. In practical terms, this means that dandelion is indicated when heat has settled in the deeper tissues (muscles, bones) and is simmering away. It is well indicated when there is decalcification from infection in the bone.
I recently recommended dandelion root with success in a young colt with mild laminitis (inflammation of the hooves) from eating too much grain. This shows the affinity to inflammation in the hard structures rather nicely. The vet considered this a mild case that would probably recover. I do not know if taraxacum could reverse a severe case, but the effect was quick in this colt.
Strangely, I have several times used dandelion root with success in manic-depression, when accompanied by a mapped tongue. Without this tongue indication I would never have suspected that it acted on such a condition. Here the mapped tongue indicates “phlegm fire obstructing the heart” in traditional Chinese medicine. That is to say, the condition is acting on the mind. One client proudly came up to the stage where I was speaking at a convention and showed me his tongue—cleared off, along with the manic-depression, after six months on dandelion root.
It should be noted that although I am using diagnostic indications from traditional Chinese herbalism to describe the properties of the Western dandelion, these indications have not been associated with Chinese dandelion per se. They are based on my own experience and the need to find words to properly express the properties of this plant. Chinese dandelion (Taraxacum mongolicum, T. sinicum, T. heterolepis olepis) is used rather similarly to Western dandelion. It is classified as bitter, sweet, and cold. It is used to reduce heat in the interior of the body, counteract toxicity, disperse swelling, heal carbuncles, stomachache, hepatitis, appendicitis, mastitis, urinary infections, head colds, tracheitis, laryngitis, and acute tonsillitis (Lu, 1994, 151).
The root is used more for the alterative effect, to thin the fluids and reduce heat. It is indicated in stagnant bile, where heat is baking down the bile and we get gallstones. It has long been used to slowly cleanse the liver, gallbladder, and pancreas. Michael Murray, ND (1995, 74), puts into words the feeling of many herbalists: “Dandelion is regarded as one of the finest liver remedies, both as food and medicine.” Studies have shown “that dandelion enhances flow of bile, improving such conditions as liver congestion, bile duct inflammation, hepatitis, gallstones, and jaundice.” The action is twofold: “It has a direct effect on the liver, causing an increase in bile production and flow to the gallbladder (a choleretic effect), and a direct effect on the gallbladder, causing contraction and release of stored bile (a cholagogue effect).” Dandelion has been used for some problems derived from liver and gallbladder stasis, such as high cholesterol, excess urea, gout, constipation, portal stagnation, varicose veins, cellulitis, eczema, acne, and herpes.
The leaf has an earthen taste, so it increases diuresis. It is indicated in tiresome, achy, feverish, chronic infections with relief from urination. Because the leaves contain sodium and potassium they act on both sides of the kidney mechanism: excreting fluids and retaining specific contents. Hence, dandelion leaf is one of the few diuretics that retains potassium and builds up the retentive side of the kidneys. I recommended it to a woman in her fifties who was suffering from potassium loss due to long-term diarrhea—she had Crohn’s disease with part of her intestine removed. The doctors gave her periodic shots of potassium or expensive potassium supplements, which she had neglected for a while. She was terribly fatigued, losing fluids via the stools and kidneys. Dandelion root stopped the condition quickly—the leaf might have been even better. As a diuretic, dandelion leaf can cause weight loss due to water removal.
Michael Moore (2003) recommends the pickled root for chronic inflammatory conditions. I’ll have to try some of this, as that is the condition for which I use dandelion root the most. He specifically mentions skin conditions, wheezy lungs, colon congestion, and “leaky gut syndrome.”
“Dandelion is the ideal spring cleanser, spring bitters, and spring tonic,” wrote Dr. Swinburne Clymer (1933, II:41). “Its mineral contents are more evenly balanced for this purpose than any ‘medicine’ any chemist could invent. Perhaps, it is needless to say, it is a desirable and efficient food at any other time of the year.” However, the leaf quickly turns bitter in summer. Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1973, 57) recommends the mineral filled greens as a diet for strengthening the enamel of the teeth, and as a constitutional remedy for diabetes, obesity, and excessive sleepiness.

### TASTE:

(root) bitter, sweet, earthen, salty • moist • oily (leaf) bitter, earthen, salty • moist, cool

### TISSUE STATE:

stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Dullness of the mind, lethargy, sleepiness, with congestion of fluids.
Mania; insomnia; manic-depressive.
HEAD
Swollen membranes around the sinuses, from chronic allergy and infection.
Mapped tongue. Patches of opaque white coating torn off in places to expose a red, raw tongue body. Also suitable when all the coating has been exfoliated by heat, or when the coating is yellow, or there are only traces of the coat, or for a dark, red tongue.
Mastoiditis; pain, swelling, or redness in the sternocleidomastoid muscle (SCM; side of neck).
Lips dark red, dry.
RESPIRATION
Bronchitis.
DIGESTION
Stomach, indigestion, gas.
LIVER
Liver congested, swollen, gallbladder congested, bile thick, adhesive, gallstone formation, lack of bile secretion, jaundice from backed-up bile, constipation from lack of bile in the intestines; anemia from lack of bile production and recycling.
CARDIOVASCULAR AND BLOOD
High cholesterol, high blood pressure; one of the best remedies for cardiorenal edema.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Diabetes type II, hypoglycemia.
Edema, stones, chronic kidney infection.
Swollen, achy feeling in kidney region, feels better after urination.
FEMALE
Breast congestion, hardness, mastitis; cancer.
PMS.
Menopausal problems from poor liver function; liver not breaking down extra hormones.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Muscular pain, swelling, heat; especially in the sternocleidomastoid muscle and neck.
Inflammation of the muscles; muscles tight and swollen; SCM involved; chronic rheumatism.
Infections in bones.
Rheumatoid arthritis, with deformation of the fingers.
SKIN
Acne, abscesses, boils, eczema, herpes, age spots.
FEVER
Acute fever with high temperature, dark red tongue or patch of dark red on the tongue.
CONSTITUTION
Tall, thin, emaciated.
Overweight, sluggish, sleepy.
Many kinds of people.
Anger; “male clients who are hard-driving and over-striving” (Kimberly Galvin).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Effective in small, medium, or large doses. Leaves are more diuretic, roots are more cholagogue, the flower acts more on the heart. Dandelion harmonizes a formula (LeSassier).

### CAUTIONS:

Dandelion leaf and root have long been used as foods and both are generally regarded as safe. However, I had a cardiac case where I gave marshmallow root and dandelion root (separately) as diuretics. The eighty-two-year-old man reacted excellently to the former and adversely to the latter. “It dried out my mouth, made my digestion bad, made me urinate five times a night.” Moistening his mouth was one thing we were trying to accomplish with the marshmallow root, since he was dry. Fortunately marshmallow root lived up to its reputation as the most mucilaginous of the diuretics.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (7–12, 15–16, 21–22), homeopathic provings and clinical experience (4, 5, 13, 17–18, 22), Matthew Wood (1–7, 17–19, 22, 25), traditional Chinese medicine (14), C. F. Leyel (20), Rudolf Weiss (20), Julia Graves (23), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (18, 21), Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1, 24).

## Thymus vulgaris, T. serpyllum. Thyme.

Thyme is an ancient Mediterranean remedy, still used in commercial products as an antiseptic and in modern European medicine as a specific for whooping cough. The name thymos comes from the Greek word for “strength.” It is a very strong remedy, deeply dredging the system, opening it up so that heat and toxins can be removed (cf. Hyssopus, Larrea) and thinning phlegm. It contains volatile oils, including thymol, carvacrol, borneol, and others.
Thyme is suited to cold, inactive conditions where there are tendencies to chills, shivering, putrefaction, sepsis, and stagnant, stuck mucus. It has proved effective against very serious infective organisms such as anthrax, typhoid, and diphtheria (cf. Olea). It is too seldom appreciated today as a deep and powerful detoxifier. It has a powerful penetrative effect, due to the volatile oils, which open pores, thin mucus, and move fluids through the tissues. It is “hot in the third degree” by the standards of Greek medicine, and thymol is “hot in the fourth degree” (burns the skin). Like most members of the mint family, thyme has a relaxant effect on the nerves.
Herbalist Matthew Becker has developed a constitutional profile for thyme. It is suited to people with deep unconscious issues that disturb the sleep. The mind may be preoccupied, but the problems come up from below consciousness. This fits the physical profile for thyme, since it is a remedy for toxins and closed passageways deep in the body. It also fits the findings of modern European medicine (Dan Kenner and Yves Requena, 2001, 239), since thyme is considered a parasympathetic relaxant. Sleep and dreams occur when the parasympathetic is running the ship, so disturbed sleep and dreams indicate that this branch of the autonomic cannot fully relax. Thyme also stimulates the thymus gland, which improves immunity and self-identity, and the adrenal cortex.
As a parasympathetic relaxant, thyme naturally acts on the gastrointestinal tract and is remedial for dyspepsia and sluggish digestion. It is an old carminative used to warm up the stomach. This is commonly the case with remedies hot in the third degree. It is used to enhance digestion and nutrition in convalescence (King, quoted by Hoffmann, 2003, 569).

### TASTE:

pungent • hot, dry • stimulating

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, constriction, atrophy, stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Persons with low-level anxiety from subconscious sources, difficulty sleeping.
Persons with low vitality, chilliness, frequent, recurrent infections and cold limbs.
Heat affecting the nervous system: nightmares, restlessness, lethargy, spasm.
People who wake with fright or startled; wake with phlegm in the throat.
Paralysis, epilepsy, fainting.
Alcoholism.
HEAD
Headache, hangover.
RESPIRATION
Laryngitis, sore throat, tonsillitis.
Constant clearing of the throat; postnasal drip.
Acute colds with shivering.
Acute and chronic bronchitis.
Highly irritable mucosa causing constant coughing; unstoppable fits of coughing; in acute or chronic respiratory disease.
Whooping cough in children.
Shortness of breath, emphysema.
Respiratory problems associated with worms.
Respiratory mucus associated with emotions (cf. Marrubium).
Wet coughs, spasmodic coughs, mucus yellow, green, thick.
Pneumonia.
Respiratory mycosis.
DIGESTION
Poor appetite, slow digestion, food ferments, creates gas and burning in the stomach, heartburn; in cold, sluggish constitutions with putrefactive tendency.
Colic, gas, cramps, diarrhea.
Rectocolitis.
Parasites.
Typhoid fever.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Genitourinary complaints including bladder infections, yeast infections, thrush, putrid genital discharges.
Childhood bedwetting and diarrhea.
FEMALE
Amenorrhea.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Cold limbs.
Gout, rheumatism.
Skin of fingers dry (cf. Crataegus, back of wrists dry).
Hookworm (thymol; 5–20 grains).
FEVER
Fever with faulty perspiration, deep heat, chills, cold extremities, putrefaction.
SKIN
Scabies, lice, crabs, athlete’s foot (external and internal).
Boils, abscesses, surface infections, mastitis, bruises, shingles (external).
OTHER
Leukopenia, low immunity, anemia.
Spleen; damp, boggy.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Tincture of the fresh leaves captures the volatile oils best. Dose: 15–20 drops, 2–3x/day and once before bed (Matthew Becker). Lemon thyme can be substituted. It has a better taste and patients take it more readily.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Thyrotoxicosis (Husemann and Wolff). The oil is violently irritating to the skin.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Rudolf Weiss, Matthew Becker (1, 4, 9, 17), Dorothy Hall (8, 36), David Hoffmann (8, 11, 13, 26), Aviva Romm (16), Rose Elliot and Carlo de Paoli (2, 3), Eva Graf (15), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (8, 10–14, 18–24, 35), Matthew Wood (30), John William Fyfe (21, 24, 31).

## Tilia europaea, T. cordata, T. platyphyllos,
T. americana. Linden Flower, Basswood.

The European linden and the American basswood are beautiful trees that provide shade, easily worked wood, bark for basket making (hence the name bast wood), and medicine. Our medical tradition owes more to Europe than North America. Hence, this remedy is frequently called by its European name, linden flower. The European species are officinal, but the American can be used in its place. Tilia contains an interesting assortment of constituents including flavonoids, tannins, amino acids, mucilaginous polysaccharides, sterols, steroidal saponins, phenolic acids, and volatile oils.
In the old days, according to Julia Graves, nearly every German village had a green with linden trees and under these trees the townsmen met to decide business. Linden has a sedative effect, and anyone who has ever attended a small town meeting can well understand the need for sedatives. In fact, next time I need to bring some business before such a venue I think I’ll spray the room with linden essence or something.
Linden flowers were anciently used for convulsion and epilepsy. “They are universally recommended in epilepsies, and all nervous distempers, and upon that account make a part of the compound piony-water” (Quincy, 1736, 74). The flowers are an old European domestic medicine, still used for children with hyperactivity or fever—excess heat conditions (Maurice Mességué). It is suited to symptoms of kidney heat and irritation, including increased blood volume, essential hypertension, orthostatic hypertension, moist, warm skin, congestion of the kidneys, scanty, dark urine, and edema. It is cooling enough to work on herpes.
The tongue calling for linden flower is usually red, sometimes flame-shaped, and usually somewhat moist. This is a handy specific indication (Matthew Wood).

### TASTE:

sweet • moist, cool

### TISSUE STATES:

irritation, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Nervousness, restlessness, hyperactivity, insomnia.
Panic attacks and anxiety.
Nervous headache, migraine and dizziness; neuralgia.
Convulsions in children.
RESPIRATION
Influenza, fever, colds, coughs, mucus in the trachea and lungs.
DIGESTION
Indigestion, nervous vomiting, painful digestion, colic, diarrhea.
CARDIOVASCULAR AND BLOOD
Heart palpitation, cramp.
Arteriosclerosis and hypertension associated with nervous tension.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Dark, scanty urine, edema.
FEMALE
Pelvic inflammatory disease, uterine pain, bearing down sensations, inflammation of the genitalia.
FEVER
Fever with profuse sweat that does not relieve.
Fever, chills, shivering; profuse sweat with no relief from fever.
Pain associated with heat, irritation, and spasm.
SKIN
Itching, burning eruptions, sores, herpes, shingles (external; the bark is beaten to soft fiber, simmered in cream, milk, or milk and water, to make a soothing poultice).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The American species, Tilia americana, can be used in place of the European, T. europaea. However, the European species are officinal. Dosage of the tincture (1:5) is 2/5 to 5 ml 3x/day (David Hoffmann), but I use small doses with effect.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Maurice Mességué (1), David Hoffmann (1, 5, 8, 9), Julia Graves, Daniel Cobb (14).

## Trifolium pratense. Red Clover.

Red clover is a member of the legume family native to central Asia but widely grown as a crop and naturalized throughout the world. It was introduced into Western herbalism by Samuel Thomson, who used a cooked-down paste for the external treatment of skin cancers. The diagnosis here is uncertain but it has demonstrated antineoplastic constituents. Subsequently it was more widely used as a blood purifier. Western herbal medicine is still getting acquainted with this simple agent. Red clover contains flavonoids, coumarins, saponins, resins, and volatile oils.
The flavor of this common pasture flower is sweet, the temperature cool and moist. As a consequence, red clover may be looked upon as a nutritive tonic, soothing to dry, irritable tissues. I have found that when it is needed the skin is usually dry. Red clover has a special affinity for the glands of the body, so that it is a remedy for swollen parotids (mumps), salivary gland congestion or excess secretion, lymphatic congestion, and other swollen, puffed up problems with glands. In addition, it thins the blood, like other members of the clover clan that possess natural coumarins.
Red clover is associated with conditions where the body walls off the offending matter, making a hard glandular cyst or node. Sometimes there might be a few along the course of a lymphatic drainage duct. Sometimes these cysts break open, especially in the sinuses, resulting in the sudden appearance of fever, sinus drainage, lung drainage. These cases are distinct in that they come on suddenly, without prior fever or sickness, then last for months (Dorothy Hall, 1988). Red clover will even help encyst cancerous tissue, but it will seldom cure cancer.
A writer to Ellingwood’s Therapeutist (1908, 2, 7, 29) makes an interesting observation: “This remedy has a peculiar influence in improving the nutrition to the brain when impaired by overwork, especially in those cases where there is loss of memory for words, confused ideas, or other mental defects from functional causes. I have prescribed this remedy in these cases and have obtained excellent results. I believe that it improves the circulation, not only of the nervous system but also of the entire capillary system, and including that of the extremities.”

### TASTE:

sweet • cool, moist

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
GLANDS, RESPIRATORY SYSTEM, SKIN
Dry tear ducts.
Salivary glands; dry; calcifications in the glands; profuse flow of saliva.
Swollen parotids (mumps).
Sudden sinus drainage, with fever, lasting for months; due to encysted infection breaking open.
Drippy, seepy discharge from the salivary glands or posterior nares, causing irritation of the throat and lungs.
Hard, swollen, encysted glands.
Mastitis; encysted glands of the breast, from mastitis.
Stomach acidity (root infusion).
Dry skin, insufficient secretion.
Cancer.
External: on ulcers (root infusion).
Cold sores and sore lips (root infusion).
Insomnia, especially in children.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The flowers are almost always the part used. They are collected in midsummer, dried for use by infusion or extracted fresh in alcohol. Dose: 1–30 drops, 1–3x/day; needs to be persisted in for at least six weeks. J. V. Cerney (1976, 73) is the only author I have seen who routinely used an infusion of the roots.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (10), William Boericke (3), Dorothy Hall (6, 7), J. V. Cerney (8, 11, 12), Matthew Wood (1, 2, 4, 5, 6, 9, 10-confirmed), Tommie Bass (13), David Hoffmann (13).

## Trigonella foenum-graecum. Fenugreek.
