Fenugreek is a legume native to the Old World (Europe, India, and China) that has been used for many centuries as a food and medicine. Because it is warm, soothing, and mucilaginous it is used to cleanse, nourish, and soothe the gastrointestinal and respiratory mucosa. It removes heat caused by putrefaction and fermentation, and is contraindicated in active heat (excitation). It contains flavonoids, mucilage, proteins, alkaloids, steroidal saponins, vitamins, and minerals.
Dorothy Hall presents a particularly detailed and useful account of fenugreek. It is indicated when there are sluggish lymphatics in indolent persons with poor dietary habits, fatty diet, high cholesterol, and increasing blood pressure. As a nutritive food it promotes lactation, hair growth, and semen production. These hormonal effects indicate great potential. One of Dorothy Hall’s students described to me a case of Graves’ disease with weight gain in a small, rotund woman that was completely stopped in two weeks, with weight loss, on fenugreek (5 drop doses). Phyllis Light has developed its use as a menopausal remedy.
Externally the seeds are ground to make a thick paste that is cooling, lubricating, soothing, and mucilaginous. It is especially good for ulcers, boils, hard-to-heal sores, and wounds. The addition of charcoal will prevent the wound from becoming septic (Jethro Kloss).

### TASTE:

pungent, sweet • warm • stimulating, mucilaginous

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Yellow complexion; averse to exercise; overweight; slack muscle tone; stagnant lymphatics.
Debility, anemia, malnutrition, rickets.
Counters excess use of milk products.
Diabetes mellitus.
HEAD
Hair loss.
RESPIRATION
Sore throat (gargle).
Chronic cough, bronchitis, asthma, emphysema; hardened mucus.
Allergies, influenza.
DIGESTION
Bad breath, poor digestion, heartburn, fermentation, flatulence, ulceration, putrefaction, nutritional debility.
Dysentery.
CARDIOVASCULAR SYSTEM AND BLOOD
Fatty deposits, cholesterol, high blood pressure; eats fatty food and suffers from it.
FEMALE
Vaginitis.
Lactation (increases).
MALE
Sterility.
LYMPH/IMMUNE
Stagnant lymphatics.
Lymphatic cancer.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Sciatica, arthritis.
SKIN
Does not perspire easily; fenugreek can cause smelly sweat for several days.
Abscesses, boils, wounds, inflammation of the skin.
FEVER
Fever; opens the pores.
Fever with excess perspiration.

### PREPARATION, TOXICITY, AND DOSAGE:

Fenugreek has a mild hormonal influence, promotes uterine bleeding, and is therefore contraindicated in pregnancy. The seed is used in decoction, gruel, powder/capsules, and paste. In addition, the seeds can be sprouted. Traditionally, the seed is often boiled in milk. Soybean or nut milk can be used as well (Jethro Kloss). The seed does not extract in cold water.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Jethro Kloss (2, 5, 6, 7, 9, 19), Vasant Lad and David Frawley, Dorothy Hall (1, 11, 18, 20), Jack Ritchason (3, 11, 15), Phyllis Light (1-confirmed), Hildegard von Bingen (21).

## Tussilago farfara. Coltsfoot.

Coltsfoot is native to Europe but widely naturalized in temperate regions of North America and perhaps elsewhere. It grows along beaten paths and roads where, in the old days, the foot of the colt would indeed have been felt. The broad leaf looks like the print of a horse. The flowers rise in the spring, before the leaves, leading to an old Roman or Italian name, “father before son.” The leaf is thick, moist, and cool. The lower side is coated with down, which remains after the leaves have been dried, making them slightly adherent. Ben Charles Harris pointed to this as a signature for clingy, adhesive mucus. Apropos of this, Dorothy Hall (1988, 143) notes that coltsfoot should not be given in large doses at the beginning of treatment as it is apt to bring up large amounts of mucus that are hard to contend with since they are adherent and stick in the throat. The name also indicates a lung usage—long-winded animals like the fox and horse are associated with the lungs.
Coltsfoot contains mucilage in abundance, tannins, a bitter glycoside, alkaloids, flavonoids, resins, saponins, inulin (muscle sugar), some fruit acids, and minerals including silicon, zinc, and sodium. The burnt leaves yield a passable salt substitute. It also contains the pyrrolizidine alkaloids considered to be hepatotoxic, but seems to lack a delivery system to get them in the body. Rudolf Weiss points out that these alkaloids are not extracted from coltsfoot in water, so the herbal tea is an effective method for use.
“It is by all received as an excellent Pectoral,” writes John Quincy (1736). Coltsfoot is used after exposure to cold, damp, wind, or germs and viruses have brought about the appearance of a deep respiratory infection. The condition often becomes chronic, the lungs may be damaged by scar tissue, the mucus is deep-seated and dried up, hence difficult to raise, or wet and sticky, making it difficult to expel. Coltsfoot is often palliative, bringing up mucus and lessening the severity of the cough. Sometimes the condition is old and incurable, at other times it needs gentle means to restore integrity to the respiratory structure and mucosa, dry up the infection, dispel the bacteria, and energize the exhausted condition of the sick person (following Dorothy Hall). Respiratory conditions where the feet are sore (Sebastian Kneipp; confirmed, Matthew Wood).
“The peasants say that it comes in time for the spring fevers,” writes Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1973, 50).

### TASTE:

salty • cool, moist, and dry • mucilaginous, astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
HEAD
Vertigo, migraine, obstruction of the nose.
RESPIRATION
Respiratory infections following exposure to cold, damp, and wind.
Sinusitis, allergies.
Sore throat, feels like broken glass, irritable, painful coughing, keeps one awake at night.
Bronchitis, asthma, catarrh, colds, irritable coughs, emphysema, pleurisy, pneumonia, tracheitis, tuberculosis, whooping cough, smoker’s cough.
Dry mucus, difficult to raise.
When the mucus comes up it is adhesive, thick, and clogging to the throat.
Scar tissue in the lungs.
Emphysema (palliative; a cup of warm tea in the morning to clear mucus).
Asthma; wet, greenish mucus (expose slowly to coltsfoot as it can bring up too much mucus at once).
Dry cough of children with rickettsial deformities (cf. Chondrus).
Whooping cough in children (half a cup of tea sweetened with honey, 4x/day).
Dry pleurisy.
LIVER AND GALLBLADDER
Liver and gallbladder.
LYMPHATICS
Scrofula, swollen glands in the neck.
FEMALE
Menstruation, stopped by cold.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Bladder infections.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Lower back pain.
Sore feet.
Strains and sprains of joints and ligaments (external).
SKIN
External ulcers, chronically weeping wounds.
Burns, insect bites.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The leaves are dried for use by infusion. Contains pyrrolizidine alkaloids, which are not soluble in water, so it is best prepared by tea. However, it can be used in small doses of the tincture (1–5 drops). In the old days coltsfoot was often smoked for relief. It is often combined with the great old respiratory standbys of European medicine: elecampane, mullein, marshmallow, and hoarhound.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Sebastian Kneipp (5, 19), Matthew Wood (4, 19-confirmed), Dorothy Hall (2, 5, 7, 8, 10, 11, 12), Samuel Henry (1, 4, 12, 15), Rudolf Weiss (9).

## Urtica dioica, U. urens. Nettle, Nettles.

Urtica is native to the Old World, but widely naturalized throughout the world. It has native cousins in the woods of North America. Nettle grows around septic systems, outhouses, and manure piles, demonstrating its utility in dealing with protein waste products. It is one of the plants highest in protein and helps all protein pathways in the body—digestion, immune response, liver metabolism, skin reactions, and kidney elimination. It contains chlorophyll, indoles (including histamine and serotonin), acetylcholine, flavonoids, vitamins (including C), proteins, and dietary fiber.
Nettle, or nettles, is one of the fundamental remedies of traditional Western herbalism. It is high in protein, vitamins, and minerals, so that it is an excellent all-around nutritive tonic. It is especially used as an iron tonic during pregnancy. More specifically, nettles acts on the protein pathways in the body. It reduces allergic reactions to proteins (here the freeze-dried herb is preferred) and eliminates mucus on membranes resulting from allergy. (The root contains sterols and lectins that may stimulate T-cell or precise immune response.) Nettle probably also helps with the digestion and absorption of protein. Entering deeper into the body, it helps the liver build blood proteins. These help keep the plasma inside the blood vessels, reducing edema and low blood pressure resulting from loss of plasma into the tissues. Dorothy Hall (1988) looks upon nettle as a specific for low blood pressure. Nettle also helps with the removal of proteinaceous waste products (urates) from the system; hence it is a remedy in gout, arthritis, muscular soreness, tissue acidity, and kidney disease. It prefers to grow downstream from an outhouse, septic system, or manure pile, indicating its affinity for high levels of protein.
In addition to general, nutritive effects and specific effects along protein pathways, nettle also has a specific action as a tropho-restorative, bringing back function to parts that have become paralyzed, atrophied, or functionally inactive. Perhaps it acts through the proteinaceous genetic material, which carries the codes for bodily functions. In the old days, the part paralyzed or arthritic or gouty was whipped with the fresh-picked nettle. I find it effective when the part has been paralyzed or depressed in function since the use of anesthesia. It also works internally on inactive kidneys, thyroid, menstruation, hormones, nerves, muscles, and so forth, to bring them back into effective operation.
Robin Rose Bennet had a famous case history where nettles restored the thyroid in a woman who had the gland surgically removed (by radioactive iodine) for hyperthyroidism, but regretted the surgery. This case history is well attested.
Here is a case history from my own records involving restored muscle function. A fifty-seven-year-old woman fell down her stairways with her leg bent back, ripping off the kneecap. She had to have football doctors reconnect the tendons to an artificial kneecap. About six weeks after the operation the muscles in the inner thighs started to atrophy and she began to lose mobility. The doctors told her she had to exercise her legs or the healing would not take place. Stuck in this situation she called me. When I put drops of nettle tincture on the inner thighs the reaction was so swift she could start hobbling around again. She returned to the path of walking and healing.
Since that time I have found that nettle is good for weakness in muscles of the inner thighs in middle-aged women, making them insecure about going down stairs. Clearly, in the above case, the wasting was the cause of the injury. In some menopausal women, especially stout, heavy ones, the pelvic attachments loosen up, the pelvis expands, and these muscles get weak. It stands to reason, therefore, that nettle would make an ideal strengthening tonic during pregnancy, toning the pelvic and thigh muscles needed for delivery. (But wait! Nettles already is a pregnancy tonic for other reasons! No bypassing women’s intuition!)
Now we can begin to appreciate the use of nettle for impotence. It was originally used by flagellation of the part, which may have given the partner some fulfillment, but this is unnecessary, to judge from the way it works internally and externally in tincture or infusion. Still, in a difficult case?
Nettle has a high level of affinity for the kidneys. It is well known in the herbal community for getting people off dialysis. Here is a simple case from my own records. A fifty-six-year-old woman called me from Maryland because she was suffering from severely swollen ankles. She had eaten some high protein foods as part of a weight loss diet. Shortly after, her urine turned pale, smelt fishy, and her ankles got swollen. I recommended nettle tea, tincture, or “whatever she could get.” She took one dose of Urtica 30c from her homeopathic kit and followed with nettle tincture and (in a few days) nettle tea. By the next day the fishy smell was gone, the urine was no longer pale, and the swelling in the ankles was going down. The ankles remained slightly swollen for several weeks.
“When in doubt, give nettles” (David Hoffmann).

### TASTE:

(young shoots and leaves) sweet, bitter, earthen • stimulating

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, atrophy, stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION
Low blood pressure and concomitant problems.
Loss of hair, hair color; poor circulation of blood to the head with lack of oxygen and iron pigment (external wash).
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Mental dullness, lack of concentration and mental acuity.
Memory lapses.
Dead fish dullness of the eyes; drooping eyelids; brings a sparkle back to the eyes on administration.
Fainting on rising, with low blood pressure.
Tired, hard to get out of a chair and get going, rises with a sigh or grunt, but stays on task once activated; begins the day slowly, hard to stop at the end of the day.
Yawning, sighing, sleepiness.
Pale, gray-pale face and skin tone.
Mouth sores.
RESPIRATION
Mucus, bronchitis, asthma; grayish-pale skin color; poor oxygenation.
Low blood pressure; pulse relaxed, slow, and deep; poor nutrition to the heart.
DIGESTION
Diarrhea, dysentery, chronic, mucus in stools.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Distention and swelling of kidneys; edema and eczema of the fingers or elsewhere.
Inactive kidneys (requiring dialysis) (seeds).
Chronic inflammation of the bladder, with abundant mucus discharge.
MALE
Impotence; difficulty producing and keeping an erection; associated with low blood pressure.
Benign prostatic hypertrophy (root).
FEMALE
Menstruation: excessive, anemia from blood loss.
Premature cessation of menses.
Pregnancy: anemia, low blood pressure.
Lactation: deficient.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Atrophy and paralysis of muscles (voluntary and involuntary).
Arthritis, gout (especially in those eating a rich, proteinaceous diet).
Hypothyroid.

Burns with excess protein burdening the kidneys (external or internal).
Hives; all bites, stings, itch, etc., that raise red, welt-like burns, stinging pain (ointment).
Deer fly bites (urtication).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The fresh-picked plant can be used directly on parts that are paralyzed and inactive. Pick the young shoots in the spring as a medicine or food. The formic acid breaks down quickly after picking, so the best preservation is by immediate freeze-drying or tincturing. Extract fresh in alcohol. Dry the leaves for nutritive use. The taste of the tea can be improved by the addition of peppermint or lemon balm.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Dorothy Hall (1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 12, 17, 19, 27), Susun Weed, Matthew Wood (14, 23), Rolla Thomas (13, 16), David Winston (15), Michael Moore (19), homeopathic provings and clinical experience, J. Compton Burnett (24), Margaret Tyler (26), Dorothy Shepherd (26), William Boericke (27), Laurel Dewey (28).

## Vaccinium myrtillus. Bilberry.

The European bilberry is closely related to the American blueberry in botany and properties. However, it has been subjected to greater scientific scrutiny and widespread marketing. Therefore, although the properties may be similar, bilberry is much more widely used as a medicine, whereas blueberry is more widely used as a food. Both are members of the heath family and have the astringent and antiseptic properties characteristic of that family. Bilberry contains flavonoids (including cyanogens), tannins, triterpenes, pectin, fructose, fruit acids, vitamin A, and vitamin C. The berry and leaves have a long history of use, primarily as an astringent and coolant. In 1927 it was noted that bilberry was used in Europe, and blueberry in America, as folk remedies for diabetes. This congruence led to research in Europe: both are indeed important remedies in this disease. During the desperate Battle of Britain in World War II, the RAF pilots learned to use bilberry to enhance night vision. This led to further pharmacological study. Bilberry is now widely used as a general remedy for eye problems, but it is specific for enhancing night vision and reducing the ill-effects of diabetes on the retina.
Bilberry leaves are used, like the leaves of other heaths, as astringent antiseptics for urinary tract infection. In addition they improve lack of tone and ulceration in the mucosa of the digestive tract. They are also diuretic and are therefore used in edema and gravel to increase kidney discharge. “A tea made from the leaves is also a remedy for diabetes if taken for a prolonged period” (Grieve, 1931, 100). As an astringent they can stop lactation.
The fruit, high in vitamin C, was used for scurvy, which would account for symptoms such as ulceration of the skin and mucosa of the mouth and gums. The flavonoids decrease the fragility and permeability of the capillaries, reducing hemorrhage and increasing blood flow into the periphery and decreasing platelet aggregation. At the same time the tannins improve tone on the venous side of the circulation. This can reduce tendencies to atherosclerosis, high blood pressure, and stroke. These properties produce a net “cooling” effect, to put it in energetic terms. The blood moves more freely and there is less congestion and heat.
John Hill (1740, 84) writes, “A syrup made of the juice of billberries, when not over ripe, is cooling and binding; it is a pleasant and gentle medicine for women whose menses are apt to be redundant, taken for a week before the time.”
High levels of sugar make bilberry nutritive or “moistening,” but this is moderated by the “drying” effect of the tannins. The anthocyanidin myrtillin helps to reduce blood sugar levels even though the berries are so sweet. This is an effect we find in many wild berries and natural sugar sources—they do not have the hyperglycemic effect of refined sugars.
The major modern contribution to our knowledge of bilberry revolves around the eye. Some of the anthocyanosides have an affinity to the retina, where they increase production of rhodopsin (visual purple). This helps the eyesight adapt to darkness and glare. Others prevent the destruction of the collagen forming the architectural framework for the structures of the eye. The antidiabetic properties protect against diabetic retinopathy. Even the tannins are constructive in some cases of cataract. Glaucoma, which is a sort of “high blood pressure of the eyes,” may respond to the increased capillary and venous blood flow due to the flavonoids and tannins.

### TASTE:

sweet, sour, moist, astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

excitation, depression
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
HEAD
Cerebral ischemia; stroke.
EYES
Visual fatigue.
Myopia.
Retinal damage.
Retinitis.
Macular degeneration.
Night blindness.
Glaucoma.
Cataract.
Diabetic retinopathy.
CARDIOVASCULAR
Atherosclerosis.
DIGESTION
Ulceration of the mouth and gums.
Inflammation of the throat and pharynx.
Gastroenteritis, Crohn’s disease, colitis.
Ulceration of the GI.
Irritable bowel syndrome.
Diarrhea, dysentery.
Diverticulosis.
Hemorrhoids.
Varicose veins.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Urinary tract infections.
Edema.
FEMALE
Vaginal discharge, leucorrhea.
Menstrual bleeding excessive.
EXTREMITIES
Raynaud’s disease.
SKIN
Dermic ulceration.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Bilberry is usually available in a commercial preparation. Dose of the tincture: 20–30 drops after meals (Daniel Gagnon).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Christopher Menzies-Trull (1–23), John Hill (24), Rudolf Weiss.

## Valeriana officinalis. Valerian.

Valerian is a native of Europe widely naturalized in the temperate regions around the world. It is an ancient traditional remedy for overexcitement and sleeplessness. With its acrid flavor, valerian root may be considered almost the archetype of the acrid and bitter/acrid herbs that treat the constricted or tense tissue state. Modern studies of the constituents and their actions validate the traditional teachings. Valerian contains a group of compounds called valepotriates that have an antispasmodic action, and together with some of their breakdown products have a depressive effect on the central nervous system. The volatile oil contains valerenone and valerenic acid; the latter also has a depressant effect on the CNS. The valerenone and valepotriates relax constricted muscles.
Valerian is principally used as a remedy for insomnia or to relax muscles and ease pain. Ellingwood (1918, 125) writes, “Its action upon the nervous system is best obtained when the circulation of those centers is inactive and feeble, especially when there is a paleness of the face and the skin is cool. It is directly indicated in hysterical conditions of whatever character with feebleness; with nervous excitement, and morbid vigilance, in hysterical epilepsy [or seizures, really] and nervous disturbances incident to menopause. Its general soothing effect in all these cases is desirable. It controls distress and imaginary pain and produces quiet, permitting sleep and rest.”
Fyfe (1909, 753) writes, “Valerian is an excellent cerebral stimulant, and is employed with advantage in diseases resulting from enfeebled cerebral circulation. Its administration often affords great relief in the high tension common to many nervous states, and in wakefulness it exerts a sleep-producing power of much value.”
Valeriana is a powerful relaxant to the nerves and muscles, but in large doses or susceptible persons (and cats) it has the opposite property—causing nervousness, restlessness, wakefulness, and twitching followed by drowsiness, sleepiness, lessened sensibility, and relaxation. It has been classified as a stimulant and a depressant, but it is ultimately a relaxant, reducing neuromuscular tension.
William LeSassier says valerian is “a knocker-outer. Makes one numb. It is generally overused and overrated. It’s great for very freaked out people who need immediate relief (Goldenseal and Lobelia too).” I agree with this assessment wholeheartedly; valerian is really just a palliative sedative with a generalized, nonspecific range of activity. Its best use is as a teaching aid to understand the pure action of the valerianic compounds, which occur in other plants.

### TASTE:

acrid

### TISSUE STATE:

constriction
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Pale complexion, cool extremities.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Nervous, irritated, tense, despondent.
Nervous breakdown, nervous fatigue, atrophy from constriction.
Insomnia, wakefulness, nervousness, restlessness, and tension, especially when associated with spinal pain, intestinal spasm, and flatulence.
“Lack of concentration and attentiveness, in tense people” (1–3 drops).
“Holds anger inside, causing knotted feeling in stomach or low back.”
HEAD
Temporal and frontal headache.
RESPIRATION
Bronchial spasm; colds, coughs.
Spasmodic cough, asthma, and dyspnea.
Asthma in thin, pale persons.
DIGESTION
Flatulence, colic; spasms and convulsions associated with nervous tension.
Stomach hyperacidity, ulcers.
“Emotional dyspepsia.”
CARDIOVASCULAR
Nervous heart palpitations; high blood pressure; slows and strengthens the beat, increases peripheral circulation.
“Emotionally induced high blood pressure.”
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Bladder spasm.
SEXUAL SYSTEM
Menstrual spasm, amenorrhea.
Labor; pains after giving birth.
Low libido, frigidity.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Lower back spasm.
Convulsions, epilepsy, incoordination.
Arthritis, gout, incoordination, paralysis.
FEVER
Fever with spasm; measles, scarlet fever.
OTHER
Counters the effects of alcohol.

### PREPARATIONS, CAUTIONS, AND DOSAGE:

The fresh root provides the best tincture, preserving the volatile oils. It is more “tonic and less reactive” than preparations from the dried root (Matthew Becker). Small doses are generally relaxing, whereas large doses are stimulating. However, reaction is idiosyncratic. In some people valerian produces the symptoms it is supposed to cure—nervousness, irritation, wakefulness, restlessness, etc. Dose: 1–60 drops.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Finley Ellingwood, John William Fyfe, Matthew Becker (5), Matthew Alfs (1-confirmed, 6, 10, 13, 15, 19).

## Verbascum thapsus. Mullein.

Mullein is a member of the Scrophulariaceae family native to the Old World but widely naturalized—I have seen it growing in Death Valley. It is one of the premier cough and respiratory remedies of the European tradition and is widely used in the western hemisphere. It contains flavonoids, mucilage, gum, resin, bitter glycosides, iridoid monoterpenes, triterpene saponins, and volatile oils.
The infusion of mullein leaf releases a salty mucilage. Thus, it is used as an emollient to bring water into hardened, closed places. It draws water into dried out tissues. (The leaf, dipped in wax, acts like a wick.) This activity causes the release of secretions from the tissues, and this brings a favorable conclusion to the disease process. Hence, mullein is indicated in conditions where the tissues are dry or where water is caught in isolated pockets. It is particularly used for dry, irritable, tickly coughs—the tickle sensation is usually evidence of inflammation conjoined with water stuck in the mucosa or skin. It is indicated in old coughs where the velvety carpet of the lungs, the hairs, are inflamed or worn down, so to speak. There may be tightness preventing full inspiration, tightness in the throat or voice box, or tightness in the sinuses and a feeling of tightness in the brain. Remember, there need not be secretion, since this is suppressed in the mullein profile. Mullein opens the lungs, reduces coughing and tightness, lubricates the mucosa, relaxes the larynx, opens the sinuses, and causes a more open feeling in the head and brain. The movement of the plant is upward, from the lungs to the head—this is analogous to the way the herb rises sharply upward from the ground.
Dr. William Cook (1869) spoke of the actions of mullein on the “absorptives” in the gut—that is, the veins and lymphatics that pick up water and food. He says it promotes absorption in cellular dropsy, chronic abscesses, pleuritic effusions, swollen glands, and similar accumulations of fluid.
I have long used mullein as a muscular and skeletal remedy. The original indications I discovered myself. Mullein improves the lubrication of the connective tissue in joints, hence improving the health of cartilage. It releases synovial fluid into the bursa and disperses internal fluids into the surrounding tissues, lubricating joints, muscles, bones, and ligaments. It is thus a remedy for complex fractures, where the bone needs to be lubricated to be returned to its place. It is also indicated in spinal dryness, inflexibility, and pain, and nerve pain along pinched or irritated nerve tracts. These indications have been further developed by some of my students and readers. Jim MacDonald, of southern Michigan, has added some additional insights. Mullein helps to straighten the spine—he uses the basal rosette leaves or root at the end of the first year, since these contain the “intelligence” that sends the stalk straight up in the second year.
Mullein also has an influence on the nerves and mind. Louise Tenney (1983, 89) writes, “Mullein is a great pain killer and helps induce sleep. It has a calming effect on all inflamed and irritated nerves. This is why it works so well in controlling coughs, cramps, and spasms. It has the ability to loosen mucus and move it out of the body. It is valuable for all lung problems because it nourishes as well as strengthens. The crushed fresh flowers have been used to remove warts. The tea has been used for dropsy, sinusitis, and swollen joints. The hot tea helps when applied to mumps, tumors, sore throat, and tonsillitis.”
William LeSassier used to say that mullein was for “intellectuals and hot air people.” I have confirmed this several times. It is for people who think too much and congest the mind, or suffer mental tightness and congestion following difficult projects. Mullein gives such a person a feeling like the mind is opened up to breezes on a fresh spring day—speaking from personal experience. William also emphasizes that the voice box is often affected. The voice can be too high or too low.

### TASTE:

(leaf) salty, mucilaginous

### TISSUE STATE:

atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
General dryness and atrophy.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
“Intellectuals and hot air people”; people who think too much.
Nervousness, hyperthyroidism.
Insomnia.

Earache with pain, wax in ear (oil of the flowers).

Voice unnaturally high or low, due to inflammation of the pharynx.
Sore throat, hoarseness, tonsillitis, mumps.
Allergies, sinusitis, subacute, relapsing bronchitis, asthma, pleurisy, pneumonia, croup, emphysema, tuberculosis.
Cough deep, “basso profundo”; from inflammation of the velvety linings of the respiratory tract.
Asthma from bronchial tension; dilates the lungs and moistens.
Cough; dry, harsh, racking, shakes the frame.
Bleeding from the lungs.

Bleeding from the bowels.

KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
