MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Painful injuries to fingers, toes, spine, areas rich in nerves.
Joints swollen, with weakness and pain.
Spinal injuries; pain, misalignment, lack of synovial lubrication.
Broken bones that are hard to set are quickly returned to their correct position under the influence of Verbascum.
Bruises.
OTHER
Chronic abscesses, with pain.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The leaves and roots are picked during the first season; the flowers in the middle of the second. The leaves are usually used as a tea. The flowers are usually extracted in olive oil in the sunshine (Constantine Herring). At least one author recommends the “tops” (Ralph Russell).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Louise Tenney (3, 4, 8, 9, 13–16, 21, 24, 25), Constantine Herring (6), William LeSassier (2, 7, 11), William Boericke (7, 10), Rosemary Gladstar (18), Hari Jot Singh (19), Matthew Wood (1, 2-confirmed, 5, 9, 12, 20, 22, 23), Jim MacDonald (22).

## Verbena officinalis. Vervain.

The European vervain has a very ancient tradition of use. It was considered one of the most important plants of the druids. It was mentioned by the Greek and Roman authors, and was used in the Renaissance down to the present time. It is one of the seven herbs of Ireland that have power over forces natural and supernatural. Dr. Bach included it among his thirty-eight flower essences. The mental state for which it is remedial is one of intense idealism and self-will, with a lack of physical energy to sustain the output. This pattern is also found in regard to the American blue vervain. To a large extent these medicines can be used interchangeably.
“The fresh gathered tops are used” in infusion, writes Sir John Hill (1740, 379). This concurs with the usual method for picking the American blue vervain. “It is warm upon the stomach, and a continued use of it will remove nervous complaints.”

### TASTE:

bitter, acrid

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Nervousness.
Headache (poultice).
Trigeminal neuralgia.
Pain in the ear (poultice).
Tinnitus.
Ophthalmic complaints.
Pulmonary catarrh.
Pleurisy.
Weakness of the heart.
Dysmenorrhea.
Parturition: induce labor.
Lactation: insufficient.
Malaria.
Intermittent chills and fever.
Cellulitis.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Dan Kenner and Yves Requena.

## Veronica officinalis. Speedwell.

The common speedwell is a member of the Scrophulariaceae family, which is so richly endowed with a wide variety of valuable medicines of diverse use (mullein, digitalis, rehmannia, scrophularia, linaria, and others). Veronica is widely dispersed in the Old World and naturalized in the New. At one time it occupied a position of importance in the folk medicine of Europe. It was one of the plants associated with the mysteries of Christ, and kept as an officinal herb in the monasteries and shops. Veronica was the name of the woman who took pity on Jesus and washed his face when he was been driven to his execution. The image of Jesus’ face remained on the cloth to produce the “veron eikon” (true image) (Fernie, 1914, 485). Today speedwell has almost completely dropped from use in Western herbalism.
“It has been long held famous among country folk as an excellent plant for coughs, asthma, and pulmonary consumption. The leaves are bitter, with a rough [astringent] taste; and a decoction of the whole plant stimulates the kidneys. The infusion promotes perspiration and reduces feverishness. The juice may be boiled into a syrup with honey, for asthma and catarrhs.
“When applied outwardly, it is said to cure the itch; and by some it has been asserted that a continued use of the infusion will overcome sterility, if taken daily as a tea” (W. T. Fernie, 1914, 484).
One of the few twentieth-century herbalists who used veronica was Ben Charles Harris (1972). He speaks of gathering it often, mostly as a substitute for watercress in salads. He considered its habitat, in low, wet places, as evidence of its usefulness in kidney problems. It is a “solvent-eliminator” acting on stones, toxins, and mucus. It also is suited to irritable throat and respiratory organs. To remove hardened mucus in coughs, bronchitis, and asthma.
“Speedwell is commended by Crato as a specifick in the cholick,” writes Salmon (1710, 56, 137). “Crato prefers the simple decoction of speedwell with sugar, before all the more famous and great medicines inwardly given for the stone: the use (saith he) of a clyster made only of a decoction of speedwell with sugar, will do more good than any medicine taken by the mouth.”
Harald Tietze (1996, 45) is one of the few modern herbalists who use speedwell. “It is preferred for any skin disorders. The fresh plant juice has proved itself best for chronic disorders of the skin. To rub the tincture into the skin is good for rheumatism and gout.” He adds, “Instead of taking it in the morning, like most other herbs, speedwell should be taken in the evening to calm the nerves and to give a peaceful sleep.”

## Vinca major, V. minor. Greater and Lesser Periwinkle.

These two plants are closely related members of the Apocynaceae family, native to Europe. In southern climes they ripen their seeds; in northern areas they grow vine-wise across the ground. The apocynaceaes are a very potent family. Hence, it is not surprising that periwinkle is sometimes known as “sorcerer’s violet” and has been used to drive away “wycked spirits.” It is also used as a “love medicine.” The Book of Secrets by pseudo-Albertus Magnus (not a very reliable author) says that it will increase love between a husband and wife. The name vinca means “to bind,” referring to its growth habits, its magical uses, and its medicinal use as an astringent. It is believed to have an effect upon the inner eye. It contains tannins (binding), indole alkaloids (mind-altering), the alkaloid reserpine, and volatile oils.
“Both the periwinkles are used in medicine for their acrid, astringent and tonic properties” (Grieve, 1931, 631). Thus they are binding to outflow, but also relaxing in cramping. Both uses are well attested. “Vinca major is used in herbal practice for its astringent and tonic properties in menorrhagia and in hemorrhages generally. For conditions of mucus in the intestines and lungs, diarrhea, congestions, hemorrhages, etc., periwinkle tea is a good remedy. In cases of scurvy and for relaxed sore throat and inflamed tonsils, it may also be used as a gargle. For bleeding piles, it may be applied externally, as well as taken internally.”
It was considered a good remedy for cramp, Lord Bacon himself testifying that a limb suffering from cramp would be cured if bands of green periwinkle were tied round it; and William Coles, in his Adam in Eden (1657), gives a case of a friend who was “vehemently tormented with the cramp for a long while which could be by no means eased till he had wrapped some of the branches hereof about his limbs.” In my experience, Vinca is for tension that begins in the legs and spreads upward to the rest of the system. In one case the woman had a very hard, tight, static sensation in the lower pulse positions on the wrist—a perfect illustration of tension in the lower extremities.
Periwinkle greater and lesser both contain alkaloids. There are some differences between the exact alkaloids in each one. Although the properties are similar, the greater has been cited as a lymphatic alterative and the lesser as a cerebral tonic (Menzies-Trull). A substance called vinblastine has been isolated that shows some promise in choriocarcinoma, Hodgkin’s disease, and other forms of cancer.

### TASTE:

acrid, astringent

### TISSUE STATES:

constriction, relaxation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Behavior disorders, lack of attention, irritability, restlessness (lesser).
HEAD
Milk crust of infants (lesser; 2–10 drops in water, 3–4x/day).
Cerebral atherosclerosis, optic atrophy, hearing deficit, tinnitus, Ménière’s disease, vertigo, poor memory (lesser).
Headache, temporal to crown, ache behind the eyes, watery eyes (lesser).
Nosebleed (lesser).
Gingivitis.
Relaxed sore throat, swollen tonsils, inflamed tonsils (gargle).
DIGESTION
Gastric hemorrhage, dyspepsia, flatulence.
Relaxation of the intestines, chronic diarrhea, chronic constipation.
Mucus in the intestines.
Bleeding internal hemorrhoids.
FEMALE
PMS (lesser).
Menorrhagia, metrorrhagia.
Vaginal discharge, leucorrhea.
Mastitis, galactorrhea.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Cramp in the lower limbs.
SKIN
Skin disorders, sores, wounds.
OTHER
Hypoglycemia (greater).
Diabetes mellitus (greater).
Cancer (greater).
Ulcers.
Dandruff.
Nightmares.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Infusion of the dried leaves. Pour 1 cup of boiling water over 1 teaspoon of dried herb and infuse for 10–15 minutes (David Hoffmann). Tincture of the fresh leaves, 1–3 drops, 1–3x/day (Matthew Wood). Tincture of the dried leaves.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Brain tumor, may cause intracranial bleeding and pressure.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Maude Grieve (2, 7, 9, 10, 11, 13, 17, 19), Christopher Menzies-Trull (1, 3–6, 8, 12–15, 17–20), Louise Tenney (9, 11, 16, 21, 22, 23), Matthew Wood (4, 16), David Hoffmann (13).

## Viola odorata. Sweet Violet.

Violets have a long history of use in European medicine. The blue varieties are generally used and sweet violet (Viola odorata) is officinal. The leaves and flowers together are used; they have a sweet, slightly mucilaginous, slightly salty taste and cool impression. Violets contain flavonoids, mucilage, salicylates, tannins, essential oils, an alkaloid, saponins, and minerals (especially calcium and magnesium). The root and seeds contain a substance like emetine, which causes vomiting; hence only the leaves and flowers are used in herbalism.
“Violets have a very mild nature and possess no harmful properties. They are always cold and moist when fresh and green and are used as balm or medicine to cool … heated and afflicted parts of the body; also inflammation of the eyes. Relieves the pain of quinsy and is good for the falling sickness in children, especially in the earlier stages” (Chalid Ottway, 1933, 285).
Viola is suited to cases where the mucosa is dry, when expectoration needs to be increased. It “has a long history of use as a cough remedy, especially for bronchitis, and may be of benefit in upper respiratory catarrh” (David Hoffmann, 2003, 594). It has an affinity to the lymphatic system, and is indicated when there is lymphatic stagnation and swollen glands, often in the throat and around the ears, in association with dry skin and constipation. As a moistening remedy it acts on the kidneys, bladder, and chronic arthritic deposits and skin conditions such as eczema.
Dorothy Hall (1988, 306) comments on the resemblance between the nodules that form on the roots and runners of violets and the nodules of the lymphatic system. “The medicinal uses of the violet, flowers, leaves and creeping roots, had previously been obscure to me but if the plant cleared lymphatic blockages and renewed normal lymph flow, then the ancient writer’s observations made sense.” Dorothy Hall seems to have the same approach I have: if she can see the signature she can understand the properties. She recommends violet leaf tincture or extract when there is lymphatic stagnation, enlargement of the spleen, and changes in the components of the blood that may foreshadow cancer.
Violet is widely used in cancer. It is one of the few remedies with a proven track record in cancer involving the breasts, lymphatics, and lungs. Also in skin cancer (Hilde Hemmes).

### TASTE:

sweet, slightly salty • mucilaginous • cool and moist

### TISSUE STATES:

atrophy, stagnation
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
CONSTITUTION, COMPLEXION, CHARACTERISTIC SYMPTOMS
Children with swollen glands, dry skin, and constipation.
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Shy, “shrinking violet.”
Shy, flabby children, with moist skin and palms.
HEAD
Severe headaches.
Inflammation of the eyes.
Infections in the mouth.
RESPIRATORY SYSTEM AND LYMPHATICS
Sinus catarrh.
Chronic congestion of the glands around the ears and in the throat.
Sore throat, hoarseness, dryness of the upper respiratory tract, chronic cough, associated with dryness (violet syrup).
Bronchitis, asthma, whooping cough (violet syrup).
Feverish pulmonary catarrh.
DIGESTION
Constipation.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Gravel.
Urinary tract infection.
FEMALE
Breast lumps, benign and malignant.
EXTREMITIES
Arthritis of the wrists.
SKIN
Skin dry; sore, raw (external).
Eczema.
OTHER
Cancer of the lymphatics, breasts, lungs, skin (poultice of fresh leaf and flower is best).
Epilepsy and nervous disorders.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Violet flowers and leaves traditionally are not boiled to extract the active parts. Sir John Hill (1740, 380) wrote, “The flowers are the part used; boiled water is to be poured upon them just enough to cover them, and it is to stand all night; when it is strained clear off, the sugar is to be added to it, at the rate of two pounds to each pint, and it is to be melted over the fire; this makes syrup of violets, an excellent gentle purge for children; the leaves are dried also, and are used in the decoctions for clysters. An infusion of them works by urine.” Infusion: cold maceration of the flowers and leaves for 8 hours, followed by slow warming to preserve the delicate properties of the plant (Rogers). Violet flower syrup mixed with almond oil is a “capital laxative for children” (W. T. Fernie, 1914, 541).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Claudia Wingo (1), Flower Essence Society (2, 3), Plinius (4), Chalid Ottway (5, 20), Ben Charles Harris (7, 9), Dorothy Hall (8, 9), W. T. Fernie (11, 12, 13, 16, 17, 20), John Hill (12, 14), David Hoffmann (14, 18), Hilde Hemmes (19).

## Viola tricolor. Pansy, Heartsease.

Wild pansy has found a small niche in European medicine as an external specific against eczema. This use originated in homeopathy. Modern studies have shown that it contains flavonoids, which reduce inflammation, and saponins, which are cleansing and diuretic. It also contains salicylates, rutin, mucilage, bitters, and resins. The root is emetic.
“Dr. Johnson derived the word Pansy from panacea, as curing all diseases, but this was a mistake. The true derivation is from the French pensee, ‘thought,’ as Shakespeare knew, when making Ophelia say: ‘There is Pansies—that’s for thoughts’ ” (W. T. Fernie, 1914, 539). And since the heart is associated with thoughts, we arrive also at the name “heartsease.” The original use of this remedy was, therefore, to reduce excessive and unwanted thoughts. Hahnemann, the founder of homeopathy, showed that Viola tricolor produced skin eruptions and reasoned out that it was a remedy for milk crust and scald head in children, and eczema in adults.

### PREPARATIONS, CAUTIONS, AND DOSAGE:

Dosage of the tincture: 15–20 drops, 2x/day. External preparations are also used. It is not considered entirely safe in pregnancy. It probably has many of the same uses as common violet.

## Viscum album. Mistletoe.

The druids valued mistletoe highly for its religious symbolism and medicinal properties. It continued to have a place in European folk practice for many centuries. Its most popular current use, as a remedy for high blood pressure, is of recent origin. For an account of how it was formerly conceived to operate we need to turn to an older source. Richard Hool (1922, 77) gives his usual excellent description of these medicinal properties, quoted below. He valued it highly as a nervine relaxant. Viscum contains flavonoids, sugars, protein, amines, terpenoids, sterols, mucilage, and tannins. Active ingredients include lectins, which stimulate neutrophils, and viscotoxin, which is cardioselective and reduces the ventricular rate. Rudolf Steiner introduced the use of mistletoe for cancer, based on the doctrine of signatures. Some of the flavonoids, sugars, and proteins have shown antineoplastic properties.
“As a nervine tonic I value mistletoe as superior to all other remedies I have come in contact with. It is now about 35 years since I first made use of it in cases of nervous complaints, and during that time I have had many opportunities of noting the results which have accrued from its employment. Where the decoction of mistletoe is given in from one or two teaspoonfuls to one wineglassful, according to the age of the patient, and the severity of the case, every one, two, or three hours, particularly in fits, epilepsy, and St. Vitus’ dance, it will be found that it soon soothes and quiets the irritability of the nervous system, at the same time giving tone and regularity of action, diminishing cerebral excitement, abating delirium, and controlling febrile excitement; exciting diaphoresis and diuresis, and accomplishing its work without any subsequent unpleasant reaction.” One can see the action on the circulation and heart in this description. This sort of usage led to the application of viscum in cardiovascular problems and high blood pressure.
Dr. Tascher, in 1892, published his findings of the utility of viscum in the treatment of cardiovascular irregularities and diseases. It is beneficial for hypertrophy of the heart, with valvular insufficiency, edema of the extremities, a small, weak pulse, shortness of breath with inability of the patient to recline on the back. Doses of 20–30 minims of the fluid extract produced a full, strong, regular pulse, arrested the dyspnea, and allowed the patient to rest in a recumbent position. In some cases, given in large doses, it produced diaphoresis, diuresis, and serous discharges from the bowels. These actions are helpful in edema, though this effect did not always occur. Subsequently, viscum came into use as a remedy for the reduction of high blood pressure, especially where there is arteriosclerosis.
Mistletoe opens up circulation to areas that have been cut off from adequate blood supply by tension. It reduces cardiovascular tension and blood pressure, while bringing in new blood, blood sugar, and healing to areas long cut off, tensed up, weakened, or damaged. It thus acts on congestion of the ovaries, uterus, and prostate.
Dr. Brodnax, of Louisiana, introduced viscum into use for obstetric purposes. Dr. Finley Ellingwood (1918, 153) was probably the best writer on obstetrics of any of the nineteenth-century botanical doctors. His accounts are most detailed, bringing forth the specific indications and character of the remedy. “It is indicated where there is a flow of blood to the brain, and frequent headache and flushing of the face. In hysteria, epilepsy and other nervous diseases; in paroxysms of tearing and rending pains, rheumatic and neuralgic, it is a pain subduer of much power. With the above conditions it is exceedingly valuable in diseases of women, in amenorrhea, dysmenorrhea, and as an oxytocic.
“In its action on the womb it is in some particulars superior to ergot. It is a drug capable of producing intermittent uterine action, as distinguished from the tonic contractions caused by some other oxytocic medicines. It exerts its full force on the long muscles of the uterus, acting on the fundus mainly, while the cervix remains soft and uncontracted.
“It may be given early in labor to give tone to the contractions; does not act spasmodically, but steadily and for a long time; it is not followed by any untoward effect; does not, like ergot, produce hourglass contractions; has a tendency to keep the womb contracted after the expulsion of the placenta and attachments; does not act on the circular muscles of the womb; is a safe oxytocic, as the effects can be continued for hours with small doses.” It can induce labor, and is contraindicated during pregnancy, except to facilitate labor in its correct time. Herbalist Kim Dudley, of Canberra, Australia, adds that viscum is also useful for hot flashes in menopausal women.
Mistletoe was at one time considered a specific for cramping, pain, and convulsion due to typhoid fever and high fevers damaging the fluids and nervous apparatus. It is specific for heart problems resulting from typhoid. It is used for asthmatic spasm, but is not supposed to be used while persons are on asthma drugs.
Eva Graf (1978, 107) says it is a powerful medicine for people who have ended a relationship and are not very happy about it. She uses it when a person has suffered the sudden loss of a loved one or a pet. She says mistletoe helps a person breathe through the emotional problem, so they can breathe more deeply. It thus promotes contemplation, reflection, and recovery. It releases nervous tension and pain associated with uptight breathing patterns and psychological resistance. One of her students pointed out that mistletoe is associated with positive feelings of the heart, like kissing under the mistletoe, but also with loss, grief, and loneliness. It soothes the nervous system and brings peace to the mind and heart. Because it is such a powerful cardiac drug we must use it with mindfulness and heartfulness. It requires wisdom, care, compassion, and caution and it brings this to the people who need it. Mistletoe is an old plant of the druids, the wise.
Rudolf Steiner introduced mistletoe for the treatment of cancer. He was led to this insight through the doctrine of signatures—the parasitical mistletoe reminds one of the parasitical cancer growing on the organism. Research has shown that if mistletoe is mixed with cancer cells it will puncture the cell walls. Although there is not much record of the use of viscum for cancer in the folk medical record, occasional references are found. New Zealand herbalist James Neil (1998, 38) wrote in 1891, “The juice of the plant evaporated makes birdlime. Applied to tumours it softens and heals them; while mixed with rosin it is said to make a good ointment in ulcers.”

### TISSUE STATE:

constriction
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
MIND, SENSES, NERVES, EMOTIONS, PERSONALITY
Mental tension, emotional pain, loss of a loved one.
Mental disturbance, nervousness, hysteria.
HEAD
Migraine headache.
Flow of blood to the brain, frequent headache, flushing of the face.
Delirium, febrile excitement.
RESPIRATION
Asthma, spasm of the lungs, spasmodic coughing.
DIGESTION
Cholera.
CARDIOVASCULAR
High blood pressure.
Hypertrophy of the heart with valvular insufficiency, edema of the extremities.
Small, weak pulse, shortness of breath.
KIDNEYS AND BLADDER
Bedwetting.
Edema; increases flow of urine.
FEMALE
Inflammation of the womb, cramping, excessive bleeding with pain.
Amenorrhea.
Oxytocic.
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Epilepsy, convulsions; paroxysmal tearing and rending pains, neuralgia.
Wild swings between tension and relaxation and swings in blood pressure.
Tension of muscles, head, spine, heart, lungs, intestines, uterus.
OTHER
Hypoglycemia.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The whole plant is gathered in berry. That from the oak was supposed to be the best (here we still see the influence of the druids) but Hool says that mistletoe from any tree will suffice, even though the parasite picks up the taste of the tree it lives on. It is impaired by exposure to air and stored in airtight jars for use as tea. It is also used in tablets, extracts, and tinctures. “The remedy has failed because the agent used was inert, dried and, perhaps, old. The preparations must be made from the green plant, and the dose must be sufficiently large and frequently given. In some cases it may be necessary to repeat the dose every fifteen minutes” (Ellingwood, 1918, 153). Even the specific medicine, which the eclectics used in some medicines as low as a drop a dose, was given in 1–40 minims. The normal dose of the tincture is 5–60 minims.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Not to be used during pregnancy. Not to be used when taking drugs for asthma.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Richard Hool (4, 5, 12, 16), Dr. Tascher (8, 9, 10, 12), Finley Ellingwood (2, 13, 14, 15, 16, 18), Eva Graf (1), Glenda Croft (17), Louise Tenney (2, 3, 6, 7, 11, 19).

## Vitex agnus-castus. Chaste Berry.

Chaste tree is a shrub native to the Mediterranean region but grown more widely as an ornamental. The berry was used in medieval Europe to decrease the sex drive and stimulate lactation. It has been studied in modern pharmacology and used on the idea that it increases progesterone and decreases estrogen. The pungent, warm, stimulating berry contains volatile oils, iridoid glycosides, flavonoids, and bitters. It is a cousin of the vervains.
Vitex is one of the darlings of modern phytotherapy. It is believed that it contains a substance that binds with dopamine receptors in the pituitary. It therefore influences pituitary secretion, including prolactin (controls lactation) and luteinizing hormone (promotes progesterone). Although it has been used successfully to remove ovarian cysts (associated with high FSH and estrogen levels?) it has also caused ovarian cysts (progesterone can accelerate the formation of the corpus luteum, trapping the egg in the ovary and causing a watery cyst). So care should be taken in its use.
I never managed to understand vitex or get it to work based on pharmacological theory. Instead, I learned from herbalists who had developed an understanding of its nature through clinical experience. The independent testimony of the following three herbalists (from Massachusetts, Colorado, and Australia) dovetails nicely.
Herbalist Margi Flint says vitex is a remedy for people “caught between the material and spiritual worlds.” They are striving for spirit, but “not yet there.” A door slams shut on their path. “It is a five-finger plant,” she remarks. It resembles the hand, “showing that there is interference, but also the capacity for change and freedom.” It is indicated in puberty in both sexes. It is beneficial for boys who are oversexed, preoccupied by sex, obsessed. “It calms them down.” In younger people it is indicated by the “hunter look.” It can normalize the cycle in teenage girls. Vitex is also indicated in menopause and older people in general, but here it tends to have a stimulating effect on the sex drive. “They had an expectation for excitement, spiciness and vitality in life but they feel that they missed it,” continues Margi. Now they have given up, “given up the ghost.” Chaste berry “brings back spice and rekindles the flame.” In older people “there is often a history of interference, of some kind, in their libido, from old stuff in childhood, teen years or young adulthood, with years of suppression.”
Herbalist Matthew Becker reports that vitex is a reliable remedy for hysteria, nervousness, and hyperactivity. These indications are ancient and traditional. He cites sources from medieval Egypt and contemporary South America. In this capacity it acts on men as well as women. It is especially indicated when “there is a feeling of being hunted,” he says. “Something is after you, looking over your shoulder.” (I guess H. P. Lovecraft could have used some chaste berry.) There is difficulty falling asleep. Matthew Becker also associates this sensation with blue vervain—I associate it with lycopus. He combines vitex with motherwort in some cases. He suggests that the medieval use, to decrease the sex drive, is based on the reduction of nervousness and excessive preoccupation.
Herbalist Patricia Huenecke, of Canberra, Australia, has used vitex as a specific for attention deficit and hyperactivity in children. She has used it successfully in more than twenty cases. It controls the heat. She surmised that it works by stimulating the pituitary, which then normalizes the energy of the body.

### TASTE:

pungent • diffusive

### TISSUE STATE:

depression

### SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:

Hysteria, nervousness, hyperactivity.
The hunted, or hunter, look; feels like a hunted animal; looking over the shoulder; haunted.
Sexual obsession and preoccupation in teenagers, especially boys; inappropriate sexual acting out.
Irregular cycles in teenage girls.
Acne; male and female.
Feels a need for change.
Difficulty falling asleep.
Lactation: initiates or increases secretion of milk, normalizes when there is too much.
Depression since cessation of lactation.
Breast inflammation, tenderness, and pain; cystic breast growth.
Depression in menopause and older life; feels a lack of spiciness, sex drive.
Lack of sex drive from sexual abuse, exploitation, overuse (small doses).
Diarrhea.
Swollen prostate.
Amenorrhea, dysmenorrhea.
PMS with breasts swollen and painful.
Infertility, sterility (give during the second half of the cycle).
Uterine fibroids, bleeding fibromas, menorrhagia.
Ovarian cysts.
Menopause; hot flashes, night sweats (cf. Verbena hastata, a botanical cousin).
Heart palpitations, tachycardia.

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

The seeds yield a spicy preparation in alcohol. Dose of the tincture, 1–60 drops (John William Fyfe). Standard dose, 15–20 drops in water, 2x/day.

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (3, 8), Matthew Becker (1, 2), Patricia Huenecke (1), Margi Flint (1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 7, 9, 11, 12, 18, 20), Christopher Menzies-Trull (5, 10, 13, 14, 15, 16, 17, 18, 20), William Holden (nineteenth-century homeopath, 12), Dan Kenner and Yves Requena (5, 8, 10, 15, 18, 21), David Hoffmann (20).

## Vitis vinifera. Grape Vine.

Various parts of the grape have been used in European folk and professional medicine from an early time. Wine was a byword for the basic needs of life—bread and wine. It reigned supreme throughout the Mediterranean. The alchemists believed that the grape was the highest production of the vegetable kingdom, and that all plants aspired to be the grape. It provided the primal substance used to extract the essence of medicinal plants, distilled alcohol or “spirits of wine.” All plants surrendered their essence of this “living water.” Thus grape was able to reveal the essential or spiritual nature of the creation. Even today, wine is recognized to have powerful medicinal properties. Grapes contain grape sugar (glucose), gum, tannins, minerals (bitartrate of potash, sulphate of potash, tartrate of calcium, magnesia, alum, iron, chlorides of potassium and sodium), fruit acids (tartaric, citric, racemic, malic), and albumin.
“We owe to the grape more medicines than to any one simple whatsoever,” wrote Sir John Hill (1740, 380). It is used in the form of wine, vinegar, tartar, grapes, grape leaves, grape vine, dried grapes (raisins), and other forms.
Among Mediterranean people wine is one of the fundamentals of life, along with water, wheat, and olive oil. It was used as a medicine from an early date. According to an old Talmudic saying, “Wine is the greatest of all medicines; where wine is lacking, drugs are necessary” (Maimonides; Rosner, 1989, III:239). This is very much the approach we find among the Hippocratic and Galenic physicians. It was widely used by laypeople as a home remedy. A quaint example of a layperson recommending wine for the stomach occurs in Paul’s letter to his disciple Timothy. In the Middle Ages herb wines were used to preserve and give herbal remedies. From the fourteenth century onward it was distilled, making a very good long-term preservative. Today we know that wine contains resveratrol, a substance believed to contribute to a healthy heart, popularly marketed as Pycnogenol®. One glass of red wine contains 160 mg. of resveratrol (David Hoffmann, 2003, 99).
WINE
The ancient Greek physicians used wine as one of their main remedies, by itself, diluted with water, vinegar, vinegar diluted with honey, or as a medium for giving herbs. The ancients universally held that wine was warming and thinning to the blood, therefore that it palliated headache, stomachache, and menstrual pain caused by congestion of blood. As a medicine, red wine is usually given, though there are exceptions.
The medieval authors continued to speak highly of it. Hildegard von Bingen (1994, 125) sings praises of all sorts of products of the vine. “Wine is the blood of the earth and is in the earth as blood is in human beings. It has a certain commonality with human blood.” Commenting on the fact that Noah was given the grapevine and the knowledge to make wine by God, Hildegard (1998, 132) writes: “Before the flood the earth was fragile, and it did not produce wine. When it had been moistened and strengthened by the flood, it produced wine. The earth now is to the earth before the flood as gravel is to the present earth.” In other words, it is now more dense. This visionary account is similar to that of a modern seer, Rudolf Steiner.
Moses Maimonides, the great Rabbi, wrote that it was a religious duty to rejoice, and therefore to drink wine appropriately. He recommended it as a medicine in full strength or diluted. “People suffering from a strong midline headache or the like, secondary to thick blood or internal coldness, are overly benefited by drinking undiluted wine.” He explains that it is warming and thinning. Wine diluted with an equal amount of water stimulates the body, warms the stomach, aids digestion, dissolves cold gases, warms the limbs, combats shivering, and thins, warms, and improves all the liquids of the body (Maimonides; Rosner, 1989, III:239).
Hildegard (1998, 132) recommends wine as an external vulnerary. “If someone had ulcers on his body or has been struck with a wound, he should add a third part of olive oil to pure, good wine. He should gently heat this oily wine, when the ulcer or wound is large, when it shows blackness, or pus on the second or third day. He should dip a linen cloth in it, and rub the ulcer or wound with it until the pus diminishes. If the wound or ulcer is small, he should dip a feather in the oily wine, when it is cold, not heated. He should gently clean the ulcer or wound with it until the pus diminishes.”
SPIRITS OF WINE
The distillation process was invented in the late Middle Ages, making possible the concentration of the alcohol in the wine. This produced “spirits of wine,” which was used to preserve herbs and medicine as tinctures and extracts. The alcohol does not deteriorate and, in sufficient amount (more than 20%), preserves vegetable contents. Spirits of wine was associated with the spirit or essence, because of its ability to extract and preserve the essence of the herb.
Spirits of wine concentrated to 80 proof (40% alcohol) is called brandy. As a medicine it is considered warming to the stomach and is sometimes taken warm with milk to sedate a nervous stomach or warm someone coming in from the cold. This is in line with the ancient uses of wine.
VINEGAR
Ordinary wine in the presence of air deteriorates into vinegar. This is a stable substance, like spirits of wine, which can be used to preserve herbs and medicines. The Greeks considered vinegar to be sour and cooling, and used it to bring out the cooling side of herbs.
Galen writes in his Greater Art of Medicine, “To find a rarefying medication that is truly completely cooling [in its effect] is something nearly impossible because vinegar, which is the most thinning of all known cooling medications that we use in therapeutics, contains some warmth and it exerts a drying [action]. Therefore, we mix into it a quantity of cold water to render it drinkable and we use it for any illness that requires cooling and moistening.
“Vinegar, in addition to fineness of its parts, has an expulsion force and a not insignificant hindrance effect. For this reason, physicians use it at the inception of cerebral illness, after having mixed oil of roses therein.” Vinegar of rose petal was a famous medicine for inflammation and fever. “If several days of the illness have already passed, mix some thyme together with the aforementioned two until they [not only] thin but warm because this medication weakens with time and its potency dissolves because of the sour substance therein.” During the dispersive “stages of an abscess next to the brain, there is no better medication.”
Galen also gives contraindications: “Vinegar produces damage in the womb and in other organs [richly endowed] with nerves because of its coolness, its thinness and its sinking into these [organs]” (above quotations from Maimonides; Rosner, 1989, III:318, 316).
OXYMEL
An essential food and remedy in Greek medicine was oxymel, or a mixture of vinegar and honey. Galen writes, “Oxymel is the most appropriate of sweet things used for a simple diet. In addition, it does not produce bad chymes, does not harm the stomach, and has nothing at all bad in it. It is also the strongest pulverizer [cutter] of all foods; and not only among foods but also among all medications.” In other words, it cuts mucus in the stomach.
“We give oxymel to some patients to drink as a medication, not as a food.”
“Oxymel that is only mildly acidic moistens the mouth and the palate, aids in the expectoration of sputum, alleviates thirst, opens the obstruction that occurs in the liver and spleen and cleanses them without harm. It also neutralizes biles that develop from the honey [in the oxymel], dissolves the bloating gases, stimulates urine flow by opening the closure, and facilitates the decent of superfluities and biles into the intestines without any harm whatsoever, in addition to which it expands the intestines [i.e., causes diarrhea], if constantly used.”
“Indeed, the vinegar and honey drink is occasionally prepared with three parts of honey to one part of vinegar. A more pleasant mixture is one with seven parts of honey and one part of vinegar. It should be cooked until [the ingredients are] well mixed and some of the foam should be removed therefrom.”
There are adverse side effects. “Oxymel, if used excessively, produces diarrhea in the intestines, stimulates a cough and damages nerve organs” (above quotations from Maimonides; Rosner, 1989, III:317).
The Old English name for vinegar and honey drink is “switchel.” Under this name it is still sometimes remembered in rural areas along the eastern seaboard. Switchel was used as a drink at harvesting time to cool off the workers. Sometimes ginger was added. Many people also considered it a general health tonic, to be consumed year-round. Later, when soda pop appeared, switchel was forgotten.
Dr. Jarvis, in his writings on Vermont folk medicine, brought the use of a daily drink of honey and vinegar back into modern attention. He commented that in the old days (before 1900) the store in his town sold more than fifty barrels of vinegar a year but now (1965) it sold fewer than ten.
Vinegar and other acids like lemon juice and whey are highly tart to the taste but their internal effect is alkalinizing and they antidote the ill-effects of the overly sweet and acidic diet we have today. (I myself try to take lemon every day. Vinegar does not agree with me as well. Concentrated whey is my favorite in taste, but does not help me as much as lemon.)
TARTAR
The tartar, or concretions formed in the bottoms of wine barrels, is used as medicine. The word comes from Tartarus, the Greek Hell (W. T. Fernie, 1914). Tartar is high in potassium salts and is beneficial for the kidneys. It was probably used on the principle of “like treats like” to rid the body of gravel. Grape tartar was a favorite remedy of Paracelsus.
BUDS
The different parts of the grapevine were used in herbal medicine, though the use of the buds is modern, coming from the school of “gemmotherapy.” The buds have been used for treating scarlatina and preventing circulatory complications that result from it (cf. Lycopus). It also acts on circulatory problems in menopause, hemorrhage, and red blotches on the face (Kenner and Requena, 2001).
GRAPEVINE DRIPPINGS
This is a very ancient remedy for cataract, glaucoma, and corneal damage. Hildegard von Bingen recommended grapevine drippings. “Anyone with blurry vision and cloudy eyes should lubricate the eyelids often with the grape drops, which run out of the vine after cutting, and allow a little to run into the eye. This will clear the eyes without a doubt” (quoted by Strehlow and Gottfried, 1988, 8). The latter authors add that grapevine drippings can be combined with apple flower extract for this purpose. They also testify to its use in pinkeye. W. T. Fernie (1914) gives this same usage for vine drippings, and the combination of vine drippings and juice of the wild crab apple for bruises.
Hildegard also recommends grapevine drippings preserved in a bottle or used as an ointment for earache. Collect the drippings between early morning and noon, or they will spoil. To make a grapevine dripping eye or ear medicine collect the drippings in a bottle until it is nine-tenths full, then add one-tenth olive oil (Strehlow and Gottfried, 1988, 11).
GRAPEVINE ASH
Hildegard von Bingen recommended grapevine ash in warm wine as a tooth powder. Pulverize grapevine ash, add while still warm to a good wine, and brush the teeth regularly for gingivitis and teeth problems (Strehlow and Gottfried, 1988, 15). The latter authors remark that it shines the teeth and improves gum health. They recommend that the teeth be brushed with cold spring or well water to harden them; soft water softens them. (Spring and well water is usually hard or alkaline.)
GRAPE
The fruit itself of the vine is rich in alkaline minerals, especially the sour wines (Chablis) grown on lime. These minerals precipitate out of solution as tartar in the wine cask, but plenty of alkalinity is transferred to the wine and vinegar, which are also alkalinizing. However, mineral content is highest in the grapes themselves, especially in the dark varieties. “They help greatly to decrease the acidity of the uric acid and to eliminate the acid from the system, thus immensely benefiting kidney function” (Harris, 1972, 105). A diet of seeded grapes alone has been used as an antidote to the formation of gravel and stones. Grapes make a good fever food since they supply blood sugar and are also restorative after much bleeding (W. T. Fernie, 1914).
GRAPE LEAF
Galen writes, “Among the things which most greatly benefit the stomach are those [qualitatively intermediate] between bitter and astringent such as blackberry leaves and vine leaves. All astringent things are beneficial to the stomach in most situations” (Maimonides; Rosner, 1989, III:319).
“The tendrils and leaves of grape vine (from unsprayed vines), also fresh grape juice, have been used very effectively by Arabs as an internal and external cure for tumours” (Juliette de Bairacli Levy, 1973, 106), here denominating swellings, not cancer.
GRAPE SEED OIL
Even the grape seed yields a valuable culinary and medicinal oil. It is notable because it is very penetrating, carrying constituents in a salve or oil deeper into the tissues than other vegetable oils. It is not as penetrating as skunk oil (lard of the skunk).
RAISINS
“Raisins of the sun,” or dried grapes, are “in constant repute” for “coughs and soreness of the lungs, and in consumptions” or wasting, writes Sir John Hill (1740, 379). “Raisin-tea is found to be of the same protein value as milk, and much more easily digested, therefore of superior use in many cases of gastric disease where milk or soups (vegetable or animal) must be disallowed,” writes W. T. Fernie (1914, 222). “For persons who suffer from coldness of the feet and hands, it is very warming and cherishing.” Raisins are a normalizer for both diarrhea and constipation (Juliette de Bairacli Levy, 1973).
A folk remedy for arthritis is raisins soaked in gin, about nine a day for several months. The juniper in the gin may be the active ingredient.
THE GRAPE CURE
This idea appeared in the late nineteenth century in Europe. Juliette de Bairacli Levy (1973, 146) describes it most elegantly. “When the human body has become sick almost beyond reasonable hope of recovery, there is still, to my mind, one recourse: for the patient to retire to the neighborhood of some vineyard where grapes are cultivated by natural methods (that is without artificial fertilizers, chemical sprays, and heavy irrigation), and there to follow a grape cure, living only on the fruit of the vine (with a few vine leaves and tendrils eaten also) and drinking only pure water.”

### CAUTIONS:

Grapes and wine are contraindicated in persons with sluggish digestion because they cause fermentation and acidity in the stomach (W. T. Fernie, 1914).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional, Hippocrates, Galen, Hildegard von Bingen, Moses Maimonides, Paracelsus, John Hill, D. Jarvis, Ben Charles Harris, W. T. Fernie.

## Zingiber officinale. Ginger.

Ginger is native to southeastern Asia, but is widely cultivated in tropical countries throughout the world. The rhizome is used in cooking and folk medicine. The pungent, sweet taste is due to the presence of two oleoresins, gingerol and shogaol, and volatile oils. Many of the traditional folk medical uses have been verified by modern pharmacology.
The warm, stimulating properties of the fresh rhizome suit it to cases where the organism or a part is cold, depressed, cramped up, or inactive. It is suited to people who feel chilly. A hot tea or soup with fresh ginger rhizome quickly opens the skin to release perspiration, chill, cold, and flu. It warms up the stomach to improve digestion, increases bile secretion, improves fat digestion and movement of food through the tract, reducing stagnation, irritation, and gas. It reduces nausea of motion sickness, pregnancy, and other causes.
A poultice of fresh ginger rhizome on the chest increases blood circulation, activates respiration, and begins to move the mucus out of the lungs. Thus it is used in pneumonia, like onion and mustard packs. In addition, of course, it stimulates diaphoresis.
Ginger has been shown to reduce platelet aggregation and thus thin the blood. This increases circulation and is cooling to the system. The rhizome, which looks like a hand with fingers, is used to reduce heat and stiffness in arthritis. As a stimulant and blood thinner it increases circulation to the part and moves fluids through the cartilage and joint, which lies beyond the reach of the capillary bed. The gingerols have been shown to reduce levels of leukotrienes and prostaglandins that stimulate inflammation. Ginger oil is excellent as an external liniment on stiff, cold, spasmed muscles.
The fresh rhizome, used in cooking, tea, or even given in tincture (30 drops, once a day), contains enough oil to be nourishing. It regulates blood sugar levels and increases immune tone.
Herbalist Margi Flint comments, “Ginger is specific for labor and delivery. It connects the mother to the child in the womb. The rhizome looks like that little expected baby and expresses the warmth of mother love. Have everyone in the room drinking the tea. It unites them all in the spirit of the delivery. In long labors it keeps up the strength. Add a little honey or maple syrup to make blood sugar available.
“Ginger is better suited to the kapha constitution (large, damp, cold) than the pitta (hot) or vata (dry), because it is so warming and stimulating. If needed for people who are not cold use in small amounts.”
Ginger oil is a superlative antispasmodic for cramped muscles, especially in chronic cases that are aggravated, because it is warming and activating. It would be contraindicated in inflamed muscles. I have often used this for spasm in the back or neck. If the oil is unavailable, some other preparation may be used.

### TASTE:

pungent, sweet • warm, moist • diffusive, stimulating

### TISSUE STATES:

depression, constriction, atrophy
SPECIFIC INDICATIONS:
HEAD
Headache, migraine, vertigo; especially associated with motion sickness.
RESPIRATION
Incipient colds, postnasal drip, influenza (hot decoction of the fresh rhizome as a diaphoretic).
Swollen glands in throat, sore throat.
Stuck mucus in the chest, pneumonia, bronchitis (warm poultices).
Dry, ticklish cough (tea).
DIGESTION
Increases or decreases appetite.
Lack of appetite, saliva, stomach acid (candied rhizome).
Inactive gastrointestinal mucosa; with indigestion, fullness, catarrh, flatulence, colic, spasm, diarrhea, dysentery; sometimes associated with dietary indiscretion, tainted food, or chills (warm tea of the fresh rhizome; candied rhizomes; capsules).
Flatulence caused by ingesta in a state of decomposition.
Gastritis of alcoholics.
Sluggish liver.
Peptic ulcers.
Digestive aid (a cup of the fresh rhizome tea in late afternoon before dinner).
Nausea of food poisoning.
Nausea of travel sickness and seasickness.
Nausea of pregnancy; “Whoa, my world is moving, where’s the horizon?” (Margi Flint).
Diarrhea of relaxed bowel, or colic, spasm.
Intestinal fermentation and putrefaction; dysentery, cholera.
HEART AND CIRCULATION
Poor peripheral circulation; coldness, stagnant blood; sticky platelets (warm tea, bath, fresh rhizome; bath of dried rhizome can cause irritation of sensitive tissue).
Blood thinner; to wean off coumadin; 2 capsules daily of ginger plus 1 tablespoon of flaxseed oil; work with their doctor (William Mitchell).
FEMALE
Warms up a cold uterus; relieves period cramps due to cold and stagnant blood (warm poultice of the fresh rhizome).
Cramps that precede the period.
Specific for parturition, labor, and delivery: maintains strength and uterine contractions (warm tea of the fresh rhizome; in prolonged labor add honey or maple syrup to provide immediate blood sugar).
MUSCULAR AND SKELETAL
Cold extremities (foot bath or tea, fresh rhizome).
Sore, cramped muscles (ginger oil or salve externally).
FEVER
Fever and chills, achy, can’t get warm enough.
Mosquito bites (bath).
OTHER
Drop in energy around three o’clock in the afternoon (warm tea).
Immune deficiencies; people feel exhausted, low energy; it is gentle and nourishing (food or tea of fresh rhizome).
Inflammation and pus in the eyes (infuse pulverized ginger root in wine overnight, apply externally).
“Mistiness of the eyes” (take the above wine, 3 parts, add 1 part rue and 1 part hyssop, apply externally).
Pimples (the above wine externally).

### PREPARATION AND DOSAGE:

Ginger rhizomes can usually be purchased fresh at a food store and are considered entirely safe. The dried rhizomes are so hot they can burn sensitive tissues and are seldom used. For tea grate 1/2 to 1 teaspoon of the fresh rhizome and steep 10 minutes. The candied rhizomes are used to warm the stomach. Average dose of the tincture of the fresh rhizome: 15–20 drops, children half that amount.

### CONTRAINDICATIONS:

Not dangerous, but too warming for some people and conditions. Dried ginger used in a bath can cause severe irritation of sensitive tissue (vagina).

### LITERATURE:

Traditional (1–27, 30–32), William Mitchell (1, 20), Margi Flint (16, 23, 28, 29), John Bastyr (22), Eva and Eugene Graf (2, 3, 6, 11, 27), Christopher Menzies-Trull (1, 7, 10, 12–14), Matthew Wood (25), John William Fyfe (8, 9), Hildegard von Bingen (8, 30, 31), Aviva Romm (4).
When you do not know the nature of a malady, leave it to nature; do not strive to hasten matters.
For either nature will bring about the cure or it will itself reveal clearly what the malady really is.
—AVICENNA (1999, 468)

Salmon, William, MD. Botanologia, or The English Herbal. London: 1710. [This is the last of the Renaissance woodcut herbals, full of many priceless gems of experience and pharmacy, yet little known today. Salmon was considered a lower class physician by the London College of Physicians. A look through his works shows that he was, however, a polymath or even a genius who mastered many subjects and wrote some of the best medical information of the time. His “sex education manual” (released under the name of “Aristotle”) was the only one of its kind in print in English from 1709 to the late nineteenth century. Salmon had some firsthand knowledge of North American flora and fauna, having participated in the settlement of South Carolina, which he calls the “West Indies.”]

[A nonherbalist talks to a bunch of highly experienced American Indian medicine people and reduces their information to almost worthless piecemeal.]
Erichsen-Brown, Charlotte. Medicinal and Other Uses of North American Plants: A Historical Survey with Special Reference to the Eastern Indian Tribes. New York: Dover, 1989. [An excellent general survey.]
Garrett, J. T. The Cherokee Herbal: Native Plant Medicine from the Four Directions. Santa Fe: Bear & Co., 2003. [An outstanding work on American Indian medicine.]
Gates, William. An Aztec Herbal: The Classic Codex of 1552. 1st ed., Baltimore: The Maya Society, 1939. Mineola, NY: Dover Publications, 2000.
Jensen, Christina C. Z. “Indian John, Frontier Herbalist of the Kansas-Nebraska Prairie.” In The Herb Quarterly. Spring 1994.
Lake, Medicine Grizzlybear. Native Healer: Initiation into an Ancient Art. Wheaton, IL; Madras, India: Quest Books, Theosophical Publishing House, 1991. [The author, otherwise known as Bobby Lake-Thom, is one of the few authors whose works on Indian medicine are really deep and authentic.]
Mooney, James. Myths of the Cherokee and Sacred Formulas of the Cherokees. Reproduction of the 19th and 7th Annual Reports of the Bureau of American Ethnology. Nashville: Charles and Randy Elder–Booksellers, 1982.
Smith, Huron H. Ethnobotany of the Menomini Indians. Bulletin of the Public Museum of the City of Milwaukee. Vol. 4, no. 1. Milwaukee: Published by Order of the Board of Trustees, 1923. [One of the best of the old ethnobotanical studies since the author used herbs and was familiar with the eclectic materia medica.]
———. Ethnobotany of the Meskwaki Indians. Bulletin of the Public Museum of the City of Milwaukee. Vol. 4, no. 2. Milwaukee: Published by Order of the Board of Trustees, 1923.
———. Ethnobotany of the Ojibwe Indians. Bulletin of the Public Museum of the City of Milwaukee. Vol. 4, no. 3. Milwaukee: Published by Order of the Board of Trustees, 1923.
———. Ethnobotany of the Forest Potawatomi Indians. Bulletin of the Public Museum of the City of Milwaukee. Vol. 7, no. 1. Milwaukee: Published by Order of the Board of Trustees, 1933.
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, AMERICAN FOLK MEDICINE
Crellin, John K., and Jane Philpott. Herbal Medicine, Past and Present: Volume II, A Reference Guide to Medicinal Plants. Durham and London: Duke University Press, 1990. [An excellent example of the “historical” method of verifying the actions and uses of an herb. The authors trace the use of medicinal plants from early America to their own time, then compare with information received from a contemporary herbalist, Tommie Bass.]
Janos, Elisabeth. Country Folk Medicine: Tales of Skunk Oil, Sassafras Tea, & Other Old-Time Remedies. New York: Galahad Books, 1990. [Old-time New England and New York area remedies collected from old folks in rest homes.]
Leighton, Ann. Early American Gardens: “For Meate or Medicine.” Boston: Houghton Mifflin Co., 1970.
Mattison, Antonette. The Occult Family Physician and Botanic Guide to Health. 1st ed., 1894. Glenwood, IL: Meyerbooks, 1993.
Moss, Kay K. Southern Folk Medicine, 1750–1820. Columbia: University of South Carolina Press, 1999.
Patton, Darryl. Mountain Medicine: The Herbal Remedies of Tommie Bass. [Birmingham, AL]: Natural Reader Press, 2004. [An excellent account of Tommie Bass by his devoted student and friend. Not too different from Crellin and Philpott, but where there are differences Patton reflects Bass more faithfully.]
Payne-Jackson, Arvilla. Folk Wisdom and Mother Wit: John Lee—An African American Herbal Healer. Westport, CT: Greenwood Publishing Group, 1993. [A sympathetic view of a traditional African American herbalist; including diagnosis by the blood.]
Richel, Jonas. The Indian Physician. New Berlin, PA: Joseph Miller, 1828. Reprint.
Russell, Ralph, MD. Home Medicine Book. Birmingham, AL: Published by the author, 1911. [This book has an unusual section on Southern folk herbal material medica.]
Smith, Peter. The Indian Doctor’s Dispensatory Being Father Smith’s Advice Respecting Diseases and Their Cure. Originally published in Cincinnati, 1812. Reprint with Biography by John Uri Lloyd. Cincinnati: Lloyd Library, Bulletin of the Lloyd Library of Botany, Pharmacy and Materia Medica, 1901.
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, EARLY AMERICAN
Bigelow, Jacob, MD. American Medical Botany. Vol. 3. Boston: Cummings and Hilliard, 1817. [An excellent example of “attitude” by a “scientific doctor” differentiating the opinions of “experts” from uneducated folk practitioners.]
Brown, O. Phelps, MD. The Complete Herbal, or, The People Their Own Doctor. Jersey City, NJ: Published by the author, 1863.
Child, Mrs. [Lydia]. The Family Nurse; or Companion of the American Frugal Housewife. Revised by a member of the Massachusetts Medical Society. Boston: Charles J. Hendee. 1st ed., 1837. Reprint. Bedford, MA: Applewood Books, 1997.
Cobb, Daniel J. The Medical Botanist, and Expositor of Diseases and Remedies. 2 vols. Castile, NY: Published by the author, 1846.
Downing, Beal P., MD. Doctor Downing’s Reformed Practice and Family Physician. Utica, NY: Roberts & Sherman, 1851. [An extremely unique herbal by a frontier physician who “practiced without a book” for twenty years, so decided to write one.]
Eberle, John, MD. A Treatise of the Materia Medica and Therapeutics. 2 vols. 4th ed. Philadelphia: Grigg & Elliot, 1834.
Gunn, A. C., MD. Gunn’s Domestic Medicine, or Poor Man’s Friend. Revised ed. Louisville, KY: Alston Mygatt, Publisher, 1847.
Henry, Samuel, MD. New and Complete American Medical Family Herbal. New York: Published by the author, 1814. [This is the second popular herbal written and published in the United States, and the first to have significant readership. Henry’s herbal is based on considerable therapeutic experience and gives many interesting case histories and details of medical practice of the time. Unfortunately, the botanical nomenclature is often faulty.]
Howard, Horton, MD. A Treatise on the Complaints Peculiar to Females: Embracing a System of Midwifery: The Whole in Conformity with the Improved System of Botanic Medicine. Columbus, OH: Published by the author, 1832.
———. An Improved System of Botanic Medicine. 3 vols. Columbus, OH: Published by the author, 1836.
———. Howard’s Domestic Medicine. Cincinnati and Philadelphia: H. M. Rulison and D. Rulison, 1859.
Monroe, John. The American Botanist and Family Physician. Compiled by Silas Gaskill. Danville, VT: Eben’r Eaton, Printer, 1824.
Rafinesque, Constantine M. Medical Flora, or Manual of the Medical Botany of the United States. 2 vols. Philadelphia: Atkinson & Alexander, 1828–30. [The classic text on Indian and pioneer uses of plants of the Eastern Woodland region of North America in the early nineteenth century. This book apparently inspired the eclectics.]
Taylor, Henry S., MD. The Family Doctor: A Counsellor in Sickness, Pain, and Distress. Philadelphia: John E. Potter, 1860.
Thresher, Leonard, MD. The Family Physician, Nurse’s Guide, and Farmer’s Horse and Cattle Doctor. Montpelier, VT: Argus and Patriot Job Printing House, 1871.
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, THOMSONIAN AND PHYSIOMEDICAL
Clymer, R. Swinburne, MD. Nature’s Healing Agents: The Medicine of Nature, (or the Natura System). Revised ed. Quakertown, PA: The Humanitarian Society, 1973. [One of the few herbals kept in print throughout the twentieth century. Physiomedical.]
Coffin, A. I., MD. Botanic Guide to Health. 49th ed. London: Haynes, Coffin & Co., c. 1900. [The author and book that brought physiomedicalism to Britain.]
Colby, Benjamin. A Guide to Health, Being an Exposition of the Principles of the Thomsonian System of Practice. 4th ed., Milford, NH: John Burns, 1848. Reprint ed., Orem, UT: BiWorld Publishers, c. 1987.
Cook, William H., MD. The Physio-Medical Dispensatory: A Treatise on Therapeutics, Materia Medica, and Pharmacy. Original ed., Cincinnati: Published by the author, 1869. Reprint. Portland, OR: Eclectic Medical Publications, 1985. [The most important and comprehensive physiomedical materia medica.]
———. A Compendium of the New Materia Medica. Chicago: Published by the author, 1898.
Fox, William, MD. The Working-Man’s Model Family Botanic Guide. Sheffield: William Fox and Sons, Medical Botanists, 1916.
Hool, Richard Lawrence, FNAMH. Common Plants and Their Uses in Medicine. Lancashire: Lancashire Branch of the NAMH, 1922. [A unique and gifted herbalist.]
Menzies-Trull, Christopher. Herbal Medicine: Keys to Physiomedicalism, including Pharmacopoeia. Newcastle: Faculty of Physiomedical Herbal Medicine, 2003. [Physiomedicalism is not dead!]
Priest, A. W., and L. R. Priest. Herbal Medication: A Clinical and Dispensary Handbook. London: L. N. Fowler & Co., Ltd., 1982. [This is a late representative of the unbroken tradition of English physiomedicalism. The terminology has become so inbred that it is somewhat hard to understand in some places. Contains a brilliant pharmacopoeia.]
Shook, Edward, ND. Advanced Treastise in Herbology. New and Enlarged Edition. Lecture notes first published 1946. Beaumont, CA: Trinity Center Press, 1978. [A classic twentieth-century American herbal textbook.]
———. Elementary Treatise. Lecture notes first published 1946. Banning, CA: Enos Press, 1978.
Skelton, John. Family Medical Advisor. 11th ed. Leeds: Published by the author, 1878.
Slack, George. Slack’s Herbal: A Treatise on the Pathology of Disease, Designed for the Use of Families. London: Potter & Clarke, 1919.
Thomson, Samuel. The Thomsonian Materia Medica, or Botanic Family Physician. “Thirteenth edition.” Albany: J. Munsell, 1841. [Actually the work of John Thomson, with some editing by his father, Samuel.]
———. Life and Medical Discoveries of Samuel Thomson, and a History of the Thomsonian Materia Medica. Bulletin of the Lloyd Library. Cincinnati: Lloyd Library, 1909.
Webb, William Henry. Standard Guide to Non-Poisonous Herbal Medicine. Southport, UK: Visitor Printing Works, 1916.
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, ECLECTIC
Beach, Wooster, MD. The American Practice Condensed, or the Family Physician. New York: James M’Alister, 1849. [The complete works of Wooster Beach, student of a New Jersey root doctor and founder of the reform or eclectic medical movement.]
Brinker, Frances, ND, ed. The Eclectic Medical Journals: A Re-Printing of Selected Articles from the Eclectic Medical Journals. Vol. I, Issues 1–6. 1995.
Coe, Grover, MD. Concentrated Organic Medicines. 1st ed., 1858. 2nd ed. New York: R. Keith & Co., 1860. [Excellent description of plant uses.]
Ellingwood, Finley, MD. American Materia Medica, Therapeutics and Pharmacognosy. Originally published 1918. Reprint ed. Portland, OR: Eclectic Medical Publications, 1989. [Probably one of the greatest books on empirical materia medica.]
———. Ellingwood’s Therapeutist: A Journal of Specific Medicine. 9 vols. Chicago: Finley Ellingwood, 1907–1915. Modern digital copy. Bisbee, AZ: Southwestern School of Botanical Medicine, c. 2005.
Felter, Harvey, MD. Materia Medica. Originally published 1927. Reprint ed. Portland, OR: Eclectic Medical Publications, 1989.
Felter, Harvey, MD, and John Uri Lloyd, MD. King’s American Dispensatory. 11th ed., 3rd revision. Entirely Rewritten and Enlarged, 1898. 2 vols. Reprint. Portland, OR: Eclectic Medical Publications, 1983.
Fyfe, John William, MD. Specific Diagnosis and Specific Medication. Cincinnati: The Scudder Brothers, 1909. [Contains an important materia medica.]
Jones, Eli, MD. Cancer: Its Causes, Symptoms & Treatment. Reprint. New Delhi: B. Jain Publishers, Ltd., c. 1990.
———. Definite Medication. 1st ed., 1910. Reprint. New Delhi: B. Jain Publishers, Ltd., 1988.
Lighthall, J. I., and W. O. Davis, MD. The Indian Household Medicine Guide. Originally published, Peoria, IL: by the author, 1882. Paperback reprint retitled The Indian Folk Medicine Guide, New York: Popular Library, 1973. [J. I. Lighthall was a patent medicine salesman of extraordinary fame for a few years before his premature death. Despite the title, this is mostly a book on eclectic materia medica with little Indian medicine.]
Locke, Frederick J., MD. Syllabus of Eclectic Materia Medica and Therapeutics. Edited by Harvey W. Felter, MD, with notes by John Uri Lloyd. Cincinnati: John M. Scudder’s Sons, 1895. [A good, solid eclectic textbook.]
Petersen, Frederick J., MD. Materia Medica and Clinical Therapeutics. Los Olivos, CA: Published by the author, 1905. [Compares the physiological or toxic reaction with the primary and secondary reactions.]
Scudder, John M., MD. Specific Diagnosis. 1st ed., 1874. 9th ed., Cincinnati: Scudder Brothers, 1893.
———. Specific Medication and Specific Medicines. 4th revision. 10th ed. 1st ed., 1870. Cincinnati: Wilstach, Baldwin & Co., Printers, 1881.
Thomas, Rolla. Eclectic Practice of Medicine. Cincinnati: Scudder Brothers, 1903.
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, MODERN AMERICAN, ENGLISH, CANADIAN, AUSTRALIAN, ETC.
de Bairacli Levy, Juliette. The Complete Herbal Book for the Dog. American ed. New York: Arco Publishing, 1973. [The author, who is still with us, approaching a hundred years of age, is at her best as a practitioner and writer in her most beloved activity, the treatment of dogs.]
———. Common Herbs for Natural Health. American ed. New York: Schocken Books, 1974.
Brooke, Elisabeth. Herbal Therapy for Woman. New York: HarperCollins, 1992.
Cameron, Matthew K. Herbal Remedies from Nature’s Pharmacy. Toronto: Canadian Free Press, a division of Magyar Napolo, 1989.
Cech, Richo. Making Plant Medicine. Williams, OR: Horizon Herbs Publishing, 2000.
Cerney, J. V., DPM. Handbook of Unusual and Unorthodox Healing Methods. West Nyack, NY: Parker Publishing Co., Inc., 1976.
Christopher, John R., ND. The School of Natural Healing. Original ed. 1976. Revised and Expanded 20th Anniversary Edition. Springville, UT: Christopher Publications, 1996. [The compendium from the most important American herbalist of the mid-twentieth century. Notable for its complete lack of an “energetic” perspective. An important modern book. Christopher was a physiomedicalist.]
Dewey, Laurel. Plant Power: The Humorous Herbalist’s Guide to Finding, Growing, Gathering & Using 30 Great Medicinal Herbs. East Canaan, CT: ATN/Safe Goods, 1999.
Elliot, Rose, and de Paoli, Carlo. Kitchen Pharmacy. London: Tiger Books International, 1991. [An excellent modern herbal with good “energetics” and beautiful culinary applications of healing herbs.]
Fleming, Thomas, ed. PDR for Herbal Medicines. Montvale, NJ: Medical Economics Company, 1998.
Gagnon, Daniel. Liquid Herbal Drops in Everyday Use. 4th ed. Santa Fe: Botanical Research and Education Institute, Inc., 2000.
Gosling, Nalda. Herbs for Colds and Flu. Boulder, CO: Shambhala, 1981.
Grieve, M[aude], FRHS. A Modern Herbal. Edited and introduction by Mrs. C. F. Leyel. 1st ed., 1931. Reprint. London: Tiger Books International, 1994. [An indispensable classic of modern herbalism.]
Hall, Dorothy. Creating Your Herbal Profile. New Canaan, CT: Keats Publishing Co., 1988. [American edition of a great book by the great Australian herbalist.]
Harris, Ben Charles. The Compleat Herbal. Barre, MA: Barre Publishers, 1972. [The second book, after William Coles’, based upon the doctrine of signatures; very influential in the education of the present author.]
Hatfield, Audrey Wynne. A Herb for Every Ill. London: J. M. Dent & Sons, Ltd., 1973.
Hemmes, Hilde. Herbs with Hilde Hemmes. Edited by Andrew Tobin. 1st ed., 1992. 4th ed. Ridgehaven, AU: 1995.
Holmes, Peter. The Energetics of Western Herbs. Vol 1. 3rd ed. Boulder, CO: Snow Lotus Press, 1997.
Johnson, Stewart. Feverfew. London: Sheldon Press, 1984.
Kloss, Jethro. Back to Eden: A Human Interest Story of Health and Restoration to Be Found in Herb, Root and Bark. 1st ed., 1939. Coalmont, TN: Longview Publishing House, 1962.
Kuts-Cheraux, A. W., MD, ND. Naturae Medicina and Naturopathic Dispensatory. Des Moines, IA: American Naturopathic Physicians and Surgeons Association, 1953.
Law, Donald. The Concise Herbal Encyclopedia. New York: St. Martin’s Press, 1973.
Leyel, Mrs. C. F. Herbal Delights. Original ed. 1938. New York: Gramercy Publishing Co., 1986.
Lucas, Richard. Magic Herbs for Arthritis, Rheumatism, and Related Ailments. West Nyack, NY: Parker Publishing Co., 1981.
———. Miracle Medicine Herbs. West Nyack, NY: Parker Publishing Co., 1991.
Luetjohann, Sylvia. The Healing Power of Black Cumin. Twin Lakes, WI: Lotus Light, 1998.
Masseurt, Otto. Herbs for Health. Originally published 1932. 5th ed. San Francisco: Mercury Press, 1951.
Mitchell, William A., ND. Plant Medicine: Using the Teachings of Dr. John Bastyr. Edinburgh: Churchill Livingstone, 2003. [Preserves the very precise indications given by Dr. Bastyr, the prominent Seattle naturopath. An excellent example of empirical medicine, and a beautiful instance of the loving preservation of the work of a master by a student.]
Moore, Michael. Medicinal Plants of the Mountain West. Revised and expanded ed. Santa Fe: Museum of New Mexico Press, 2003. [The great classic text of Western American medicinal plants.]
Neil, James. The New Zealand Family Herb Doctor: A Guide to Recipes and Herbal Remedies. First published in 1891 by Mills, Dick & Company, Dunedin, New Zealand. Reprint. Twickenham, UK: Senate, an Imprint of Tiger Books International, 1998.
Ritchason, Jack, ND. The Little Herb Encyclopedia. 3rd ed. Pleasant Grove, UT: Woodland Health Books, 1995. [A good review of modern herb uses, but lacking in energetics.]
Roberts, Frank, Captain, MNIMH. The Encyclopaedia of Digestive Disorders. London: Thorsons Publishers, Ltd., 1957. [Excellent.]
Rogers, Robert Dale. Rogers’ Herbal Manual. Edmonton, Alberta: Karamat Wilderness Ways, 2000. [An excellent treatment of our far northern pharmacopoeia.]
Santillo, Humbart. Natural Healing with Herbs: A Complete Manual for the Use of Herbs for Every Dimension of Life. Prescott Valley, AZ: Hohm Press, 1984.
Smith, Keith Vincent. The Illustrated Earth Garden Herbal: A Herbal Companion. Victoria, Australia: Lothian, 1978.
Tenney, Louise. Today’s Herbal Health. 2nd ed. Provo, UT: Woodland Books, 1983. [A good review of modern herb uses, but lacking in energetics. Good documentation of sources.]
Thomas, Lalitha. 10 Essential Herbs. Originally published 1992. 2nd ed. Prescott, AZ: Hohm Press, 1996.
Tierra, Michael, CA, OMD. Planetary Herbology: An Integration of Western Herbs into the Traditional Chinese and Ayurvedic Systems. Edited and supplement by David Frawley, OMD, and Christopher Hobbs, CA. Santa Fe: Lotus Press, 1988.
Tietze, Harald W. Herbal Teaology. Bermagui South, AU: Published by the author, 1996. [Full of Australian color and very precise indications on tea.]
Watt, Martin, and Wanda Sellar. Frankincense & Myrrh: Through the Ages and a Complete Guide to Their Use in Herbalism and Aromatherapy Today. Saffron Waldon: The C. W. Daniel Co., Limited, 1996.
Willard, Terry, PhD Textbook of Modern Herbology. Calgary: Progressive Publishing Inc., 1988.
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, NUTRITIONAL APPROACH
Adams, Rex. Miracle Medicine Foods. Paramus, NJ: Prentice Hall, 1977. [Provides case histories.]
Clymer, R. Swinburne, MD. The Natura Physician: Inculcating the Natura System of Therapeutics for the Prevention and Elimination of Disease. 2 vols. Quakertown, PA: Philosophical Publishing Company, 1933. [Surprisingly different from Clymer’s other book on herbalism.]
Harris, Ben Charles. Kitchen Medicines. 1st ed., 1961. Barre, MA: Barre Publishers, 1968.
Heinerman, John. Heinerman’s Encyclopedia of Healing Juices. Englewood Cliffs, NJ: Prentice Hall, 1994.
———. Heinerman’s Encyclopedia of Fruits, Vegetables and Herbs. Paramus, NJ: Prentice Hall, 1988.
Jensen, Bernard, DC, ND. Nature Has a Remedy. Escondido, CA: privately published, 1979.
Pedersen, Mark. Nutritional Herbology: A Reference Guide to Herbs. Warsaw, IN: Wendell W. Whitman Company, 1994. [Original work.]
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, BIOMEDICAL
Murray, Michael T., ND. The Healing Power of Herbs. Revised and expanded 2nd ed. Rocklin, CA: Prima Publishing, 1995.
Bisset, Norman Grainger, ed. and trans., and Max Wichtl, ed. Herbal Drugs and Phytopharmaceuticals: A Handbook for Practice on a Scientific Basis. Stuttgart: Medpharm Scientific Publishers, 1994. [Contains German E Commission reports.]
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, GERMAN
Aloysius, Brother. Comfort to the Sick. Originally published in Holland, 1903. Reprint ed. New York: Samuel Weiser, 1992.
Gümbel, Dietrich, PhD Principles of Holistic Therapy with Herbal Essences. 2nd revised and expanded English ed. Brussels: Haug, 1993. [A priceless book; conveys a deep understanding of the physiological action of herbs.]
Kneipp, Sebastian, Father. The Kneipp Cure: An Absolutely Verbal and Literal Translation of “Meine Wasserkur.” Complete American ed, translated from the Fiftieth German Edition. New York: The Kneipp Cure Publishing Company, 1896. [Mostly an account of hydrotherapy; contains some herbal treatments.]
Kroeber, Ludwig, MD. Das neuzeitliche Kräuterbuch. 3 vols. Stuttgart: Hippockrates-Verlag Marquarte & CIE., 1948–49.
Pahlow, Mannfried. Das grosse Buch der Heilplanzen. Munich: Gräfe und Unzer, 1989.
Rademacher, Johann Gottfried, MD. Rechtfertigung der von den Gelehrten misskannten, verstandesrechten Erfahrungsheillehre der alten scheidenkunstigen Geheimarzte und treue Mittheilung des Ergebnisses einer 25 jahrigen Erprobung dieser Lehre am Krankenbette. 2 vols. Berlin: G. Reimer, 1841. [One of the finest books on empirical medicine published in Europe.]
Treben, Maria. Health through God’s Pharmacy. English translation. Steyr: Wilhelm Ennsthaler, 1984.
de Vries, Jan. The Pharmacy Guide to Herbal Remedies. Edinburgh and London: Mainstream Publishing, 2001.

HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, FRENCH
Kenner, Dan, and Yves Requena. Botanical Medicine: A European Professional Perspective. Brookline, MA: Paradigm Publications, 2001. [An attempt to analyze European and Western herbs in terms of traditional Chinese medicine.]
Mességué, Maurice. Health Secrets of Plants and Herbs. New York: William Morrow and Co., 1979.
———. Of Men and Plants: The Autobiography of the World’s Most Famous Plant Healer. French edition, 1970. New York: The Macmillan Company, 1972. [Entertaining reading. This was my first herbal.]
Palaiseul, Jean. Grandmother’s Secrets: Her Green Guide to Health from Plants. French ed., 1972. Translated from the French by Pamela Swinglehurst. New York: G. P. Putnam’s Sons, 1974.
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, EASTERN EUROPEAN
Hutchins, Alma R. A Handbook of Native American Herbs. Boston and London: Shambhala, 1992. [Despite the title, this book contains extensive information on Russian herbal research and experience with very little on American Indian usage.]

Zevin, Igor Vilevich. A Russian Herbal: Traditional Remedies for Health and Healing. Rochester, VT: Healing Arts Press, 1996. [Highly recommended.]
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, TRADITIONAL CHINESE MEDICINE
Bensky, Dan, and Andrew Gamble. Chinese Herbal Medicine: Materia Medica. Seattle: Eastland Press, 1986. [Essential modern textbook.]
Kaptchuk, Ted, OMD. The Web That Has No Weaver. Chicago: Congdon & Weed, 1983.
Lu, Henry C. Chinese Natural Cures: Traditional Methods for Remedies and Preventions. New York: Black Dog and Leventhal Publishers, 1994. [One of the few modern Chinese herbals that contains a lot of folklore.]
Zhongjing, Zhang. Treatise on Febrile Disease Caused by Cold. Translated by Luo Xiwen, PhD Beijing: New World Press, 1985. [The writings of one of the greatest masters of herbal medicine of all time.]
———. Synopsis of Prescriptions of the Golden Chamber: A Classic of Traditional Chinese Medicine. Translated by Luo Xiwen, PhD Beijing: New World Press, 1987. [Translation of one of the great classics on Chinese herbalism.]
HERBAL MATERIA MEDICA, AYURVEDIC
Kacera, Walter, DN, PhD Ayurvedic Tongue Diagnosis. Twin Lakes, WI: Lotus Press, 2006.
